1 Encoding guidelines

1.1 Introduction and encoding principles

These TEI Guidelines serve as a reference tool for encoders working primarily with Special Collections material within Manchester Digital Collections (MDC). Using clear, natural-language definitions and real-world examples drawn from MDC practice, they provide recommendations and rationale for TEI encoding, supporting consistency and greater semantic precision in the creation of metadata.

TEI is a flexible and widely adopted standard, but it can be applied in many different ways, with variation in how elements and attributes are used. What brings us together as a community is not simply the use of TEI itself, but a shared approach to its application. By defining and documenting TEI encoding conventions, the Guidelines establish a shared framework for defining best practice, developing common standards, and applying them consistently, ensuring that metadata is reliable, interoperable, and reusable across collections and systems.

These Guidelines are situated within the TEI Consolidated Schema Community (TEI-CSC), an open, collaborative initiative that brings together institutions to establish shared approaches to encoding Special Collections metadata. Within this framework, the Consolidated Schema defines a common validation layer, while these Guidelines provide the interpretative layer, translating shared principles into practical application. This model enables institutions to work collectively as a unified community, not only within the UK but also within the wider international TEI community, aligning practice and contributing to the development and advancement of TEI best practice.

The TEI-CSC, which extends and builds on the work of the Text Encoding Initiative Consortium (TEI-C), develops and refines these shared approaches as part of an evolving body of practice. The Guidelines presented here form part of that broader effort, translating shared principles into practical, implementable guidance.

The development of these Guidelines builds on work undertaken over an extended period across multiple institutions as part of a broader movement towards greater standardisation in TEI practice. This has involved a sustained and focused phase of authorship and refinement, during which TEI encoding practices have been critically reviewed and developed. Central to this work has been the adoption of a consolidated schema, a coherent editorial approach, and a transparent and consistent method for encoding description across collections and institutional contexts. This process has been informed by ongoing engagement with established community practices, including those developed through the Union Catalogue of Manuscripts from the Islamicate World (FIHRIST), the Bodleian Libraries, and Cambridge Digital Library (CUDL), ensuring alignment with recognised standards while addressing the specific requirements within MDC and its technical infrastructure.

The MDC platform operates within a broader technical ecosystem developed by teams at Cambridge University Library. This includes the Cambridge Digital Collection Platform, which underpins the Cambridge Digital Library (CUDL) and other institutional implementations. It is a platform that takes source metadata and images and processes them for online display and discovery, with IIIF capability. The Guidelines reflect the requirements of this environment, ensuring that encoding practices are both semantically robust and technically aligned with platform constraints.

Developed within The University of Manchester Library, Collection Strategies Directorate, Metadata and Discovery, the MDC TEI Guidelines are intended to support local practice within MDC and beyond. They do not replace the TEI-C Guidelines, but document how TEI is applied in this specific context, focusing on the elements and attributes in active use. Their development represents a shift away from project-specific or ad hoc approaches towards a more unified and sustainable model of practice.

The Guidelines represent current best practice and will continue to evolve in response to emerging requirements. They are available via the TEI for Special Collections website, with contributions, feedback, and suggestions welcomed through the Consolidated Schema GitHub repository.

1.1.1 TEI-CSC core working group

These Guidelines have been authored by Ourania Karapasia, in consultation with Yasmin Faghihi and Thomas Higgins, and developed through the work of a core working group operating within the TEI-CSC, whose members met regularly to shape and refine the framework. The development process has also benefited from contributions and feedback from colleagues within the TEI-CSC.

Ourania Karapasia

Digital Metadata Specialist, Metadata & Discovery, Collection Strategies, The University of Manchester Library

Yasmin Faghihi

Head of Near and Middle Eastern Manuscripts and Printed Collections, FIHRIST (Chair), Cambridge University Library

Head of Digital Library Unit, Digital Humanities Coordinator, Cambridge University Library

Software Developer, Digital Library Coordinator, Digital Humanities, Cambridge University Library

Technical Director, Senior Software Developer, Digital Humanities, Cambridge University Library

1.1.2 TEI encoding framework, workflow, and principles

While the Guidelines aim to translate XML concepts into more accessible language, they remain inherently technical and presuppose a basic understanding of XML, TEI, and Special Collections metadata practices. Encoders new to TEI are encouraged to begin with an introductory tutorial before engaging with this material, such as the Learn the TEI available on the TEI-C website.

Working with Oxygen XML Editor

All encoding work for MDC is carried out using the Oxygen XML Editor.

The software is available as a 30-day free trial, and The University of Manchester also provides an academic licence, accessible via the IT Services Software Centre and Company Portal on University-managed computers.

Guidance on using Oxygen XML Editor is available on the Oxygen website.

The following guidance outlines recommended configuration and usage of Oxygen XML Editor to support consistent formatting, readability, and structure in TEI-XML files.

Oxygen Format

XML files can be formatted and indented using Oxygen’s built-in settings to ensure a consistent and readable layout.

The following configuration is preferred as it provides a clearer structure and improves the readability of TEI-XML.

In Oxygen, go to Options → Preferences → Editor → Format

Set the Indent size to 2, ensure that Indent on enter and Enable smart enter are enabled, and set the Line width in Format and Indent to 200. Leave all other settings as the default.

Use the Format and Indent toolbar button during editing and before submission to maintain a consistent and well-structured XML file.

Oxygen Fonts

By default, Oxygen XML Editor uses generic fonts, such as Monospaced or Serif, which do not support all Unicode scripts. As a result, some characters may not display correctly.

Non-Latin scripts, such as the Ethiopic, used for languages including Geʿez, may appear as empty squares (tofu) characters if the selected font does not support them.

To ensure correct rendering in such cases, select a font that supports the script in use. Ebrima is an OpenType font that supports a range of African scripts, including Ethiopic, and can be used to display these characters correctly.

In Oxygen, go to Options → Preferences → Appearance → Fonts

Set the Editor font to Ebrima. If using Author mode, also set the Author default font to Ebrima.

If Ebrima is not available, other fonts that support the Ethiopic Unicode block, such as Abyssinica SIL, may be installed manually.

Oxygen whitespace

Unnecessary whitespace between elements should be avoided. Whitespace must not be introduced within element content, as it may affect how the text is rendered or interpreted. Excessive whitespace can increase file size and affect consistency and readability, so any unnecessary gaps should be removed prior to submission.

Oxygen Check Spelling

Oxygen XML Editor includes a builtin spellchecking tool for reviewing textual content in a TEI file. The tool will highlight potential spelling errors and provide suggested corrections.

In Oxygen, go to Edit → Check Spelling | Set the Default language to English (United Kingdom)

Oxygen Find/Replace

Oxygen XML Editor provides tools for locating and updating content within a single file or across multiple files.

Use the Find/Replace dialog box to search for and replace text within the current file. This is useful for correcting repeated errors, updating terminology, or making consistent changes to markup.

For batch updates, use Find/Replace in Files to search across multiple files within a selected directory. This should be used with caution, particularly when modifying markup, to avoid unintended changes.

In Oxygen, access these tools via:

Edit → Find/Replace

Edit → Find/Replace in Files

Find/Replace in Files allows you to refine your search using a range of options, for example, Case sensitive, Whole words only, and others), and to define the scope of the search by selecting a Specified path, Project, or other.

Find/Replace in Files is also useful for searching across multiple files to identify how a particular element or attribute is used in other files. This can support the consistent application of standards and practices. However, existing usage may not always be correct. Use with caution. Encoding identified through these results may not always be correct, so all results should be carefully reviewed before applying changes.

The following section outlines the end-to-end TEI workflow for MDC, including the tools and editorial processes involved.

TEI workflow and editorial process

TEI files are created and maintained within the Bitbucket version control workflow, beginning either with the conversion of MARC data to TEI using MarkEdit, the upload of a template or post-converted file, or the creation of a new file from scratch. The TEI file is then committed to the mdc-contents Bitbucket repository using GitHub Desktop, enabling version control and collaborative editing.

Encoding and editorial work are carried out in Oxygen XML Editor, using the appropriate templates and schemas for the material type. File naming follows the MDC reference number normalisation convention and should be applied consistently from the outset.

During the encoding process, the MDC Preview Tool is used to preview how TEI files will render in the MDC interface before uploading them to the Staging environment. This supports early validation of structure and display, reduces the need for repeated uploads to Staging, and enables quality checking of TEI XML prior to publication.

Once encoding is complete, manifests are prepared in accordance with MDC guidance, and the TEI files are uploaded to the MDC Staging environment. The MDC Content Dashboard is then used to review the TEI, supporting final validation and the identification of issues such as incorrect or incomplete <locus>, and other display or linking errors, prior to publication. Where relevant, additional platforms such as Sketchfab may be used for the integration of 3D content.

MDC Preview Tool

MDC Content Dashboard

MDC filenaming conventions

How to create a manifest for MDC

Workflow for TEI using GitHub Desktop and Bitbucket

Metadata Conversion - Alma MARC to MARCXML via MarcEdit

How to embed Sketchfab models in TEI for MDC

Controlled vocabularies, authority files, linked data, and FAIR principles

The metadata created for inclusion in MDC follows linked data principles through the use of persistent identifiers from recognised external authority files, such as VIAF, LC, and Getty vocabularies. These identifiers support disambiguation, consistency, and interoperability, contributing particularly to the Findability and Interoperability aspects of the FAIR principles.

Within TEIXML, authority control is implemented by recording externally defined identifiers using key, with the source authority indicated via source and documented explicitly in the TEI header using <taxonomy>. This establishes a clear, machinereadable association between encoded entities and external authoritative resources, while maintaining TEI metadata that is humanauthored and grounded in established descriptive practice.

At present, metadata is delivered and maintained as TEIXML rather than being published directly as linked open data. The consistent use of standardised vocabularies and persistent identifiers ensures that the metadata is FAIRaligned and structured for reuse and can support future transformation or publication in linked open data formats should appropriate technical infrastructure and organisational frameworks be introduced.

Where applicable, values for names, places, subjects, roles, languages, materials, and techniques should be drawn from the following controlled vocabularies and authority files:

Library of Congress Subject Headings (LCSH)

ISO 639-2 Codes for the Representation of Names of Languages (LC)

MARC Code List for Relators (LC)

Virtual International Authority File (VIAF)

Getty Thesaurus of Geographic Names® (TGN)

Getty Art & Architecture Thesaurus® (AAT)

General encoding principles

TEI encoding should be consistent, purposeful, and semantically precise. Elements and attributes should be used to express clearly defined meanings, and applied consistently across records.

Avoid leaving empty elements unless explicitly required. Where information is not yet available, elements should be removed or temporarily commented out rather than left empty, particularly where they correspond to display fields.

Encoding decisions should be guided by intended use. Names, dates, and places should only be encoded where they support indexing, display, or analysis. Redundant or excessive encoding should be avoided.

Comments <!-- --> may be used during editing to retain notes or placeholders but should be removed prior to final publication unless they serve a defined structural purpose.

Textual formatting in TEI should be applied using <hi> with rend, rather than using elements such as <label> for presentational purposes. Supported values include italic, bold, superscript, and subscript.

Follow the agreed customisation, its schema, and the documented encoding rules, rather than mixing multiple encoding systems or practices, or inventing new solutions. Any extensions or changes must be made through the agreed customisation and documentation process, not introduced ad hoc within individual files.

Record the same information in the same elements across files, using consistent attributes and controlled values, so the metadata remains predictable, interoperable, and maintainable.

Keep <teiHeader> metadata-focused and avoid transcription-oriented encoding from <text>. Select TEI elements for the semantic claim they make, not for convenience or appearance.

Maintain one meaning per element and attribute across files. Use the same encoding consistently from one record to the next, and avoid over-encoding repeated entities when a single, clear encoding is sufficient.

Templates

Templates provide the starting point for TEI encoding and should be selected according to the nature and structural characteristics of the item. They define the structural framework within which metadata is recorded and must be used consistently across files. The following templates are used as the basis for encoding.

template-master

(general single-unit items)

template-ms

(manuscript single-unit items)

template-pr

(printed books single-unit items)

template-msPart

(composite or multi-part items)

template-correspDesc

(correspondence items)

template-vis

(visual collections single-unit items)

The selection between template-ms or -pr and template-msPart begins with determining whether the item represents a single coherent unit or a composite structure. This distinction is particularly relevant for manuscript material but applies across formats.

There are no fixed levels of metadata. Each item is described as a discrete entity, and the level of detail recorded will vary depending on the nature of the material and the information available. Wherever possible, metadata should be as granular and descriptive as the source permits, while remaining consistent with the schema and established encoding practices.

All placeholders must be replaced with actual values, and elements that are not applicable should be removed. Comments used during encoding should be cleared prior to final publication unless they serve a defined structural purpose.

1.2 <TEI> | TEI document

<TEI> (TEI document) contains a single TEI-conformant document, combining a single TEI header with one or more members of the model.resource class.

All TEI files in MDC follow a standardised structure. Each begins with an XML declaration and a processing instruction that informs any program reading the file that it is written in XML. This declaration is always the same. It contains two attributes:

version specifies the version of XML being used (1.0)

encoding specifies the character encoding of the file (UTF-8)

<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>

MDC TEI files conform to the Consolidated Schema maintained by the TEI for Special Collections community. The schema repository provides two main customisations:

odd/cdcp.odd used by the Cambridge Digital Collection Platform (CDCP)

odd/manuscripts-catalogue.odd used by the Medieval Manuscripts in Oxford Libraries and FIHRIST

For MDC files, the relevant profile is odd/cdcp.odd. To reference this schema for validation, include the following processing instructions immediately before the <TEI> root element:

<?xml-model href="https://raw.githubusercontent.com/tei-for-special-collections/consolidated-schema/refs/heads/main/releases/rng/cdcp.rng" type="application/xml" schematypens="http://relaxng.org/ns/structure/1.0"?> <?xml-model href="https://raw.githubusercontent.com/tei-for-special-collections/consolidated-schema/refs/heads/main/releases/rng/cdcp.rng" type="application/xml" schematypens="http://purl.oclc.org/dsdl/schematron"?>

These instructions associate the file with the correct Relax NG and Schematron schemas for validation in XML editors such as oXygen and ensure that the files conform to the Consolidated Schema.

MDC files must always use the CDCP profile (cdcp.rng).

Items originally catalogued in FIHRIST and subsequently ingested into MDC will retain their existing metadata. Through subsequent manual or automated re-editing, these records will be aligned with MDC standards as defined in the MDC TEI profile (odd/cdcp.odd).

Each file is a TEI document, with <TEI> as the root element. This element serves as the container for all other components, providing the structural foundation for encoding texts in a standardised, machine-readable format.

All valid XML files must have one, and only one, top-level element. Since MDC files are TEI files, this top-level element will always be <TEI>.

The mandatory root element <TEI> must always contain the declaration of the TEI namespace URI http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0, using xmlns and must also include xml:id, which provides a unique identifier for the digital resource.

@xml:id

xml:id provides a unique identifier for the digital resource (TEI-XML file + digital facsimile).

<TEI xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="MS-LATIN-00022">

Psalter of Joan of Navarre: Latin MS 22

The value MS-LATIN-00022 is derived from the source item’s classmark, Latin MS 22, and is normalised for use as both the TEI-XML filename and xml:id on <TEI>. It is used on <TEI>, rather than on <msDesc>, because it serves as the identifier for the digital resource as a whole: the TEI-XML file, the manuscript description contained in <msDesc>, and the associated digital facsimile metadata included in <facsimile>.

In manuscript catalogue schemas such as Bodleian Medieval and FIHRIST, the root <TEI> xml:id follows a different workflow. In those schemas, it is a pre-allocated manuscript record identifier, assigned independently of the manuscript classmark and its TEI-XML filename. It provides a stable identifier for the catalogue record. For example, in FIHRIST, xml:id=manuscript_26527 is used on <TEI> for Persian MS 1008. The value Persian_MS_1008 corresponds to the TEI-XML filename derived from the manuscript identifier Persian MS 1008, and is used as xml:id on <msDesc>.

For further information on how XML filenames are derived from item identifiers, see MDC filenaming conventions. For manifests, see How to create a manifest for MDC

The namespace of the Text Encoding Initiative (TEI) was agreed by the TEI Technical Council in 2002, is regarded as permanent and is not expected to change.

1.2.1 General structure of a TEI-XML file in MDC

Within <TEI>, three core elements appear in a fixed order: <teiHeader>, <facsimile>, and <text>. The final line of the file concludes with the closing <TEI>, marking the end of the root element.

The overall structure of a TEI-conformant XML file in MDC may be summarised as follows:

<TEI xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0">
<!-- Root element of the TEI file -->
 <teiHeader>
<!-- Metadata describing the digital resource: bibliographic and descriptive information about TEI-XML file itself as well as the source (analogue or digital), from which it derives -->
<!-- Contains <fileDesc>|<encodingDesc>|<profileDesc>|<revisionDesc> -->
<!-- Mandatory. Primary focus of these guidelines -->
 </teiHeader>
 <facsimile>
<!-- Metadata describing, and linking to, digital images of the file -->
<!-- Contains <graphic> within <surface> -->
<!-- Mandatory. Generated, using the Manifest -->
<!-- Boilerplate in MDC TEI-XML template. DO NOT amend this! -->
 </facsimile>
 <text>
<!-- Transcription of the item -->
<!-- Contains <div> within <body> -->
<!-- Conditional. Inclusion depends on project-specific practice -->
<!-- Boilerplate in MDC TEI-XML template. DO NOT amend this! -->
 </text>
</TEI>

A digital resource in MDC refers to the combination of a digitised item and its structured metadata and optional transcription, encoded in TEI. The TEI file represents the resource in a machine-readable form, linked to a digital facsimile via <facsimile>.

1.3 <teiHeader> | TEI header

<teiHeader> (TEI header) supplies descriptive and declarative metadata associated with a digital resource or set of resources.

Every MDC digital resource (an XML file conforming to the TEI standard attached to a digital facsimile) must include a TEI header. It is the first mandatory component of a TEI-XML file and therefore always appears immediately after the opening <TEI> root element. It is encoded using a single <teiHeader>, which contains various sub-elements to organise different parts of the description.

Although <teiHeader> is not primarily intended for digital collections, it is used for this purpose in MDC. Its core function is to enable the structured, semantic description of a digital resource using TEI-conformant encoding.

<teiHeader> records not only descriptive metadata about the item itself, but also its source, the encoding practices applied, and the processes involved in its creation.

<teiHeader> provides the flexibility to describe both textual and non-textual aspects of a digital resource. Therefore, it is highly recommended to include as much relevant metadata as possible to ensure semantic richness and interoperability.

<teiHeader> comprises several elements that together enable a structured, semantic representation of metadata. These elements can be extended with additional sub-elements and attributes to support the level of detail required by a given project. As a result, <teiHeader> may range from a simple, concise metadata description to a large and complex metadata structure, depending on project needs.

<teiHeader> presents all metadata in four principal elements:

<teiHeader>
 <fileDesc>
<!-- Metadata about the digital resource: the TEI file + facsimile, its contributors, its publication, and its source item -->
<!-- Contains <titleStmt>|<publicationStmt>|<sourceDesc> -->
 </fileDesc>
 <encodingDesc>
<!-- Metadata about encoding choices, including taxonomies used -->
<!-- Contains <projectDesc> and <classDecl> -->
 </encodingDesc>
 <profileDesc>
<!-- Metadata describing contents in terms of subject headings -->
<!-- Contains <keywords> -->
<!-- Metadata for correspondence letters -->
<!-- Contains <correspDesc> -->
 </profileDesc>
 <revisionDesc>
<!-- Metadata describing changes to the TEI-XML file -->
<!-- Contains <change> -->
 </revisionDesc>
</teiHeader>

Some parts of <teiHeader> are always required, while others are conditional or optional, allowing encoders to adjust the level of metadata to suit project needs and resource types.

1.4 <fileDesc> | file description

<fileDesc> (file description) contains a full bibliographic description of an electronic file.

<fileDesc> is the first major subdivision of <teiHeader> and always appears as its first child element. As the foundation of the metadata structure, it is a key component of <teiHeader>, providing essential metadata about the digital resource, including its bibliographic and publication-related details.

<fileDesc> contains three primary elements:

<teiHeader>
 <fileDesc>
  <titleStmt>
<!-- Metadata about the title of the digital resource and the associated contributors -->
<!-- Contains <title> and <resp>|<persName> within <respStmt> -->
  </titleStmt>
  <publicationStmt>
<!-- Metadata providing publication details of the digital resource -->
<!-- Contains <publisher>|<date>|<pubPlace>|<availability> -->
<!-- Boilerplate in MDC TEI-XML template. DO NOT amend this! -->
  </publicationStmt>
  <sourceDesc>
<!-- Descriptive metadata about the source material from which the digital resource is derived. -->
<!-- Contains <msDesc>-->
  </sourceDesc>
 </fileDesc>
 <encodingDesc/>|<profileDesc/>|<revisionDesc/>
</teiHeader>

1.5 <titleStmt> | title statement

<titleStmt> (title statement) groups information about the title of a work and those responsible for its content.

<titleStmt> is the first component of <fileDesc> and refers to the digital resource (TEI-XML file + digital facsimile). For the most part, the digital resource retains the descriptive information of the physical source item while also recording responsibility for the creation of its digital version.

The following elements are used to record information about the scope and authorship of the digital resource:

<teiHeader>
 <fileDesc>
  <titleStmt>
<!-- Metadata containing the title given to the digital resource, together with information identifying those responsible for its creation -->
<!-- May contain <funder> and <principal> -->
   <title>
<!-- Metadata providing a title for the digital resource -->
   </title>
   <respStmt>
<!-- Metadata listing direct or indirect contributors -->
<!-- Contains <resp> and <persName> -->
   </respStmt>
  </titleStmt>
  <publicationStmt/>|<sourceDesc/>|</fileDesc>|<encodingDesc/>|<profileDesc/>|<revisionDesc/>
</teiHeader>

<titleStmt> provides key information about the title of the digital resource (comprising the TEI-XML file and digital facsimile) and the individuals or organisations responsible for its creation. It does not describe the source item, which is recorded in <sourceDesc>.

1.6 <title> | title

<title> (title) contains a title for any kind of work.

<title> is the first component of <titleStmt> and refers to the digital resource (TEI-XML file + digital facsimile). It contains the primary name for the digital resource, which is the unique identifier of the physical source item.

While <title> within <titleStmt> is required in the TEI structure, it is not displayed in MDC. Instead, it serves as an internal reference for identifying the resource within the TEI file.

<titleStmt> must include <title> containing the unique identifier (reference or classmark number) of the item.

<TEI xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="MS-LATIN-00022">
<teiHeader>
<fileDesc>
<titleStmt>
<title>Latin MS 22</title>
<respStmt/>
</titleStmt>
</fileDesc>
</teiHeader>
</TEI>

Psalter of Joan of Navarre: Latin MS 22

For printed books, shelfmarks may be less self-explanatory than manuscript references, which often include a prefix or siglum such as Latin MS. <title> should therefore give a contextualised form of the identifier by combining the collection name with the item number.

For example, if an item has a shelfmark that is a short identifier, such as a two-digit number like 15 from the Incunable Collection, it should be encoded as follows:

<TEI xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="PR-INCU-00015">
<teiHeader>
<fileDesc>
<titleStmt>
<title>Incunable 15</title>
<respStmt/>
</titleStmt>
<!-- additional metadata -->
<msIdentifier>
<!-- additional metadata -->
<collection>Incunable Collection</collection>
<idno>15</idno>
</msIdentifier>
</fileDesc>
</teiHeader>
</TEI>

Grammatica rhythmica (cum commento): 15

<title> provides a simplified and contextualised identifier, while <msIdentifier> ensures the full collection name and detailed shelfmark are included.

<titleStmt> provides key information about the title of the digital resource (comprising the TEI-XML file and any associated digital facsimile) and the individuals or organisations responsible for its creation, including activities such as digitisation, content coordination, data conversion, metadata processing, editing, standardisation, proofreading, cataloguing, conservation, and overall project supervision.

In addition, <titleStmt> may include other project-related details such as funding bodies and principal investigators. These are encoded using <funder> and <principal> respectively. Both elements are optional, and their inclusion depends on the requirements of the relevant project framework. In MDC, only <funder> displays in the interface under the field label Funding.

1.7 <funder> | funding body

<funder> (funding body) specifies the name of an individual, institution, or organisation responsible for the funding of a project or text.

MDC display label — Funding

<funder> may be used within <titleStmt>, immediately after <title>, to record funding bodies such as grants, foundations, research councils, or other agencies that financially supported the creation of the digital resource.

• Funding: Arts and Humanities Research Council (AHRC)

<teiHeader>
 <fileDesc>
  <titleStmt>
   <title>Aldine 18873</title>
   <funder>Arts and Humanities Research Council (AHRC)</funder>
   <respStmt/>
  </titleStmt>
 </fileDesc>
</teiHeader>

Le terze rime di Dante: 18873

1.8 <principal> | principal researcher

<principal> (principal researcher) supplies the name of the principal researcher responsible for the creation of an electronic text.

<principal> is typically reserved for the academic lead responsible for instigating and overseeing the entire project but may not be the actual encoder. It is used to indicate overall responsibility for the resource. <respStmt> on the other hand, is intended to credit individuals for specific tasks or contributions within the project. If an individual has both overarching responsibility for the project and responsibility for specific tasks, they should generally be recorded in <principal> only, unless the specific task warrants explicit attribution. This approach minimises redundancy and simplifies the metadata structure.

<teiHeader>
 <fileDesc>
  <titleStmt>
   <title/>
   <funder/>
   <principal>Vanellope von Schweetz</principal>
   <respStmt/>
  </titleStmt>
 </fileDesc>
</teiHeader>

Both <funder> and <principal> are repeatable and may be used to encode multiple funding bodies or academic leads associated with the project.

At a minimum, <titleStmt> must include <title>, typically containing the item's shelfmark, and one or more <respStmt>, listing the responsible individuals or organisations.

1.9 <respStmt> | statement of responsibility

<respStmt> (statement of responsibility) supplies a statement of responsibility for the intellectual content of a text, edition, recording, or series, where the specialized elements for authors, editors, etc. do not suffice or do not apply. May also be used to encode information about individuals or organizations which have played a role in the production or distribution of a bibliographic work.

<respStmt> should list any person or organisation who played a direct or indirect role in the creation of the digital resource. Given that many digital resources result from collaborative or cumulative effort, it is essential to list and acknowledge all significant contributors.

<respStmt> is used to record the role and name of individuals or organisations whose contributions are secondary to those listed in <sourceDesc> or <msDesc>. Those two elements identify the primary contributors responsible for the intellectual content of the original source item, such as authors, editors, publishers, transcribers, and illustrators.

By contrast, <respStmt> identifies individuals or organisations involved in the creation, processing, or management of the digital resource, including those responsible for encoding, metadata creation, digitisation, or project coordination.

<respStmt> contains two elements: <resp> is used to record the role and responsibility of the individual or organisation involved, while <persName> is used to document the name of the contributor.

1.10 <resp> | responsibility | <persName> | personal name

<resp> (responsibility) contains a phrase describing the nature of a person's intellectual responsibility, or an organisation's role in the production or distribution of a work.

<persName> (personal name) contains a proper noun or proper-noun phrase referring to a person, possibly including one or more of the person's forenames, surnames, honorifics, added names, etc.

Together, these two elements provide a structured and precise mechanism for recording contributions, ensuring clarity in the attribution of roles and responsibilities.

When recording contributions, it is important to represent the roles and responsibilities of each individual or organisation clearly and consistently. Each contributor should be encoded within a separate <respStmt>. Where applicable, multiple <resp> may be used within a single <respStmt> to capture different aspects of that contributor’s involvement.

The first <resp> should specify the contributor’s primary role or job title. Subsequent <resp> should describe the nature of their contributions, outlining specific tasks or responsibilities undertaken.

<respStmt>
 <resp>Digital Metadata Specialist</resp>
 <resp>TEI-XML editing and encoding</resp>
 <persName>Ourania Karapasia</persName>
</respStmt>
<respStmt>
 <resp>Curator (Visual Collections)</resp>
 <resp>Reviewing and proofreading</resp>
 <persName>Anne Marie Anderton</persName>
</respStmt>
<respStmt>
 <resp>Project Photographer</resp>
 <resp>Digitisation</resp>
 <persName>James Robinson</persName>
 <persName>Jo Castle</persName>
</respStmt>
<respStmt>
 <resp>Manchester Digital Collections (MDC) Content Coordinator</resp>
 <persName>Charlotte Hoare</persName>
</respStmt>
<respStmt>
 <resp>Senior Software Developer</resp>
 <resp>Metadata processing</resp>
 <persName>Thomas Higgins</persName>
</respStmt>

Psalter of Joan of Navarre: Latin MS 22

A single <persName> may be associated with multiple <resp> to reflect different roles undertaken at various times or in distinct capacities. Conversely, when a particular role or responsibility is shared among individuals, a single <resp> may be followed by multiple <persName>. In both cases, all relevant role and contributor information should be contained within a single <respStmt>.

Maintaining a clear and consistent structure is essential. Distinct roles should always be separated into individual <resp>. Descriptions should be sufficiently detailed to capture the full scope of each contributor’s work. This approach ensures an organised and transparent representation of contributions within the TEI file.

Contributor or participant roles | associated responsibilities

Here are some standardised contributor or participant roles and their associated responsibilities commonly applicable in MDC.

Photographer [Project | Senior]

Digitisation

Manchester Digital Collections (MDC) Content Coordinator

Digital Metadata Specialist

TEI-XML editing and encoding standardisation, reviewing and supervision

Conversion to TEI-XML from ALMA-MARC [LUNA-DC-VRA, EMu, ELGAR-EAD, etc.]

Metadata Assistant

TEI-XML proofreading, editing, and encoding

Conversion to TEI-XML from ALMA-MARC [LUNA-DC-VRA, EMu, ELGAR-EAD, etc.]

Senior Software Developer

Metadata processing

Software Developer

Metadata mapping, conversion to TEI-XML

Curator (Visual Collections)

Reviewing and proofreading

Curator (Global Manuscripts)

TEI-XML editing and encoding

Rare Print Metadata Specialist

ALMA-MARC metadata

Post-doctoral Research Assistant (PDRA)

Transcription

Conservator

Binding description, physical condition

Roles, responsibilities, and the overall scope and standardisation practices are discussed and defined in consultation with the Digital Metadata Specialist at the initiation of a project. For internal staff contributors or participants, the first <resp> should reflect the official job title as listed on the Library Intranet.

The inclusion of xml:id on <respStmt> and when on <resp> is optional and should be determined by the requirements of the project.

1.11 <publicationStmt> | publication statement

<publicationStmt> (publication statement) groups information concerning the publication or distribution of an electronic or other text.

<publicationStmt> provides critical details about the publisher and rights associated with the digital resource. It contains the following sub-elements:

<teiHeader>
 <fileDesc>
  <titleStmt/>
  <publicationStmt>
<!-- Metadata specifying who is publishing the digital resource, as well as where and when the publication occurs, along with information about the distribution rights for images and metadata associated with the resource -->
   <publisher>
<!-- Metadata about the publisher of the digital resource -->
<!-- Contains <date> -->
   </publisher>
   <pubPlace>
<!-- Metadata about the place of publication -->
<!-- Contains <address> -->
   </pubPlace>
   <availability>
<!-- Metadata about rights and distribution -->
<!-- Contains <licence> -->
   </availability>
  </publicationStmt>
  <sourceDesc/>
 </fileDesc>
 <encodingDesc/>|<profileDesc/>|<revisionDesc/>
</teiHeader>

<publicationStmt> provides a comprehensive declaration of publication details and rights management for the TEI-based digital resource. It specifies the publisher, publication date, and usage restrictions for images and metadata.

1.12 <publisher> | publisher | <pubPlace> | publication place

<publisher> (publisher) provides the name of the organisation responsible for the publication or distribution of a bibliographic item.

<pubPlace> (publication place) contains the name of the place where a bibliographic item was published.

<publisher> and <pubPlace> are mandatory. Together, they identify the institution responsible for publishing the digital resource and its location. In the context of MDC, it is The University of Manchester Library, which manages and disseminates the content.

<publicationStmt>
 <publisher>The University of Manchester Library</publisher>
 <date when="2025">2025</date>
 <pubPlace>
  <address>
   <addrLine>The John Rylands Library</addrLine>
   <street>150 Deansgate</street>
   <settlement>Manchester</settlement>
   <postCode>M3 3EH</postCode>
   <addrLine>
    <ref target="https://www.library.manchester.ac.uk/rylands/special-collections/">The John Rylands Library</ref>
   </addrLine>
   <addrLine>
    <email>uml.special-collections@manchester.ac.uk</email>
   </addrLine>
  </address>
 </pubPlace>
<!-- additional metadata -->
</publicationStmt>

This is the standard publication statement for Special Collections items housed at The John Rylands Library and published by The University of Manchester Library.

When a resource is specifically associated with an institution other than, or in addition to, The University of Manchester Library, such as the Manchester Museum, <authority> should be included within <publicationStmt>.

<authority> (release authority) supplies the name of a person or other agency responsible for making a work available, other than a publisher or distributor.

<authority> should identify the body responsible for the intellectual authority of the resource, while <publisher> identifies the organisation responsible for publishing it. For example, when a resource is associated with the Manchester Museum, <authority> should identify the Museum as the authoritative body, while The University of Manchester Library should be recorded as the publisher.

<publicationStmt>
 <authority>Manchester Museum</authority>
 <publisher>The University of Manchester Library</publisher>
 <date when="2025">2025</date>
 <pubPlace>
  <address>
   <addrLine>The John Rylands Library</addrLine>
<!-- additional metadata -->
  </address>
 </pubPlace>
<!-- additional metadata -->
</publicationStmt>

1.13 <availability> | availability | <licence> | licence

<availability> (availability) supplies information about the availability of a text, for example any restrictions on its use or distribution, its copyright status, any licence applying to it, etc.

<availability> defines usage restrictions for the digital resource, with separate sections for display images, downloadable images, and metadata. It specifies rights conditions and licensing terms, distinguishing between different types of content and their permitted uses.

<availability> specifies any restrictions on the distribution of a resource, either through a concise prose statement or a formal licensing declaration using <licence>, with a link to the full version of the licence provided via target.

<licence> (licence) contains information about a licence or other legal agreement applicable to the text.

The following shows how access and rights conditions for images and metadata are structured and encoded using <availability> within <publicationStmt> in the TEI header.

<publicationStmt>
<!-- additional metadata -->
 <availability xml:id="displayImageRights" status="restricted">
  <p>
<!-- Conditions governing the display and reuse of images are defined following rights assessment and in accordance with institutional access and rights management policies -->
  </p>
 </availability>
 <availability xml:id="downloadImageRights" status="restricted">
  <licence target="...">
<!-- Conditions governing the download and reuse of images are defined according to project-specific rights and access requirements -->
  </licence>
 </availability>
 <availability xml:id="metadataRights" status="restricted">
  <licence target="...">
<!-- Conditions governing the download and reuse of metadata are defined according to project-specific rights and access requirements -->
  </licence>
 </availability>
</publicationStmt>

Rights and licensing requirements may vary between items. All rights information must be verified with the relevant stakeholders before content is published on MDC. Where licensing information is required, it should be recorded using <licence>, with any supplementary explanatory information, where necessary, provided in one or more <p>.

Some materials may be subject to pre-existing copyright or legal constraints. These may arise from third party ownership, contractual agreements, donor restrictions, or other legal obligations associated with the material.

In line with MDC open access principles, downloads of images and metadata should normally be enabled for end users. Where restrictions apply, for example, due to project-specific requirements or other policy and governance considerations, the relevant <availability> statement relating to images and or metadata should be omitted. This will prevent the Download Image and or Download Metadata button from appearing in the user interface.

Project leads are responsible for ensuring that rights and licensing requirements are identified and verified during the proposal and approval process, in consultation with the relevant curator and Digital Special Collections governance groups where necessary.

Further detailed guidance and a formal policy relating to rights assessment and access conditions are currently in development. <!-- hyperlink to relevant website when ready -->

1.13.1 Content warnings

<availability> may also be used within <publicationStmt> to record content warnings, which are displayed as a prominent notice in the MDC public interface.

Content warnings are brief statements alerting users to potentially sensitive material. They support responsible access and contextualisation in line with the University of Manchester Library’s approach to sensitive content in digital collections.

Project leads are responsible for ensuring that potentially sensitive content is identified and clearly described during the proposal and approval process, in consultation with the relevant curator and Digital Special Collections governance groups where necessary.

Further detailed guidance and a formal Content Warning Policy are in development. <!-- hyperlink to relevant website when ready -->

1.14 <sourceDesc> | source description

<sourceDesc> (source description) describes the source(s) from which an electronic text was derived or generated, typically a bibliographic description in the case of a digitized text, or a phrase such as ‘born digital’ for a text which has no previous existence.

<sourceDesc> within <fileDesc> brings together the elements used to describe the source item. It typically contains <msDesc>, which provides a structured description of that item.

1.15 <msDesc> | manuscript description

<msDesc> (manuscript description) contains a description of a single identifiable manuscript or other text-bearing object such as an early printed book.

<msDesc> within <sourceDesc> provides a structured description of the source item. It brings together information relating to its identification, contents, physical characteristics, and history.

Despite its name, <msDesc> is used broadly within the MDC framework to describe a wide range of Special Collections materials. This includes not only manuscripts but also visual collections such as photographs, objects, maps, and paintings, as well as archives and rare books, ensuring a standardised approach to documenting diverse materials.

While <sourceDesc> provides the overarching description of the source material, <msDesc> delivers in-depth, structured information about the item. It contains the following elements:

<teiHeader>
 <fileDesc> | <titleStmt/> | <publicationStmt/>
  <sourceDesc>
<!-- Structured, descriptive metadata about the source from which the digital resource is derived -->
   <msDesc>
<!-- Metadata describing a single identifiable item (manuscript, printed book, photograph, archival record) -->
    <msIdentifier>
<!-- Metadata uniquely identifying the item: location, holding institution and shelfmark -->
<!-- Contains <country>|<region>|<settlement>|<institution>|<repository>|<collection>|<idno> -->
<!-- May contain <altIdentifier> -->
    </msIdentifier>
    <head>
<!-- Metadata indicating section headings, overarching titles, or standardised labels -->
<!-- Conditional -->
    </head>
    <msContents>
<!-- Metadata about the intellectual contents -->
<!-- Contains <summary> | <msItem> -->
    </msContents>
    <physDesc>
<!-- Metadata describing all physical characteristics: material, size, format, script, decoration, binding, etc. -->
<!-- Contains <objectDesc>|<handDesc>|<typeDesc>|<decoDesc>|<additions>|<bindingDesc>|<sealDesc>|<accMat> -->
    </physDesc>
    <history>
<!-- Metadata about the origin and ownership history -->
<!-- Contains <origin> | <provenance> | <acquisition> -->
    </history>
    <additional>
<!-- Metadata providing administrative information -->
<!-- Contains <adminInfo> | <listBibl>, -->
    </additional>
    <msPart>
<!-- Metadata about composite manuscripts -->
<!-- Conditional -->
    </msPart>
   </msDesc>
  </sourceDesc>
 </fileDesc>|
<encodingDesc/>|<profileDesc/>|<revisionDesc/>
</teiHeader>

Together, these elements provide a comprehensive description of the item's intellectual contents, physical characteristics, history, and administrative information and should appear in the order listed above.

In the case of a composite manuscript, a full description may also include one or more <msPart>, each describing a distinct part of the manuscript (see composite manuscripts - Hyperlink).

The degree of obligation for including these elements depends on the nature of the item and the requirements of the project. While a more structured and precise description is generally preferable, the level of detail should be balanced against the time and effort required to create and maintain it.

1.16 <msIdentifier> | manuscript identifier

<msIdentifier> (manuscript identifier) contains the information required to identify the manuscript or similar object being described.

MDC display label — Classmark

<msIdentifier> is used to provide the information required for unique and unambiguous identification of the item. Identification is typically achieved through a structured, hierarchical approach comprising three components: geopolitical location, holding institution, and object identifier.

Information about the identity of the item should be given in <msIdentifier> using the following sub-elements in the following order:

<teiHeader> <fileDesc><titleStmt>|<publicationStmt>|<sourceDesc> <msDesc> <msIdentifier> <!-- Metadata about the identity of the item containing details such as the repository, shelfmark, and other identifiers. --> <country> <!-- Metadata specifying the nation where the institution | repository are located --> </country> <region> <!-- Metadata specifying area or administrative region --> </region> <settlement> <!-- Metadata specifying the city where the institution is located --> </settlement> <institution> <!-- Metadata naming the overarching organisation to which the item belongs --> </institution> <repository> <!-- Metadata indicating the specific institution, where the item is held --> </repository> <collection> <!-- Metadata naming the collection to which the item belongs --> </collection> <idno> <!-- Metadata providing the formal, unique identifier (shelfmark or reference number | classmark) for the item within the repository --> </idno> </msIdentifier> <msContents>|<physDesc>|<history>|<additional> </msDesc> </sourceDesc>|</fileDesc>|<encodingDesc>|<profileDesc>|<revisionDesc> </teiHeader>

Together, these elements provide a precise and hierarchical way to reference the item within the repository.

1.17 <country> | country | <region> | region | <settlement> | settlement

geo-political location information

<country> (country) contains the name of a geo-political unit, such as a nation, country, colony, or commonwealth, larger than or administratively superior to a region and smaller than a bloc.

<region> (region) contains the name of an administrative unit such as a state, province, or county, larger than a settlement, but smaller than a country.

<settlement> (settlement) contains the name of a settlement such as a city, town, or village identified as a single geo-political or administrative unit.

The geo-political location provides context for the item's physical location. This includes the country where the item is housed, the region (if applicable), and the settlement, typically the city or town where the institution and repository are located.

1.18 <institution> | institution | <repository> | repository

repository information

<institution> (institution) contains the name of an organization such as a university or library, with which a manuscript or other object is identified, generally its holding institution.

<repository> (repository) contains the name of a repository within which manuscripts or other objects are stored, possibly forming part of an institution.

MDC display label — Physical Location

The repository information specifies the institution that owns or manages the collection, such as a university, along with the specific repository where the manuscript is stored, such as a library or department within that institution.

1.19 <collection> | collection | <idno> | identifier

item identifier information

<collection> (collection) contains the name of a collection of manuscripts or other objects, not necessarily located within a single repository.

MDC display label — Collection

<idno> (identifier) supplies any form of identifier used to identify some object, such as a bibliographic item, a person, a title, an organization, etc. in a standardized way.

MDC display label — Classmark

The final layer of identification is the object-specific information, which includes the name of the collection or grouping to which the item belongs within the repository, and a unique identifier like a shelfmark or call number.

A typical <msIdentifier> for The John Rylands Research Institute and Library provides a structured and hierarchical representation of the item's identification details. It includes the sovereign country where the manuscript is located, the city of the repository, the name of the institution, in this case, The University of Manchester, the specific repository, The John Rylands Library, the name of the collection or grouping to which the item belongs within the repository, and finally the item's unique identifier, such as a shelfmark or call number.

• Physical Location: The John Rylands Library

• Classmark: Latin MS 22

<msIdentifier>
 <country>United Kingdom</country>
 <region>Greater Manchester</region>
 <settlement>Manchester</settlement>
 <institution>The University of Manchester</institution>
 <repository>The John Rylands Library</repository>
 <collection>Latin Manuscripts</collection>
 <idno>Latin MS 22</idno>
</msIdentifier>

Psalter of Joan of Navarre: Latin MS 22

<msIdentifier> may occasionally differ in cases where an item's physical location and ownership belong to an institution external to The University of Manchester, but the item has been digitised and published in MDC.

• Physical Location: Cadbury Research Library

• Classmark: HS/A

<msIdentifier>
 <country>United Kingdom</country>
 <region>West Midlands</region>
 <settlement>Birmingham</settlement>
 <institution>The University of Birmingham</institution>
 <repository>Cadbury Research Library</repository>
 <collection>The Simon Papers</collection>
 <idno>HS/A</idno>
</msIdentifier>

Correspondence and other papers (1844-1860): HS/A

<msIdentifier> contains the item's current shelfmark or classmark and records information about the holding institution. Former shelfmarks, classmarks, and other identifiers may also be recorded.

1.20 <altIdentifier> | alternative identifier | <idno> | identifier

<altIdentifier> (alternative identifier) contains an alternative or former structured identifier used for a manuscript or other object, such as a former catalogue number.

<idno> (identifier) supplies any form of identifier used to identify some object, such as a bibliographic item, a person, a title, an organization, etc. in a standardized way.

MDC display label — Alternative Identifier(s)

<altIdentifier> is used only when alternative or former identifiers need to be recorded, such as previous shelfmarks, call numbers, or identifiers assigned by earlier holding institutions. <altIdentifier> is not required for a valid <msIdentifier> and should be included only when additional identifiers are necessary for research, reference, or historical context.

<altIdentifier> is repeatable, allowing for the inclusion of multiple alternative <idno> for an item, including former shelfmarks, accession numbers, classmarks, or catalogue numbers that are no longer in active use. Such identifiers should be included only when they are designated as Alternative Identifier in EMu, the Library's collections management system. <altIdentifier> should not be used for identifiers assigned by published catalogues, reference works, external directories, online catalogues, or other databases.

type may be used on both <altIdentifier> and <idno>, but it has a different function in each element.

<altIdentifier> @type

type specifies whether the alternative identifier is intended for public display.

The following values may be used:

internal identifiers that are strictly for internal use and not intended for public display.

For example, temporary identifiers, project or acquisition, accession numbers, legacy system references, reorganisation or tracking numbers.

external identifiers that are public-facing and should be displayed.

type on <altIdentifier> does not define the kind of identifier. It only indicates visibility. Additional specificity about the identifier itself is handled by type on <idno>.

<idno> @type

type defines the type of alternative identifier and provides specific context for its use.

The following value must be used:

former is for identifiers that were previously assigned but are no longer in active use.

For example, a shelfmark or classmark that was changed during reorganisation.

Identifiers that originate from systematic catalogues or reference works, such as catalogue numbers assigned in published library catalogues, should not normally be encoded using <idno type="former"> unless they are recorded in EMu.

Alternative identifiers that should be visible must not be encoded as type=internal. Use type=external where the identifier should be publicly displayed in MDC. The decision depends on whether the alternative identifier should be is public-facing or reserved for internal use.

• Physical Location: The John Rylands Library

• Classmark: Greek P 457

• Alternative Identifier(s): P52

<msIdentifier>
 <country>United Kingdom</country>
 <region>Greater Manchester</region>
 <settlement>Manchester</settlement>
 <institution>The University of Manchester</institution>
 <repository>The John Rylands Library</repository>
 <collection>Greek Manuscripts</collection>
 <idno>Greek P 457</idno>
 <altIdentifier type="external">
  <idno type="former">P52</idno>
 </altIdentifier>
</msIdentifier>

Gospel of John: Greek P 457

• Classmark: Gaster Hebrew MS 1647

• Alternative Identifier(s): Gaster 1647

<msIdentifier>
 <collection>Hebrew Manuscripts</collection>
 <idno>Gaster Hebrew MS 1647</idno>
 <altIdentifier type="external">
  <idno type="former">Gaster 1647</idno>
 </altIdentifier>
</msIdentifier>

Alternative identifiers associated with specialised directories, online catalogues, or other external databases, such as Trismegistos or Gregory-Aland numbers, should be encoded in <listBibl> within <additional>. This enables the catalogue, reference work, or database from which the identifier derives to be cited alongside the identifier itself.

<altIdentifier> should be used for identifiers that remain current or are still cited in secondary sources. If information about a former identifier or past ownership is not directly relevant to the current identification of the item, but instead forms part of its broader historical context, it should be recorded in <provenance> rather than in <altIdentifier>.

Include <altIdentifier> only when the item has a former identifier, such as a previous shelfmark, that is recorded in EMu, the collections management system used by the Library.

1.21 <head> | heading

<head> (heading) contains any type of heading, for example the title of a section, or the heading of a list, glossary, manuscript description, etc.

<head> is a flexible and general-purpose. It may be used for headings at all levels within a TEI file, most often within <text>, but also within <teiHeader>. Its function depends on the context in which it occurs.

Within <text>, <head> is commonly used to encode section headings, such as titles within <div> and list headings.

Within <msDesc>, <head> provides a general title for the entire item, serving as an overarching title, especially for composite manuscripts that include <msPart> (see section xx – Hyperlink).

Within <decoDesc>, <head> is used in combination with <list> to create a structured entry for detailed decoration description (see section xx – Hyperlink).

For uniform titles, not to be confused with type=uniform, <head> may be used instead of or in addition to <title> to prioritise a standardised title over a title proper (see section xx – Hyperlink).

1.22 <msContents> | manuscript contents

<msContents> (manuscript contents) describes the intellectual content of a manuscript, manuscript part, or other object either as a series of paragraphs or as a series of structured manuscript items.

<msContents> is the next major sub-element of <msDesc> and provides a detailed description of an item’s intellectual contents. It consists of at least one or a series of <msItem>. These can, in turn, contain further <msItem>, which can be prefaced by <summary>.

1.23 <summary> | summary

<summary> contains an overview of the available information concerning some aspect of an item or object (for example, its intellectual content, history, layout, typography etc.) as a complement or alternative to the more detailed information carried by more specific elements.

<summary> provides a basic overview of the item. It should briefly describe its contents, significance, and context, including its relationship to other items where relevant. While it may highlight key aspects of the item, it should function as a high-level overview, complementing rather than duplicating information recorded elsewhere in the description.

Content within <summary> must be encoded using one or more <p>. At a minimum, <summary> should consist of one or two well-formed sentences that clearly outline the item's textual and illustrative content. It may be expanded into multiple paragraphs for more complex cases but should remain concise.

minimal summary

Astronomia (Latin MS 53)

Christianus Prolianus's Astronomia, produced circa 1470-1480. Decorated and probably copied and illuminated by Joachinus de Gigantibus de Rottenburg, a German illuminator, at Naples.

<msContents>
 <summary>
  <p>Christianus Prolianus's Astronomia, produced circa 1470-1480. Decorated and probably copied and illuminated by Joachinus de Gigantibus de Rottenburg, a German illuminator, at Naples.</p>
 </summary>
 <msItem>
<!-- additional metadata -->
 </msItem>
</msContents>

Astronomia: Latin MS 53

Extended summaries may provide descriptions of items, incorporating textual and contextual analysis, as well as media integration. The latter is not limited to extended summaries but also applies to minimal ones. Where applicable, 3D models can enhance these summaries by offering an interactive visual representation. The following example demonstrates an extended summary incorporating a Sketchfab model.

extended summary

Fragment of the Gospel of John (Greek P 457)

Papyrus fragment of the Gospel of John, Chapter 18, verses 31-33 (recto) and 37-38 (verso), ...

Widely regarded as the earliest portion of any New Testament writing ever found, ...

The majority of papyri which survive from this date are in the form of book-rolls, ...

Greek P 457 by The John Rylands Research Institute and Library on Sketchfab

<msContents>
 <summary>
  <p>Papyrus fragment of the Gospel of John, Chapter 18, verses 31-33 (recto) and 37-38 (verso), ...</p>
  <p>Widely regarded as the earliest portion of any New Testament writing ever found, ...</p>
  <p>The majority of papyri which survive from this date are in the form of book-rolls, ...</p>
  <p><iframe width="600" height="480"
     src="https://sketchfab.com/3d-models/st-john-fragment-greek-p-457-da71708e8fe44175baa5a8cc203e569c/embed" frameborder="0"allowfullscreen="true"
     mozallowfullscreen="true" webkitallowfullscreen="true"
     onmousewheel=""></iframe> <ref target="... ... ..."> ... </ref>
  </p>
 </summary>
 <msItem>
<!-- additional metadata -->
 </msItem>
</msContents>

Fragment of the Gospel of John: Greek P 457

Items that have been 3D imaged are added to the Library’s Sketchfab page. The Sketchfab viewer can be embedded in a TEI-XML file for display on MDC. For instructions on embedding an existing Sketchfab model into a file, see Section [X]: How to embed Sketchfab models in TEI for MDC .

<summary> should be easily understood while offering depth where necessary. It serves as an entry point for cataloguers and researchers, providing a high-level, concise overview before the detailed description unfolds. It should be descriptive, detached, specific, and justified by evidence at every stage.

Avoid echoing the style and tone of auction or sales catalogues.

1.24 <msItem> | manuscript item

<msItem> (manuscript item) describes an individual work or item within the intellectual content of a manuscript, manuscript part, or other object.

<msItem> appears immediately after <summary> within <msContents> and is used to record the item’s intellectual contents in a structured way, describing each discrete component it contains.

<msItem> may be a complete work or a self-contained part of a larger work. The first, top-level <msItem> functions as the main container for the intellectual contents of a single physical unit, such as a codex. Any nested <msItem> describe the contents of that unit. Each nested <msItem> records a discrete intellectual component, such as an individual work, text, or section. This nesting creates a clear part-to-whole hierarchy and reflects the intellectual structure of the item.

<msContents> must contain at least one <msItem>. Even if an item consists of a single text or single intellectual component, best practice is to wrap it inside an <msItem> to ensure clarity, consistency, and future proofing. This applies regardless of the item’s nature. For an object, photograph, or map, a single <msItem> is sufficient, whereas a manuscript or rare book often contains multiple works or sections, requiring multiple <msItem> to record its structure accurately.

The following demonstrates at least three levels of structural complexity within <msContents>, progressing from a simple structure to a more complex nested hierarchy.

Level 1

Level 2

Level 3

<msContents>
 <summary>...</summary>
<!-- entire item -->
 <msItem>...</msItem>
</msContents>
<msContents>
 <summary>...</summary>
<!-- entire item -->
 <msItem>
<!-- contents of the item -->
  <msItem n="1">...</msItem>
  <msItem n="2">...</msItem>
 </msItem>
</msContents>
<msContents>
 <summary>...</summary>
<!--entire item-->
 <msItem>
<!-- contents of the item -->
  <msItem n="1">...</msItem>
  <msItem n="2">
   <msItem n="2.1">...</msItem>
  </msItem>
 </msItem>
</msContents>

Each nested <msItem> should be numbered using n, and, unlike the top-level <msItem>, these nested elements should include <locus> to indicate where each section begins and ends within the entire item.

@n

n gives a number (or other label) for an element which is not necessarily unique within the document. It is typically used to specify the numbering of chapters, sections, list items, etc.

1.25 <locus> | locus

<locus> (locus) defines a location within a manuscript, manuscript part, or other object typically as a (possibly discontinuous) sequence of folio references.

The location of a nested <msItem> should be recorded using <locus> with from and to.

@from @to

from specifies the starting point of the location in a normalised form, typically a page number.

to specifies the endpoint of the location in a normalised form, typically as a page number.

A single, top-level <msItem> is generally understood to represent the item as a whole and does not require <locus>. A one-leaf <locus> should be expressed using identical from and to values

1.25.1 Level 1 | single item, self-contained

no internal divisions | table of contents not included (not displayed)

A single <msItem> describes the entire item, without any internal divisions such as sections, chapters, or subsections. The structure remains flat, ensuring the item is treated as a unified entity with no further breakdown. No nested <msItem> or subdivisions are present, maintaining a simple, undivided structure.

manuscript | roll

Exultet Roll (Latin MS 2)

Exultet Roll, produced in the artistic milieu of Bari, Italy in the early eleventh century.

• Classmark: Latin MS 2

• Title: Exultet Roll

• Language: Latin

<msContents>
 <summary>Exultet Roll, produced in the artistic milieu of Bari, Italy in the early eleventh century.</summary>
 <msItem>
  <title>Exultet Roll</title>
  <textLang mainLang="lat">Latin</textLang>
 </msItem>
</msContents>

Exultet Roll: Latin MS 2

1.25.2 Level 2 | multi-item, structured

internal divisions | table of contents included (displayed)

A top-level <msItem> describes the entire item while also containing multiple nested <msItem> that describe distinct sections or individual works. Each nested <msItem> is an independent unit within the larger structure, allowing for a more detailed breakdown without additional hierarchical levels. This level provides a structured representation of an item with multiple identifiable components.

manuscript

La Commedia (Italian MS 48)

Incomplete manuscript of the Commedia by Dante Alighieri, 1265-1321, containing the Inferno and the Purgatorio. The contents are somewhat disarranged, and some sections are missing.

• Title: La Commedia

• Alternative Title(s): La Divina Commedia

• Language: Italian

• Author(s): Dante Alighieri, 1265-1321

<msContents>
 <summary>Incomplete manuscript of the Commedia by Dante Alighieri, 1265-1321, containing the Inferno and the Purgatorio. The contents are somewhat disarranged and some sections are missing.</summary>
 <msItem>
  <title>La Commedia</title>
  <title type="alt">La Divina Commedia</title>
  <textLang mainLang="ita">Italian</textLang>
  <author source="#viaf" key="97105654">Dante Alighieri, 1265-1321</author>
  <msItem n="1">
   <locus from="1r" to="114v">1r-114v</locus>
   <title>Inferno</title>
  </msItem>
  <msItem n="2">
   <locus from="115r" to="200v">115r-200v</locus>
   <title>Purgatorio</title>
  </msItem>
 </msItem>
</msContents>

La Commedia: Italian MS 48

1.25.3 Level 3 | multi-item, hierarchically structured

internal divisions | table of contents included (displayed with nested subdivisions)

A top-level <msItem> incorporates multiple layers of nested <msItem> to o reflect a hierarchical structure. Each level of nesting represents a further subdivision of the work. This approach is particularly suitable for works with clear multi-tiered subdivisions.

manuscript

Il Canzoniere di Petrarca e Le Rime di Dante (Italian MS 1)

.... .... ...

Information about this document

—

  • Title: Il Canzoniere di Petrarca e Le Rime di Dante
  • Alternative Title(s): Petrarca Canzoni e Dante Sonetti; Petrarch’s Canzoniere and Dante’s Rime
  • Language(s): Italian

—

Section shown in images 10 to 10

  • Title: Bernardo della Casa’s dedicatory sonnet
  • Language(s): Italian

—

Section shown in images 13 to 305

  • Title: Il Canzoniere di Petrarca
  • Alternative Title(s): Rime sparse (Scattered rhymes)
  • Language(s): Italian
  • Author(s): Petrarca, Francesco, 1304-1374
  • Note(s): Canzoniere are also sometimes called Rime sparse ... ... ...

—

Section shown in images 13 to 216

  • Title: Rime in vita di Madonna Laura | Canzoni I-CCLXIII (1-263)
  • Language(s): Italian

—

Section shown in images 309 to 359

  • Title: Le Rime di Dante
  • Language(s): Italian
  • Author(s): Dante Alighieri, 1265-1321
  • Note(s): The sections belong to the Rime, ... ... ...

Section shown in images 309 to 312

  • Title: Rime petrose | Rime CIII (103) | Cosi nel mio parlar voglio esser aspro
  • Language(s): Italian

—

Section shown in images 356 to 359

  • Title: Rime dell’esilio | Rime CXVI (116) | Amor di che convien pur chio mi doglia
  • Language(s): Italian

—

<msContents>
 <summary>... .... ...</summary>
 <msItem>
  <title>Il Canzoniere di Petrarca e Le Rime di Dante</title>
  <title type="alt">Petrarca Canzoni e Dante Sonetti</title>
  <title type="alt">Petrarch’s Canzoniere and Dante’s Rime</title>
  <textLang mainLang="ita">Italian</textLang>
  <msItem n="1">
   <locus from="2v" to="2v">2v</locus>
   <title>Bernardo della Casa’s dedicatory sonnet</title>
   <textLang mainLang="ita">Italian</textLang>
<!-- additional metadata -->
  </msItem>
  <msItem n="2">
   <locus from="3r" to="148r">3r-148r</locus>
   <title>Il Canzoniere di Petrarca</title>
   <title type="alt">Rime sparse (Scattered rhymes)</title>
   <textLang mainLang="ita">Italian</textLang>
   <author source="#viaf" key="39382430">Petrarca, Francesco, 1304-1374</author>
   <note>Canzoniere are also sometimes called Rime sparse ... ... ...</note>
   <msItem n="2.1">
    <locus from="3r" to="104v">3r-104v</locus>
    <title>Rime in vita di Madonna Laura | Canzoni I-CCLXIII (1-263)</title>
    <textLang mainLang="ita">Italian</textLang>
<!-- additional metadata -->
   </msItem>
   <msItem n="2.2">
<!-- additional metadata -->
   </msItem>
  </msItem>
  <msItem n="3">
   <locus from="149r" to="174r">149r-174r</locus>
   <title>Le Rime di Dante</title>
   <textLang mainLang="ita">Italian</textLang>
   <author source="#viaf" key="97105654">Dante Alighieri, 1265-1321</author>
   <note>The sections belong to the Rime, ... ... ...</note>
   <msItem n="3.1">
    <locus from="149r" to="150v">149r-150v</locus>
    <title>Rime petrose | Rime CIII (103) | Cosi nel mio parlar voglio esser aspro</title>
    <textLang mainLang="ita">Italian</textLang>
<!-- additional metadata -->
   </msItem>
<!-- msItems 3.2 to 3.14 -->
   <msItem n="3.15">
    <locus from="172v" to="174r">172v-174r</locus>
    <title>Rime dell’esilio | Rime CXVI (116) | Amor di che convien pur chio mi doglia</title>
    <textLang mainLang="ita">Italian</textLang>
<!-- additional metadata -->
   </msItem>
  </msItem>
 </msItem>
</msContents>

Il Canzoniere di Petrarca e Le Rime di Dante: Italian MS 1

<msItem> may include xml:id, though its use is optional and depends on the project’s requirements. For items containing multiple sections or independent works, xml:id on nested <msItem> may be beneficial where citation, linking, or indexing is required or anticipated. In MDC, the root <TEI> includes xml:id identifying the item as a whole; therefore, a separate xml:id is not normally required on a single top-level <msItem>.

Where xml:id is used on nested <msItem>, the following section provides further guidance.

<msItem> @xml:id

xml:id provides a unique identifier for the element bearing the attribute

In MDC, the root <TEI> includes xml:id derived from the unique identifier of the physical source item and matching the XML filename. Where xml:id is used on nested <msItem>, the value should extend the root identifier using the suffix -item followed by the unit number.

<TEI xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="MS-LATIN-000XX"> <teiHeader> <!-- additional metadata --> <msContents> <summary/> <msItem> <!-- top-level <msItem> | additional metadata --> <msItem n="1" xml:id="MS-LATIN-000XX-item1"> <!-- First item | additional metadata --> </msItem> <msItem n="2" xml:id="MS-LATIN-000XX-item2"> <!-- Second item | additional metadata --> </msItem> <msItem n="3" xml:id="MS-LATIN-000XX-item3"> <!-- Nested <msItem> within msItem 3 --> <!-- additional metadata --> <msItem n="3.1" xml:id="MS-LATIN-000XX-item3.1"> <!-- Nested <msItem> within the third item --> </msItem> </msItem> </msItem> </msContents> <!-- additional metadata --> </teiHeader>

xml:id on <msItem> is most useful where individual works or sections need to be identified, cited, linked, indexed, or associated with transcriptions. Where such requirements do not exist, xml:id on <msItem> may not be necessary.

In addition to xml:id, defective may be used on <msItem>. Where <incipit> and <explicit> are present, defective should normally be used to indicate whether a text begins or ends defectively. If <incipit> and <explicit> are not present, defective on <msItem> is sufficient to indicate that the work is defective or incomplete.

Where defective is used on <msItem>, the following section provides further guidance.

<msItem> @defective

defective indicates whether the passage being quoted is defective, i.e. incomplete through loss or damage.

defective provides the means of distinguishing fragmentary texts. The following values should be used:

true the text is incomplete due to loss or damage

false the text is complete and unaffected by loss or damage

unknown it is uncertain whether the text is incomplete due to loss or damage

Here is the structured encoding with defective added where relevant:

<msItem n="1" xml:id="MS-LATIN-000XX-item1" defective="false">
<!-- The text is complete and unaffected by loss or damage -->
<!-- additional metadata -->
</msItem>
<msItem n="2" xml:id="MS-LATIN-000XX-item2" defective="true">
<!-- The text is incomplete due to loss or damage -->
<!-- additional metadata -->
 <msItem n="2.1" xml:id="MS-LATIN-00XX-item2.1" defective="true">
<!-- Nested <msItem> within <msItem> -->
<!-- The text is incomplete due to loss or damage -->
<!-- additional metadata -->
 </msItem>
</msItem>

Several elements can be included within <msItem>. These elements provide a detailed and structured description of the intellectual content. Include the following sub-elements whenever relevant.

<teiHeader> <fileDesc><titleStmt>|<publicationStmt>|<sourceDesc> <msDesc>|<msIdentifier>|<msContents>|<summary> <msItem> <title> <!-- Metadata providing the title(s) --> </title> <textLang> <!-- Metadata indicating the language(s) of the text --> </textLang> <author> <!-- Metadata specifying the author(s) --> <!-- May contain <persName> | <addName>--> </author> <editor> <!-- Metadata specifying contributors other than the author(s) --> <!-- May contain <persName> | <addName> --> </editor> <rubric> <!-- Metadata introducing the text of any rubric or heading --> <!-- Contains <locus> --> </rubric> <incipit> <!-- Metadata introducing the opening words of the text: incipit --> <!-- Contains <locus> --> </incipit> <explicit> <!-- Metadata introducing the closing words of the text: explicit --> <!-- Contains <locus> --> </explicit> <finalRubric> <!-- Metadata introducing a closing rubric --> <!-- Contains <locus> --> </finalRubric > <colophon> <!-- Metadata providing the colophon of an item --> <!-- Contains <locus> --> </colophon> <quote> <!-- Metadata providing a phrase or passage, attributed to an external source: quotation --> </quote> <filiation> <!-- Metadata about an item's relationship to other surviving items or versions of the same text or content. --> <!-- May contain <ref> --> </filiation> <note> <!-- Metadata providing additional notes about the text or item --> </note> </msItem> </msContents>|<physDesc>|<history>|<additional> </msDesc>|</sourceDesc>|<fileDesc>|<encodingDesc>|<profileDesc>|<revisionDesc> </teiHeader>

1.26 <title> | title

<title> (title) contains a title for any kind of work.

MDC display label — Title

<title> is used to record a standardised uniform title or a descriptive title for the item, manuscript, book or other object, along with any known variants. Within the top-level <msItem>, it contains the principal title of the entire item. This is the chief title displayed prominently in MDC, ensuring high visibility, and it also appears in the Title display field.
