<title> in the top-level <msItem> provides the overarching title for the complete item, while nested or subordinate <msItem> may contain individual titles corresponding to specific component texts or parts.

If the item has more than one title, including alternative forms or titles in different languages, additional <title> with relevant attributes may be used to reflect this variation.

In the context of retroconversion, use the form of the title as given in the source catalogue, if one is provided. For new encoding, or when the converted record does not supply a title, use a standard form taken from established reference works. If no standard form can be found, a suitable title should be supplied by the encoder.

Additionally, if a new, more appropriate title is assigned during this process, the original title from the source catalogue should also be included as an alternative title. This ensures that the historical form of the title is preserved and that the item remains traceable through earlier catalogue references.

<title> may be repeated to record multiple titles. Use type to indicate whether a title is alternative, descriptive, or uniform in relation to the main title. For titles that are translations or transliterations, include xml:lang to specify the language or script in which the title is given.

@type | @xml:lang

type classifies the title according to some convenient typology.

xml:lang indicates the language of the title.

The following values should be used for type:

alt Alternative Title(s) any title used in addition to the main title, such as a translation, abbreviation, or variant.

desc Descriptive Title(s) a title that paraphrases or describes the content of the work.

uniform Uniform Title a standard title used when a work has no title or appears under multiple titles.

If the work lacks a formal or standardised title, a descriptive title should be provided using <title type="desc">. This should represent a descriptive rendering rather than an official or conventional title.

If both the main <title> and <title type="desc"> are present, the main, formal <title> still serves as the primary point of reference, while the descriptive title offers additional context or clarification.

A descriptive title should not be used on its own in place of a formal main title. Otherwise, the same text may appear in both the Title and Descriptive Title(s). If no formal title exists, a suitable main title should be devised rather than relying solely on <title type="desc">.

Prayer to Mary (Greek P 470)

• Title: Prayer to Mary

• Alternative Title(s): Sub Tuum Praesidium

• Descriptive Title(s): Christian Prayer to the Mother of God

<msItem>
 <title>Prayer to Mary</title>
 <title type="desc">Christian Prayer to the Mother of God</title>
 <title type="alt">Sub Tuum Praesidium</title>
<!-- additional metadata -->
</msItem>

Prayer to Mary: Greek P 470

The main, formal <title> of the work does not have type.

Arbor Caritatis et Misercordiae (Latin MS 18)

• Title: Arbor Caritatis et Misercordiae

• Alternative Title(s): Tree of Salvation

<msItem>
 <title xml:lang="lat">Arbor Caritatis et Misercordiae</title>
 <title type="alt" xml:lang="eng">Tree of Salvation</title>
<!-- additional metadata -->
</msItem>

Arbor Caritatis et Misercordiae: Latin MS 18

The use of xml:lang=lat specifies that the title Arbor Caritatis et Misercordiae is in Latin (lat). Meanwhile, type=alt and xml:lang=eng indicate that the alternative title Tree of Salvation is in English (eng).

Esther Scroll (Hebrew MS 54)

• Title: Esther Scroll

• Alternative Title(s): מגילת אסתר; Megilat Ester

<msItem>
 <title xml:lang="eng">Esther Scroll</title>
 <title type="alt" xml:lang="heb">מגילת אסתת</title>
 <title type="alt" xml:lang="heb-Latn-x-lc">Megilat Ester</title>
<!-- additional metadata -->
</msItem>

Esther Scroll: Hebrew MS 54

heb-latn-x-lc specifies that the title Megilat Ester is the romanised form of the hebrew name מגילת אסתת, following the ALA-LC Romanisation standard:

heb is the ISO 639-3 language code for Hebrew.

latn is the ISO 15924 script code for the Latin alphabet. Normally, Hebrew is written in the Hebrew script, but here it specifies that the content is in Hebrew but written using the Latin alphabet (romanisation).

x-lc is a custom extension sub-tag indicating adherence to the ALA-LC Romanisation standard.

The ALA-LC Romanisation Tables are widely used in library cataloguing and bibliographic metadata for converting non-Latin scripts into Latin script.

<title type="uniform"> corresponds to the MARC 21 240 Uniform Title field. It records a standardised title used to bring together editions, translations, and other manifestations of the same work that may appear under different titles.

Le terze rime di Dante (R52150)

• Title: Le terze rime di Dante

• Alternative Title(s): Inferno e'l Purgatorio e'l Paradiso di Dante Alaghieri; Lo'nferno e'l Purgatorio e'l Paradiso di Dante Alaghieri; Inferno e'l Pvrgatorio e'l Paradiso di Dante Alaghieri

<msItem>
 <title xml:lang="ita">Le terze rime di Dante</title>
 <title type="uniform" xml:lang="ita">Divina Commedia</title>
 <title type="alt" xml:lang="ita">Le Inferno e'l Purgatorio e'l Paradiso di Dante Alaghieri</title>
 <title type="alt" xml:lang="ita">Lo'nferno e'l Purgatorio e'l Paradiso di Dante Alaghieri</title>
 <title type="alt" xml:lang="ita">Inferno e'l Pvrgatorio e'l Paradiso di Dante Alaghieri</title>
<!-- additional metadata -->
</msItem>

Le terze rime di Dante: R52150

General remarks: title formulation and style

For items with multiple titles, use the appropriate type to distinguish each title where required.

Avoid using placeholder phrases such as title unknown or untitled as titles. Provide a meaningful or descriptive title wherever possible.

Do not enclose any title, whether devised or supplied, in square brackets.

Do not include reference numbers, classmarks, or other identifiers in the title.

Do not end titles with a full stop.

Apply sentence capitalisation: capitalise only the first word and proper nouns, such as Bible, Psalter, Book of Hours, and named individuals or places. Preserve the original capitalisation found on the item unless the title appears entirely in uppercase, in which case convert it to title case.

Ensure consistent capitalisation across all titles within a TEI file unless variation is justified.

Quotations may be included within a title but should neither begin nor completely enclose it.

Avoid including creation dates and places in a title, unless necessary to uniquely identify the item, for example, newspapers or items with generic or similar titles.

Omit full stops in honorifics such as Dr, Mr, Ms, and Mrs.

Use senior and junior, not abbreviations such as Snr, Sr, Jnr, or Jr.

Spell out months, days of the week, and place names: September not Sept., Thursday not Thurs., Netherlands not Neth.

Use Saint not St or St., for example, Saint Christopher not St or St. Christopher.

Use Virgin Mary not St Mary, unless referring to specific foundations, for example, The Benedictine abbey of St Mary.

Use full names, not acronyms, for example, Allgemeine Juristische Zeitung, not AJZ.

For correspondence, spell out the full names of the sender and recipient if available, using the format: Letter to | from, or from | to . <!--see section x : <correspDesc>-->

For composite manuscripts, <head> may be used in addition to <title> to prioritise a standardised title over the title proper. (see section xx – Hyperlink).

1.27 <textLang> | text language

<textLang> (text language) describes the languages and writing systems identified within the bibliographic work being described, rather than its description.

MDC display label — Language(s)

<textLang> within <msItem> is required for items with linguistic content and appears immediately after <title>. At the top-level <msItem>, it records the item’s primary language and, where applicable, any additional languages.

<textLang> must include mainLang to encode the predominant language. otherLangs should be used where more than one language is present.

@mainLang | @otherLangs

mainLang (main language) supplies a code which identifies the chief language used in the bibliographic work.

otherLangs (other languages) one or more codes identifying any other languages used in the bibliographic work.

mainLang and otherLangs should use three-letter codes from the ISO 639-2 series as unique identifiers for languages. These codes are maintained and published by the Library of Congress, which offers a comprehensive and up-to-date list, and should be followed throughout.

iso639-2

ISO 639-2 Codes for Representation of Names of Languages (LC)

The ISO 639-2 Codes for Representation of Names of Languages is a standard within the ISO 639 series that provides three-letter (alpha-3) codes for representing language names. Developed primarily for libraries, information services, and publishers, these codes facilitate the uniform representation and processing of languages across various computerised systems and databases. Widely adopted by the library community, ISO 639-2 codes are essential for any application that requires language identification in a coded format.

<taxonomy xml:id="iso639-2">
 <bibl>
  <ref target="https://www.loc.gov/standards/iso639-2/php/code_list.php">ISO 639-2 Codes for Representation of Names of Languages (LC)</ref>
 </bibl>
</taxonomy>

Do not include date ranges, such as (ca. 1050–1350), in the textual content of <textLang>. Use the standard language name as listed in ISO 6392. Where a language has both bibliographic (B) and terminology (T) codes in ISO 6392, use the bibliographic (B) code. For example, Persian has the ISO 6392 codes per (B) and fas (T). The correct code is per.

Although <textLang> is repeatable within the same <msItem>, it is advisable not to do so. Instead, for items with more than one language, use a single <textLang> with mainLang and otherLangs, using space-separated codes.

Where an item contains no linguistic content, use:

<textLang mainLang="zxx">No linguistic content</textLang>

1.27.1 Use of <textLang> in <msDesc> and <msItem>

This section outlines where to place <textLang> within <msDesc> structures, which parts of the description it applies to within <msContents>, <msItem>, and <msPart>, and how these choices affect MDC display. It sets out the requirement that structured contents include at least one <msItem>, and that <textLang> is included within the relevant <msItem> so that language information remains associated with a defined intellectual unit. It also explains the required use of a top-level <head> when <msPart> is present, supplying an overarching title for the whole item while allowing part-level and item-level <textLang> to govern language display within individual components.

1.27.2 Pattern A | best practice

This is the recommended structure and is both correct and schema-compliant. <textLang> is recorded within the top-level <msItem> and displays reliably in MDC.

<msContents>
 <summary>...</summary>
 <msItem>
  <title>...</title>
  <textLang mainLang="...">...</textLang>
 </msItem>
</msContents>

• Title: Arbor Caritatis et Misercordiae

• Alternative Title(s): Tree of Salvation

• Language(s): Latin

<msItem>
 <title xml:lang="lat">Arbor Caritatis et Misercordiae</title>
 <title type="alt" xml:lang="eng">Tree of Salvation</title>
 <textLang mainLang="lat">Latin</textLang>
<!-- additional metadata -->
</msItem>

Arbor Caritatis et Misercordiae: Latin MS 18

1.27.3 Pattern B | partially correct practice

A top-level <head> is required only when <msPart> is used, providing an overarching title for the whole item. Regardless of structure, each <msContents> should include at least one <msItem>, and <textLang> should be recorded within an <msItem>.

The following encoding may appear valid but is only partially correct because it omits <msItem>. Although this may be tolerated where the manuscript contains a single, undivided work and the absence of <msItem> does not obscure the intellectual structure, the inclusion of <msItem> is recommended in all cases for consistency and future-proofing.

<head>...</head>
<msContents>
 <summary>...</summary>
 <textLang mainLang="...">...</textLang>
</msContents>

1.27.4 Pattern C | incorrect, not good practice

This pattern is incorrect because <textLang> is not enclosed within an <msItem> and there is no <head>. As a result, the language information is not associated with a defined intellectual unit and may not display correctly in MDC.

<msContents>
 <summary>...</summary>
 <textLang mainLang="...">...</textLang>
 <msItem>
  <title>...</title>
 </msItem>
</msContents>

1.27.5 Multi-item structure | nested <msItem>, one language

Use a top-level <msItem> to summarise the contents and language for the item as a whole. Each nested <msItem> must also include <textLang>, even when the language is the same, so that language information is associated with each intellectual unit for MDC display.

<msContents>
 <summary>...</summary>
 <msItem>
<!-- top-level <msItem> for the entire item-->
  <title>...</title>
<!-- one language entire item-->
  <textLang mainLang="lat">Latin</textLang>
  <msItem n="1">
<!-- first nested <msItem> with the same <textLang> -->
   <locus from="1r" to="50v">1r-50v</locus>
   <title>...</title>
   <textLang mainLang="lat">Latin</textLang>
  </msItem>
  <msItem n="2">
<!-- second nested <msItem> with the same <textLang> -->
   <locus from="60r" to="120v">60r-120v</locus>
   <title>...</title>
   <textLang mainLang="lat">Latin</textLang>
   <msItem n="2.1">
<!-- first subsection nested within second <msItem> with the same <textLang> -->
    <locus from="60r" to="80v">60r-80v</locus>
    <title>...</title>
    <textLang mainLang="lat">Latin</textLang>
   </msItem>
  </msItem>
 </msItem>
</msContents>

1.27.6 Multi-item structure | nested <msItem>, multiple languages

In items containing more than one language, a top-level <textLang> should record all languages present across the item as a whole. Nested <msItem> may then record the language or languages applicable to each intellectual unit. This supports both whole-item and item-level language display in MDC.

<msContents>
 <summary>...</summary>
 <msItem>
<!-- top-level <msItem> for the entire item-->
  <title>...</title>
<!-- more than one language entire item-->
  <textLang mainLang="lat" otherLangs="grc">Latin and Ancient Greek</textLang>
  <msItem n="1">
<!-- first nested <msItem> with its own <textLang> -->
   <locus from="1r" to="50v">1r-50v</locus>
   <title>...</title>
   <textLang mainLang="lat">Latin</textLang>
  </msItem>
  <msItem n="2">
<!-- second nested item with the different <textLang> -->
   <locus from="60r" to="120v">60r-120v</locus>
   <title>...</title>
   <textLang mainLang="grc">Ancient Greek</textLang>
   <msItem n="2.1">
<!-- first subsection nested within second <msItem> with the same <textLang> -->
    <locus from="60r" to="80v">60r-80v</locus>
    <title>...</title>
    <textLang mainLang="grc">Ancient Greek</textLang>
   </msItem>
  </msItem>
 </msItem>
</msContents>

1.27.7 Multi-part, composite items using <msPart>

In multi-part, composite items structured with one or more <msPart>, use a top-level <msItem> within the main <msContents> to record the languages present across the item as a whole. <textLang> within this top-level <msItem> must include mainLang and, where applicable, otherLangs. For further guidance on the use and structure of <msPart>, see Section X <!--insert hyperlink-->.

Language, script, and romanisation: use of @xml:lang and <textLang>

This section outlines how to encode language and script using xml:lang and <textLang>, with particular attention to transliteration and romanisation. It distinguishes between the encoding of individual textual forms and the description of the languages and writing systems present within an item. It also clarifies the use of BCP 47 language tags, including ISO 639 language codes and ISO 15924 script codes, and the role of private-use subtags for conventions such as ALA–LC romanisation.

1.27.8 Use of @xml:lang on individual elements or strings

xml:lang is used to encode the language and writing system of a specific textual value, such as a title, name, or short passage. At this level, it identifies the form in which the content of an individual element is expressed.

<title xml:Lang="ara-latn-x-lc">Sūrat al-Fātiḥah</title>
<persName xml:Lang="ara-latn-x-lc">Ibn Sīnā</persName>

The value ara-latn-x-lc denotes Arabic language content encoded in Latin script according to the ALA–LC Romanization Tables.

xml:lang=ara → Arabic in Arabic

xml:lang=ara-latn → Arabic expressed in Latin

xml:lang=ara-latn-x-lc → Arabic expressed in Latin according to ALA–LC romanisation

The use of xml:lang is appropriate at the level of individual elements, particularly where the content of an element is represented in a writing system different from that normally associated with the language, for example through transliteration or romanisation.

Use of <textLang> for the text or manuscript as a whole

<textLang> describes the languages and writing systems identified within the item being described. It records the predominant language using mainLang and any additional languages using otherLangs, optionally combining each with a writing system.

<textLang mainLang="ara-latn">Arabic in Latin script.</textLang>
<textLang mainLang="lat-arab">Latin in Arabic script.</textLang>

The value ara-latn indicates that the language of the text is Arabic and that it is written in the Latin writing system. The value lat-arab indicates that the language of the text is Latin and that it is written in the Arabic writing system.

mainLang=ara → Arabic in Arabic script

mainLang=ara-latn → Arabic language written in Latin

mainLang=latn-arab → Latin language written in Arabic

Such combinations correspond to transliteration, that is, the representation of the text of one language in the writing system of another. Transliteration is not limited to the Latin writing system. By contrast, romanisation refers specifically to transliteration into the Latin writing system, often according to a defined standard such as ALA–LC.

The use of <textLang> is appropriate where the combination of language and writing system characterises the text or a defined textual unit within it. The same principle applies to the use of otherLangs.

xml:lang is used to encode the language and writing system of a specific textual form, identifying how the content of an individual element is expressed.

<textLang> is used to record the languages and writing systems present in the work, identifying how the text itself is composed linguistically.

Since BCP 47 language tags are case-insensitive, lowercasing does not alter meaning and may be adopted to ensure consistency with normalised attribute values across TEI attributes, in line with W3C guidance that casing in language tags is conventional rather than mandatory.

In the following example a manuscript written chiefly in Old Church Slavonic is described:

<textLang mainLang="chu">Old Church Slavonic</textLang>

A manuscript item will sometimes contain material in more than one language. mainLang should be used only for the chief language. Other languages used may be specified using otherLangs as in the following example:

<textLang mainLang="chu" otherLangs="rus grc">Mostly Old Church Slavonic, with some Russian and Greek.</textLang>

Since Old Church Slavonic may be written in either Cyrillic or Glagolitic scripts, and even occasionally in both within the same manuscript, it might be preferable to use a more explicit identifier:

<textLang mainLang="chu-cyrs">Old Church Slavonic in Cyrillic script.</textLang>

1.28 <author> | author

<author> (author) in a bibliographic reference, contains the name(s) of an author, personal or corporate, of a work; for example in the same form as that provided by a recognized bibliographic name authority.

MDC display label — Author(s)

<author> is used to record the name of a person or organisation responsible for the intellectual content of the work.

<author> should contain the normalised form of an author's name, providing a standardised form regardless of how the name appears in the source item.

Any person or organisation identified as the author of an item should be consistently encoded within <author> as a standard entry. When applicable, it must include both source and key.

@source @key

source specifies the authority or source from which the identifier is derived.

key provides a persistent, unique identifier for the named entity, supplied by that source.

source and key should together provide a unique identifier from an authority file. The preferred source is the Virtual International Authority File (VIAF).

viaf

Virtual International Authority File (VIAF)

The Virtual International Authority File is a collaborative initiative hosted by the Online Computer Library Center (OCLC). It integrates authority files from numerous national libraries into a unified global database, standardising personal and corporate names to enhance the consistency and accuracy of bibliographic records across international libraries. VIAF promotes interoperability by linking authority data from various sources, enabling researchers and libraries to reliably identify entities across different languages and cultural barriers, thereby improving the utility of library catalogues and databases worldwide.

<taxonomy xml:id="viaf">
 <bibl>
  <ref target="https://viaf.org/">Virtual International Authority File (VIAF)</ref>
 </bibl>
</taxonomy>

When the value #viaf is used for source, it indicates that the identifier recorded in key is drawn from this authority file. The prefix # indicates a reference to an identifier defined elsewhere in the same file. In this case, it refers to the <taxonomy> declared in the header with xml:id=viaf. This value can be selected from a controlled dropdown list when entering the attribute and does not need to be typed manually.

Names are encoded in the standard format (last name, first name, birth YYYY-death YYYY), as authorised by the Library of Congress . key must include a unique numeric ID for the name from the Virtual International Authority File (VIAF):

William Shakespeare (1564-1616) = Shakespeare, William, 1564-1616

VIAF ID: 96994048 (Personal) | source="#viaf" key="96994048"

Permalink: http://viaf.org/viaf/96994048

<author source="#viaf" key="96994048">Shakespeare, William, 1564-1616</author>

Roman Catholic Church = Catholic Church

VIAF ID: 130782063 (Corporate) | source="#viaf" key="130782063"

Permalink: http://viaf.org/viaf/130782063

<author source="#viaf" key="130782063">Catholic Church</author>

Consult VIAF and use the standard form of a name authorised by the Library of Congress.

If a VIAF entry exists but is not authorised by the Library of Congress, include the VIAF identifier in key and record the name in the standard format: last name, first name, birth YYYY–death YYYY.

If no VIAF entry exists, record the name in the standard format: last name, first name, birth YYYY–death YYYY.

The value of source should reference a declared authority source. While VIAF is preferred, other external or local authority files may be used. Each authority source must be declared in a corresponding <taxonomy> within the header and referenced by its xml:id.

For anonymous works and cases where authorship is unknown, see <author> Unknown | Anonymous. <!--Insert hyperlink-->

• Title: Chaucer's Canterbury Tales and other works

• Alternative Title(s): Canterbury Tales

• Language(s): Middle English

• Authors(s): Chaucer, Geoffrey, -1400

<msItem>
 <title xml:lang="eng">Chaucer's Canterbury Tales and other works</title>
 <title type="alt" xml:lang="eng">Canterbury Tales</title>
 <textLang mainLang="enm">Middle English</textLang>
 <author source="#viaf" key="100185203">Chaucer, Geoffrey, -1400</author>
</msItem>

Chaucer's Canterbury Tales and other works: English MS 113

<author> may be repeated to accommodate multiple authors.

• Title: Roman de la Rose

• Language(s): Middle French

• Author(s): Guillaume, de Lorris, active 1230; Jean, de Meun, approximately 1240-approximately 1305

<msItem> <title>Roman de la Rose</title> <textLang mainLang="frm">Middle French</textLang> <author source="#viaf" key="184963682">Guillaume, de Lorris, active 1230</author> <author source="#viaf" key="86621958">Jean, de Meun, approximately 1240-approximately 1305</author> </msItem> </msContents>

Roman de la Rose: French MS 66

Il Canzoniere di Petrarca e Le Rime di Dante (Italian MS 1)

... .... ...

Information about this document

—

  • Title: Il Canzoniere di Petrarca e Le Rime di Dante
  • Alternative Title(s): Petrarca Canzoni e Dante Sonetti; Petrarch’s Canzoniere and Dante’s Rime
  • Language(s): Italian

—

Section shown in images 10 to 10

  • Title: Bernardo della Casa’s dedicatory sonnet
  • Language(s): Italian

—

Section shown in images 13 to 305

  • Title: Il Canzoniere di Petrarca
  • Alternative Title(s): Rime sparse (Scattered rhymes)
  • Language(s): Italian
  • Author(s): Petrarca, Francesco, 1304-1374

—

Section shown in images 309 to 359

  • Title: Le Rime di Dante
  • Language(s): Italian
  • Author(s): Dante Alighieri, 1265-1321

—

<msContents>
 <summary>... .... ...</summary>
 <msItem>
  <title>Il Canzoniere di Petrarca e Le Rime di Dante</title>
  <title type="alt">Petrarca Canzoni e Dante Sonetti</title>
  <title type="alt">Petrarch’s Canzoniere and Dante’s Rime</title>
  <textLang mainLang="ita">Italian</textLang>
  <msItem n="1">
   <locus from="2v" to="2v">2v</locus>
   <title>Bernardo della Casa’s dedicatory sonnet</title>
   <textLang mainLang="ita">Italian</textLang>
<!-- additional metadata -->
  </msItem>
  <msItem n="2">
   <locus from="3r" to="148r">3r-148r</locus>
   <title>Il Canzoniere di Petrarca</title>
   <title type="alt">Rime sparse (Scattered rhymes)</title>
   <textLang mainLang="ita">Italian</textLang>
   <author source="#viaf" key="39382430">Petrarca, Francesco, 1304-1374</author>
<!-- additional metadata -->
  </msItem>
  <msItem n="3">
   <locus from="149r" to="174r">149r-174r</locus>
   <title>Le Rime di Dante</title>
   <textLang mainLang="ita">Italian</textLang>
   <author source="#viaf" key="97105654">Dante Alighieri, 1265-1321</author>
<!-- additional metadata -->
  </msItem>
 </msItem>
</msContents>

Il Canzoniere di Petrarca e Le Rime di Dante: Italian MS 1

As a general rule, encode descriptive information at the most specific level to which it applies. This principle applies to both <msItem> and <msPart>. Accordingly, <author> should normally be encoded within the most specific <msItem> to which the authorship relates.

Where no nested <msItem> or <msPart> structure is present, the top-level <msItem> is the appropriate level at which to record authorship. Where a single author is responsible for the entire work, <author> may be recorded once on the top-level <msItem> rather than repeated within nested components.

<author> name variants

Where an author's name appears in more than one form, whether due to transliteration, translation, or script variation, use <persName> within <author> to record each form separately. Each <persName> should include xml:lang to specify the language and script of that form. Record the authority source and identifier once on <author>, using source and key.

Here is an example of names in both Latin and Farsi script:

<author source="#viaf" key="90042997">
 <persName xml:lang="per-Latn-x-lc">Jāmī, 1414-1492</persName>
 <persName xml:lang="per">جامی</persName>
</author>

The value per-Latn-x-lc combines the ISO 639-2 language code for Persian (per), the ISO 15924 script code for Latin (Latn), and the private-use extension x-lc to indicate Library of Congress romanisation.

Do not use <foreign> in this context.

<author> unknown | anonymous

For items where the author is anonymous, <author> should not be omitted. Where authorship is unknown, the use of <author> is optional and depends on the project’s requirements. In either case, the status of the authorship should be recorded clearly and consistently.

Unknown authorship

unknown the identity of the author is unknown or cannot be determined.

Use <author> with cert=unknown to indicate that the identity of the author is not ascertainable with the current information. If the absence is the result of analytical judgement, include evidence=conjecture as well.

<author cert="unknown" evidence="conjecture">Unknown</author>

Where adopted, it should be applied consistently across a project wherever authorship cannot be determined.

Consider using Unknown when:

No authorship information: there is no available information about the author, and historical or textual analysis does not suggest any intentional anonymity.

Lost identity: historical records do not provide any clues about the author's identity, possibly due to the age of the work or loss of records over time.

Inadequate evidence: you cannot conclusively determine why there is no author credited due to insufficient evidence.

Anonymous authorship

Anonymous is used when it is known that the author deliberately chose not to reveal their identity. This status is typically confirmed through historical or contextual evidence.

Use <author> without cert, as the anonymity is a known and intentional attribute of the work.

Anonymous

VIAF ID: 53223366 (Personal) | source="#viaf" key="53223366"

Permalink: http://viaf.org/viaf/53223366

<author source="#viaf" key="53223366">Anonymous</author>

Use Anonymous when:

Intentional anonymity: there is evidence (textual, historical, contextual) that suggests the author deliberately chose not to reveal their identity. This could be inferred from the text itself, prefaces, or from historical practices and norms of the time.

Cultural or social reasons: the work fits into a broader cultural or social practice where works were commonly published without author names for reasons like humility, fear of persecution, or a cultural norm that de-emphasized individual authorship.

Explicit indications: the work or accompanying documentation explicitly states that the author wished to remain anonymous.

The terms Unknown and Anonymous should always be capitalised when used as textual values. Care should be taken to distinguish between unknown authorship and deliberate anonymity. Anonymous should be used only where there is evidence that the author's identity was intentionally concealed. Unknown should be used only where the author's identity cannot be determined from the available evidence. This distinction should be applied consistently throughout a project.

1.29 <editor> | editor | translator | photographer, etc.

<editor> contains a secondary statement of responsibility for a bibliographic item, for example the name of an individual, institution or organisation, (or of several such) acting as editor, compiler, translator, etc.

MDC display label — Editors(s) | Translator(s) | Photographer(s), etc.

<editor> is used to record the name of a person or organisation that has contributed significantly to a work in a capacity other than the author.

One or more <editor> may be used, with roles specified accurately to ensure clear attribution of contributions beyond primary authorship.

<editor> follows the same principles as <author>. When applicable, it must include both source and key to provide a persistent identifier from an authority file, such as VIAF. <persName> may be used to encode name variants.

<editor> must include an appropriate role.

@role

role specifies the exact role of a person or organisation in relation to the item.

role should be encoded using standardised codes from the MARC Code List for Relators.

marcrelator

MARC Code List for Relators | Library of Congress

The MARC Code List for Relators is a standardised list published by the Library of Congress that provides three-letter (alpha-3) codes used in MARC (Machine-Readable Cataloguing) records to designate the roles of people, organisations or entities in the creation or contribution to a work.

<taxonomy xml:id="marcrelator">
 <bibl>
  <ref target="https://www.loc.gov/marc/relators/relaterm.html">MARC Code List for Relators (LC)</ref>
 </bibl>
</taxonomy>

role on <editor> is constrained by a controlled vocabulary defined in our customised TEI schema, extended via an ODD (One Document Does it all). The permitted values for the MARC relator codes appear in the XML editor as a drop-down list. Additional codes may be included through the ODD. Only these values are valid. Any other value will result in a schema validation error.

Additional codes may be added through the ODD where required. Their inclusion may also require implementation by the software developer to ensure the correct display.

visual | photograph album

• Photographers(s): Fenton, Roger, 1819-1869; Bedford, Francis, 1816-1894; Wilson, George Washington, 1823-1893

<msItem>
<!-- additional metadata -->
 <editor source="#viaf" key="66536514" role="pht">Fenton, Roger, 1819-1869</editor>
 <editor source="#viaf" key="22414011" role="pht">Bedford, Francis, 1816-1894</editor>
 <editor source="#viaf" key="54421826" role="pht">Wilson, George Washington, 1823-1893</editor>
<!-- additional metadata -->
</msItem>

English architecture and landscapes: VPH.7

printed | map

• Cartographer(s): Mitsukuri, Shōgo, 1821-1846　箕作, 省吾

• Engraver(s): Takeguchi, Teisai　竹口, 貞斎; Sugita, Yūsai　杉田, 祐斎

• Contributor(s): Ōtsuki, Bankei, 1801-1878　大槻, 盤渓

<msItem>
<!-- additional metadata -->
 <editor source="#viaf" key="19539126" role="ctg">
  <persName xml:lang="jpn-Latn-x-lc">Mitsukuri, Shōgo, 1821-1846</persName>
  <persName xml:lang="jpn">箕作, 省吾</persName>
 </editor>
 <editor role="egr">
  <persName xml:lang="jpn-Latn-x-lc">Takeguchi, Teisai</persName>
  <persName xml:lang="jpn">竹口, 貞斎</persName>
 </editor>
 <editor role="egr">
  <persName xml:lang="jpn-Latn-x-lc">Sugita, Yūsai</persName>
  <persName xml:lang="jpn">杉田, 祐斎</persName>
 </editor>
 <editor source="#viaf" key="122320757" role="ctb">
  <persName xml:lang="jpn-Latn-x-lc">Ōtsuki, Bankei, 1801-1878</persName>
  <persName xml:lang="jpn">大槻, 盤渓</persName>
 </editor>
<!-- additional metadata -->
</msItem>

Shinsei yochi zenzu: Japanese 118a

printed | incunable

• Author(s): Dante Alighieri, 1265-1321

• Commentator(s): Landino, Cristoforo, 1424-1504

• Editor(s): Pietro, da Figino, active 15th century

• Printer(s): Piasi, Piero de, active 15th century

<msItem>
<!-- additional metadata -->
 <author source="#viaf" key="97105654">Dante Alighieri, 1265-1321</author>
 <editor source="#viaf" key="30887708" role="cwt">Landino, Cristoforo, 1424-1504</editor>
 <editor source="#viaf" key="39514283" role="edt">Pietro, da Figino, active 15th century</editor>
 <editor source="#viaf" key="311356730" role="prt">Piasi, Piero de, active 15th century </editor>
<!-- additional metadata -->
</msItem>

Comento di christophoro Landino fiorentino sopra la comedia di Danthe alighieri poeta fiorentino: 5121

Always consider the specific nature of each role's involvement in a work. Roles concerned with physical production or general association are often more appropriately recorded elsewhere, such as within <physDesc> or <history>. For example, an artist may be more appropriately encoded within <decoDesc>, and a former owner within <provenance>.

Where possible, contributors should be encoded within the most contextually relevant element for the role being recorded, using <name> with role, together with <persName> or <orgName>. Where this is not feasible, the use of <editor> is acceptable.

Once a person or organisation has been encoded in full at its first occurrence, it is not necessary to repeat the same level of encoding for subsequent occurrences.

Where the same person or organisation has more than one role, record all applicable codes within the same role, separated by a single space. Do not separate multiple codes with commas or other punctuation.

Do not use <persName type="alt">.

Recommended best practice | <name> | @role

The preferred approach is to record individuals and organisations within the most contextually relevant element for the role being recorded, using <name> with role containing either <persName> or <orgName>.

This is particularly useful where a distinction is required between the form of a name used in prose and the form used for indexing.

One common application of this approach is within <decoDesc>, where artists, illuminators, engravers, and other individuals or organisations associated with the decoration may be recorded.

Any name associated with the production or execution of the decoration should be encoded using <name>, containing either <persName> or <orgName> as appropriate, and must include role indicating the nature of the relationship to the decoration. If the name is listed in VIAF, include both source and key on <name> to reference the relevant VIAF authority record. Where both display and standard forms are required, include two <persName> or <orgName> within <name>, one with type=display and one with type=standard. Otherwise, a single <persName> or <orgName> within <name> may be used.

When the value #viaf is used for source, it indicates that the identifier recorded in key is drawn from this authority file. The prefix # indicates a reference to an identifier defined elsewhere in the same file. In this case, it refers to the <taxonomy> declared in the header with xml:id=viaf. This value can be selected from a controlled dropdown list when entering the attribute and does not need to be typed manually.

role specifies the relationship of a person or organisation to the decoration. In most cases, this value will be art, used for artists. Other MARC relator codes, such as ilu for illuminators, ill for illustrators, or egr for engravers, may be used where appropriate, and additional values may also be applied as required.

• Illuminator(s): Simone Camaldolese, Don, active 1378-1405

—

• Decoration:

The illuminated decoration on 3r, 105r, and 149r is attributed to Don Simone Camaldolese (active 1378-1405), also known as Simone da Siena. A monk of the Camaldolese Order at the Monastery of Santa Maria degli Angeli in Florence, Don Simone was a prominent miniaturist and illuminator of the late fourteenth century, primarily active in Florence and Tuscany. ... ... ...

<decoDesc>
 <summary>
  <p>The illuminated decoration on <locus from="3r" to="3r">3r</locus>, <locus from="105r" to="105r">105r</locus>, and <locus from="149r" to="149r">149r</locus> is attributed to <name source="#viaf" key="313279739" role="ilu">
    <persName type="display">Don Simone Camaldolese (active 1378-1405), also known as Simone da Siena</persName>
    <persName type="standard">Simone Camaldolese, Don, active 1378-1405</persName>
   </name>. A monk of the Camaldolese Order at the Monastery of Santa Maria degli Angeli in Florence, Don Simone was a prominent miniaturist and illuminator of the late fourteenth century, primarily active in Florence and Tuscany. ...</p>
 </summary>
<!-- additional metadata -->
 <decoNote>
<!-- additional metadata -->
 </decoNote>
</decoDesc>

Il Canzoniere di Petrarca e Le Rime di Dante: Italian MS 1

type=display provides the form used in the prose description, while type=standard records the authorised form used for indexing and display fields in MDC. role determines the corresponding display label. <name> encoded in <provenance> with role=fmo will display as Former Owner(s), and in <decoDesc> with role=ilu will display as Illuminator(s), among others.

Attributed name(s)

In some cases, a person is traditionally or historically associated with a work, but conclusive evidence of authorship is lacking. This may include attributions found in manuscript rubrics, colophons, catalogue records, or bibliographic tradition. Such cases should be encoded using role=att on the most appropriate element, such as <author>, <editor>, or <persName> within <name>.

<author role="att"> should be used when the individual is regarded as the author of the work, even if the attribution is uncertain or disputed. This preserves the authorial relationship and is the most semantically appropriate encoding where attributed authorship remains central to the intellectual identity of the work.

In MDC, however, names encoded as <author role="att"> are displayed under Author(s). Where it is preferable to distinguish attributed authorship from established authorship in the user interface, <editor role="att"> may be used instead. Within MDC, <editor> functions as a broader contributor element and is displayed under Attributed name(s) when used with role=att.

<name role="att"> may be used where the attributed name appears within a context-specific element such as <summary>, <physDesc>, or <history>. This approach also supports extraction to the MDC field Attributed name(s).

An explanatory <note> should accompany any use of role=att, outlining the nature of the attribution and the evidence on which it is based.

1.30 rubrics, incipits, explicits, and other quotations

In a manuscript description, it is customary to record the opening and closing words of a text, along with any headings or colophons. The specialised elements <rubric>, <incipit>, <explicit>, <finalRubric>, <colophon>, which are all contained within <msItem>, support this, along with the more general <quote>, for recording other excerpts of the text not covered by these elements.

<rubric> | <incipit> | <explicit> | <finalRubric> | <colophon>

<rubric> (rubric) contains the text of any rubric or heading attached to a particular manuscript item, that is, a string of words through which a manuscript or other object signals the beginning of a text division, often with an assertion as to its author and title, which is in some way set off from the text itself, typically in red ink, or by use of different size or type of script, or some other such visual device.

<incipit> (incipit) contains the incipit of a manuscript or similar object item, that is the opening words of the text proper, exclusive of any rubric which might precede it, of sufficient length to identify the work uniquely; such incipits were, in former times, frequently used a means of reference to a work, in place of a title.

<explicit> (explicit) contains the explicit of an item, that is, the closing words of the text proper, exclusive of any rubric or colophon which might follow it.

<finalRubric> (final rubric) contains the string of words that denotes the end of a text division, often with an assertion as to its author and title, usually set off from the text itself by red ink, by a different size or type of script, or by some other such visual device.

<colophon> (colophon) contains the colophon of an item: that is, a statement providing information regarding the date, place, agency, or reason for production of the manuscript or other object.

MDC display label — Excerpts: Rubric | Incipit | Explicit | Final Rubric | Colophon

These elements are optional. They should be used within <msItem> for the text or section to which the excerpt belongs. Where there are no nested <msItem>, they may be used within the top-level <msItem>.

<locus> should be included within each element to indicate the location of the excerpt within the item.

The language of <rubric>, <incipit>, <explicit>, <finalRubric>, and <colophon> is normally assumed to be the same as that specified in <textLang>. It is therefore not necessary to use xml:lang on these elements unless an excerpt is in a different language, for example, where a manuscript contains a rubric in French but an incipit in Latin.

defective may be used on <incipit> and <explicit> to indicate whether the beginning or end of a text is s not preserved. In the case of an incipit, the excerpt records the first surviving words of the text rather than its true opening. In the case of an explicit, the excerpt records the final surviving words of the text rather than its true ending.

—

  • Title: Il Canzoniere di Petrarca e Le Rime di Dante
  • Alternative Title(s): Petrarca Canzoni e Dante Sonetti; Petrarch’s Canzoniere and Dante’s Rime
  • Language(s): Italian

—

Section shown in images 13 to 305

  • Title: Il Canzoniere di Petrarca
  • Alternative Title(s): Rime sparse (Scattered rhymes)
  • Language(s): Italian
  • Author(s): Petrarca, Francesco, 1304-1374
  • Note(s): Canzoniere are also sometimes called Rime sparse ... ... ...

Section shown in images 13 to 216

  • Title: Rime in vita di Madonna Laura | Canzoni I-CCLXIII (1-263)
  • Language(s): Italian

Excerpts:

Rubric: 3r Viri illustris atque poete celeberrimi Francisci Petrarce de Florentia, Rome Laureati, fragmentorum liber incipit feliciter.

Incipit: 3r Voi chascoltate in rime sparse il suono

Explicit: 104v di castita par chelladorni et fregi.

Final Rubric: 104v lncliti uafis Francisci petrarche uulgaria expliciunt metrica ditta que in laudem domine laurette durn uiuent amore dittante composuit.

Section shown in images 309 to 359

  • Title: Le Rime di Dante
  • Language(s): Italian
  • Author(s): Dante Alighieri, 1265-1321
  • Note(s): The sections belong to the Rime, ... ... ...

Section shown in images 309 to 312

  • Title: Rime petrose | Rime CIII (103) | Cosi nel mio parlar voglio esser aspro
  • Language(s): Italian

Excerpts:

Rubric: 149r Qui cominciano le canconi distese del chiaro poeta Dante Allighieri di Firenp, nelle quali diuarie cose tractando nella prima la rigidita della sua donna con rigide rime dimostra. Cuius principio adsit feliciter spiritus almus.

Incipit: 149r Cosi nel mio parlar voglio esser aspro

Explicit: 150v Che bello honor sacquista i far vedetta.

Section shown in images 356 to 359

  • Title: Rime dell’esilio | Rime CXVI (116) | Amor di che convien pur chio mi doglia
  • Language(s): Italian

Excerpts:

Rubric: 172v Cancone. xv. di Dante. nella quale si duole della rigidita duna crudel donna.

Incipit: 172v Amor di che convien pur chio mi doglia

Explicit: 174r Non a di ritornar qui libertate.

Final Rubric, colophon: 174r Beatissimi spetiosique huius voluminis adepto fieri laus sit et gloria deo, qui Laurentio Karoli di Strozis qui ipsum fieri fecit Pauloque scriptori eiusdem felicem tribuat uitam per tempora longiora. Amen.

Il Canzoniere di Petrarca e Le Rime di Dante: Italian MS 1

If the final rubric and colophon are combined, encode the complete text using <finalRubric> with type=colophon. <colophon> itself cannot carry a type.

The placement of <colophon> largely depends on its relevance. If the colophon pertains to a particular text or section of the item, it should be encoded within the corresponding nested <msItem>. If the colophon relates to the item as a whole, it should be encoded within the top-level <msItem>, especially where the intellectual content has not been further subdivided using nested <msItem>.

Le terze rime di Dante: R52150

1.31 <quote> | quotation

<quote> (quotation) contains a phrase or passage attributed by the narrator or author to some agency external to the text.

MDC display label — Quotation

<quote> may be used within the top-level <msitem> to encode a short excerpt or quotation from the manuscript that does not fall within the scope of <rubric>, <incipit>, <explicit>, <finalRubric>, <colophon>. Longer texts should be transcribed in <text>.

Use <quote> to record short textual excerpts that are significant for identification, description, or interpretation of the item. Typical examples include citations, formulas, invocations, and charms in magical, amuletic, or talismanic manuscripts.

• Title: Tefillin

• Language(s): Hebrew

—

• Quotation(s):

"והיה כי יבאך יהוה אל ארץ הכנעני..."

<msItem>
 <title>Tefillin</title>
 <textLang mainLang="heb">Hebrew</textLang>
<!-- additional metadata -->
 <quote>והיה כי יבאך יהוה אל ארץ הכנעני...</quote>
 <note/>
</msItem>

Tefillin: Gaster Amulet 196

1.32 <filiation> | filiation

<filiation> (filiation) contains information concerning the manuscript or other object's filiation, i.e. its relationship to other surviving manuscripts or other objects of the same text or contents, its protographs, antigraphs and apographs.

MDC display label — Filiations

<filiation> may be used to record the relationship between the text being described and other witnesses of the same textual tradition. This may include direct copies, shared exemplars, or classification into manuscript families or textual lineages. It may also note the manuscript’s place within a scholarly stemma or filiation hypothesis.

<filiation> is repeatable. When more than one relationship or hypothesis of descent is present, it is acceptable to record them together within a single <filiation>. However, best practice is to record each separately, using multiple <filiation> to ensure clarity, consistency, and ease of future processing.

<filiation> is typically placed after core textual excerpt elements and quotations. Each entry within <filiation> should follow this format:

Institution Name, City: Identifier

The institution name should appear first, followed by the city in which the holding institution is located. The identifier, such as a catalogue number, shelfmark, accession number, or collection code, should follow the city and be separated by a colon (:). It should be transcribed exactly as used by the institution, including any prefixes, spacing, punctuation, and capitalisation.

If a stable URL or persistent digital identifier is available for the item, the entire string, Institution Name, City: Identifier, should be wrapped in <ref target="...">...<ref>.

Multiple <filiation> | structured, one per relationship

Multiple <filiation> should be used when recording distinct relationships, witnesses, or hypotheses of descent. Each relationship should be recorded separately, using one <filiation> per entry. When the content of <filiation> comprises a simple citation, pointer, or reference, whether structured or descriptive, the entry should be recorded directly within <filiation>, without enclosing it in a <p>.

• Title: Le terze rime di Dante

—

• Author(s): Dante Alighieri, 1265-1321

—

• Filiations:

Aldine Collection: R213785

The Morgan Library & Museum, New York: PML 1257

<!-- additional metadata -->

<!-- additional metadata -->

 <ref target="https://www.digitalcollections.manchester.ac.uk/view/PR-ALDI-R-00002-13785/1">Aldine Collection: R213785</ref>
</filiation>
<filiation>
 <ref target="https://www.themorgan.org/printed-books/175532">The Morgan Library &amp; Museum, New York: PML 1257</ref>
</filiation>

Le terze rime di Dante: 18873

When the reference is internal to the same holding institution, for example, Aldine Collection: R213785, it is not necessary to include the institution name and city. In such cases, provide only the collection name and identifier, following local practice.

Single <filiation> | narrative prose

A single <filiation> containing multiple entries may also be used in descriptive or interpretative contexts, particularly when hypotheses are closely connected and cannot be meaningfully separated. In such cases, prose may be used directly within <filiation> to describe how a manuscript or book fits within a textual tradition or to refer to a scholarly classification.

Section shown in images 231 to 594

• Title: Ἑξαημέρον

• Author(s): Anastasius, Sinaita, Saint, active 640-700

—

• Filiations:

Kuehn and Baggarly have placed this manuscript in family IIIc of this text. This family also contains Biblioteca Nacional, Madrid: MS 4773, Biblioteca Nazionale, Turin: MS C.IV.26, Biblioteca Vallicelliana, Rome: MS F.58 (99), and more distantly related: Madrid, Biblioteca Nacional, Madrid: MS 4775 and Real Biblioteca, Escorial: MS Ψ.I.4.

 <author source="#viaf" key="314808225">Anastasius, Sinaita, Saint, active 640-700</author>
<!-- additional metadata -->
 <filiation>Kuehn and Baggarly have placed this manuscript in family IIIc of this text. This family also contains <ref target="http://bdh.bne.es/bnesearch/detalle/bdh0000246391">Biblioteca Nacional, Madrid: MS 4773</ref>, Biblioteca Nazionale, Turin: MS C.IV.26, Biblioteca Vallicelliana, Rome: MS F.58 (99), and more distantly related: <ref target="http://bdh.bne.es/bnesearch/detalle/bdh0000246393">Biblioteca Nacional, Madrid: MS 4775</ref>, and Real Biblioteca, Escorial: MS Ψ.I.4.</filiation>
</msItem>

Late Antique commentaries on the Book of Genesis: Part III: Ἑξαημέρον: Cambridge, University Library, MS Ff.4.2

Where multiple works within an item have independent filiations, record each <filiation> within the appropriate <msItem>.

1.33 <note> | note

<note> (note) contains a note or annotation.

MDC display label — Note(s)

<note> is intended for recording annotations or explanatory comments. While it is not considered a core element in MDC, it serves as a valuable supplementary field that can enhance the description and interpretation of the material.

<note> is highly flexible and can be used in various sections of <teiHeader>. In the context of MDC, its placement within <msItem> allows the inclusion of general notes about the item or its intellectual content, particularly when such information does not fit naturally into <summary> or other elements within <teiHeader>, with a preference for using more semantically precise elements wherever possible.

<note> may consist of one or more <p> to structure content into separate paragraphs.

With the exception of specialised <note type="..."> defined in Section X <!--insert hyperlink-->: customised <note> types and display fields, all <note> within a top-level <msItem> will be surfaced in the Note(s) field in the MDC interface.

In some projects, particularly those involving MARC-derived metadata and retroconversion workflows, TEI XML files have not been further edited or refined after conversion. They therefore remain largely in their post-conversion state and may contain one or more <note> with multiple <p>. This should be understood as an outcome of the conversion process, rather than as a model for TEI encoding practice.

1.34 <physDesc> | physical description

<physDesc> (physical description) contains a full physical description of a manuscript, manuscript part, or other object optionally subdivided using more specialized elements from the model.physDescPart class.

<physDesc> within <msDesc> brings together the elements used to describe the item's physical form, production, appearance, and related physical characteristics.

The following elements may be included within <physDesc> whenever information is available:

<teiHeader> <fileDesc>|<titleStmt>|<publicationStmt>|<sourceDesc> <msDesc>|<msIdentifier>|<msContents> <physDesc> <!-- Metadata describing all physical characteristics: material, size, format, script, decoration, binding, etc. --> <objectDesc> <!-- Metadata describing the object: form, type, support, extent, and layout --> <!-- Contains <objectType> | <supportDesc> | <layoutDesc> --> <!-- May contain <material> | <extent> | <foliation> | <collation> | <condition> --> </objectDesc> <handDesc>|<typeDesc> <!-- Metadata describing the style(s) of scripts or typeface used in the item --> <!-- Contain <handNote>|<typeNote> --> </handDesc>|<typeDesc> <musicNotation> <!-- Metadata about musical notation if present --> </musicNotation> <decoDesc> <!-- Metadata describing decorative features --> <!-- Contains <decoNote> </decoDesc> <additions> <!-- Metadata describing any later additions to the item --> </additions> <bindingDesc> <!-- Metadata describing the binding of the item --> <!-- Contains <binding> </bindingDesc> <sealDesc> <!-- Metadata describing applied and pendent seals --> <!-- Contains <seal> </sealDesc> <accMat> <!-- Metadata describing any material not originally part of the item but bound with it or otherwise accompanying it --> </accMat> </physDesc> <history>|<additional> </msDesc>|</sourceDesc>|</fileDesc>|<encodingDesc>|<profileDesc>|<revisionDesc> </teiHeader>

The degree of obligation for including specific elements within <physDesc> depends on the nature of the item and the requirements of the project. A structured and precise description is usually preferable, but the time required for detailed encoding should be balanced against the value it adds to the resource.

<physDesc> may take one of two forms:

Unstructured | a sequence of prose paragraphs, using one or more <p>, describing physical features narratively.

Structured | a group of specialised elements from the model.physDescPart class, which may themselves contain one or more <p>.

Although unstructured encoding using one or more <p> is permitted, it is not recommended as the default. Wherever possible, physical description should be organised using specialised elements, as this supports clearer distinction between different types of physical characteristics.

Combining structured and unstructured content within a single <physDesc> is generally discouraged. Hybrid encoding should be treated as an exception rather than standard practice. It may occasionally be necessary in retroconversion workflows, particularly when some information can be encoded using specialised elements, but other details cannot be reliably separated from prose or mapped to an appropriate element.

In the MDC interface, any <p> placed immediately after <physDesc> will be pulled through into the display field labelled Physical Description. This happens regardless of whether additional structured elements, such as <objectDesc>, <handDesc>, or <decoDesc>, are present. If this is not the intended behaviour, the content of the <p> should instead be placed within appropriate specialised elements to avoid unintended display output.

1.35 <objectDesc> | object description

<objectDesc> (object description) contains a description of the physical components making up the object which is being described.

MDC display label — Format

<objectDesc> is the first component within <physDesc> and brings together elements used to describe the item’s physical format, support, extent, and layout.

form should be used on <objectDesc> to describe the physical format of the item in its current state, not its original or intended format at the time of creation.

@form

form a term identifying the physical form or format of the item

Values for form should be selected from the approved controlled vocabulary below. The Getty Art & Architecture Thesaurus (AAT) should be consulted where appropriate to confirm the meaning and scope of terms.

The form value must be a concise, singular noun that accurately describes the physical structure or format of the item. It should not be used to record content, function, or genre.

form

<objectDesc> | form

accordion

Accordion

<objectDesc form="accordion">

Physical format comprising a continuous or joined substrate folded into accordion- or concertina-style pleats, often attached at one or both ends to covers or boards.

Related GETTY (AAT) | accordion books | ID: 300438814

The use of form=accordion covers:

  • Manuscripts and printed items with a continuous or joined substrate folded into zigzag or pleated panels (accordion, concertina, fanfold, or screenfold structure)
  • Folded books such as medieval European almanacs (faltbuchs)
  • Asian folding manuscripts, such as Thai samut khoi or Burmese parabaik
  • Leporello-style books and similar folded formats

booklet

Booklet

<objectDesc form="booklet">

Physical format comprising small books consisting of a few sheets that are glued, stitched or stapled together between thin card or paper covers.

Related GETTY (AAT) | booklets | ID: 300311670

The use of form=booklet covers:

  • Self-contained slim publications, such as pamphlets or chapbooks, including examples later incorporated into bound volumes
  • Single issues of magazines or periodicals issued in paper wrappers or light covers.
  • Manuscript traditions, including many Naxi Dongba | Mo-so manuscripts, comprising separate leaves stitched or thread-bound along one edge for leaf-turning.

codex

Codex

<objectDesc form="codex">

Physical format comprising multiple gatherings of separate leaves, cut or folded, stacked, and bound along one edge within a cover or binding, designed to be read by turning the leaves.

Related GETTY (AAT) | codices (bound manuscripts) | ID: 300224200

The use of form=codex covers:

  • Bound volumes consisting of multiple leaves or gatherings
  • Handwritten manuscripts, early printed books, incunabula
  • Structurally bound modern works such as notebooks, diaries, albums, scrapbooks
  • Bound chapbooks, periodicals, pamphlets, and similar items.
  • Composite manuscripts or volumes whose overall physical structure remains codex-form.

fragment

Fragment

<objectDesc form="fragment">

Physical format comprising incomplete or detached portions of a formerly whole object.

Related GETTY (AAT) | fragments (object portions) | ID: 300117130

The use of form=fragment covers:

  • Detached or broken pieces of a manuscript or other object.
  • Disassociated elements from scrolls, codices, or tablets.
  • Cuttings or torn pieces lacking evidence of original structure.
  • Material remains without binding or support, possibly reused.

Use form=fragment for broken or disassociated parts of an object whose original wholeness is presumed or documentable, especially when the object was not purposefully reused for inscription.

<msFrag> may be used within <msDesc> when encoding one or more fragments of a scattered or fragmented manuscript.

leaf

Leaf

<objectDesc form="leaf">

Physical format comprising a single leaf originating from a codex or gathering structure, typically consisting of recto and verso surfaces.

Related GETTY (AAT) | leaves (gathered matter) | ID: 300115833

The use of form=leaf covers:

  • Individual manuscript leaves surviving separately from their original codex structure.
  • Detached printed or written leaves that show codicological features.
  • Reuse cases where leaves were repurposed in bindings, or inserted into albums, scrapbooks, or collections but clearly originated from a codex.
  • Unbound gatherings or quires of two or more bifolia surviving independently.

object

Object

<objectDesc form="object">

Physical format comprising discrete three-dimensional artefacts or a composite material object.

Related GETTY (AAT) | objects | ID: 300196838

The use of form=object covers:

  • Three-dimensional artefacts such as coins, medals, seals, textiles, models, instruments, sculptural pieces, and composite or mixed-media items.
  • Detached or decorative binding elements, including independent covers, treasure bindings, and jewelled bindings incorporating materials such as metal, ivory, or gemstones.
  • Encased or three-dimensional photographic formats, including cased photographs, framed photographic works, sculptural photographic objects, and photographic jewellery or miniatures.

Getty AAT uses the term object in the inclusive, museum-object sense, encompassing anything that can be accessioned or catalogued, including two-dimensional works such as photographs. In this guidance, form=object is used primarily for three-dimensional physical forms rather than flat textual supports.

ostracon

Ostracon

<objectDesc form="ostracon">

Physical format comprising fragments of pottery or stone containing inscriptions.

Related GETTY (AAT) | ostraka | ID: 300185396

The use of form=ostracon covers:

  • Inscribed fragments of pottery or stone, typically casual or administrative
  • Greek, Coptic, and Egyptian voting ostraca
  • Everyday notetaking on broken ceramic or stone shards

Use form=ostracon when the inscriptional use of the broken ceramic or stone defines the object (i.e. the fragment was repurposed as an intentional writing surface).

pothi

Pothi

<objectDesc form="pothi">

Physical format comprising a stack of separate leaves, typically in horizontal orientation, not bound along one edge and designed to be read by flipping the leaves upwards rather than sideways, with leaves either loose or strung and often held between protective boards or within wrappers.

GETTY (AAT) | N/A

The use of form=pothi covers:

  • Traditional South and Southeast Asian manuscripts (India, Nepal, Tibet, Sri Lanka) in pothī format.
  • Palm-leaf, bark, and paper manuscripts produced within this tradition.

roll

Roll

<objectDesc form="roll">

Physical format comprising long strips of flexible material used for written documents and rolled for ease of handling and storage.

GETTY (AAT) | N/A

The use of form=roll covers:

  • Rolled manuscripts made of paper, parchment, papyrus, or similar material
  • Inscribed rolls intended for reading or ceremonial use (Torah scrolls, historical scrolls)
  • Literary, religious, or administrative documents produced as continuous inscribed rolls

sheet

Sheet

<objectDesc form="sheet">

Physical format comprising a single flat support, typically of paper, parchment, or similar material, not primarily defined by attachment to a codex or gathering structure.

Related GETTY (AAT) | sheets (paper artifacts) | ID: 300189648

The use of form=sheet covers:

  • Single flat sheets bearing written, printed, drawn, or photographic content.
  • Broadsides, flyers, proclamations, single-sheet maps, charters, architectural plans, and posters.
  • Drawings, prints, and other flat works preserved as self-contained units.
  • Flat items for which no clear evidence suggests origin within a codex or gathering structure.
  • Correspondence letters, including folded letters, letterfolds, and other self-enclosing forms.
  • Mounted flat photographic prints such as cabinet cards, together with unmounted photographic prints such as silver gelatin and albumen prints.
  • Photographic supports in sheet form, including glass plate negatives and sheet film.

Use form=sheet for single, flat items that retain the physical characteristics of a sheet, typically unbound, but also applicable to individual components within bound volumes.

tablet

Tablet

<objectDesc form="tablet">

Physical format comprising flat rigid surfaces used for writing or inscription.

Related GETTY (AAT) | writing tablets (information artifacts) | ID: 300262572

The use of form=tablet covers:

  • Clay tablets with cuneiform script
  • Wax, wooden, metal, and stone tablets bearing script.

New values should not be introduced without consultation with the Digital Metadata Specialist. Additional values may be approved where necessary to describe physical formats that are not represented adequately within the existing vocabulary.

<object> vs <msDesc>

As of TEI P5 version 3.5.0, <object> is a general-purpose container for describing identifiable physical objects, regardless of whether they bear text. It expands on the functionality of <msDesc> to support a wider range of cultural heritage artefacts, including non-textual, photographic, and 3D artefacts.

At present, MDC does not yet support <object> and its associated sub-elements. Items that would logically be encoded under <object>, for example, photographs or artefacts, must currently be described using <objectDesc> embedded within <msDesc>.

A practical example is the Gaster Amulets in MDC: In the current encoding, amuletic objects are described using <msDesc>, although structurally they would be more appropriately modelled under <object> once MDC supports it.

As <object> continues to evolve within TEI P5, projects should anticipate minor structural refinements and expanded support for a broader range of object types in future guideline versions. Accordingly, support for <object> within MDC will also develop in line with these updates.

<objectDesc> consists of three principal elements: <objectType>, which identifies the functional or semantic classification of the object, <supportDesc>, which describes the physical support, that is, the material carrier on which the text is written or printed, and <layoutDesc>, which describes the layout, that is, the manner in which the text is arranged on the support.

<teiHeader> <fileDesc>|<titleStmt>|<publicationStmt>|<sourceDesc> <msDesc>|<msIdentifier>|<msContents> <physDesc> <objectDesc> <objectType> <!-- Metadata describing the type of object --> <!-- Contains <objectType> <supportDesc> <!-- Metadata describing the physical support (carrier) of the item --> <support> <!-- Metadata describing the materials comprising the physical support --> <!-- Contains <material> <!-- May contain <watermark> --> </support> <extent> <!-- Metadata describing the quantity and size of the leaves --> <!-- Contains <measure> | <dimensions> | <height> | <width> --> </extent> <foliation> <!-- Metadata describing the foliation [or pagination] system used --> </foliation> <collation> <!-- Metadata describing the structure of gatherings | quires --> <!-- Contains <list> | <signatures> | <catchwords> | <secFol> --> <!-- May contain <formula> --> </collation> <condition> <!-- Metadata describing any damage with direct impact on the text, not the entire item --> <!-- Contains <p> --> </condition> </supportDesc> <layoutDesc> <!-- Metadata describing how the text is arranged on the support --> <layout> <!-- Metadata detailing the text layout such as columns and lines --> <!-- May contain <dimensions> --> </layout> </layoutDesc> </objectDesc> <handDesc><typeDesc>|<musicNotation>|<decoDesc>|<additions>|<bindingDesc>|<sealDesc>|<accMat> </physDesc> <history>|<additional> </msDesc>|</sourceDesc>|</fileDesc>|<encodingDesc>|<profileDesc>|<revisionDesc> </teiHeader>

The degree of obligation for including specific elements within <objectDesc> depends on the nature of the item and the specific requirements of the project.

1.36 <objectType> | object type

<objectType> (object type) contains a word or phrase describing the type of object being referred to.

MDC display label — Type

<objectType> within <objectDesc> is used to record the semantic, functional, or bibliographic type of an item.

form on <objectDesc> records the general physical format of the item, such as codex, sheet, or roll, while <objectType> further defines the nature of that item. A codex may therefore be a manuscript or incunabulum (book), a sheet may be a broadside or map, and a roll may be a scroll.

<objectType> is repeatable, allowing a single physical format to be associated with multiple object types.

<objectType> must include source and key where a controlled vocabulary is used.

@source @key

source specifies the source from which some aspect of this element is drawn.

key provides a persistent, unique identifier for the named entity, supplied by that source.

source and key should together provide a unique identifier drawn from a controlled vocabulary or classification system. The preferred source is the Getty Art & Architecture Thesaurus (AAT).

aat

Getty Art & Architecture Thesaurus® (AAT)

The Getty Art & Architecture Thesaurus® (AAT) is a structured vocabulary for describing concepts relating to art, architecture, and material culture. It provides a hierarchical framework that ensures consistency in cataloguing, indexing, and scholarly research.

<taxonomy xml:id="aat">
 <bibl>
  <ref target="https://www.getty.edu/research/tools/vocabularies/aat">Getty Art &amp; Architecture Thesaurus® (AAT)</ref>
 </bibl>
</taxonomy>

When the value #aat is used for source, it indicates that the identifier recorded in key is drawn from this authority file. The prefix # indicates a reference to an identifier defined elsewhere in the same file. In this case, it refers to the <taxonomy> declared in the header with xml:id=aat. This value can be selected from a controlled dropdown list when entering the attribute and does not need to be typed manually.

The value of <objectType> should be recorded in lowercase. Terms should not be capitalised for display purposes, as display capitalisation is applied automatically by the XSLT transformation.

The following examples focus on <objectType> but also show how <material> is encoded using source and key with AAT identifiers.

codex | manuscript

• Format: Codex

• Type: Manuscript

• Material(s): Parchment

<objectDesc form="codex">
 <objectType source="#aat" key="300028569">manuscript</objectType>
 <supportDesc material="parchment">
  <support>
   <material source="#aat" key="300011851">Parchment</material>
  </support>
<!-- additional metadata -->
 </supportDesc>
</objectDesc>

GETTY (AAT) | manuscripts (documents) | ID:300028569

codex | Incunable (pre-1500)

• Format: Codex

• Type: Incunabulum (book)

• Material(s): Paper

<objectDesc form="codex">
 <objectType source="#aat" key="300055021">incunabulum (book)</objectType>
 <supportDesc material="paper">
  <support>
   <material source="#aat" key="300014109">Paper</material>
  </support>
<!-- additional metadata -->
 </supportDesc>
</objectDesc>

GETTY (AAT) | incunabulum (book) | ID:300055021

codex | early printed book (post-1500)

• Format: Codex

• Type: Book

• Material(s): Paper

<objectDesc form="codex">
 <objectType source="#aat" key="300028051">book</objectType>
 <supportDesc material="paper">
  <support>
   <material source="#aat" key="300014109">Paper</material>
  </support>
<!-- additional metadata -->
 </supportDesc>
</objectDesc>

GETTY (AAT) | book | ID:300028051

Use clearly defined categories based on historical period and printing technology to ensure consistent terminology. Incunabulum refers to books printed before 1501 using the hand press. Early printed book refers to books printed between 1501 and around 1800, also using the hand press. Modern book refers to books printed after 1800 using mechanised printing methods.

Book format + paper size variants

fo | 4to | 8vo | 12mo | 16mo | 24mo | 32mo

imperial – royal – medium – chancery

When describing printed books, particularly those associated with historical paper sizes or bibliographic formats, <objectDesc> should normally be encoded with form=codex. While form records the physical format of the item, one <objectType> may be used to record the type of item, such as book or incunabulum (book), and a second <objectType> may be used to record that format, such as folio (fo), quarto (4to), or octavo (8vo). source and key should be included with the corresponding AAT identifiers.

Typological paper sizes, such as imperial, royal, or medium, should be recorded separately using <material> with type=paper and an appropriate subtype. source and key should be included with the corresponding AAT identifiers.

• Format: Codex

• Type: Incunabulum (book); Folio (fo)

• Material(s): Royal paper

<objectDesc form="codex">
 <objectType source="#aat" key="300055021">incunabulum (book)</objectType>
 <objectType source="#aat" key="300265438">folio (fo)</objectType>
 <supportDesc material="paper">
  <support>
   <material type="paper" subtype="royal" source="#aat" key="300265462">Royal paper</material>
  </support>
<!-- additional metadata -->
 </supportDesc>
</objectDesc>

La Commedia (Comm. Martin Paolo Nidobeato): 19561

Bibliographic formats such as folio (fo), quarto (4to), and octavo (8vo), and paper sizes such as imperial and royal, describe different characteristics of a printed book and should be encoded separately. Bibliographic format refers to how a sheet has been folded to create the leaves of a volume, whereas paper size refers to the dimensions of the original sheet before folding. Where both are known, both may be recorded. For example, a royal folio consists of a sheet of royal paper folded once. Include whichever is relevant, based on context and the information available in ALMA.

bibliographic format (how the sheet was folded)

paper size (the original size of the sheet)

quarto (4to)

GETTY (AAT) | octavo | ID:300265439

imperial

GETTY (AAT) | imperial (paper size) | ID:300265456

octavo (8vo)

GETTY (AAT) | octavo | ID:300265440

royal

GETTY (AAT) | royal (paper size) | ID:300265462

folio (fo)

GETTY (AAT) | folio | ID:300265438

medium | median

GETTY (AAT) | medium (paper size) | ID:300265461

duodecimo (12mo)

GETTY (AAT) | twelvemo | ID:300265445

chancery

GETTY (AAT) | n/a

sextodecimo (16mo)

GETTY (AAT) | sixteenmo | ID:300265441

crown

GETTY (AAT) | crown (paper size) | ID:300265463

vigesimo-quarto (24mo)

GETTY (AAT) | twenty-fourmo | ID:300265447

demy

GETTY (AAT) | demy | ID:300265460

trigesimo-secundo (32mo)

GETTY (AAT) | thirty-twomo | ID:300265442

post

GETTY (AAT) | post (paper size | ID:300265457

Unless you must preserve historical specificity, use the British traditional set as your standard: imperial, royal, medium (median), chancery, crown, demy, post, etc.

codex | book

• Format: Codex

• Type: Book; Lithographic book (chap-i sangī)

• Material(s): Paper

<objectDesc form="codex">
 <objectType source="#aat" key="300028051">book</objectType>
 <objectType>lithographic book (chap-i sangī)</objectType>
 <supportDesc material="paper">
  <support>
   <material source="#aat" key="300014109">Paper</material>
  </support>
<!-- additional metadata -->
 </supportDesc>
</objectDesc>

GETTY (AAT) | book | ID:300028051

local (in-house) | lithographic book (chap-i sangī)

codex | scrapbook

• Format: Codex

• Type: Scrapbook

• Material(s): Paper

<objectDesc form="codex">
 <objectType source="#aat" key="300027341">scrapbook</objectType>
 <supportDesc material="paper">
  <support>
   <material source="#aat" key="300014109">Paper</material>
  </support>
<!-- additional metadata -->
 </supportDesc>
</objectDesc>

GETTY (AAT) | scrapbooks | ID:300027341

Scrapbook with newspaper cuttings and photographs: Gaster Samaritan MS Add 15

codex | compilation manuscript

• Format: Codex

• Format: Compilation manuscript

• Material(s): Paper

<objectDesc form="codex">
 <objectType source="#aat" key="300403970">compilation manuscript</objectType>
 <supportDesc material="paper">
  <support>
   <material source="#aat" key="300014109">Paper</material>
  </support>
<!-- additional metadata -->
 </supportDesc>
</objectDesc>

GETTY (AAT) | compilation manuscripts | ID:300403970

The Pupil: SIM/1

codex | Composite volume

• Format: Codex

• Format: Sammelbände (composite volume)

• Material(s): Paper

<objectDesc form="codex">
 <objectType source="#aat" key="300391488">Sammelbände (composite volume)</objectType>
 <supportDesc material="paper">
  <support>
   <material source="#aat" key="300014109">Paper</material>
  </support>
<!-- additional metadata -->
 </supportDesc>
</objectDesc>

GETTY (AAT) | compilation manuscripts | ID:300391488

codex | photograph album

• Format: Codex

• Type: Photograph album

• Material(s): Paper

<objectDesc form="codex">
 <objectType source="#aat" key="300026695">photograph album</objectType>
 <supportDesc material="paper">
  <support>
   <material source="#aat" key="300014109">Paper</material>
  </support>
<!-- additional metadata -->
 </supportDesc>
</objectDesc>

GETTY (AAT) | photograph albums | ID:300026695

Album 'commenced Nov. 26th 1857': JA/33/1/1

When individual sheets are mounted in a codex, such as a photograph album, they retain the physical form of sheets. If each item is described as a distinct unit using <msPart>, use <objectDesc form="sheet"> within each <msPart>. The codex structure applies only to the container volume, not to the mounted components.

codex | sheet — photograph album | photographic prints

• Format: Sheet

• Type: Photographic print

• Material(s): Paper

<objectDesc form="sheet">
 <objectType source="#aat" key="300127104">photographic print</objectType>
 <supportDesc material="paper">
  <support>
   <material source="#aat" key="300014109">Paper</material>
  </support>
<!-- additional metadata -->
 </supportDesc>
