</objectDesc>

GETTY (AAT) | photographic prints | ID:300127104

Album 'commenced Nov. 26th 1857': JA/33/1/1

sheet | albumen print

• Format: Sheet

• Type: Albumen print

• Material(s): Albumen paper

<objectDesc form="sheet">
 <objectType source="#aat" key="300127121">albumen print</objectType>
 <supportDesc material="paper">
  <support>
   <material type="paper" subtype="albumen" source="#aat" key="300411855">Albumen paper</material>
  </support>
<!-- additional metadata -->
 </supportDesc>
</objectDesc>

GETTY (AAT) | albumen print | ID:300127121

sheet | glass plate negative

• Format: Sheet

• Type: Glas plate negative

• Material(s): Glass

<objectDesc form="sheet">
 <objectType source="#aat" key="300393160">glass plate negative</objectType>
 <supportDesc material="glass">
  <support>
   <material source="#aat" key="300010797">Glass</material>
  </support>
<!-- additional metadata -->
 </supportDesc>
</objectDesc>

GETTY (AAT) | glass plate negatives | ID:300393160

object | 3D photographic formats

• Format: Object

• Type: Jewellery; Cased photograph

• Material(s): Enamel

<objectDesc form="object">
 <objectType source="#aat" key="300209286">jewellery</objectType>
 <objectType source="#aat" key="300127264">cased photograph</objectType>
 <supportDesc material="enamel">
  <support>
   <material source="#aat" key="300178264">Enamel</material>
  </support>
<!-- additional metadata -->
 </supportDesc>
</objectDesc>

GETTY (AAT) | jewellery | ID:300209286

GETTY (AAT) | cased photographs | ID:300127264

Photo Jewellery: Portrait of a mother and daughter: VPH.35

fragment | amulet

• Format: Fragment

• Type: Amulet

• Material(s): Parchment

<objectDesc form="fragment">
 <objectType source="#aat" key="300266585">amulet</objectType>
 <supportDesc material="parchment">
  <support>
   <material source="#aat" key="300011851">Parchment</material>
  </support>
<!-- additional metadata -->
 </supportDesc>
</objectDesc>

GETTY (AAT) | amulets | ID:300266585

Amulet for protection and success (fragment): Gaster Amulet 104

Object type = amulet → functional role: protective talisman.

Amulet = Protective object worn on the person (may also attract good fortune).

Talisman = Protective/healing object, not necessarily worn, often inscribed with magical or astrological symbols.

leaf | single manuscript leaf

• Format: Leaf

• Type: Manuscript

• Material(s): Parchment

<objectDesc form="leaf">
 <objectType source="#aat" key="300028569">manuscript</objectType>
 <supportDesc material="parchment">
  <support>
   <material source="#aat" key="300011851">Parchment</material>
  </support>
<!-- additional metadata -->
 </supportDesc>
</objectDesc>

GETTY (AAT) | leaves (gathered matter) | ID:300115833

Missing leaf | Psalm 118:71–89: Latin MS 22 A

Object type = manuscript → the document type the leaf is part of.

ostrakon | inscribed pottery fragment

• Format: Ostracon

• Material(s): Ceramic

<objectDesc form="ostracon">
 <supportDesc material="ceramic">
  <support>
   <material source="#aat" key="300235507">Ceramic</material>
  </support>
<!-- additional metadata -->
 </supportDesc>
</objectDesc>

GETTY (AAT) | ostraka | ID:300185396

Object type = letter | receipt | inscription → semantic type depending on content.

scroll | rolled manuscript

• Format: Roll

• Type: Megillat Esther

• Material(s): Parchment

<objectDesc form="roll">
 <objectType source="#aat" key="300028629">scroll</objectType>
 <objectType>Megillat Esther</objectType>
 <supportDesc material="parchment">
  <support>
   <material source="#aat" key="300011851">Parchment</material>
  </support>
<!-- additional metadata -->
 </supportDesc>
</objectDesc>

GETTY (AAT) | scrolls (information artifacts) | ID:300028629

Esther scroll: Hebrew MS 22

Object type = Megillat Esther → functional role: Jewish liturgical manuscript.

Manuscript → Generic handwritten scroll (content not otherwise specified). Other examples of liturgical scrolls include Torah scrolls (Jewish scripture used in worship), Sutra scrolls (Buddhist scripture) and other ceremonial scripture scrolls. Other examples for scrolls more broadly include charter, decree, map, genealogy, plan, poem, music rolls depending on the scroll’s content and purpose.

sheet | map

• Format: Sheet

• Type: Map

• Material(s): Paper

<objectDesc form="sheet">
 <objectType source="#aat" key="300028094">map</objectType>
 <supportDesc material="paper">
  <support>
   <material source="#aat" key="300014109">Paper</material>
  </support>
<!-- additional metadata -->
 </supportDesc>
</objectDesc>

GETTY (AAT) | maps (documents) | ID:300028094

The drink map of Manchester: C17:70 Manchester (3)

Object type = map → functional category.

Other examples of documents on sheets include chart, plan, diagram, poster, print, broadside and letter.

sheet | lithograph

• Format: Sheet

• Type: Lithographic engraving (print)

• Material(s): Paper

<objectDesc form="sheet">
 <objectType source="#aat" key="300254773">lithographic engraving (print)</objectType>
 <supportDesc material="paper">
  <support>
   <material source="#aat" key="300014109">Paper</material>
  </support>
<!-- additional metadata -->
 </supportDesc>
</objectDesc>

GETTY (AAT) | lithographic engravings (prints) | ID:300254773

Object type = lithographic engraving (print) → functional category.

tablet | cuneiform tablet

• Format: Tablet

• Type: Writing tablet

• Material(s): Clay

<objectDesc form="tablet">
 <objectType source="#aat" key="300262572">writing tablet</objectType>
 <supportDesc material="clay">
  <material source="#aat" key="300010439">clay</material>
<!-- additional metadata -->
 </supportDesc>
</objectDesc>

GETTY (AAT) | writing tablets (information artifacts) | ID:300262572

Object type = writing tablet → functional category.

1.37 <supportDesc> | support description

<supportDesc> (support description) groups elements describing the physical support for the written part of a manuscript or other object.

<supportDesc> within <objectDesc> should appear immediately after <objectType>, if present, or otherwise directly after its start tag. It is used to describe the material that forms the physical support of an item.

<supportDesc> should include material to record the principal support material of the item.

@material

material (material) a short project-defined name for the material composing the majority of the support.

A corresponding description of the material should also be encoded using <material> within <support>. The value recorded in material may be repeated there and, where appropriate, further refined or described in greater detail.

1.38 <support> | support

<support> (support) contains a description of the materials etc. which make up the physical support for the written part of a manuscript or other object.

<support> within <supportDesc> should be used to provide additional descriptive detail about the physical support. This may include not just the type of material, but also its appearance, condition, method of preparation, or other notable features.

If the content within <support> consists of more than one sentence or requires a fuller narrative description, use one or more <p> to contain the text.

The support material should be specified using <material>.

1.39 <material> | material

<material> (material) contains a word or phrase describing the material of which the object being described is composed.

MDC display label — Material(s)

Although <material> is a phrase-level element and could theoretically be used wherever a material is referenced, such as within <binding> and <seal>, its use is restricted to <support>.

In this context, <material> provides the textual form of the controlled value used in material. Both material and <material> should use standardised vocabulary wherever possible.

The material of the physical support should be encoded using <material>. When applicable, it must include both source and key.

@source @key

source specifies the source from which some aspect of this element is drawn.

key provides a persistent, unique identifier for the named entity, supplied by that source.

source and key should together provide a unique identifier for the material from a recognised authority file. The preferred source is the Getty Art & Architecture Thesaurus (AAT).

aat

Getty Art & Architecture Thesaurus® (AAT)

The Getty Art & Architecture Thesaurus® (AAT) is a structured vocabulary, including terms, descriptions, and other metadata for generic concepts related to art, architecture, conservation, archaeology, and other cultural heritage. Included are work types, styles, materials, techniques, and others.

<taxonomy xml:id="aat">
 <bibl>
  <ref target="https://www.getty.edu/research/tools/vocabularies/aat">Getty Art &amp; Architecture Thesaurus® (AAT)</ref>
 </bibl>
</taxonomy>

When the value #aat is used for @source, it indicates that the identifier recorded in key is drawn from this authority file. The prefix # indicates a reference to an identifier defined elsewhere in <msDesc>. In this case, it refers to the <taxonomy> declared in the header with xml:id=aat. This value can be selected from a controlled dropdown list when entering the attribute and does not need to be typed manually.

<material> may include type, and subtype may also be used where further sub-classification is required.

type | subtype

type provides the primary classification of the material.

subtype provides a further sub-classification of that material.

The following values should be used for material and <material>.

material

<material> | type | subtype

bamboo

bamboo

<material source="#aat" key="300011873">bamboo</material>

GETTY (AAT) | bamboo (material) | ID:300011873

bark

birch bark

<material type="bark" subtype="birch" source="#aat" key="300379355">birch bark</material>

GETTY (AAT) | birch bark (plant material) | ID:300379355

bone

bone

<material source="#aat" key="300011798">bone</material>

GETTY (AAT) | bone (material) | ID:300011798

ceramic

ceramic

faience

<material source="#aat" key="300235507">ceramic</material>

GETTY (AAT) | ceramic (material) | ID:300235507

<material type="ceramic" subtype="faience" source="#aat" key="300010668">faience </material>

GETTY (AAT) | faience (earthenware) | ID:300010668

clay

clay

<material source="#aat" key="300010439">clay</material>

GETTY (AAT) | clay | ID:300010439

enamel

enamel

<material source="#aat" key="300178264">enamel</material>

GETTY (AAT) | enamel (visual works) | ID:300178264

faience

faience

<material source="#aat" key="300265183">faience</material>

GETTY (AAT) | faience (composite material) | ID:300265183

glass

glass

<material source="#aat" key="300010797">glass</material>

GETTY (AAT) | glass (material) | ID:300010797

tusk

ivory

<material source="#aat" key="300011856">tusk</material>

GETTY (AAT) | tusk (material) | ID:300011856

leather

leather

<material source="#aat" key="300011845">leather</material>

GETTY (AAT) | leather (processed animal material ...) | ID:300011845

metal

metal

brass

bronze

copper alloy

copper

gold

silver

steel

<material source="#aat" key="300010900">metal</material>

GETTY (AAT) | metal | ID:300010900

<material type="metal" subtype="brass" source="#aat" key="300010946">brass</material>

GETTY (AAT) | brass (alloy) | ID:300010946

<material type="metal" subtype="bronze" source="#aat" key="300010957">bronze</material>

GETTY (AAT) | bronze (metal) | ID:300010957

<material type="metal" subtype="copperAlloy" source="#aat" key="300010942">copper alloy</material>

GETTY (AAT) | copper alloy | ID:300010942

<material type="metal" subtype="copper" source="#aat" key="300011020">copper</material>

GETTY (AAT) | copper (metal) | ID:300011020

<material type="metal" subtype="gold" source="#aat" key="300011021">gold</material>

GETTY (AAT) | gold (metal) | ID:300011021

<material type="metal" subtype="silver" source="#aat" key="300011029">silver</material>

GETTY (AAT) | silver (metal) | ID:300011029

<material type="metal" subtype="steel" source="#aat" key="300133751">steel</material>

GETTY (AAT) | steel (alloy) | ID:300133751

mineral

mineral

alum

<material source="#aat" key="300011068">mineral</material>

GETTY (AAT) | mineral | ID:300011068

<material type="mineral" subtype="alum" source="#aat" key="300183621">alum</material>

GETTY (AAT) | alum | ID:300183621

palmLeaf

palm leaf

palm leaf (Corypha umbraculifera)

<material source="#aat" key="300011894">palm leaf</material>

GETTY (AAT) | palm leaf (material) | ID:300011894

<material type="palmLeaf" subtype="corypha" source="#aat" key="300375531">palm leaf (Corypha umbraculifera)</material>

GETTY (AAT) | Corypha umbraculifera (species) | ID:300375531

paper

paper

cardboard

mulberry paper

Nepali paper

photographic paper

rag paper

<material source="#aat" key="300014109">paper</material>

GETTY (AAT) | paper (fibre product) | ID:300014109

<material type="paper" subtype="cardboard" source="#aat" key="300014224">cardboard</material>

GETTY (AAT) | cardboard (pasteboard (paper) | ID:300014224

<material type="paper" subtype="nepali">Nepali (Lokta) paper</material>

GETTY (AAT) | n/a

<material type="paper" subtype="mulberry" source="#aat" key="300183846">mulberry paper</material>

GETTY (AAT) | mulberry paper (Japanese paper (handmade paper) | ID:300183846

<material type="paper" subtype="photographic" source="#aat" key="300014190">photographic paper</material>

GETTY (AAT) | photographic paper | ID:300014190

<material type="paper" subtype="rag" source="#aat" key="300014130">rag paper</material>

GETTY (AAT) | rag paper | ID:300014130

papyrus

papyrus

<material source="#aat" key="300055047">papyrus</material>

GETTY (AAT) | papyrus (fibre product) | ID:300014127

parchment

parchment

<material source="#aat" key="300011851">parchment</material>

GETTY (AAT) | parchment (animal material) | ID:300011851

plaster

plaster

<material source="#aat" key="300014922">plaster</material>

GETTY (AAT) | plaster (composite coating) | ID:300014922

shell

shell

tortoise shell

<material source="#aat" key="300014922">shell</material>

GETTY (AAT) | shell (animal material) | ID:300011829

<material type="shell" subtype="tortoise" source="#aat" key="300011837">tortoise shell</material>

GETTY (AAT) | tortoise shell | ID:300011837

stone

stone

limestone

marble

gemstone

emerald

ruby

<material source="#aat" key="300011176">stone</material>

GETTY (AAT) | stone (worked rock) | ID:300011176

<material type="stone" subtype="limestone" source="#aat" key="300011286">limestone</material>

GETTY (AAT) | limestone | ID:300011286

<material type="stone" subtype="marble" source="#aat" key="300011443">marble</material>

GETTY (AAT) | marble (rock) | ID:300011443

<material type="stone" subtype="gemstone" source="#aat" key="300201964">gemstone</material>

GETTY (AAT) | gemstone | ID:300201964

<material type="stone" subtype="emerald" source="#aat" key="300011074">emerald</material>

GETTY (AAT) | emerald (mineral) | ID:300011074

<material type="stone" subtype="ruby" source="#aat" key="300011082">ruby</material>

GETTY (AAT) | ruby (mineral) | ID:300011082

textile

textile

canvas

cotton

fibre

silk

<material source="#aat" key="300231565">textile</material>

GETTY (AAT) | textile materials | ID:300231565

<material type="textile" subtype="canvas" source="#aat" key="300014078">canvas</material>

GETTY (AAT) | canvas (textile material) | ID:300014078

<material type="textile" subtype="cotton" source="#aat" key="300014067">cotton</material>

GETTY (AAT) | cotton (textile) | ID:300014067

<material type="textile" subtype="fibre" source="#aat" key="300014024">fibre</material>

GETTY (AAT) | fibre | ID:300014024

<material type="textile" subtype="silk" source="#aat" key="300243428">silk</material>

GETTY (AAT) | silk (textile) | ID:300243428

wood

wood

<material source="#aat" key="300011914">wood</material>

GETTY (AAT) | wood (plant) | ID:300011914

mixed

Multiple <material> with appropriate @type | @subtype values, where applicable (see below).

unknown

N/A | no specific material type identified.

The textual value within <material> should be capitalised according to its position in the sentence: use uppercase if it appears at the start of a sentence and lowercase if it appears mid‑sentence. Do not include a full stop within <material>. Where a full stop is required in prose, place it after the closing <material>. When <material> is encoded as a standalone term, whether simple or compound, do not include a full stop.

References to materials outside <support>, for example in <binding>, <decoNote>, <seal> or other descriptive contexts, should be encoded using <term type="material">.

material on <supportDesc> is constrained by a controlled vocabulary defined in the customised TEI schema and extended through the ODD. Permitted values, such as paper and parchment, appear in the XML editor as a drop-down list. Only these values are valid. Any other value will result in a schema validation error.

Additional values may be introduced where necessary, provided the proposed value is discussed with the Digital Metadata Specialist and checked against the Getty Art & Architecture Thesaurus (AAT). This helps ensure that the new value does not conflict with an existing term or convey an unintended or overlapping meaning. Once approved, a request should be logged in GitHub so that the value can be added to the schema.

Multi-word | compound terms

In contexts where more granular or domain-specific classification is required, compound material terms, which are multi-word expressions referring to a single material, for example, palm leaf, may be encoded in camelCase in material, type, and subtype.

• Format: Codex

• Material(s): Palm leaf (Corypha umbraculifera)

<objectDesc form="codex">
 <supportDesc material="palmLeaf">
  <support>
   <material type="palmLeaf" subtype="corypha" source="#aat" key="300375531">palm leaf (Corypha umbraculifera)</material>
  </support>
<!-- additional metadata -->
 </supportDesc>
</objectDesc>

GETTY (AAT) | Corypha umbraculifera (species) | ID: 300375531

Compound material terms, for example, birch bark, are encoded by placing the broader category in type and the more specific term in subtype within <material>.

• Format: Codex

• Material(s): Birch bark

<objectDesc form="codex">
 <supportDesc material="bark">
  <support>
   <material type="bark" subtype="birch" source="#aat" key="300011851">birch bark</material>
  </support>
<!-- additional metadata -->
 </supportDesc>
</objectDesc>

GETTY (AAT) | birch bark (plant material) | ID: 300379355

Mixed | composite material terms

In contexts where a single item consists of multiple distinct materials, for example, metal, ivory or gemstone that cannot be adequately captured with a single material value, use material=mixed and encode each material component separately using multiple <material>. Where applicable, add type and subtype to clarify each component.

treasure binding | metal; ivory; gemstone

• Format: Object

• Type: Treasure binding

• Material(s): Metal, ivory, and gemstone.

<objectDesc form="object">
 <objectType source="#aat" key="300417549">treasure binding</objectType>
 <supportDesc material="mixed">
  <support>
   <p>
    <material source="#aat" key="300010900">Metal</material>, <material source="#aat" key="300011857">ivory</material>, and <material type="stone" subtype="gemstone" source="#aat" key="300201964">gemstone</material>.</p>
  </support>
<!-- additional metadata -->
 </supportDesc>
</objectDesc>

GETTY (AAT) | treasure bindings | ID:300417549

GETTY (AAT) | metal | ID:300010900

GETTY (AAT) | ivory (material) | ID:300011857

GETTY (AAT) | gemstone | ID:300201964

Avoid encoding multiple separate materials within a single <material>. Use multiple <material> if needed.

Both material and <material> should use a term from a controlled vocabulary.

Descriptive modifiers, such as fine-quality parchment or heavily foxed paper, should not be included within <material>. Instead, such elaboration should be expressed in the broader <support>. For example:

<supportDesc material="paper">
 <support>
  <p>Fine quality <material source="#aat" key="300014109">paper</material>.</p>
 </support>
<!-- additional metadata -->
</supportDesc>

Do not use chart. For all kinds of paper-writing materials, use material=paper.

Do not use vellum, perg, part, or parch. For all kinds of animal-skin writing materials, use material=parchment.

For all photographic prints, use material=paper and <material type="paper" subtype="photographic" use a more specific term, such as albumen paper, only if the paper type is known with certainty <material type="paper" subtype="albumen">.

When the material of an item or component cannot be determined with confidence, use <supportDesc material="unknown">. This ensures that uncertainty is explicitly recorded, rather than leaving the material description blank or omitting it entirely. It is appropriate where no identification is possible from the item itself, associated documentation, or visual analysis, where further research may or may not resolve the uncertainty, and where it is important to assert that the material is unknown rather than simply unspecified.

1.40 <watermark> | watermark

<watermark> (watermark) contains a word or phrase describing a watermark or similar device.

<watermark> is a phrase-level element, typically used within <support>, to describe watermarks in manuscripts, early printed books, and other paper-based materials.

<term> with type=motif should be used within <watermark> to semantically encode the principal motif or design.

Only one <term type="motif"> should be used per <watermark> unless the watermark clearly features more than one distinct motif.

Additional visual or contextual details, such as framing elements, decorative features, and orientation, should be described as free text within <watermark> after <term>.

<support>
 <p>
  <material source="#aat" key="300014109">Paper</material> with watermark on <locus from="d3r" to="d3r">d3r</locus>: <watermark>
   <term type="motif">Anchor</term> in a circle, surmounted by a star</watermark>
 </p>.

</support>

The textual value within <term type="motif"> must always be capitalised, regardless of its position in the sentence.

When possible, <ref> should be used to cite or link to an external resource that supports or verifies the identification of a watermark. When encoding watermark descriptions, <ref> is typically used to reference entries in recognised watermark catalogues such as Briquet, Piccard, Gravell, or databases like The Memory of Paper. target should provide a resolvable link (URL or internal ID) that points to the cited source.

<support>
 <p>
  <material source="#aat" key="300014109">Paper</material> with watermark on <locus from="d3r" to="d3r">d3r</locus>: <watermark>
   <term type="motif">Anchor</term> in a circle, surmounted by a star</watermark> (<ref target="https://memoryofpaper.eu/briquet/?refnr=478">Briquet 478</ref>).</p>
</support>

Multiple references can be included where a motif corresponds to more than one catalogue entry.

The following section outlines four structured approaches to encoding watermarks in MDC using <watermark>, ranging from a single identified watermark to more complex cases involving multiple watermarks or advanced imaging.

Single watermark

A single watermark with a reference to a recognised watermark catalogue.

• Format: Codex

• Type: Book

• Material(s):

Paper with watermark on d3r: Anchor in a circle, surmounted by a star (Briquet 478).

<objectDesc form="codex">
 <objectType source="#aat" key="300028051">book</objectType>
 <supportDesc material="paper">
  <support>
   <p>
    <material source="#aat" key=" aat:300014109">Paper</material> with watermark on <locus from="d3r" to="d3r">d3r</locus>: <watermark>
     <term type="motif">Anchor</term> in a circle, surmounted by a star</watermark> (<ref target="https://memoryofpaper.eu/briquet/?refnr=478">Briquet 478</ref>).</p>
  </support>
<!-- additional metadata -->
 </supportDesc>
</objectDesc>

Where identification is unavailable, the core motif may still be encoded using <term type="motif"> without a catalogue reference. This maintains consistency across files.

Multiple watermarks

When multiple watermarks occur in different parts of an item, use <list> containing one or more <item>, with each <item> representing a single watermark. Each <item> should include <locus> identifying the location of the watermark, <watermark> containing a motif identified using <term type="motif">, and, where available, <ref> citing a relevant catalogue entry or authority file.

• Format: Codex

• Type: Book

• Material(s):

Paper with watermarks.

Watermarks

10r Ox, plain or Bull, with three-pointed tail; with turned skull in en-face view (Briquet 2785).

100r Anchor in a circle, surmounted by a star (Briquet 478).

<objectDesc form="codex"> <objectType source="#aat" key="300028051">book</objectType> <supportDesc material="paper"> <support> <p><material source="#aat" key="300014109">Paper</material> with watermarks.</p> <list> <head>Watermarks</head> <item> <locus from="10r" to="10r">10r</locus> <watermark><term>Ox</term>, plain or <term>Bull</term>, with three-pointed tail; with turned skull in en-face view</watermark> (<ref target="https://memoryofpaper.eu/briquet/?refnr=2785">Briquet 2785</ref>). </item> <item> <locus from="100r" to="100r">100r</locus> <watermark><term type="motif">Anchor</term> in a circle, surmounted by a star</watermark> (<ref target="https://memoryofpaper.eu/briquet/?refnr=478">Briquet 478</ref>). <item> </list> </support> <!-- additional metadata --> </supportDesc> </objectDesc>

This approach allows each watermark to be described separately and associated with its own location and catalogue reference.

Watermarks | advanced imaging

Watermarks are inherently partially or entirely invisible in standard digital images because they are embedded within the structure of the paper rather than applied to its surface. Advanced imaging techniques, such as transmitted light, raking light, or multispectral imaging, may reveal details that are not otherwise visible. Where such images are available, additional <locus> may be used to reference the corresponding image identifier recorded in the manifest.

• Format: Codex

• Type: Book

• Material(s):

Paper with a watermark revealed on 49r via transmitted light capture: Anchor in a circle, surmounted by a star (Briquet 478).

<objectDesc form="codex">
 <objectType source="#aat" key="300028051">book</objectType>
 <supportDesc material="paper">
  <support>
   <p>
    <material source="#aat" key="300014109">Paper</material> with a watermark revealed on <locus from="49r-anchor-tl" to="49r-anchor-tl">49r</locus> via transmitted light capture: <watermark>
     <term type="motif">Anchor</term> in a circle, surmounted by a star</watermark> (<ref target="https://memoryofpaper.eu/briquet/?refnr=478">Briquet 478</ref>).</p>
  </support>
<!-- additional metadata -->
 </supportDesc>
</objectDesc>

The <locus> value 49r-anchor-tl refers to a specific image identifier recorded in the manifest’s page reference column (typically Column B), corresponding to 49r and showing the watermark under transmitted light.

from and to contain this internal image identifier, 49r-anchor-tl, which uniquely identifies the appropriate imaging variant. The textual content of the <locus>, 49r, records the leaf on which the watermark occurs.

The inclusion of the term anchor in the image identifier helps identify the watermark motif and distinguish between multiple watermark images within the same manuscript, particularly where more than one watermark occurs on a single leaf.

Where more than one watermark of the same motif occurs on a single leaf, an additional distinguishing component, such as 1 or 2, may be added to the identifier, for example, 49r-anchor-1-tl and 49r-anchor-2-tl.

The use of consistent suffixes in <locus> identifiers is essential for distinguishing imaging modes and maintaining reliable links between TEI metadata and image files. These suffixes should reflect the imaging technique used to capture a particular variant of a folio or leaf. Recommended suffixes include:

-tl for transmitted light

-uv for ultraviolet illumination

-rti for reflectance transformation imaging

-msi for multispectral imaging

The above examples offer practical and widely applicable encoding patterns. This is the most straightforward way to encode watermarks. It offers a simple and practical solution. However, depending on a project’s requirements or the level of detail needed, more advanced encoding is possible. The TEI P5 Customization and Encoding Guidelines for Manuscript Description, specifically Section 1.6.4.2.1.1.1.1 Watermarks, outline richer, more structured encoding approaches. While these support detailed representation of features such as motif, position, and dimensions, they involve customised elements and attributes, and may require further definition, refinement, and formalisation.

Databases and catalogues for watermarks

Several online platforms provide access to structured and richly described collections of watermarks, supporting research in the history of paper, manuscript studies, book history, and related disciplines.

WZMA Online Databases (ÖAW

The Wasserzeichen des Mittelalters (WZMA) project, hosted by the Österreichische Akademie der Wissenschaften (Austrian Academy of Sciences), offers detailed online catalogues of watermarks found in medieval manuscripts.

WZIS Wasserzeichen-Informationssystem

The Wasserzeichen-Informationssystem (WZIS) is a collaborative and decentralised platform for the systematic documentation of watermarks. Developed with participation from the Austrian Academy of Sciences and German institutions, it enables cross-institutional contributions and harmonised data structures.

Online Portal Bernstein: The Memory of Paper

The Bernstein — The Memory of Paper portal serves as a meta-platform, integrating access to a wide range of watermark databases, including both WZMA and WZIS.

1.41 <extent> | extent

<extent> (extent) describes the approximate size of a text stored on some carrier medium or of some other object, digital or non-digital, specified in any convenient units.

MDC display label — Extent

<extent> within <supportDesc> is used to record both the quantity of an item and, where appropriate, the size of its components.

In manuscript description, traditional codicological practice distinguishes between the core leaves of the volume and any endleaves | flyleaves at the front and back, when identifiable. These may be further differentiated according to whether they are original to the manuscript or later additions. Lower-case Roman numerals are used for original endleaves, and Arabic numerals are used for added endleaves and for the core leaves.

Based on this tradition, various codicological formulas, such as 254 leaves (ii+254+i) | ii+254+i | ii+254+i leaves | 254 folios (ii+254+i) | ff. 257 (ii+254+i) | 254 folios (ii+ 254+i) | 254 folios | etc., have been used to encode the total extent and structural composition of a codex, typically distinguishing between the main text block and any original or later endleaves.

However, best practice, particularly when retroconverting from catalogues or where detailed structural information is unavailable, is to record only the total number of leaves. A formula such as ii + 254 + i should be simplified to 257 leaves.

<extent> must always record the total number of leaves present in the codex as it physically survives, regardless of whether the endleaves are original or later additions. The total should reflect the physical extent of the complete item.

When a detailed <collation> is supplied, it provides the full codicological breakdown, including the identification of original or modern endleaves. In this case, <extent> may simply state the total number of leaves without repeating the breakdown: <extent>257 leaves</extent>.

When a detailed <collation> is unavailable, <extent> may include a simplified breakdown in brackets to show the probable or observed structure of the volume: <extent>257 leaves (ii + 254 + i)</extent>.

In all cases, leaves should be used in preference to folios or folia unless specifically describing a foliation. For printed books, extent is conventionally expressed in leaves rather than pages. Consistency in terminology across files is important.

Even in simple cases, machine-readable encoding should be considered the minimum standard. This means using <measure> with quantity and unit.

1.42 <measure> | measure

<measure> (measure) contains a word or phrase referring to some quantity of an object or commodity, usually comprising a number, a unit, and a commodity name.

<measure> is a phrase-level element, and within <extent>, it is used to encode quantifiable information such as leaves, sheets, membranes, or other countable units.

@quantity | @unit

quantity (quantity) specifies the number of the specified units that comprise the measurement.

unit (unit) specifies the unit of measurement.

In legacy encoding practice, type was sometimes used in place of unit, for example, type=leaf. Similarly, <num> with value was sometimes used to normalise Roman numerals where catalogues did not distinguish endleaves from the main textblock. Both practices may still be encountered in older files but are not recommended for current practice.

The correct approach is to use <measure> with quantity and unit, allowing the numerical value and its unit of measurement to be encoded explicitly.

Level 1 | total + "formula" | <collation> is absent

Use when no <collation> is available and only a plain statement is required. The total is encoded using <measure>, and the structural formula is retained in brackets.

• Format: Codex

• Type: Manuscript

• Material(s): Parchment

• Extent: 254 leaves (ii+254+i)

<objectDesc form="codex">
 <objectType source="#aat" key="300028569">manuscript</objectType>
 <supportDesc material="parchment">
  <support>
   <material source="#aat" key="300011851">Parchment</material>
  </support>
  <extent>
   <measure quantity="257" unit="leaf">257 leaves (ii+254+i)</measure>
  </extent>
<!-- additional metadata -->
 </supportDesc>
</objectDesc>

Level 2 | total only | <collation> is present

Used when <collation> is recorded separately and need not be repeated, or when structural detail is not available in the source record, as is often the case with MARC-derived metadata. The total number of leaves is encoded using <measure> only.

• Format: Codex

• Type: Book

• Material(s): Paper

• Extent: 244 leaves

<objectDesc form="codex">
 <objectType source="#aat" key="300028051">book</objectType>
 <supportDesc material="paper">
  <support>
   <material source="#aat" key="300014109">Paper</material>
  </support>
  <extent>
   <measure quantity="244" unit="leaf">244 leaves</measure>
  </extent>
<!-- additional metadata -->
 </supportDesc>
</objectDesc>

Level 3 | best practice

<extent> records both the primary physical unit being described and, where relevant, the quantity of any significant constituent components. Multiple <measure> may therefore be used within a single <extent> to record different aspects of quantity.

The first <measure> should normally record the principal physical unit of description, such as a volume, roll, or sheet. Additional <measure> may be used to record countable constituent components, such as leaves, membranes, items, or other discrete units that contribute to the physical extent of the resource.

Examples include a codex containing multiple leaves, a roll comprising multiple membranes, a photograph album containing numerous mounted items, or an archival unit containing multiple discrete documents.

For a single, standalone item, always use a self-closing <measure>. This avoids redundant display text, such as 1 codex or 1 volume, as the physical format is already declared via form on <objectDesc> and may be further refined using <objectType>. The self-closing <measure> captures structured metadata via quantity and unit, while keeping the output concise and non-repetitive. For example, <measure quantity="1" unit="roll"/> unambiguously encodes a single scroll without requiring a textual value.

Self-closing <measure>:

<measure quantity="1" unit="volume"/>

For codices of all kinds, including bound manuscripts, printed books, etc.

<measure quantity="1" unit="leaf"/>

For a single, detached leaf from a codex or bound volume.

<measure quantity="1" unit="roll"/>

For scrolls or continuous paper, or parchment rolls.

<measure quantity="1" unit="sheet"/>

For flat, flexible paper items, including unbound printed sheets or manuscript folios, and photographic formats.

<measure quantity="1" unit="object"/>

For standalone 3D or non-documentary artefacts such as seals, tools, textiles, stereographs, or mounted photographs.

Use quantity=1 to indicate that a single complete item is being described. The value of unit should reflect the physical structure of the item. For example, use volume for codices. Avoid deriving unit from <objectType>, as that element refers to the item's semantic category or intellectual function, not its countable physical form.

codex | known leaf count

• Format: Codex

• Type: Manuscript

• Material(s): Paper

• Extent: 244 leaves

<objectDesc form="codex">
 <objectType source="#aat" key="300028569">manuscript</objectType>
 <supportDesc material="paper">
  <support>
   <material source="#aat" key="300014109">Paper</material>
  </support>
  <extent>
   <measure quantity="1" unit="volume"/>
   <measure quantity="244" unit="leaf">244 leaves</measure>
  </extent>
<!-- additional metadata -->
 </supportDesc>
</objectDesc>

roll | known membrane count

• Format: Roll

• Type: Scroll

• Material(s): Parchment

• Extent: 12 membranes

<objectDesc form="roll">
 <objectType source="#aat" key="300028629">Scroll</objectType>
 <supportDesc material="parchment">
  <support>
   <material source="#aat" key="300011851">Parchment</material>
  </support>
  <extent>
   <measure quantity="1" unit="roll"/>
   <measure quantity="12" unit="membrane">12 membranes</measure>
  </extent>
<!-- additional metadata -->
 </supportDesc>
</objectDesc>

In addition to recording physical components such as leaves or membranes, <measure> may also be used to encode the number of contained items using unit=item. This is particularly useful for photograph albums, scrapbooks, archival collections, and other compound resources containing multiple distinct items.

Containers used solely for storage or housing should not normally be recorded in <extent>. Where an original, contemporary, or historically significant container forms part of the resource being described, it may be encoded using an additional <measure> with an appropriate unit value, such as folder.

codex | scrapbook with known item count

• Format: Codex

• Type: Scrapbook

• Material(s): Paper

• Extent: 274 items

<objectDesc form="codex">
 <objectType source="#aat" key="300027341">scrapbook</objectType>
 <supportDesc material="paper">
  <support>
   <material source="#aat" key="300014109">Paper</material>
  </support>
  <extent>
   <measure quantity="1" unit="volume"/>
   <measure quantity="274" unit="item">274 items</measure>
  </extent>
<!-- additional metadata -->
 </supportDesc>
</objectDesc>

archival collection | loose material with known item count

• Format: Sheet

• Type: Archive (grouping)

• Material(s): Paper

• Extent: 50 items

<objectDesc form="sheet">
 <objectType source="#aat" key="300375748">archive (grouping)</objectType>
 <supportDesc material="paper">
  <support>
   <material source="#aat" key="300014109">Paper</material>
  </support>
  <extent>
   <measure quantity="50" unit="item">50 items</measure>
  </extent>
<!-- additional metadata -->
 </supportDesc>
</objectDesc>

archival collection | loose material in original folder with known item count

• Format: Sheet

• Type: Archive (grouping)

• Material(s): Paper

• Extent: 50 items

<objectDesc form="codex">
 <objectType source="#aat" key="300375748">archive (grouping)</objectType>
 <supportDesc material="paper">
  <support>
   <material source="#aat" key="300014109">Paper</material>
  </support>
  <extent>
   <measure quantity="1" unit="folder"/>
   <measure quantity="50" unit="item">50 items</measure>
  </extent>
<!-- additional metadata -->
 </supportDesc>
</objectDesc>

Where the size of leaves (or equivalent unit) is known, it should be encoded using <dimensions>.

1.43 <dimensions> | dimensions

<dimensions> (dimensions) contains a dimensional specification.

<dimensions> is a phrase-level element and may be used wherever dimensions are recorded. It may be embedded directly in <extent> or placed elsewhere, depending on what is being measured. Typical placements include <binding> for binding size, in <layout> for written or ruled space, and any element where dimensions are relevant.

In the context of <extent>, <dimensions> serves as a container for recording measurements of a specified component or aspect of the item. Attributes such as type and unit define the scope of the measurements and the units used, while child elements such as <height>, <width>, and <depth> contain the corresponding values.

@type | @unit

type identifies the aspect or component being measured

unit=mm specifies the unit of measurement in millimetres

The unit value must always be given in millimetres (mm) when encoding dimensions, whether cataloguing afresh or retroconverting legacy data, rather than replicating the system used in a source catalogue. The unit of measurement in millimetres (mm) should not be included in prose, as it is generated by the XSLT transformation.

type provides a common typology of the dimensions of a given item source. The following values should be used:

leaf for a single loose leaf from a codex, or more leaves bound in a codex

roll for a continuous scroll formed from multiple membranes joined together.

membrane for individual membranes that form the roll, i.e. scroll

sheet for loose, unbound flat items, such as maps, letters, broadsides, and photographic formats

envelope for a separate envelope used to house or protect the item.

mount for mount, backing board, or similar support to which an item (e.g. photographic print) is attached.

object for the overall dimensions of a complete artefact as a whole

Always include type on <dimensions>, even if only one set of dimensions is recorded. Wherever possible, select the most specific type value appropriate to the material or component described.

1.44 <height> | height | <width> | width

<height> (height) contains a measurement measured along the axis at a right angle to the bottom of the object.

<width> (width) contains a measurement of an object along the axis parallel to its bottom, e.g. perpendicular to the spine of a book or codex.

<height> and <width> give vertical and horizontal dimensions. <depth> may also be used to record thickness, though for codices this is often implied by the number of leaves in <extent>. Where conservation or technical documentation provides a depth measurement, such as the thickness of a binding, this may also be encoded, commonly within <binding>.

In legacy encoding practice, dimensions were sometimes encoded solely as plain textual content, for example <height>210</height> and <width>140</width>. In other cases, the same values were encoded using n, for example <height n="210">210</height> and <width n="140">140</width>. While this is valid TEI, since <height> and <width> may contain character data only, and the use of n is technically permitted, neither is recommended for recording dimensions. In both cases, the value is treated as unstructured text, with no explicit semantic indication that it represents a physical measurement. Best practice is to use quantity to encode the measurement values explicitly.

<dimensions type="leaf" unit="mm">
 <height quantity="210">210</height>
 <width quantity="140">140</width>
</dimensions>

The following examples demonstrate how to combine <measure> with <dimensions>, before moving on to cases involving uncertainty, approximate ranges, or partial measurements.

codex | uniform leaf size

• Format: Codex

• Type: Manuscript

• Material(s): Paper

• Extent: 244 leaves. Leaf height: 210 mm, width: 140 mm.

<objectDesc form="codex">
 <objectType source="#aat" key="300028569">manuscript</objectType>
 <supportDesc material="paper">
  <support>
   <material source="#aat" key="300014109">Paper</material>
  </support>
  <extent>
   <measure quantity="1" unit="volume"/>
   <measure quantity="244" unit="leaf">244 leaves</measure>.
  <dimensions type="leaf" unit="mm">
    <height quantity="210">210</height>
    <width quantity="140">140</width>
   </dimensions>
  </extent>
<!-- additional metadata -->
 </supportDesc>
</objectDesc>

A full stop followed by a space must be manually inserted immediately after the closing <measure> if it is followed by <dimensions>. The display of <dimensions>, whether used once or multiple times, is automatically formatted by the system. Capitalisation, such as Leaf height, and punctuation, such as the full stop after width followed by the measurement in millimetres (mm), are generated by the XSLT transformation.

single, loose leaf from a codex

• Format: Leaf

• Type: Manuscript

• Material(s): Parchment

• Extent: Leaf height: 262 mm, width: 170 mm.

<objectDesc form="codex">
 <objectType source="#aat" key="300028569">manuscript</objectType>
 <supportDesc material="parchment">
  <support>
   <material source="#aat" key="300011851">Parchment</material>
  </support>
  <extent>
   <measure quantity="1" unit="leaf"/>
   <dimensions type="leaf" unit="mm">
    <height quantity="262">262</height>
    <width quantity="170">170</width>
   </dimensions>
  </extent>
<!-- additional metadata -->
 </supportDesc>
</objectDesc>

Missing leaf | Psalm 118:71–89: Latin MS 22 A

Use a self-closing <measure> for a single roll when the number of membranes is known but their individual dimensions are not. Pair this with a second <measure> containing both quantity and a content value, such as 12 membranes, to support structured metadata and ensure clear display.

scroll made of multiple membranes

Case 1: membrane count known, dimensions unknown

• Format: Roll

• Type: Scroll

• Material(s): Parchment

• Extent: Roll height: 6401 mm, width: 851 mm.

<objectDesc form="roll">
 <objectType source="#aat" key="300028629">Scroll</objectType>
 <supportDesc material="parchment">
  <support>
   <material source="#aat" key="300011851">Parchment</material>
  </support>
  <extent>
   <measure quantity="1" unit="roll"/>
   <dimensions type="roll" unit="mm">
    <height quantity="6401">6401</height>
    <width quantity="851">851</width>
   </dimensions>
  </extent>
<!-- additional metadata -->
 </supportDesc>
</objectDesc>

Arbor Caritatis et Misercordiae: Latin MS 18

When dimensions for the roll and each membrane are available, encode these using separate <dimensions>, clearly specifying type=roll and type=membrane to distinguish the overall object from its components.

scroll made of multiple membranes

Case 2: membrane count and dimensions both known

• Format: Roll

• Type: Scroll

• Material(s): Parchment

• Extent: 12 membranes. Roll height: 6401 mm, width: 765 mm. Membrane height: 533 mm, width: 765 mm.

<objectDesc form="roll">
 <objectType source="#aat" key="300028629">Scroll</objectType>
 <supportDesc material="parchment">
  <support>
   <material source="#aat" key="300011851">Parchment</material>
  </support>
  <extent>
   <measure quantity="1" unit="roll"/>
   <measure quantity="12" unit="membrane">12 membranes</measure>.
  <dimensions type="roll" unit="mm">
    <height quantity="6401">6401</height>
    <width quantity="765">765</width>
   </dimensions>
   <dimensions type="membrane" unit="mm">
    <height quantity="533">533</height>
    <width quantity="765">765</width>
   </dimensions>
  </extent>
<!-- additional metadata -->
 </supportDesc>
</objectDesc>

Sheet size refers to the full physical dimensions of the paper or other support material on which the map is printed, drawn, or mounted. This includes all margins, blank areas, and borders. Map size refers to the extent of the cartographic content itself, excluding margins, borders, and legends, and should be measured to the outermost edges of the map area. Folded size refers to the physical dimensions of the item when folded for issue, handling, or storage.

map

• Format: Sheet

• Type: Map

• Material(s): Paper

• Extent: Sheet height: 765 mm, width: 607 mm.

<objectDesc form="sheet">
 <objectType source="#aat" key="300028094">map</objectType>
 <supportDesc material="paper">
  <support>
   <material source="#aat" key="300014109">Paper</material>
  </support>
  <extent>
   <measure quantity="1" unit="sheet"/>
   <dimensions type="sheet" unit="mm">
    <height quantity="765">765</height>
    <width quantity="607">607</width>
   </dimensions>
  </extent>
<!-- additional metadata -->
 </supportDesc>
</objectDesc>

When known, record the dimensions of the cartographic area and the paper support using separate <dimensions> within <extent>, using type=map and type=sheet respectively. This allows the size of the map itself to be distinguished from the size of the supporting sheet.

map | sheet size

• Format: Sheet

• Type: Map

• Material(s): Paper

• Extent: Map height: 600 mm, width: 440 mm. Sheet height: 710 mm, width: 580 mm.

<objectDesc form="sheet">
 <objectType source="#aat" key="300028094">map</objectType>
 <supportDesc material="paper">
  <support>
   <material source="#aat" key="300014109">Paper</material>
  </support>
  <extent>
   <measure quantity="1" unit="sheet"/>
   <dimensions type="map" unit="mm">
    <height quantity="600">600</height>
    <width quantity="440">440</width>
   </dimensions>
   <dimensions type="sheet" unit="mm">
    <height quantity="710">710</height>
    <width quantity="580">580</width>
   </dimensions>
  </extent>
<!-- additional metadata -->
 </supportDesc>
</objectDesc>

The Drink Map of Manchester: C17:70 Manchester (3)

If the dimensions of the item when folded are known, record them using a separate <dimensions> within <extent>, using @type="folded">. Use type=mount to record the dimensions of a mount supporting the item.

map | folded

• Format: Sheet

• Type: Map

• Material(s): Paper

• Extent: Sheet height: 353 mm, width: 467 mm. Folded height: 156 mm, width: 88 mm.

<objectDesc form="codex">
 <objectType source="#aat" key="300028094">map</objectType>
 <supportDesc material="paper">
  <support>
   <material source="#aat" key="300014109">Paper</material>
  </support>
  <extent>
   <measure quantity="1" unit="sheet"/>
   <dimensions type="sheet" unit="mm">
    <height quantity="353">353</height>
    <width quantity="467">467</width>
   </dimensions>
   <dimensions type="folded" unit="mm">
    <height quantity="156">156</height>
    <width quantity="88">88</width>
   </dimensions>
  </extent>
<!-- additional metadata -->
 </supportDesc>
</objectDesc>

Kaihō Kyō Ezu: Japanese 48

Correspondence letter sheet size refers to the full physical dimensions of the writing paper. Folded size refers to the dimensions of the letter when folded for handling, storage, or self-enclosure. Envelope size refers to the dimensions of a separate envelope, where present.

Use <extent> to record both the quantity and the dimensions of a sheet by including a <measure> for quantity and <dimensions> with type="sheet"> for size. If the dimensions of the item when folded are known, record them using a separate <dimensions> with type=folded. Structural or descriptive information, such as folded once to form two leaves, should be recorded as prose within <support>. If a separate envelope is present, record it using <measure unit="envelope">. Where its dimensions are known, record them using a separate <dimensions> with type=envelope.

correspondence letter | single sheet

• Format: Sheet

• Type: Letter (correspondence)

• Material(s): Paper

• Extent: Sheet height: 180 mm, width: 230 mm.

<objectDesc form="sheet">
 <objectType source="#aat" key="300026879">letter (correspondence)</objectType>
 <supportDesc material="paper">
  <support>
   <material source="#aat" key="300014109">Paper</material>
  </support>
  <extent>
   <measure quantity="1" unit="sheet"/>
   <dimensions type="sheet" unit="mm">
    <height quantity="180">180</height>
    <width quantity="230">230</width>
   </dimensions>
  </extent>
<!-- additional metadata -->
 </supportDesc>
</objectDesc>

Although Level 3 best practice generally recommends using the self-closing <measure> in a display-neutral form without textual value, a non-self-closing <measure> with textual value such as 2 sheets or 12 membranes, must be used when encoding multiple physical components. In these cases, both quantity and the textual value are required.

correspondence letter | two sheets

• Format: Sheet

• Type: Letter (correspondence)

• Material(s): Paper

• Extent: 2 Sheets. Sheet height: 256 mm, width: 204 mm.

<objectDesc form="sheet">
 <objectType source="#aat" key="300026879">letter (correspondence)</objectType>
 <supportDesc material="paper">
  <support>
   <material source="#aat" key="300014109">Paper</material>
  </support>
  <extent>
   <measure quantity="2" unit="sheet">2 sheets</measure>.
  <dimensions type="sheet" unit="mm">
    <height quantity="256">256</height>
    <width quantity="204">204</width>
   </dimensions>
  </extent>
<!-- additional metadata -->
 </supportDesc>
</objectDesc>

For objects such as stereographs and other mounted photographic prints, it is often necessary to record both the dimensions of the photographic sheet and those of the mount or supporting leaf. These measurements should be distinguished using type on <dimensions>. The photographic print should be encoded using type=sheet, while the mount or backing, such as a stereocard, should be encoded using type=mount.

object | stereograph

• Format: Object

• Type: Stereograph

• Material(s): Albumen paper mounted on cardboard.

• Extent: Sheet height: 50 mm, width: 80 mm. Mount height: 90 mm, width: 160 mm.

<objectDesc form="object">
 <objectType source="#aat" key="300127197">stereograph</objectType>
 <supportDesc material="paper">
  <support>
   <material type="paper" subtype="albumen" source="#aat" key="300411855">Albumen paper</material> mounted on <material type="paper" subtype="cardboard" source="#aat" key="300014224">cardboard</material>.
  </support>
  <extent>
   <measure quantity="1" unit="object"/>
   <dimensions type="sheet" unit="mm">
    <height quantity="50">50</height>
    <width quantity="80">80</width>
   </dimensions>
   <dimensions type="mount" unit="mm">
    <height quantity="90">90</height>
    <width quantity="160">160</width>
   </dimensions>
  </extent>
<!-- additional metadata -->
 </supportDesc>
</objectDesc>

General remarks: extent and measurements

Structured quantitative information, such as counts of sheets, leaves, or enclosures, should be encoded using <measure> with appropriate quantity and unit. Narrative descriptions of physical construction or folding should not be included within <extent>, as this disrupts the structured format.

At Level 3, single physical units are normally encoded using a self-closing <measure>, while multi-sheet or display-dependent extents should be encoded using a <measure> containing textual content:

single codex, roll, object—self-closing

<measure quantity="1" unit="volume"/>

single sheet (letter or map)—self-closing

<measure quantity="1" unit="sheet"/>

multi-sheet item (letters)—non-self-closing with display text

<measure quantity="2" unit="sheet>2 sheets</measure>

Measurements of ruled or written areas, such as the text block or inscribed space, must be encoded within <layout>, not <extent>. This information concerns the arrangement of text on the page, not the dimensions of the physical support. Although some catalogues present these measurements alongside leaf size, this approach should not be reproduced in TEI encodings, even for retroconverted files.

Encoding scope, ranges and uncertainty

Measurements are not always exact. Dimensions may vary due to trimming, rebinding, warping, or the presence of added components, and in some cases, direct measurement may not be possible. Measurements may therefore be encoded using additional attributes to represent ranges, approximate values, or estimates. This richer level of detail is particularly valuable where significant variation in size occurs within an item.

In legacy encoding practice, the global attributes precision (for measurement accuracy) and cert (for encoder confidence) were sometimes used. Such usage may still be encountered in older records but is not recommended for current practice. Instead, variation and uncertainty must be encoded using min, max, atLeast, and atMost.

@min | @max | @atLeast | @atMost

min where the measurement summarises more than one observation or a range, supplies the minimum value observed.

max where the measurement summarises more than one observation or a range, supplies the maximum value observed.

atLeast gives a minimum estimated value for the approximate measurement.

atMost gives a maximum estimated value for the approximate measurement.

Exact, uniform dimensions

The item has been measured directly, and all components, such as leaves, membranes, or sheets share the same dimensions with no variation. This includes uniformly trimmed manuscripts and books, single sheet maps, and folded letters where all sides match in size. Do not use this model if there is any measurable variation within the item. The measurements must be:

Known | not estimated or inferred — Exact | taken directly from the physical item — Uniform | the same across all measurable parts

• Extent: 254 leaves. Leaf height: 450 mm, width: 325 mm.

<extent>
 <measure quantity="254" unit="leaf">254 leaves</measure>.
<dimensions type="leaf" unit="mm">
  <height quantity="450">450</height>
  <width quantity="325">325</width>
 </dimensions>
</extent>

Observed size variation

The item contains components that vary measurably in size, such as leaves of different heights or membranes with uneven widths. If the variation between components exceeds 10 mm, encode the range using min and max. The measurements are:

Observed | derived from direct measurement of parts of the item — Variable | the dimensions differ across the internal structure —

Summarised | as a range spanning all known values

• Extent: 254 leaves. Leaf height: 205-215 mm, width: 135-145 mm.

<extent>
 <measure quantity="254" unit="leaf">254 leaves</measure>.
<dimensions type="leaf" unit="mm">
  <height min="205" max="215">205-215</height>
  <width min="135" max="145">135-145</width>
 </dimensions>
</extent>

No quantity on <height> or <width>, because we are not asserting an exact size.

Estimated dimensions | expressed as a range

The item cannot be measured precisely, but an estimated range can be recorded based on available evidence, such as digital images. The measurements are:

Estimated | not taken directly from the item — Approximate | given as a lower and upper bound using atLeast and atMost — Applied to the entire item | not summarising variation, but expressing uncertainty for the full size

• Extent: 254 leaves. Leaf height: 300-310 mm, width: 220-225 mm.

<extent>
 <measure quantity="254" unit="leaf">254 leaves</measure>.
<dimensions type="leaf" unit="mm">
  <height atLeast="300" atMost="310">300-310</height>
  <width atLeast="220" atMost="225">220-225</width>
 </dimensions>
</extent>

Multiple size groups within the Item

The item contains two or more physically distinct groups of components, such as specific leaf ranges in a codex or batches of membranes or sheets, each with different dimensions. Record each size cohort separately by repeating <dimensions> within the same <extent>. Clarify the leaf ranges using <note> in element content. The measurements are:

Grouped | recorded separately for each distinct set of leaves, membranes, or sheets — Precise | taken directly from the physical object, not estimated — Parallel | each set is described in its own <dimensions> block, within a shared <extent>

• Format: Codex

• Material(s): Parchment

• Extent: 156 leaves (ii+152 +ii). Leaf height: 450 mm, width: 325 mm. Leaf height: 242 mm, width: 210 mm. The leaves are in two sizes: leaves 5–6 differ from leaves 7–146.

<objectDesc form="codex">
 <supportDesc material="parchment">
  <support>
   <material source="#aat" key="300011851">Parchment</material>
  </support>
  <extent>
   <measure quantity="152" unit="leaf">156 leaves (ii+152 +ii)</measure>.
  <dimensions type="leaf" unit="mm">
    <height quantity="240">240</height>
    <width quantity="205">205</width>
   </dimensions>
   <dimensions type="leaf" unit="mm">
    <height quantity="242">242</height>
    <width quantity="210">210</width>
   </dimensions>
   <note>The leaves are in two sizes: leaves 5–6 differ from leaves 7–146.</note>
  </extent>
<!-- additional metadata -->
 </supportDesc>
</objectDesc>

Dersāna Mikā'ēl: Ethiopic MS 11

A full stop followed by a space must be manually inserted immediately after the closing <measure> if it is followed by <dimensions>. The display of <dimensions>, whether used once or multiple times, is automatically formatted by the system. Capitalisation, such as Leaf height, and punctuation, such as the full stop after width followed by the measurement in millimetres (mm), are generated by the XSLT transformation.

1.45 <foliation> | foliation

<foliation> (foliation) describes the numbering system or systems used to count the leaves or pages in a codex or similar object.

MDC display label — Foliation

<foliation> is used to record the foliation system of a manuscript or printed book, including the sequence of leaf numbers, the writing medium and placement of the numbers, and any information about when and by whom the numbering was added. It may also describe anomalies, errors, or the presence of multiple foliation systems.

When the front and back of each leaf are assigned separate numbers, the item is considered paginated. In TEI P5, there is no pagination element. Instead, <foliation> is used to record both foliation and pagination. The numbering method should be clearly indicated in the prose within <foliation>.

<foliation> is repeatable. It must contain at least one <p>. If multiple foliation or pagination systems are present, they may be recorded together in a single <foliation> using multiple <p>. However, best practice is to record each system separately, using multiple <foliation>, each with its own <p>.

In legacy encoding practice, multiple foliation systems were sometimes distinguished using xml:id. Such usage may still be encountered in older files but is not recommended for current practice. Instead, <foliation> should be encoded using contemporary.

@contemporary

contemporary (contemporary) specifies whether or not the foliation is contemporary with its contents.

The following values should be used:

true the foliation is original, contemporary with its contents

false the foliation is not original, not contemporary with its contents

unknown the date of foliation is unknown

In addition to contemporary, dating attributes may be used on <foliation> when the numbering can be dated with reasonable confidence. notBefore and notAfter may be used to express a date range, including approximate dates and periods such as centuries. Where only a single year is known, they may be assigned the same value. Where an exact date is known, when should be used.

If the person responsible for the foliation is known, their name should be encoded using <name role="asn"><persName/><name>. If the personal name is listed in VIAF, include both source and key on <name> to reference the appropriate VIAF authority record. Where both display and standard forms are required, include two <persName> or <orgName> within <name>, one with type=display and one with type=standard. Otherwise, a single <persName> or <orgName> within <name> may be used.

When the value #viaf is used for source, it indicates that the identifier recorded in key is drawn from this authority file. The prefix # indicates a reference to an identifier defined elsewhere in the same file. In this case, it refers to the <taxonomy> declared in the header with xml:id=viaf. This value can be selected from a controlled dropdown list when entering the attribute and does not need to be typed manually.

Record the form of foliation or pagination, indicating whether the numbering system is in Arabic numerals or Roman numerals, both of which must always be capitalised.

Record the placement of foliation or pagination using consistent terminology:

upper right corner— upper left corner

upper centre margin— lower centre margin

lower right corner— lower left corner

Indicate whether numbering appears on each recto, verso, or on both sides of each leaf. For Islamic and Hebrew manuscripts, specify whether it appears on side a, side b, or on both sides of each leaf.

... Arabic numerals in the upper right corner of each recto.

... Arabic numerals in the upper left corner of each side a.

Specify the medium, ink, pencil, or other.

Where relevant, note any later additions, corrections, errors, or irregularities in the numbering.

If significant, note the absence of foliation or pagination.

single <foliation> with multiple <p> | acceptable

• Foliation:

Sixteenth-century ink foliation in Roman numerals, with i and xvi missing, and the sequence ending at lxxxxiiii.

1–97: modern pencil foliation in Arabic numerals, in the upper right corner of each recto, added by the art historian Eric George Millar (1887–1966) in approximately 1925.

<foliation>
 <p>Sixteenth-century ink foliation in Roman numerals, with i and xvi missing, and the sequence ending at lxxxxiiii.</p>
 <p>1–97: modern pencil foliation in Arabic numerals, in the upper right corner of each recto, added by the art historian <name source="#viaf" key="88705920">
   <persName type="display">Eric George Millar (1887–1966)</persName>
   <persName type="standard">Millar, Eric George, 1887–1966</persName>
  </name> in approximately 1925.</p>
</foliation>

multiple <foliation>, with contemporary and date range | best practice

• Foliation:

Sixteenth-century ink foliation in Roman numerals, with i and xvi missing, and the sequence ending at lxxxxiiii.

1–97: modern pencil foliation in Arabic numerals, in the upper right corner of each recto, added by the art historian Eric George Millar (1887–1966) in approximately 1925.

<foliation contemporary="true" notBefore="1501" notAfter="1600">
 <p>Sixteenth-century ink foliation in Roman numerals, with i and xvi missing, and the sequence ending at lxxxxiiii.</p>
</foliation>
<foliation contemporary="false" notBefore="1925" notAfter="1925">
 <p>1–97: modern pencil foliation in Arabic numerals, in the upper right corner of each recto, added by the art historian <name source="#viaf" key="88705920">
   <persName type="display">Eric George Millar (1887–1966)</persName>
   <persName type="standard">Millar, Eric George, 1887–1966</persName>
  </name> in approximately 1925.</p>
</foliation>

Colonna Missal II: Latin MS 32A

single <foliation> | unknown date

• Foliation:

Pencil foliation in Arabic numerals, the upper right corner of each recto.

<foliation contemporary="unknown">
 <p>Pencil foliation in Arabic numerals, in the upper right corner of each recto.</p>
</foliation>

page numbering | pagination

• Foliation:

1–480: nineteenth-century pencil pagination in Arabic numerals, in the upper right corner of each leaf.

<foliation contemporary="false" notBefore="1801" notAfter="1900">
 <p>1–480: nineteenth-century pencil pagination in Arabic numerals, in the upper right corner of each leaf.</p>
</foliation>

1.46 <collation> | collation

<collation> (collation) contains a description of how the leaves, bifolia, or similar objects are physically arranged.

MDC display label — Collation

<collation> is used to describe the physical structure and arrangement of quires (or gatherings) within a codex, whether manuscript or printed. It records the composition and sequence of these gatherings, providing evidence of the item’s production and any subsequent alteration, such as the addition or loss of leaves or the insertion of new material.

<collation> may include one or more of the following child elements, in this order: <formula>, <signatures>, <catchwords>, <secFol>.

<collation> may begin with one or more <p> describing the overall structure of the codex, such as the total number of quires and their predominant composition, and noting any irregularities or uncertainties, for example, missing, replaced, or conjoined leaves. The arrangement of the quires may then be encoded either as a concise collation formula using <formula>, or more usually as <list> detailing the endleaves and quires in sequence.

<formula> and <list> both record the structure of quires within <collation>, but they differ in purpose and level of detail.

<formula> provides a concise summary of the quire structure, often expressed in conventional bibliographical notation, for example, A–C⁸ D⁴. It is most appropriate when reproducing collation information from earlier catalogue descriptions, when recording printed book collations in traditional form, or when a brief overview is sufficient.

<list>, by contrast, allows a detailed and structured description of each quire, including endleaves, irregularities, and physical features, and is therefore preferred in most cases.

Although both elements may appear within <collation>, they normally should not be used together.

Within <collation>, each <item> within <list> records a single quire or component of the physical structure. Endleaves are recorded at the beginning and end of the list but are not assigned a quire formula. Each quire entry should include the quire number, the number of leaves expressed as a superscript using <hi rend="superscript">, and, if known, the leaf range in brackets.

Regular quires

Complete quires composed of regular bifolia, meaning gatherings that retain their original structure and full complement of leaves without insertions, losses, or irregularities, should be recorded in <collation> as individual quire entries.

• Collation:

2 modern paper endleaves

1 original parchment endleaf

Quire 16 (1–6)

Quire 28 (7–14)

Quire 310 (15–24)

Quire 412 (25–36)

2 modern paper endleaves

<collation>
 <list>
  <item>2 modern paper endleaves</item>
  <item>1 original parchment endleaf</item>
  <item>Quire 1 <hi rend="superscript">6</hi> (1–6)</item>
  <item>Quire 2 <hi rend="superscript">8</hi> (7–14)</item>
  <item>Quire 3 <hi rend="superscript">10</hi> (15–24)</item>
  <item>Quire 4 <hi rend="superscript">12</hi> (25–36)</item>
  <item>2 modern paper endleaves</item>
 </list>
<!-- additional metadata -->
</collation>

Leaf ranges are expressed using bare numerals, for example, 1–8, to show the sequence of leaves within a quire. This is the standard and sufficient way of describing the extent of a quire in <collation>, referring implicitly to eight leaves, each with recto and verso sides. Because <collation> records the physical structure of the volume rather than the text’s position on each side, recto and verso indicators (r | v) are normally unnecessary. They should be added only when reference to a particular side is structurally significant, such as when noting a missing half-leaf or an inserted singleton.

Within <item>, the use of <locus> with from and to is optional and it is not required for structural descriptions of quires. Leaf ranges given in brackets, for example (1–8), are sufficient and standard practice.

Adjoining quires

When several adjoining quires share the same regular structure, they may be described together in a single entry. For example, the entry <item>Quires 1–4<hi rend="superscript">12</hi> (2–49)<item> indicates that quires 1 to 4 each consist of 12 leaves, covering leaves 2–49.

• Collation:

1 original endleaf

Quire 1-412 (2–49)

1 original endleaf

<collation>
 <list>
  <item>1 original endleaf</item>
  <item>Quires 1-4 <hi rend="superscript">12</hi> (2–49)</item>
  <item>1 original endleaf</item>
 </list>
<!-- additional metadata -->
</collation>

Irregular quires

When the manuscript structure is irregular, or where leaves have been inserted, lost, or conjoined, begin <collation> with one or more <p> that summarise these conditions before listing individual gatherings.

The following terminology should be used consistently when describing the physical structure:

Not present when a leaf has been removed or lost, without affecting the contents of the volume, but for reasons that cannot presently be established.

Excised when a leaf has clearly been cut out deliberately (especially if blank).

Missing when a leaf has been lost, perhaps but not necessarily accidentally, causing the text or decorative scheme to become imperfect.

Cancelled when a leaf has been removed but there is no evident interruption to the text or decorative scheme (e.g. leaf with an error, removed during copying).

Inserted when an extra leaf is contemporary with the production of the manuscript (e.g. a miniature on a single leaf in a Book of Hours)

Added when an extra leaf is a later insertion.

The symbols + and − can be used to indicate added or missing leaves: <hi rend="superscript">12+1</hi> or <hi rend="superscript">12-2</hi>. When the total cannot be expressed numerically, it may be written in words, for example <hi rend="superscript">eleven</hi>. Explanations of irregularities should precede the leaf range, leaving the brackets to contain only the range itself.

• Collation:

The manuscript consists of 11 irregular quires. Some bifolia may have been formed from originally separate single sheets that were joined together. Some leaves may have been inserted as singletons, and some noted below as ‘removed’ may never in fact have formed part of the original volume.

2 modern paper endleaves

1 original parchment endleaf

Quires 112-1+1 first leaf missing before leaf 1, replaced in the 17th century to supply missing text (1–12)

Quire 2-412 (13–48)

Quire 512+1 third leaf inserted after leaf 51 (49–61)

Quire 612 fourth leaf after leaf 64 and ninth leaf after leaf 69 are conjoined singletons (62–73)

Quire 7eleven (74–84)

Quire 812-2 eleventh and twelfth leaves after leaf 94 excised, probably blank (85–94)

1 original parchment endleaf

2 modern paper endleaves

<collation>
 <p>The manuscript consists of 11 irregular quires. Some bifolia may have been formed from originally separate single sheets that were joined together. Some leaves may have been inserted as singletons, and some noted below as ‘removed’ may never in fact have formed part of the original volume.</p>
 <list>
  <item>2 modern paper endleaves</item>
  <item>1 original parchment endleaf</item>
  <item>Quires 1<hi rend="superscript">12-1+1</hi> first leaf missing before leaf 1, replaced in the 17th century to supply missing text (1–12)</item>
  <item>Quire 2-4<hi rend="superscript">12</hi> (13–48)</item>
  <item>Quire 5 <hi rend="superscript">12+1</hi> third leaf inserted after leaf 51 (49–61)</item>
  <item>Quire 6 <hi rend="superscript">12</hi> fourth leaf after leaf 64 and ninth leaf after leaf 69 are conjoined singletons (62–73)</item>
  <item>Quire 7<hi rend="superscript">eleven</hi> (74–84)</item>
  <item>Quire 8<hi rend="superscript">12-2</hi> eleventh and twelfth leaves after leaf 94 excised, probably blank (85–94)</item>
  <item>1 original parchment endleaf</item>
  <item>2 modern paper endleaves</item>
 </list>
<!-- additional metadata -->
</collation>

For multi-part, composite manuscripts, the collation of each part should be recorded at the level of <msPart>. It may also be recorded at the level of the manuscript as a whole, if this aids clarity. In retroconversion this is likely to involve reorganisation of the formula.

<list> should not be nested within a <p> within <collation>.

Do not include a full stop at the end of each <item> within <list>.

Avoid legacy or informal terms such as wants, wanting, lacks, lacking, gone, (gone), cut out, or replaced. Use the preferred terms defined above (excised, missing, cancelled, inserted, added).

endleaves | free endleaves | pastedowns

Within <collation>, use endleaf or endleaves as the general, neutral term for any leaves located before or after the main textblock. This term applies to both manuscripts and printed books and includes all leaves that form the physical or structural connection between the textblock and the binding. Where greater precision is required, the following distinctions may be made:

Free endleaves are the endleaves not adhered to the inner surface of the board or cover. These may be the conjugate of a pastedown or may consist of one or more unattached leaves positioned between the pastedown and the main textblock. The term flyleaf is not used due to its imprecise and inconsistent application across descriptive traditions.

Pastedowns are the endleaves adhered to the inner surface of the covers or boards, anchoring the textblock within the binding. As structural components of the binding, pastedowns are normally described in <binding>, but may be referenced in <collation> if they are relevant to the quire sequence or clarify the arrangement of endleaves.

The term endpaper is not used. Although it is common in bibliographical descriptions of printed books, it is imprecise in structural terms and conflates pastedowns and free endleaves. For clarity and consistency, use endleaves as the general term, with free endleaves and pastedowns used where greater precision is required.

Endleaves should be included in <collation>, with descriptive entries placed at the beginning and end to represent those at the front and rear of the volume. Original and later endleaves should be clearly distinguished and recorded descriptively, for example, 2 modern endleaves, rather than incorporated into the main collation formula.

original | modern endleaves

Use original and modern to record endleaves according to their relationship to the manuscript’s physical structure.

original for endleaves that were produced with, or are integral to, the manuscript’s first binding or original structure.

modern for endleaves added in any subsequent binding, repair, or restoration, regardless of date.

Avoid using medieval, as it denotes a historical period rather than a structural relationship. Its use can introduce ambiguity, since additions from a medieval rebinding are not necessarily original. Use original for endleaves integral to the first binding, and modern for anything added later.

leaf vs folio

Always use leaf as the descriptive term for the physical structure of manuscripts and early printed books, never f., ff. or folio, fol. or folios, fols. The <collation> records how the volume is constructed, not the text’s position on each side of a leaf, so sides (r | v) are normally irrelevant.

Use bare numerals to express leaf ranges and quire extents, for example, 49–61. This is the standard and sufficient way to indicate the sequence of leaves within a quire. Include r | v indicators only when reference to a specific side is structurally significant. For example, when noting an inserted or missing half-leaf.
