Use numerals (1, 2, 3 ...) for quantitative and structural information, and spelled-out ordinals (first, second, third), for sequential references within prose, for example, the first leaf is missing before leaf 1 | The manuscript consists of 11 irregular quires.

Following the structural formula, <collation> may include details of quire and leaf <signatures>, <catchwords>, and <secFol>, where present. These features, which complement the gathering formula, provide further evidence of the item’s physical structure, forming the concluding part of the collation description.

Each of these elements is governed by a specific content model. <signatures> belongs to the macro.specialPara model class, which means it can contain either straightforward inline text or, when more structure is required, one or more <p>. In contrast, both <catchwords> and <secFol> are defined by the more restrictive macro.phraseSeq class. As such, they are limited to phrase-level content and must not include any block-level elements like <p> within them.

1.47 <signatures> | signatures

<signatures> (signatures) contains discussion of the leaf or quire signatures found within a codex or similar object.

<signatures> is a phrase-level element that, within <collation>, records information about quire or leaf signatures in a manuscript or printed book. It may contain a concise formula, an explanatory note, or a combination of both. It is used to describe signature systems, whether alphabetic, alphanumeric, or based on an ad hoc system, together with their sequence, placement, irregularities, interruptions, or later modifications.

<signatures> can be used for leaf signatures alone or for a combination of quire and leaf signatures. The optional type may be used where the distinction needs to be made explicit.

@type

type distinguishes between leaf and quire signatures

The following values should be used:

leaf for leaf or bifolium signatures, usually found in the lower right corner of the recto side of leaves in the first half of a quire, and usually following an alphanumerical sequence.

quire for quire signatures or numbers, usually found on the verso of the last leaf of the quire, or sometimes on the recto of the first leaf, and used to indicate the sequence of quires.

This is a customisation and not currently part of the TEI standard.

Because <signatures> is governed by the macro.specialPara content model, it may contain either paragraph-level or phrase-level content. It is typically treated as block-level and may contain multiple <p>. Each <p> should be a distinct descriptive note. Use a separate <p> for any additional information, so that distinct descriptive details remain clearly separated.

In legacy encoding practice, <signatures> was sometimes enclosed within a <p> or a <p> was placed within <signatures> contained inside <collation>, to separate it from the structural formula and other descriptive content or elements, ensuring consistent paragraph spacing and display. Such usage may still be encountered in older files but is not recommended in current practice. Neither <signatures><p/></signatures> nor <p><signatures/></p> should be used. <signatures> should occur directly within <collation>, and paragraph spacing and display are generated by the XSLT transformation.

The <signatures> content should convey the physical characteristics of these marks, noting how they are expressed, where they are positioned, and any evidence of later intervention.

If the name of the individual or organisation responsible for the quire or leaf signatures is known, it should be encoded using <name>, containing either <persName> or <orgName> as appropriate. If the name is listed in VIAF, include both source and key on <name> to reference the appropriate VIAF authority record. Where both display and standard forms are required, include two <persName> or <orgName> within <name>, one with type=display and one with type=standard. Otherwise, a single <persName> or <orgName> within <name> may be used.

The following outlines the preferred form, terminology, and stylistic conventions for recording this information in a clear and consistent manner:

Record the form of the signatures (alphabetic, alphanumeric, or some ad hoc system), noting whether numerals are Arabic or Roman, or used in combination with letters.

Record the placement of the signature marks using consistent terminology:

lower inner margin — lower centre margin — lower outer margin (most common)

Specify the medium used, such as ink, pencil.

Where relevant, note any later additions, corrections, errors, or irregularities in the numbering.

If significant, note the absence of signatures.

leaf | quire signatures

• Collation:

—

Leaf signatures in typical alphanumeric form ai–aiiii to mi–miiii, written in pencil in the lower right corner of rectos in the first half of each quire, in the ninth-century part of the manuscript only, by Henry Bradshaw (1831–1886), University Librarian 1867-1886.

Quire signatures in Arabic numerals (1–20), written in ink by an eighteenth- or nineteenth-century hand in the centre of the bottom margin of the recto of the first leaf of each quire, extending across the later medieval additions and the ninth-century core.

<collation>
<!-- additional metadata -->
 <signatures type="leaf">Leaf signatures in typical alphanumeric form ai–aiiii to mi–miiii, written in pencil in the lower right corner of rectos in the first half of each quire, in the ninth-century part of the manuscript only, by <name source="#viaf" key="37074791">
   <persName type="display">Henry Bradshaw (1831–1886)</persName>
   <persName type="display">Bradshaw, Henry, 1831–1886</persName>
  </name>, University Librarian 1867-1886.</signatures>
 <signatures type="quire">Quire signatures in Arabic numerals (1–20), written in ink by an eighteenth- or nineteenth-century hand in the lower centre margin of the recto of the first leaf of each quire, extending across the later medieval additions and the ninth-century core.</signatures>
<!-- additional metadata -->
</collation>

Write centuries in words and lowercase, such as eighteenth century, rather than using numeric forms such as 18th century or 18th-century. Insert a hyphen when the term is used adjectivally, for example, eighteenth-century hand, and when expressing an uncertain or alternative range, connect the terms with a hyphen (-) and or, for instance, eighteenth- or nineteenth-century hand.

For early printed books, <collation> may include both a collation formula, which describes the sequence and extent of gatherings, for example, a–z⁸ A–F⁸ G¹², and a description of printed signature marks, the marks actually printed on certain leaves, typically at the foot of the first few rectos of each gathering, such as a, aii, aiii, aiiii.

For further information on collation formulas, see: STCV. Bibliography of the Hand Press Book: Collation Formula

During MARC-to-TEI conversion, content from MARC field 500 (General Note) or 591 (Local Note) that begins with the string Signatures: usually represents a collation formula, even though it is “labelled” as signatures. In TEI, this information should be encoded using <formula>, while any supplementary notes concerning signature marks, such as mis-signed leaves, omissions, or irregularities, should be recorded within <signatures>. Other physical notes, such as blank or leaves, should be described in a separate <p> within <collation>.

<formula> | MARC signatures

• Collation: pi2 a-z8 A-H8

Leaf b2 is mis-signed a2.

Leaf l2 (82) is blank except for foliation and signature; leaf H7 (penultimate leaf) is blank.

<collation>
 <formula>pi<hi rend="superscript">2</hi> a–z<hi rend="superscript">8</hi> A-H<hi rend="superscript">8</hi>
 </formula>
 <signatures>Leaf b2 is mis-signed a2.</signatures>
 <p>Leaf l2 (82) is blank except for foliation and signature; leaf H7 (penultimate leaf) is blank.</p>
</collation>

Dante col sito, et forma dell'inferno tratta dalla istessa descrittione del poeta: 5541

Use folio when a single numbered side is blank, for example, folio 20v is blank. Use leaf when both sides of a single leaf are blank, for example, leaf 20 is blank.

1.48 <catchwords> | catchwords

<catchwords> (catchwords) describes the system used to ensure correct ordering of the quires or similar making up a codex, incunable, or other object typically by means of annotations at the foot of the page.

<catchwords> is a phrase-level element that, within <collation>, records the presence and placement of catchwords and describes their distribution across the gatherings of a codex.

Catchwords typically appear in the lower margin of the final verso of each quire and may occur regularly or intermittently throughout the collation. They are usually plain but are sometimes enclosed within decorative frames, features that help distinguish them from the main text.

In legacy encoding practice, <catchwords> was sometimes enclosed within a <p> inside <collation> to separate it from the structural formula and other descriptive content or elements, ensuring consistent paragraph spacing and display. Such usage may still be encountered in legacy files but is not recommended in current practice. Instead, <catchwords> should appear directly within <collation>. Paragraph breaks for display are generated by the XSLT transformation.

The <catchwords> content should convey the physical characteristics of these marks, noting how they are expressed, where they are positioned, and any evidence of later intervention. The following outlines the preferred form, terminology, and stylistic conventions for recording this information in a clear and consistent manner.

Record the orientation and placement of catchwords using consistent terminology:

horizontal — vertical — diagonal

lower inner margin — lower centre margin— lower outer margin

Specify the medium used, such as ink, pencil.

If the catchwords are enclosed within a decorative frame or ruled box, record this.

Specify orientation and location on the leaf, using <locus> with from and to where visible.

Where relevant, specify the scribe or hand, and include references to any later additions or changes.

If significant, note the absence of catchwords.

• Collation:

Horizontal catchwords in the lower centre margin of the verso of the last leaf of each quire: 10v, 18v, …172v.

<collation>
 <catchwords>Horizontal catchwords in the lower centre margin of the verso of the last leaf of each quire: <locus from="10v" to="10v">10v</locus>, <locus from="18v" to="18v">18v</locus>, <locus from="26v" to="26v">26v</locus>, … <locus from="172v" to="172v">172v</locus>. </catchwords>
</collation>

Il Canzoniere di Petrarca e Le Rime di Dante*:* Italian MS 1

  • In this example, the use of an ellipsis (…) indicates that intermediate instances have been omitted for brevity. In total, 21 catchwords are recorded.

1.49 <secFol> | second folio

<secFol> (second folio) marks the word or words taken from a fixed point in a codex (typically the beginning of the second leaf) in order to provide a unique identifier for it.

<secFol> is a phrase-level element, used to record the secundo folio, also known as dictio probatoria, consisting of the first two or three words on the second leaf of a codex. Medieval and Renaissance libraries widely used the secundo folio to distinguish individual copies with identical incipits.

In legacy encoding practice, <secFol> was sometimes enclosed within a <p> contained inside <collation> to separate it from the structural formula and other descriptive content, ensuring consistent paragraph spacing and display. In other cases, <secFol> appeared within a <p> directly after <physDesc>. Such usage may still be encountered in legacy files but is not recommended in current practice. Instead, <secFol> should be encoded directly within <collation>. Paragraph breaks for display are generated by the XSLT transformation.

<secFol> is typically placed after <signatures> and <catchwords> within <collation>. Each entry inside a <secFol> should follow this format:

Secundo folio: transcription

The phrase Secundo folio should appear at the beginning, followed by a location reference (folio | page number) using <locus> with from and to, then a colon (:), and finally a transcription of the opening words from the second leaf. The transcription should be given exactly as it appears in the source, preserving original spelling, punctuation, and letterforms, and should be encoded using <hi rend="italic">.

• Collation:

—

Secundo folio on10v: To bathe.

<collation>
<!-- additional metadata -->
 <secFol>Secundo folio on <locus from="1r" to="1r">1r</locus>: <hi rend="italic">To bathe </hi>.</secFol>
<!-- additional metadata -->
</collation>

Siege of Troy: English MS 1

1.50 <condition> | condition

<condition> (condition) contains a description of the physical condition of the manuscript or object.

MDC display label — Condition

<condition> is a context-dependent element, and may be used within an appropriate structural container, such as <supportDesc> for leaves, <bindingDesc> for bindings, or <sealDesc> for seals. It should not be used to describe conservation activity, which belongs in <custodialHist>.

In the context of <supportDesc>, <condition> is used to provide a brief description of the state of the physical support. This includes any physical alteration or damage, whether intentional or unintentional, affecting the legibility of the text or the clarity of the decoration. This may include detached, loose, torn, mutilated, or otherwise damaged leaves, flaking ink or pigments, ink corrosion or bleed-through, deliberate damage to decorative elements, and retouching of the text or decoration. It is not necessary to repeat information about excised, missing or cancelled leaves or gatherings, as these are more appropriately recorded in <collation>.

While <condition> remains a lightweight, unstructured element intended for concise description of physical condition, its content must always be enclosed in one or more <p>, depending on the level of detail required.

• Condition:

The manuscript is incomplete and damaged, with several leaves excised and removed at various points, leading to the loss of both text and illuminations. Additionally, intentional damage is evident in several areas where the manuscript has been defaced, likely due to the nature of its content.

<condition>
 <p>The manuscript is incomplete and damaged, with several leaves excised and removed at various points, leading to the loss of both text and illuminations. Additionally, intentional damage is evident in several areas where the manuscript has been defaced, likely due to the nature of its content.</p>
</condition>

Psalter of Joan of Navarre: Latin MS 22

• Condition:

The legibility of the text is affected in parts due to ink stains (blots and smudges).

The text on the inner margin is partially obscured by the binding.

<condition>
 <p>The legibility of the text is affected in parts due to ink stains (blots and smudges).</p>
 <p>The text on the inner margin is partially obscured by the binding.</p>
</condition>

Letter to Heinrich Simon from his sisters Hermine, Auguste, and Julie Simon: SIM/1/194

General remarks: condition

<condition> should be used only where a significant or clearly observable condition issue is present. Items in good or stable condition do not require an explicit statement.

Damage affecting the binding, such as weak joints, detached boards, loose headbands or other structural defects, should be recorded within <bindingDesc>.

Damage affecting a seal, such as chips, cracks, detachment or surface wear, should be recorded within <sealDesc>.

<condition> may also be used to describe changes or repairs. However, where a documented conservation intervention exists, detailing when, by whom and what was done, this information should more appropriately be recorded as part of the custodial history within <custodialHist>.

1.51 <layoutDesc> | layout description

<layoutDesc> (layout description) collects the set of layout descriptions applicable to a manuscript or other object.

MDC display label — Layout

<layoutDesc> is used to describe both the layout and the physical preparation of the written surface, that is, how text is arranged on the leaves and how the leaves were physically prepared. This includes, for example, the number of written, ruled, or pricked lines and columns, the size of margins, and the presence of distinct textual blocks such as glosses, commentaries, or diagrams.

One or more different layouts may be identified within a single codex. Each should be described using a separate <layout>, which may contain one or more <p> describing the layout in detail.

In addition, attributes on <layout> can be used to provide structured information, such as the number of columns or written lines, writing orientation, or ruling method.

1.52 <layout> | layout

<layout> (layout) describes how text is laid out on the page or surface of the object, including information about any ruling, pricking, or other evidence of page-preparation techniques.

<layout> within <layoutDesc> is used to provide structured information about the layout of the written surface. Where the layout is sufficiently regular, the element’s attributes may be adequate on their own. However, a prose description should also be included to provide a fuller context.

Since <layout> is repeatable, multiple distinct layouts may be described within a single <layoutDesc>, using a combination of attributes and prose description, particularly where the arrangement of text varies significantly across the item.

<layout> includes columns, writtenLines, and ruledLines, which provide quantitative information about the number of columns and the number of written and ruled lines.

@columns | @ruledLines | @writtenLines

Each attribute accepts one or two values of type teidata.count, separated by space:

columns (columns) specifies the number of columns per page

ruledLines (ruled lines) specifies the number of ruled lines per column

writtenLines (written lines) specifies the number of written lines per column

If a single number is given, it applies uniformly across all relevant pages or columns. If two numbers are given, the value varies between the lower and upper bounds. If columns is omitted, it is assumed that each page has one column.

Describe the layout in one or more <p>, using the following conventions:

feature

preferred form

avoid

number of columns

one column | two columns

1 column, single column | 2 columns, double column

number of written lines

15 lines | 15-16 lines

fifteen lines | 15 to 16 lines

number of ruled lines

30 lines | 30-32 lines

thirty lines | 30 to 32 lines

ruling method

dry point | plummet | ink | pencil

hardpoint, leadpoint, pen

bounding lines

frame (all four sides of the text block)

single | double — vertical | horizontal bounding lines (not full frame)

1, 2, one, two thirty horizontal lines

pricking

pricking marks visible in the inner | outer margin

do not record absence unless significant.

first line of text

above top line | below top line

(particularly useful for dating 13th century MSS)

See the Glossary in the Library of Congress Medieval and Renaissance Facsimiles and Incunables: A Resource Guide.

At a minimum level, you should provide the number of columns and written lines.

• Layout:

One column, 22 lines.

<layoutDesc>
 <layout columns="1" writtenLines="22">
  <p>One column, 22 lines.</p>
 </layout>
</layoutDesc>

Measurements of the written or ruled space should be encoded using <dimensions> with @type= written and ruled, as appropriate.

• Layout:

One column, 22 lines.

Written height: 170 mm, width: 110 mm.

<layoutDesc>
 <layout columns="1" writtenLines="22">
  <p>One column, 22 lines.</p>
  <dimensions type="written" unit="mm">
   <height quantity="170">170</height>
   <width quantity="110">110</width>
  </dimensions>
 </layout>
</layoutDesc>

The Pore Caitif: English MS 87

• Layout:

One column, 26 lines, written below the top line.

Ruled in dark ink, with vertical and horizontal bounding lines forming a frame extending to the margins.

Pricking is visible on the outer margins of many leaves, for example 117r.

Written height: 110 mm, width: 75 mm.

<layoutDesc>
 <layout columns="1" writtenLines="22">
  <p>One column, 26 lines, written below the top line.</p>
  <p>Ruled in dark ink, with vertical and horizontal bounding lines forming a frame extending to the margins.</p>
  <p>Pricking is visible on the outer margins of many leaves, for example <locus from="117r" to="117r">117r</locus>.</p>
  <dimensions type="written" unit="mm">
   <height quantity="110">110</height>
   <width quantity="75">75</width>
  </dimensions>
 </layout>
</layoutDesc>

Later Version Wycliffe New Testament: English MS 75

<dimensions> should not be wrapped in <p>. It will be rendered independently, typically as a distinct display line in output systems such as MDC.

For early printed books, the concept of written lines does not apply, and ruling is not always present or relevant. As such, ruledLines and writtenLines are generally not applicable. However, the number of columns should be encoded using columns, and, if possible, the number of lines should be included in the accompanying prose description.

• Layout:

Printed in one column, 28-30 lines.

<layoutDesc>
 <layout columns="1">
  <p>Printed in one column, 28-30 lines.</p>
 </layout>
</layoutDesc>

Le terze rime di Dante: 18873

Where the number of written lines varies across the item, supply a range of values. The lower and upper bounds should be separated by a space rather than a hyphen (-) or dash (_). This indicates that the number of written lines varies between the two values.

• Layout:

One column, 27-28 lines, written below the top line.

Ruled in plummet (leadpoint), with vertical and horizontal bounding lines forming a frame extending to the margins.

<layoutDesc>
 <layout columns="1" writtenLines="27 28">
  <p>One column, 27-28 lines, written below the top line.</p>
  <p>Ruled in plummet (leadpoint), with vertical and horizontal bounding lines forming a frame extending to the margins.</p>
 </layout>
</layoutDesc>

Il Canzoniere di Petrarca e Le Rime di Dante: Italian MS 1

Where the number of ruled lines per column differs from the number of written lines, use ruledLines and writtenLines to distinguish between the two.

• Layout:

One column, 15–16 lines, written below the top line.

Ruled in very pale brown ink, visible in places, with 30-32 ruled lines and single vertical bounding lines extending to the margins.

Ruled height: 255 mm, width: 160 mm.

<layoutDesc>
 <layout columns="1" ruledLines="30 32" writtenLines="15 16">
  <p>One column, 15–16 lines, written below the top line.</p>
  <p>Ruled in very pale brown ink, visible in places, with 30-32 ruled lines and single vertical bounding lines extending to the margins.</p>
  <dimensions type="ruled" unit="mm">
   <height quantity="255">255</height>
   <width quantity="160">160</width>
  </dimensions>
 </layout>
</layoutDesc>

Later Version Wycliffe New Testament: English MS 75

Distinct differences may occur between sections of a manuscript that were nevertheless produced around the same time or within the same context, perhaps prompted by a change in scribal hand or by differences in how the quires were prepared for writing. In such cases, include as many <layout> entries as necessary, and use <locus> within the descriptive paragraph to specify the portions of the manuscript to which each layout applies.

• Layout:

1r-220v: Two columns, 52 written lines. No pricking marks.

221r-350v: One column, 45 ruled lines and 33 written lines. Pricking marks visible along the outer margin, mostly cropped.

<layoutDesc>
 <layout columns="2" writtenLines="52">
  <p>
   <locus from="1r" to="220v">1r-220v</locus>: Two columns, 52 written lines. No pricking marks.</p>
 </layout>
 <layout columns="1" ruledLines="45" writtenLines="33">
  <p>
   <locus from="221r" to="350v">221r-350v</locus>: One column, 45 ruled lines and 33 written lines. Pricking marks visible along the outer margin, mostly cropped.</p>
 </layout>
</layoutDesc>

After <objectDesc>, <physDesc> allows more specific descriptions of the item's physical, textual, and decorative features, including its written, printed, decorative, and notational content, together with any later additions, bindings, seals, and accompanying material.

The following elements may be included within <physDesc> whenever information is available:

<teiHeader> <fileDesc>|<titleStmt>|<publicationStmt>|<sourceDesc> <msDesc>|<msIdentifier>|<msContents> <physDesc> <objectDesc/> <handDesc> <!-- Metadata describing the script in which an item is written --> <!-- May contain <summary> --> <handNote> <!-- Metadata detailing the script and the hands of multiple scribes --> <!-- Contains <term> --> <!-- May contain <persName> within <name> --> </handNote> </handDesc> <typeDesc> <!-- Metadata describing the typefaces or other aspects of the printing of a source --> <!-- May contain <summary> <typeNote> <!-- Metadata detailing the typefaces or typographic settings --> <!-- Contains <term> --> <!-- May contain <persName> within <name> --> </typeNote> </typeDesc> <musicNotation> <!-- Metadata notated music present in the item --> <!-- Contains <p> --> </musicNotation> <decoDesc> <!-- Metadata describing the script in which an item is written --> <!-- May contain <summary> <decoNote> <!-- Metadata detailing the script and the hands of multiple scribes --> <!-- May contain <summary> | <persName> within <name> --> </decoNote> </decoDesc> <additions> <!-- Metadata describing additions to script, entered in later hands --> <!-- Contains <p> --> </additions> <bindingDesc> <!-- Metadata describing the binding of an item --> <binding> <!-- Metadata detailing all aspects of a binding --> <!-- Contains <dimensions> --> <!-- May contain <decoNote> | <condition> | <term> | <persName> within <name> --> </binding> </bindingDesc> <sealDesc> <!-- Metadata describing the seal attached to an item --> <!-- May contain <summary> <seal> <!-- Metadata detailing all aspects of a seal --> <!-- May contain dimensions | <decoNote> | <condition> | <persName> within <name> --> </seal> </sealDesc> <accMat> <!-- Metadata about material that are not part of the original item --> <!-- Contains <p> --> </accMat> </physDesc> <history>|<additional> </msDesc>|</sourceDesc>|</fileDesc>|<encodingDesc/>|<profileDesc>|<revisionDesc> </teiHeader>

1.53 <handDesc> | description of hands

<handDesc> (description of hands) contains a description of all the different hands used in a manuscript or other object.

MDC display label — Script

<handDesc> is used to record the description of the script found within a manuscript or other handwritten document. It provides structured information about the number, identity, and characteristics of the hands (i.e. individual scribes or writers) responsible for the written content.

The number of distinct hands identified in a manuscript should always be encoded using hands on <handDesc>, whether there is one hand or several. Further descriptive detail may be supplied using one or more <handNote>.

@hands

hands (hands) specifies the number of distinct hands identified within the manuscript

hands should take values such as 1, 2, 3, and so on, representing the number of hands involved in the original production and should not include later additions or annotations.

<handDesc> may include <summary> with one or more <p> providing a general account of the scripts or scribes. This must be followed by one or more <handNote>, each describing a particular hand or group of hands.

1.54 <handNote> | note on hand

<handNote> (note on hand) describes a particular style or hand distinguished within a manuscript.

<handNote> within <handDesc> typically includes details such as the script style and the extent of the hand, scribe attribution where known, distinctive features of the writing, and medium. These details may be encoded using scope, script, and medium, together with descriptive prose in <p>. The script may be further characterised using <term type="script">.

@scope

scope specifies how widely this hand is used in the manuscript

The following values should be used:

sole only this hand is used throughout the manuscript

major this hand is used through most of the manuscript

minor this hand is used occasionally in the manuscript

@script

script characterises the particular script or writing style used by this hand

script is used for consistent, high-level script classification, following approved nomenclature.

In Latin-script manuscripts, script values are typically Latin-derived terms that identify the palaeographical class of writing, such as textualis, cursiva, or humanistica. script functions as a controlled, language-neutral identifier for the script family. Its values must always be encoded in lowercase, without accents or spaces, using camelCase only to join logical parts of the term. For example, textualisNorthern | textualisSouthern | cursivaAntiquior.

Where possible, the accompanying <term type="script"> should repeat the same Latin family root and may include a simple descriptive qualifier, for example, textualis quadrata, textualis rotunda, cursiva libraria, humanistica cursiva. Traditional script terms such as anglicana, anglicana formata, bâtarde, and secretary may also be used as <term type="script"> values, provided they are compatible with the script family identified in script. For instance, script=cursivaAntiquior with <term type="script">anglicana</term>. It must contain only the script term itself.

Avoid using vague or hybrid English terms such as bastard secretary, Gothic script, Gothic bookhand in either script or <term type="script">. Consistency in terminology is important. The formal script commonly referred to as textualis, textura, or Gothic bookhand represents a single script type. These terms are synonymous, and the preferred term is textualis.

In <term type="script">, capitalise script names that derive from personal names, ethnonyms, geographic or regional designations, languages, or language-derived adjectives, such as Caroline, Beneventan, Tamil, Ethiopic, Ashkenazi or Rashi. Use lowercase for generic script-style or typological terms, including minuscule, uncial, cursiva, naskh, nastaʿlīq, textualis or semi-square.

Suggested values for Latin-script manuscripts are:

script

<term type="script">

capitalSquare

square capitals

<term type="script">square capitals</term>

Narrower Roman capitals.

uncial

uncial

<term type="script">uncial</term>

Rounded majuscule script.

halfuncial

half-uncial

<term type="script">half-uncial</term>

Transitional majuscule. Combines uncial and minuscule features.

majuscule

majuscule

<term type="script">majuscule</term>

Unspecified majuscule hand, not assignable to a subtype.

majusculeInsular

Insular majuscule

<term type="script">Insular majuscule</term>

Formal Insular bookhand.

minusculeInsular

Insular minuscule

<term type="script">Insular minuscule</term>

Standard Insular bookhand.

minusculeCaroline

Caroline minuscule

<term type="script">Caroline minuscule</term>

Clear, rounded Carolingian bookhand, revived in Renaissance humanistic scripts.

minusculeVernacular

vernacular minuscule

<term type="script">vernacular minuscule</term>

English vernacular script influenced by Caroline minuscule.

minusculeBeneventan

Beneventan minuscule

<term type="script">Beneventan minuscule</term>

Southern Italian minuscule, used in Benevento and Monte Cassino.

minusculeVisigothic

Visigothic minuscule

<term type="script">Visigothic minuscule</term>

Iberian Latin minuscule.

minuscule

minuscule

<term type="script">minuscule</term>

Unspecified minuscule hand, not assignable to a subtype.

protogothic

protogothic

<term type="script">protogothic</term>

Transitional hand between Caroline and Gothic.

textualisNorthern

textualis quadrata

textualis semi-quadrata

textualis prescissa

textualis libraria

textualis currens

textualis rotunda

<term type="script">textualis quadrata</term>

Northern Gothic bookhand with diamond-shaped feet on minims (pedes).

<term type="script">textualis semi-quadrata</term>

Northern Gothic variant with partly developed feet. Intermediate between quadrata and prescissa.

<term type="script">textualis prescissa</term>

Northern gothic variant with sine pedibus (without feet); minims cut flat along the baseline.

<term type="script">textualis libraria</term>

Regular bookhand of northern textualis.

<term type="script">textualis currens</term>

Rapid or informal northern textualis. Transitional, towards cursiva.

textualisSouthern

textualis rotunda

<term type="script">textualis rotunda</term>

Southern Gothic bookhand (Italy | Iberia) with rounded letterforms.

semitextualis

semitextualis

<term type="script">semitextualis</term>

Transitional class between textualis and cursiva. Moderately angular.

Script classification is not primarily about geography. It's about the style of the handwriting.

textualisNorthern France, England, Germany, Angular, compressed, vertical

textualisSouthern Italy, Iberia, Southern France, Rounded, open forms (rotunda)

cursivaAntiquior

cursiva antiquior

anglicana

anglicana formata

cursiva libraria

cursiva currens

<term type="script">cursiva antiquior</term>

Early Gothic cursive, precursor of later Gothic cursives.

<term type="script">anglicana</term>

English variety of cursiva antiquior.

<term type="script">anglicana formata</term>

Formalised anglicana. A regularised book or display hand within cursiva antiquior.

<term type="script">cursiva libraria</term>

Standard “library” or formal bookhand of cursiva antiquior.

<term type="script">cursiva currens</term>

Rapid, informal cursive of cursiva antiquior.

cursiva

cursiva

cursiva libraria

bâtarde

secretary

<term type="script">cursiva</term>

Later Gothic cursive. Includes a wide range of regional styles.

<term type="script">cursiva libraria</term>

Formal bookhand variety of cursiva, known as Gothica cursiva libraria.

<term type="script">bâtarde</term>

Formal French-Burgundian variety of cursiva. Also known as bastarda or lettre bâtarde.

hybrida

hybrida

<term type="script">hybrida</term>

Fusion of textualis and cursiva features.

gothicoAntiqua

gothico-antiqua

<term type="script">gothico-antiqua</term>

Transitional fifteenth-century Italian script combining Gothic textualis and humanistic features; also known as gothico-humanistica or fere-humanistica.

humanistica

humanistica

humanistica textualis

humanistica semitextualis

humanistica cursiva

<term type="script">humanistica</term>

General or indeterminate humanist hand. Use when the script is recognisably humanistic but not assignable to a subtype.

<term type="script">humanistica textualis</term>

Renaissance revival of Caroline minuscule.

<term type="script">humanistica semitextualis</term>

Semi-formal humanist book hand, intermediate between textual and cursive forms.

<term type="script">humanistica cursiva</term>

Humanist chancery cursive (cancelleresca all’italiana). Prototype of italic script and type.

kurrent

kurrent

<term type="script">kurrent</term>

German cursive (Kurrentschrift), 16th-20th century. Distinct from Latin cursiva.

other

N/A

For clearly identifiable scripts outside the current controlled vocabulary.

unknown

N/A

Used when the script cannot be securely identified.

The classification for early scripts is based on Brown (1990) with modifications, for gothic scripts on Derolez (2003), for humanistic scripts on Derolez (2011).

Suggested values for non-Latin manuscripts are:

script

<term type="script">

naskh

naskh

<term type="script">naskh</term>

nastaliq

nastaʿlīq

<term type="script">nastaʿlīq </term>

taliq

taʿlīq

<term type="script">taʿlīq</term>

shikastah

shikastah

<term type="script">shikastah</term>

divani

dīvānī

<term type="script">dīvānī</term>

thuluth

thuluth

<term type="script">thuluth</term>

rayhani

rayḥānī

<term type="script">rayḥānī</term>

ruqah

ruqʿah

<term type="script">ruqʿah</term>

riqa

riqa

<term type="script">riqā</term>

tughra

ṭughrā

<term type="script">ṭughrā</term>

ajami

ʿajamī

<term type="script">ʿajamī</term>

maghribi

maghribī

<term type="script">maghribī</term>

kufic

kufic

<term type="script">kufic</term>

muhaqqaq

muḥaqqaq

<term type="script">muḥaqqaq</term>

siyaq

sīyāq

<term type="script">sīyāq</term>

eastSyriac

East Syriac

<term type="script">East Syriac</term>

estrangela

estrangela

<term type="script">estrangela</term>

estrangela Syriac script

serto

serto

<term type="script">serto</term>

serto Syriac script

ethiopic

Ethiopic

<term type="script">Ethiopic</term>

Ethiopic script | Geʽez language

asomtavruli

asomtavruli

<term type="script">asomtavruli</term>

asomtavruli Georgian script

nuskhuri

nuskhuri

<term type="script">nuskhuri</term>

nuskhuri Georgian script

mkhedruli

mkhedruli

<term type="script">mkhedruli</term>

mkhedruli Georgian script

erkatagir

erkatʿagir

<term type="script">erkatʿagir </term>

erkatʿagir Armenian script

bolorgir

bolorgir

<term type="script">bolorgir</term>

bolorgir Armenian script

notrgir

nōtrgir

<term type="script">nōtrgir</term>

nōtrgir Armenian script

devanagari

devanāgarī

<term type="script">devanāgarī</term>

jainanagari

jainānāgarī

<term type="script">jainānāgarī</term>

naipalanagari

naipālanāgarī

<term type="script">naipālanāgarī</term>

nepalakshara

<term type="script">nepālākṣarā</term>

ranjana

<term type="script">rañjanā</term>

bhaiksuki

bhaikṣukī

<term type="script">bhaikṣukī</term>

sarada

śāradā

<term type="script">śāradā</term>

grantha

Grantha

<term type="script">Grangtha</term>

tamil

Tamil

<term type="script">Tamil</term>

malayalam

Malayalam

<term type="script">Malayalam</term>

telugu

Telugu

<term type="script">Telugu</term>

sinhala

Sinhala

<term type="script">Sinhala</term>

bengali

Bengali

<term type="script">Bengali</term>

bihari

Bihari

<term type="script">Bihari</term>

dbuCan

dbu can

<term type="script">dbu can</term>

dbu can Tibetan script

dbuMed

dbu med

<term type="script">dbu med</term>

dbu med Tibetan script

mongolian

Mongolian

<term type="script">Mongolian</term>

rashi

Rashi

<term type="script">Rashi</term>

Rashi Hebrew script

squareOriental

Oriental square

<term type="script">Oriental square</term>

Oriental square Hebrew script

semiSquareOriental

<term type="script">Oriental semi-square</term>

Oriental semi-square Hebrew script

semiSquareByzantine

cuneiform

<term type="script">cuneiform</term>

hieroglyphic

<term type="script">hieroglyphic</term>

Egyptian hieroglyphic script

demotic
