<term type="script">demotic</term>

coptic

Coptic

<term type="script">Coptic</term>

other

N/A

For clearly identifiable scripts outside the current controlled vocabulary.

unknown

N/A

Used when the script cannot be securely identified.

The distinction between Latin-script and non-Latin-script manuscripts used here is a technical one based on the writing system rather than on current geopolitical boundaries or cultural hierarchies. Scripts are grouped according to their palaeographical lineage and graphic form, so a manuscript is treated as non-Latin-script whenever it is written wholly or predominantly in a script other than the Latin alphabet.

A single hand (or scribe) is responsible for the entire text, written in a single script that remains uniform throughout.

• Script:

• Written in textualis rotunda by a single hand.

<handDesc hands="1">
 <handNote scope="sole" script="textualisSouthern" medium="ink">
  <p>Written in <term type="script">textualis rotunda</term> by a single hand.</p>
 </handNote>
</handDesc>

Colonna Missal II: Latin MS 32a

• Script:

• Written in kurrent (Kurrentschrift) by a single hand. Alchemical symbols are consistently rendered in red throughout.

<handDesc hands="1">
 <handNote scope="sole" script="kurrent" medium="ink">
  <p>Written in <term type="script">kurrent</term> (Kurrentschrift) by a single hand. Alchemical symbols are consistently rendered in red throughout.</p>
 </handNote>
</handDesc>

Sammlung Alchymistischer Schriften: German MS 3

More than one distinct hand has been identified, all using the same script. This can be encoded using <summary> within <handDesc>, followed by one <handNote> for each hand. Each <handNote> must include xml:id and one or more <p>. Where appropriate, <locus> may be used to indicate the folio range where the hand appears. xml:id should take values such as hand1, hand2, hand3, numbered in the order in which the hands appear.

• Script:

Written in northern textualis script by two hands, the manuscript is punctuated throughout with punctus elevatus, punctus interrogativus, and punctus, the latter used to mark metrum, minor pauses, and the ends of verses.

• 4r–84v written in textualis semi-quadrata by the first hand. • 85r–168r written in textualis semi-quadrata by the second hand.

<handDesc hands="2">
 <summary>Written in <hi rend="italic">northern textualis</hi> by two hands, the manuscript is punctuated throughout with punctus elevatus, punctus interrogativus, and punctus, the latter used to mark metrum, minor pauses, and the ends of verses.</summary>
 <handNote xml:id="hand1" scope="major" script="textualisNorthern" medium="ink">
  <p>
   <locus from="4r" to="84v">4r–84v</locus> written in <term type="script">textualis semi-quadrata</term> by the first hand.</p>
 </handNote>
 <handNote xml:id="hand2" scope="major" script="textualisNorthern" medium="ink">
  <p>
   <locus from="85r" to="168r">85r–168r</locus> written in <term type="script">textualis semi-quadrata</term> by the second hand.</p>
 </handNote>
</handDesc>

Psalter of Joan of Navarre: Latin MS 22

• Script:

Written in kurrent (Kurrentschrift) by four hands.

• The first and largest section of the letter is written in the hand of Heinrich's father, Herrmann Simon.

• The second section is written in the hand of Heinrich's Heinrich's mother, Minna Simon.

• The third section is written in the hand of Heinrich's brother, Gustav Simon.

• The fourth section is written in the hand of Heinrich's sister, Julie Simon.

<handDesc hands="4">
 <summary>Written in <term type="script">kurrent</term> (Kurrentschrift) by four hands.</summary>
 <handNote xml:id="hand1" scope="major" script="kurrent">
  <p>The first and largest section of the letter is written in the hand of Heinrich's father, Herrmann Simon.</p>
 </handNote>
 <handNote xml:id="hand2" scope="minor" script="kurrent">
  <p>The second section is written in the hand of Heinrich's Heinrich's mother, Minna Simon.</p>
 </handNote>
 <handNote xml:id="hand3" scope="minor" script="kurrent">
  <p>The third section is written in the hand of Heinrich's brother, Gustav Simon.</p>
 </handNote>
 <handNote xml:id="hand4" scope="minor" script="kurrent">
  <p>The fourth section is written in the hand of Heinrich's sister, Julie Simon. </p>
 </handNote>
</handDesc>

Letter to Heinrich Simon from Herrmann, Minna, Gustav, and Julie Simon: SIM/2/142

In the example above, the names are not encoded because this is a correspondence letter. The senders' names are already properly encoded as authors within <correspDesc> under <correspAction type="send"><name role="aut">...</name>.

More than one distinct hand has been identified, with more than one script used across the manuscript.

• Script:

Written by three different hands. Headings are in red and black ink, sometimes omitted

• 1b-6b written in black ink in a neat naskh script. • 7a-118b written in black ink in nasta‘līq script.

• 119a-309a written in black ink in a different, more legible nasta‘līq script.

<handDesc hands="3">
 <summary>Written by three different hands. Headings are in red and black ink, sometimes omitted.</summary>
 <handNote xml:id="hand1" scope="minor" script="naskh" medium="ink">
  <p>
   <locus from="1b" to="6b">1b-6b</locus> written in black ink in a neat <term type="script">naskh</term> script.</p>
 </handNote>
 <handNote xml:id="hand2" scope="minor" script="nastaliq" medium="ink">
  <p>
   <locus from="7a" to="118b">7a-118b</locus> written in black ink in <term type="script">nasta‘līq</term> script.</p>
 </handNote>
 <handNote xml:id="hand3" scope="major" script="nastaliq" medium="ink">
  <p>
   <locus from="119a" to="309a">119a-309a</locus> written in black ink in a different, more legible <term type="script">nasta‘līq</term> script.</p>
 </handNote>
</handDesc>

Galen's On the Usefulness of the Parts of the Body: Arabic MS 780

Where the scribe is known, their name should be encoded using <name role="scr"><persName/></name>. If the personal name is listed in VIAF, include both source and key on <name> to reference the appropriate VIAF authority record. Where an authority-controlled form is available, or where a distinction between display and indexed forms is required, include both <persName type="display"> and <persName type="standard"> within <name>. Otherwise, a single <persName> within <name> may be used.

• Scribe(s): Paolo

—

• Script:

• Written in textualis rotunda by a single hand, identified in the colophon on 174r as the work of the scribe Paolo.

<handDesc hands="1">
 <handNote scope="sole" script="textualisSouthern" medium="ink">
  <p>Written in <term type="script">textualis rotunda</term> by a single hand, identified in the colophon on <locus from="174r" to="174r">174r</locus> as the work of the scribe <name role="scr">
    <persName>Paolo</persName>
   </name>.</p>
 </handNote>
</handDesc>

Il Canzoniere di Petrarca e Le Rime di Dante: Italian MS 1

Where it is not feasible to encode a scribe within <handDesc>, for example, when their name appears in multiple forms due to transliteration, translation, or script variation, the use of <editor> within <msItem> is acceptable. In such instances, <editor role="scr"> follows the same principles outlined in the section on <author> name variants <!--insert hyperlink to that section -->.

The following encoding is incorrect because it combines multiple scripts within a single <handNote>.

<handDesc>
 <handNote scope="major" script="nastaliq naskh" medium="ink">Written in very clear, neat black nasta‘līq script, with Arabic passages in naskh, often in red, as well as other subheaders.
 </handNote>
</handDesc>

Each script should be described in its own <handNote>. The main text may be encoded in a <handNote scope="major">, while the naskh script used for Arabic passages, headings, or other subsidiary text should be described in a separate <handNote scope="minor">.

<handNote> @medium

medium describes the type of ink or other writing medium.

medium on <handNote> may be used to record the writing medium employed by a particular hand. Its values identify the substance used, most commonly ink, but may also refer to other writing materials such as pencil, where this was used for the main hand.

In most cases, it is not necessary to specify ink colour. Ink may be assumed unless there is a specific reason to identify the colour or type of ink. Where it is necessary to specify colour or type, and where this is known with certainty, medium value should be encoded in camelCase, without spaces, for example, inkBlack for colour or inkIronGall for ink type.

In addition to medium, <term type="medium"> may optionally be included within the prose content of <handNote>. This is useful for historically specific, culturally specific, or source-specific terminology, such as India ink, sumi ink. When available, <term type="medium"> may include source and key with the relevant AAT identifier.

• Script:

• Written in carbon black, India ink.

<handDesc hands="1">
 <handNote scope="sole" medium="inkCarbonBlack">
  <p>Written in carbon black, <term type="medium" source="#aat" key="300015018">India ink</term>.</p>
 </handNote>
</handDesc>

Rubrication and ink colours

Aspects of the item relating to the presentation or structuring of the text, such as rubrication, should not be encoded in medium. Rubrication refers to the use of coloured inks, not limited to red, to mark headings, structural divisions, or other paratextual elements. These should instead be described in <decoDesc> using <decoNote type="rubrication">.

• Script:

• Written in black ink in a neat naskh script.

—

• Decoration:

Subheadings and instructions written in red ink.

<handDesc hands="1">
 <handNote scope="sole" script="naskh" medium="inkBlack">
  <p>Written in <term type="medium" source="#aat" key="300015012">black ink</term> in a neat <term type="script">naskh</term> script.</p>
 </handNote>
</handDesc>
<!-- additional metadata -->
<decoDesc>
 <decoNote type="rubrication">
  <p>Subheadings and instructions written in <term type="medium">red ink</term>.</p>
 </decoNote>
</decoDesc>

Non-ink media, such as pencil or crayon, may be recorded in medium where they are used by the hand being described. Later additions, annotations, corrections, or other interventions, where they are not part of the original scribal hand responsible for the main text, should normally be described in <additions> or elsewhere within <physDesc>, as appropriate.

The following medium and <term type="medium"> values are recommended, together with AAT identifiers where an appropriate match is available.

medium

<term type="medium">

ink

ink

<term type="medium" source="#aat" key="300015012">ink</term>

inkBlack

black ink

<term type="medium">black ink</term>

inkIronGall

iron gall ink

<term type="medium" source="#aat" key="300015012">iron gall ink</term>

inkCarbonBlack

carbon black ink

India ink

Chinese ink

sumi ink

<term type="medium" source="#aat" key="300081180">carbon black ink</term>
<term type="medium" source="#aat" key="300015018">India ink</term>
<term type="medium" source="#aat" key="300015012">Chinese ink</term>
<term type="medium" source="#aat" key="300015012">sumi ink</term>

inkSepia

sepia ink

<term type="medium" source="#aat" key="300015025">sepia ink</term>

1.55 <typeDesc> | typeface description | <typeNote> | typographic note

<typeDesc> (typeface description) contains a description of the typefaces or other aspects of the printing of an incunable or other printed source.

<typeNote> (typographic note) describes a particular font or other significant typographic feature distinguished within the description of a printed resource.

MDC display label — Typeface

<typeNote> within <typeDesc> are used to describe the typographic features of a printed item, in a manner analogous to <handDesc> and <handNote>, which describe hands and scripts.

<typeNote> includes scope, and just as <handNote> may include script and <term type="script">, in the context of printed items <typeNote> may include typeface and <term type="typeface"> to record the typeface.

@typeface

typeface characterises the particular typeface or letterform design used by the printer.

typeface is used for consistent, high-level typeface classification, following approved nomenclature. The following controlled values are suggested for typeface. Values may be further characterised using <term type="typeface"> within descriptive prose in <p>.

typeface

<term type="typeface">

blackletter

blackletter type

<term type="typeface">blackletter type</term>

Gothic-derived printing types.

roman

roman type

<term type="typeface">roman type</term>

italic

italic type

<term type="typeface">italic type</term>

fraktur

fraktur type

<term type="typeface">fraktur type</term>

German blackletter type, distinct from generic blackletter.

civilite

civilité type

<term type="typeface">civilité type</term>

French cursive type, used mainly for French-language works, primers, proclamations

financiere

financière type

<term type="typeface">financière type</term>

French cursive type associated with vernacular printing.

other

N/A

For clearly identifiable typefaces outside the current controlled vocabulary.

unknown

N/A

Used when the typeface cannot be securely identified.

Printers are normally encoded using <editor role="prt">, particularly in MARC-derived metadata and retroconversion workflows. Where a printer is directly relevant to the typographic description, their name may also be encoded within <typeface>, following the same convention as in <handNote>, using <name role="prt"><persName></name>. Printers may also be recorded elsewhere, for example, within <origin> using <note type= "printersDevice">, as printer's devices are generally treated as evidence for the book's printer, place of production, and origin.

• Printer(s): Caxton, William, approximately 1422-1491 or 1492

—

• Typeface:

• Printed by William Caxton (approximately 1422-1491 or 1492) using Type 6:120G, a blackletter type.

<typeDesc>
 <typeNote scope="sole" typeface="blackletter">
  <p>Printed by <name source="#viaf" key="68979076" role="prt">
    <persName type="display">William Caxton (approximately 1422-1491 or 1492)</persName>
    <persName type="standard">Caxton, William, approximately 1422-1491 or 1492</persName>
   </name> using Type 6:120G, a <term type="typeface">blackletter type</term>.</p>
 </typeNote>
</typeDesc>

Within <typeDesc>, each <typeNote> should describe a single printing feature. In its simplest form, where only a single typographical characteristic is known, <typeDesc> may consist of a single <typeNote> containing <p>.

• Typeface:

• Printed in roman type.

<typeDesc>
 <typeNote scope="sole" typeface="roman">
  <p>Printed in <term type="typeface">roman type</term>.</p>
 </typeNote>
</typeDesc>

Le terze rime di Dante: 18873

A single <typeNote> is used when all the observations concern one typographical feature. In this case, the feature is the typeface, defined as the design of the type used in printing, such as roman or italic. All details, including where the italic type appears and how particular characters are set on specific leaves, relate directly to this single feature. Because the notes describe only the use and behaviour of the italic typeface, they belong together within one <typeNote>.

• Typeface:

• Printed in italic type. Line 3 of the title on [pi1r] ends De-, and line 1 on [pi2r] ends et.

<typeDesc>
 <typeNote scope="sole" typeface="italic">
  <p>Printed in <term type="typeface">italic type</term>. Line 3 of the title on
  <locus from="[pi1r]" to="[pi1r]">[pi1r]</locus> ends <hi rend="italic">De=</hi>, and line 2 on <locus from="[pi2r]" to="[pi2r]">pi2r</locus> begins <hi rend="italic">et</hi>.</p>
 </typeNote>
</typeDesc>

Dante col sito, et forma dell'inferno tratta dalla istessa descrittione del poeta: R213743

A single <typeNote> may record both the typeface, defined as the design of the type used in printing, such as roman or italic, and any variant states arising during printing, such as omissions, stop-press corrections, or substitutions in the typesetting. Combining these observations keeps the typographical information for a given impression together within <typeDesc>.

• Typeface:

• Printed in italic type.

Variant A: three lines in Purgatorio II, 64-66 on V7r have been omitted.

Variant B: the missing lines in Purgatorio II, 64-66 on V7r have been added by hand-printing.

<typeDesc>
 <typeNote scope="sole" typeface="italic">
  <p>Printed in <term type="typeface">italic type</term>.</p>
  <p>Variant A: three lines in Purgatorio II, 64-66 on <locus from="v7r" to="V7r">V7r</locus> have been omitted.</p>
  <p>Variant B: the missing lines in Purgatorio II, 64-66 on <locus from="v7r" to="V7r">V7r</locus> have been added by hand-printing.</p>
 </typeNote>
</typeDesc>

La Comedia di Dante Aligieri [sic] con la noua espositione di Alessandro Vellutello : R52857

<typeDesc> may include <summary> with one or more <p> giving a general account of the typography, such as noting a mixture of roman or italic types, followed by separate <typeNote> for each typographical feature. Where appropriate, <locus> may be used to indicate where each typeface occurs. Where this level of detail is unavailable, as is often the case with MARC-derived metadata, it is acceptable to use a single <typeNote> to record the typographical information supplied by the source record.

• Typeface:

• Printed in italic type, italic type and roman type.

<typeDesc>
 <typeNote>
  <p>Printed <term type="typeface">roman type</term> and <term type="typeface">italic type</term>.</p>
 </typeNote>
</typeDesc>

Dante con nuoue, et utili ispositioni. Aggiuntoui di più una ... : 611

Unless stated in the MARC, record only the typeface of the main text and ignore brief or peripheral sections set in a different typeface. If the core text is set in one typeface, such as italic, and smaller sections not integral to the main text, such as headings, title-page words, privileges, colophons, or similar paratexts, appear in another typeface, only the typeface of the main text is recorded. These minor sections do not constitute separate typographical features.

Where information is required about both typography and written script, <handDesc> and <typeDesc> should be supplied. The same applies when the source is a typescript that includes significant handwritten content.

• Typeface:

• Written in kurrent (Kurrentschrift), in the hand of an unidentified school teacher.

• Signature in the hand of Herrmann Simon.

• Printed in fraktur (Frakturschrift).

<handDesc hands="2">
 <handNote xml:id="hand1" scope="major" script="kurrent">
  <p>Written in <term type="script">kurrent</term> (Kurrentschrift), in the hand of an unidentified school teacher.</p>
 </handNote>
 <handNote xml:id="hand2" scope="minor" script="kurrent">
  <p>Signature in the hand of Herrmann Simon.</p>
 </handNote>
</handDesc>
<typeDesc>
 <typeNote>
  <p>Printed <term type="typeface">fraktur</term> (Frakturschrift).</p>
 </typeNote>
</typeDesc>

Weekly school report for Heinrich Simon from the Königliches Friedrichs-Gymnasium zu Breslau: SIM/1/1a

1.56 <musicNotation> | music notation

<musicNotation> (music notation) contains description of type of musical notation.

MDC display label — Music notation

<musicNotation> is used to record any musical notation found in an item. It may describe the type of notation, such as neumatic, mensural, modern, or staffless notation, its appearance or layout, such as a four-line staff, red lines, or square notation, or its function within the item.

Music notation should be described in <p>, using <locus> where relevant. Where more than one type of notation is present, use a separate <p> for each type.

• Music notation:

Black square notation on 3- and 4-line red staves on 4r, 8v-12r, 19v, 26r-29r, 44v, 55r, 65r-68v, 80v-83v, 85v-86r.

<musicNotation>
 <p>Black square notation on 3- and 4-line red staves on <locus from="4r" to="4r">4r</locus>, <locus from="8v" to="12r">8v-12r</locus>, <locus from="19v" to="19v">19v</locus>, <locus from="26r" to="29r">26r-29r</locus>, <locus from="44v" to="44v">44v</locus>, <locus from="55r" to="55r">55r</locus>, <locus from="65r" to="68v">65r-68v</locus>, <locus from="80v" to="83v">80v-83v</locus>, <locus from="85v" to="86r">85v-86r</locus>.</p>
</musicNotation>

Colonna Missal II: Latin MS 32a

1.57 <decoDesc> | decoration description

<decoDesc> (decoration description) contains a description of the decoration of a manuscript or other object, either as in paragraphs, or as one or more <decoNote> elements.

MDC display label — Decoration

<decoDesc> is used to record the decorative features of an item, including visual and textual components such as illustrations, initials, borders, and rubrication.

<decoDesc> may contain <summary> with one or more <p> providing a general overview of the decorative programme, followed by a series of <decoNote>, each describing a particular aspect or class of decoration.

1.58 <decoNote> | note on decoration

<decoNote> (note on decoration) contains a note describing either a decorative component of a manuscript or other object, or a fairly homogenous class of such components.

Within <decoDesc>, one or more <decoNote> should be used to organise the decoration into discrete categories, each treated as an independent unit of description. These should be arranged in a consistent, hierarchical order, beginning with illustrations (including miniatures), followed by initials (historiated or otherwise), then borders, and concluding with rubrication and other forms of textual decoration. Where decorative features are closely integrated, this order may be adapted to reflect the structure of the decorative programme, provided that clear categorisation is maintained.

Where the decorative programme is closely integrated, however, this sequence may be adapted to reflect the structure of the material, for example, where borders are directly associated with specific initials. In such cases, the order of description should follow the most logical and coherent presentation of the decoration while maintaining clear categorisation.

Each <decoNote> should include type, and subtype should also be used where further sub-classification is required.

type | subtype

type provides the primary classification of the decorative feature.

subtype provides a further sub-classification of that feature.

When describing decoration, the following type and subtype values are recommended. All values should be encoded in the singular form, as they denote conceptual categories rather than multiple instances.

<decoNote> | type | subtype

illustrations

<decoNote type="illustration">

Any pictorial or graphic representations functioning as illustrations, applicable across different materials and disciplines. The use of type=illustration covers:

  • Figural and non-figural illustrations.
  • Ornamental full-page or marginal visual compositions.
  • Diagrammatic representations.
  • Tabular visual material described for its visual presentation rather than textual structure.
  • May be used alone or refined with a subtype.

Extent may be described in prose: full-page | half-page | quarter-page.

Placement, such as marginal or bas-de-page, should also be described in prose rather than encoded as subtype values, since they indicate spatial position or layout role rather than illustration class.

figurative illustrations

<decoNote type="illustration" subtype="figurative">

Pictorial illustrations treated as miniatures within manuscript traditions, typically an independent composition not incorporated into another decorative element such as a border or initial.

The use of subtype=figurative covers:

  • Figural or narrative illustrations depicting a scene or identifiable subject, particularly in medieval and Renaissance manuscripts.
  • Standalone pictorial compositions, including full-page and smaller illustrations.
  • Illustrations described in manuscript studies as miniatures, including medallions or roundels.

Extent may be described in prose: full-page | half-page | quarter-page.

Placement such as marginal or bas-de-page should also be described in prose.

ornamental illustrations

<decoNote type="illustration" subtype="ornamental">

Non-figurative decorative illustrations treated as independent ornamental compositions rather than narrative or diagrammatic representations.

The use of subtype=ornamental covers:

  • Non-figural decorative compositions composed primarily of foliate, geometric, interlace, or abstract ornament.
  • Carpet pages and similar full-page ornamental designs.
  • Decorative frontispieces and ornamental title pages.
  • Ornamental medallions, frames, or decorative visual programmes functioning primarily as embellishment.
  • Headpieces, consisting of decorative illustrations or ornaments placed at the beginning of a chapter, section, or major textual division, including Islamic ‘unwān or sarlawḥ headpieces.
  • Tailpieces, consisting of decorative illustrations or ornaments positioned at the end of a chapter, section, or major textual division.

Extent may be described in prose: full-page | half-page | quarter-page

Placement such as marginal or bas-de-page should also be described in prose.

diagrammatic illustrations

<decoNote type="illustration" subtype="diagrammatic">

Graphic representation used to explain or organise information visually.

The use of subtype=diagrammatic covers:

  • Cosmological, genealogical, and astronomical diagrams, and other structured visual forms.

<layoutDesc> may supplement the description where page design or spatial arrangement is significant, for example, in complex layouts such as the tree-structured diagram in Latin MS 18.

tabular

illustrations

<decoNote type="illustration" subtype="tabular">

Decorative concordance tables used in Gospel manuscripts, typically arranged in architectural frames to show parallel passages.

The use of subtype=tabular covers:

  • Canon tables and other concordance tables.
  • Tables organised primarily for comparison, reference, or systematic arrangement of information.
  • Tabular visual compositions structured through grids, columns, arcades, or compartmentalised frameworks.
  • Genealogical, calendrical, computistical, or schematic tables when described for their visual presentation rather than their textual content.

heraldic

illustrations

<decoNote type="illustration" subtype="heraldic">

Illustrations of a coat of arms or heraldic devices functioning as the primary illustrations, such as standalone heraldic devices or armorial manuscripts, for example, Armorial: Latin MS 28. Where appropriate, heraldic content may also be encoded using <heraldry>. Heraldry indicating ownership or patronage should normally be recorded in <provenance>.

historiated initials

<decoNote type="initial" subtype="historiated">

Initials in which figures participate in identifiable actions or episodes, often related to the text. The use of subtype=historiated covers:

  • Figural and narrative initials with recognisable scenes or subjects, including biblical, hagiographic, or allegorical themes.

Extent may be described in prose: 1-line | 2-line | 3-line etc.

inhabited initials

<decoNote type="initial" subtype="inhabited">

Initials in which figures of humans, animals, or hybrids occupy or embellish the letter without forming a narrative scene. The use of subtype=inhabited covers:

  • Zoomorphic, anthropomorphic, or grotesque initials.

Extent may be described in prose: 1-line | 2-line | 3-line etc.

decorated initials

<decoNote type="initial" subtype="decorated">

Initials that incorporate ornament or decorative elaboration, without narrative or integrated figural subjects. The use of subtype=decorated covers:

  • Foliate, floriated, interlaced, puzzle, pen-flourished, penwork, geometric, or epigraphic forms. Use for decorative initials that are not narrative and not inhabited by figures.
  • Versals may also be classified as decorated where they incorporate ornamental features such as pen-flourishing or other forms of elaboration.
  • Cadels are treated as decorated initials, specifically elaborate penwork initials formed of looping calligraphic flourishes, often extending into margins.

Extent may be described in prose: 1-line | 2-line | 3-line etc.

plain initials

  <decoNote type="initial" subtype="plain">

Initials with minimal or no ornamental features, typically executed in a single colour or with minimal enhancement. The use of subtype=plain covers:

  • Coloured initials, including rubricated and colour-stroked forms, as well as Lombards and versals.
  • Versals and Lombards are typological forms that can sometimes be decorative.

Extent may be described in prose: 1-line | 2-line | 3-line etc.

historiated borders

  <decoNote type="border" subtype="historiated">

Borders in which figures participate in identifiable actions or episodes, often related to the text. The use of subtype=historiated covers:

  • Figural and narrative borders with recognisable scenes or subjects, including biblical, hagiographic, or allegorical themes.

Extent may be described in prose: 4-sided | 3-sided | 2-sided | 1-sided | intercolumnar.

inhabited borders

  <decoNote type="border" subtype="inhabited">

Borders in which figures of humans, animals, or hybrids occupy or embellish the border without forming a narrative scene. The use of subtype=inhabited covers:

  • Zoomorphic, anthropomorphic, or grotesque borders.

Extent may be described in prose: 4-sided | 3-sided | 2-sided | 1-sided | intercolumnar.

decorated borders

  <decoNote type="border" subtype="decorated">

Borders with ornamental features and no narrative or integrated figural subjects. The use of subtype=decorated covers:

  • Bar borders consisting of painted vertical or horizontal bands with sprouting foliage or tendrils
  • Foliate or floral borders composed of stylised or naturalistic vines, leaves, flowers, and gold bezants, including spray borders and white vine-stem borders.
  • Strewn, scatter, or trompe l'oeil borders with naturalistic flora, fruit, insects, or small animals arranged decoratively across the margins.
  • Heraldic borders featuring coats of arms, badges, or dynastic symbols used ornamentally.
  • Friezes treated as borders, consisting of horizontal ornamental bands running along one or more margins.

Extent may be described in prose: 4-sided | 3-sided | 2-sided | 1-sided | intercolumnar.

plain borders

  <decoNote type="border" subtype="plain">

Borders with minimal or no ornamental features. The use of subtype=plain covers:

  • Simple bar borders consisting of painted vertical or horizontal bands without additional ornament.
  • Borders in panelled form functioning as simple frames with minimal or no decorative extension.
  • Framing borders in printed books, including woodcut or engraved surrounds, where these remain structurally simple.

Extent may be described in prose: 4-sided | 3-sided | 2-sided | 1-sided | intercolumnar.

rubrication

  <decoNote type="rubrication">

Rubrication is the use of coloured ink, most commonly red but also other colours, to highlight headings, instructions, or structural elements within a text. The use of type=rubrication covers:

Rubrication in red ink as well as other colours such as blue, green, and purple.

  • Chrysography (writing in gold).
  • Headings or section titles.
  • Running titles or marginal indicators.
  • Paraph marks (¶ - pilcrows) indicating textual divisions.
  • Verse or section markers.

The medium and colour of execution may be described in prose using <term type="medium">. Common type=medium values include:

inkRed standard rubrication in red ink

inkBlue rubrication in blue ink

inkGreen rubrication in green ink

inkPurple rubrication in purple ink

inkGold rubrication in gold ink (chrysography)

inkSilver rubrication in silver ink

Rubricated initials are not classified here but are encoded under plain initials, with rubrication noted in the description.

line fillers

<decoNote type="lineFiller">

Decorative devices used to fill unused space at the end of a line of text.

The use of type=lineFiller covers:

  • Penwork, geometric, floral, zoomorphic, anthropomorphic, or grotesque motifs.

manicules

<decoNote type="manicule">

Hand-shaped pointing symbols used to draw attention to a passage. Manicules (manicula) are primarily functional rather than decorative. They should be recorded in <decoNote> only when they form part of the manuscript’s original execution and are integrated into its visual or decorative programme, for example, Sammlung Alchymistischer Schriften: German MS 3.

Manicules added by later readers should normally be recorded in <additions>, unless they take the form of elaborate or ornamental drawings that function as decorative elements.

missing

subtype=missing

A decoration that was originally executed but is no longer present, as indicated by physical traces such as cutting, erasure, abrasion, or other forms of loss.

unfilled

subtype=unfilled

A decoration that was planned but never executed, as indicated by reserved space, guide letters, or layout preparation, without evidence of completion.

other

subtype=other

Any decorative feature not covered by the controlled categories above. This value should be used sparingly and only where no existing controlled subtype is appropriate.

<decoNote> may begin with one or more <p> summarising the features of the decoration within that category. These paragraphs may include information such as the total number of components, their scale, illumination or colour, and any attribution to an artist, workshop, or school.

Numbers should be written in words when they express narrative or descriptive quantities within prose. This distinction reflects the difference between discursive description and standardised descriptive notation: counts such as five miniatures, thirty-two leaves, or one hundred and seventy-nine initials are treated as part of the narrative and written in words, whereas fixed descriptive forms relating to line-height, such as 3-line, 5-line, and 7-line initials, are expressed using numerals for clarity and consistency.

Any name associated with the production or execution of the decoration should be encoded using <name>, containing either <persName> or <orgName> as appropriate, and must include role indicating the nature of the relationship to the decoration. If the name is listed in VIAF, include both source and key on <name> to reference the relevant VIAF authority record. Where both display and standard forms are required, include two <persName> or <orgName> within <name>, one with type=display and one with type=standard. Otherwise, a single <persName> or <orgName> within <name> may be used.

When the value #viaf is used for source, it indicates that the identifier recorded in key is drawn from this authority file. The prefix # indicates a reference to an identifier defined elsewhere in <msDesc>. In this case, it refers to the <taxonomy> declared in the header with xml:id=viaf. This value can be selected from a controlled dropdown list when entering the attribute and does not need to be typed manually.

role specifies the relationship of a person or organisation to the decoration. In most cases, this value will be art, used for artists. Other MARC relator codes, such as ilu for illuminators, ill for illustrators, or egr for engravers, may be used where appropriate, and additional values may also be applied as required.

<list> may be used within <decoNote> to record several decorative features of the same category. This is appropriate when, for example, a series of miniatures or historiated initials appears throughout the item. Each feature should be encoded as an <item>, and each <item> should contain <locus> with from and to indicating the folio or page on which the feature appears.

<locus> should contain only the foliation or pagination value, without additional descriptive text or punctuation. Terms such as Folio, fol., f., page, or p., and punctuation such as a trailing colon (:), should not be encoded either within <locus> or immediately adjacent to it within <item>.

<list> should not be enclosed within an introductory <p>. Instead, it should appear as a separate element, maintaining a clear distinction between continuous prose and the list structure. Within <item>, one or more <p> may be used where the content contains meaningful internal divisions.

The use of <head> within <list> is optional. Where a visible category label or heading is required, it may instead be supplied in a preceding <p> using <hi rend="bold">. <head> should be used only where it adds genuine structural value.

<head> may be used within <list> to provide a heading for the list. Where a heading is required but no <list> is being used, a preceding <p> containing <hi rend="bold"> may be used instead.

Where <decoNote> contains multiple comparable entries, <list> is normally preferred. Where only a single entry is required, or where the description is better expressed in continuous prose, one or more <p> may be used instead.

• Illuminator(s): Simone Camaldolese, Don, active 1378-1405

—

• Decoration:

The illuminated decoration on 3r, 105r, and 149r is attributed to Don Simone Camaldolese (active 1378-1405), also known as Simone da Siena. A monk of the Camaldolite Order at the Monastery of Santa Maria degli Angeli in Florence, Don Simone was a prominent miniaturist and illuminator of the late fourteenth century, primarily active in Florence and Tuscany. His distinctive style has led art historian Ada Labriola (2012) to attribute these specific folios to him, noting strong stylistic connections with other manuscripts he is known to have illuminated, including the following works:

Divina commedia (Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library: Beinecke MS 458) Divina commedia (Biblioteca Apostolica Vaticana: Vat.lat. 4776) Canzoniere (Biblioteca Nazionale Centrale di Firenze: MS Pal. 184) Genealogia deorum gentilium (The Hanna Holborn Gray Special Collections Research Center: Codex MS 100)

At the beginning of each major section, three historiated initials depicting the poets are accompanied by full border decorations portraying their inamoratas. The arms of the Florentine Strozzi family are emblazoned in the lower borders of the first two.

Historiated initials

In vita di madonna Laura

3r 9-line U | V[oi chascoltate] | Petrarch, hooded and in blue, green, and red robes, is sitting at a desk, holding a quill or stylus in his right hand and writing in an open book. He is pierced by an arrow, symbolically shot by Cupid or Eros.

In morte di madonna Laura

105r 6-line I[vo pensando] | Petrarch, hooded and in blue, green, and red robes, is standing with his hands gently clasped.

In lode di Beatrice

149r 5-line C[osi nel mio] | Dante, hooded and in pink, blue, and red robes, is sitting at a desk, holding a quill or stylus in his right hand and writing in an open book.

Historiated borders

In vita di madonna Laura

3r 4-sided border filled with vibrant floral and foliate motifs in blue, red, green, and gold. In the upper border, the winged god of Love, Cupid or Eros, is holding a bow and aiming an arrow at Petrarch. In the outer border, Laura, crowned with laurel and in a flowing pink or violet gown, is standing, pierced by an arrow shot by Cupid or Eros. In the lower border, a heraldic shield contains the coat of arms of the House of Strozzi.

In morte di madonna Laura

105r 4-sided border filled with vibrant floral and foliate motifs in blue, red, green, and gold. In the outer border, Laura, crowned with laurel and in a flowing blue gown, is lying on her deathbed with her head resting on a pillow and her arms crossed. In the upper border, the skeletal figure of Death, with bat-like wings and in a dark grey garment, is holding a raised scythe in his right hand and a spear in his left. In the lower border, a radiant golden sun contains the coat of arms of the House of Strozzi.

In lode di Beatrice

149r 4-sided border filled with vibrant floral and foliate motifs in blue, red, green, and gold. In the outer border, Beatrice, in a flowing blue gown, is standing with her right arm bent across her chest, facing left towards Dante. In the upper border, a blue-winged figure is hovering and holding a flaming torch.

Pen-flourished initials

Each poem opens with a 2- to 3-line pen-flourished initial, alternating red and blue. Examples on 28r, 123r, and 160r.

Plain initials

Each verse begins with a 1-line plain initial highlighted with strokes of pale-yellow wash.

Rubrication

Each stanza begins with a paraph mark (pilcrow), alternating red and blue. Headings are rubricated in red ink throughout.

<decoDesc>
 <summary>
  <p>The illuminated decoration on <locus from="3r" to="3r">3r</locus>, <locus from="105r" to="105r">105r</locus>, and <locus from="149r" to="149r">149r</locus> is attributed to <name source="#viaf" key="313279739" role="ilu">
    <persName type="display">Don Simone Camaldolese (active 1378-1405), also known as Simone da Siena</persName>
    <persName type="standard">Simone Camaldolese, Don, active 1378-1405</persName>
   </name>. A monk of the Camaldolite Order at the Monastery of Santa Maria degli Angeli in Florence, Don Simone was a prominent miniaturist and illuminator of the late fourteenth century, primarily active in Florence and Tuscany. His distinctive style has led art historian Ada Labriola (2012) to attribute these specific folios to him, noting strong stylistic connections with other manuscripts he is known to have illuminated, including the following works:</p>
  <list>
   <item>Divina commedia (<ref target="https://collections.library.yale.edu/catalog/2052913">Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library: Beinecke MS 458</ref>)</item>
   <item>Divina commedia (<ref target="https://spotlight.vatlib.it/dante/catalog/Vat_lat_4776">Biblioteca Apostolica Vaticana: Vat.lat. 4776 </ref>)</item>
   <item>Canzoniere (Biblioteca Nazionale Centrale di Firenze: MS Pal. 184)</item>
   <item>Genealogia deorum gentilium (<ref target="https://www.lib.uchicago.edu/e/scrc/findingaids/view.php?eadid=ICU.SPCL.MS100#idp150024776">The Hanna Holborn Gray Special Collections 458 Research Center: Codex MS 100</ref>)</item>
  </list>
  <p>At the beginning of each major section, three historiated initials depicting the poets are accompanied by full border decorations portraying their <hi rend="italic">inamoratas</hi>. The arms of the Florentine Strozzi family are emblazoned in the lower borders of the first two. </p>
 </summary>
 <decoNote type="initial" subtype="historiated">
  <list>
   <head>Historiated initials</head>
   <item>

    </p>
    <p>
     <locus from="3r" to="3r">3r</locus> 9-line <hi rend="bold">U | V</hi>[oi chascoltate] | Petrarch, hooded and in blue, green, and red robes, is sitting at a desk, holding a quill or stylus in his right hand and writing in an open book. He is pierced by an arrow, symbolically shot by Cupid or Eros.</p>
   </item>
   <item>
    <p>
     <hi rend="italic">In morte di madonna Laura</hi>
    </p>
    <p>
     <locus from="105r" to="105r">105r</locus> 6-line <hi rend="bold">I</hi>[vo pensando] | Petrarch, hooded and in blue, green, and red robes, is standing with his hands gently clasped. </p>
   </item>
   <item>
    <p>
     <hi rend="italic">In lode di Beatrice</hi>
    </p>
    <p>
     <locus from="149r" to="149r">149r</locus> 5-line <hi rend="bold">C</hi>[osi nel mio] | Dante, hooded and in pink, blue, and red robes, is sitting at a desk, holding a quill or stylus in his right hand and writing in an open book.</p>
   </item>
  </list>
 </decoNote>
 <decoNote type="border" subtype="historiated">
  <list>
   <head>Historiated borders</head>
   <item>
    <p>
     <hi rend="italic">In vita di madonna Laura</hi>
    </p>
    <p>
     <locus from="3r" to="3r">3r</locus> 4-sided border filled with vibrant floral and foliate motifs in blue, red, green, and gold. In the upper border, the winged god of Love, Cupid or Eros, is holding a bow and aiming an arrow at Petrarch. In the outer border, Laura, crowned with laurel and in a flowing pink or violet gown, is standing, pierced by an arrow shot by Cupid or Eros. In the lower border, a heraldic shield contains the coat of arms of the House of Strozzi.</p>
   </item>
   <item>
    <p>
     <hi rend="italic">In morte di madonna Laura</hi>
    </p>
    <p>
     <locus from="105r" to="105r">105r</locus> 4-sided border filled with vibrant floral and foliate motifs in blue, red, green, and gold. In the outer border, Laura, crowned with laurel and in a flowing blue gown, is lying on her deathbed with her head resting on a pillow and her arms crossed. In the upper border, the skeletal figure of Death, with bat-like wings and in a dark grey garment, is holding a raised scythe in his right hand and a spear in his left. In the lower border, a radiant golden sun contains the coat of arms of the House of Strozzi.</p>
   </item>
   <item>
    <p>
     <hi rend="italic">In lode di Beatrice</hi>
    </p>
    <p>
     <locus from="149r" to="149r">149r</locus> 4-sided border filled with vibrant floral and foliate motifs in blue, red, green, and gold. In the outer border, Beatrice, in a flowing blue gown, is standing with her right arm bent across her chest, facing left towards Dante. In the upper border, a blue-winged figure is hovering and holding a flaming torch.</p>
   </item>
  </list>
 </decoNote>
 <decoNote type="initial" subtype="decorated">
  <p>
   <hi rend="bold">Pen-flourished initials</hi>
  </p>
  <p>Each poem opens with a 2- to 3-line pen-flourished initial, alternating red and blue. Examples on <locus from="28r" to="28r">28r</locus>, <locus from="123r" to="123r">123r</locus>, and <locus from="160r" to="160r">160r</locus>.</p>
 </decoNote>
 <decoNote type="initial" subtype="plain">
  <p>
   <hi rend="bold">Plain initials</hi>
  </p>
  <p>Each verse begins with a 1-line plain initial highlighted with strokes of pale-yellow wash.</p>
 </decoNote>
 <decoNote type="rubrication">
  <p>
   <hi rend="bold">Rubrication</hi>
  </p>
  <p>Each stanza begins with a paraph mark (pilcrow), alternating red and blue.</p>
  <p>Headings are rubricated in red ink throughout.</p>
 </decoNote>
</decoDesc>

Il Canzoniere di Petrarca e Le Rime di Dante: Italian MS 1

The following partial example comes from a retroconversion of James’s catalogue, with only minimal subsequent editorial refinement. The wording within <p> was only lightly revised at a later stage and otherwise reflects the language and terminology of that period, which is not recommended. The example also demonstrates a straightforward approach without the use of headings or labels.

• Decoration:

The principal decoration consists of full-page illustrations and borders of fluid gold, incorporating birds, notably peacocks, together with naturalistic flowers and fruit. The borders, as in the first instance, are divided into panels by gold frames, with each panel set against a ground of blue or purple-red. The style is Flemish. The illustrations are full-page except where otherwise specified.

13v Salue sanctafacies. Blue ground. Bust of Christ in purple robe holding a crystal orb with gold cross, and fleury. His head is rayed with flourished gold rays. The opposite page has full border, as in nearly all cases.

16v Hours of the Cross. The Crucifixion. The Virgin, John, and woman on left: two Jews on right. Peacocks in the border.

23v Hours of the Holy Ghost. Pentecost. The Virgin kneels at a desk in centre facing right. Apostles kneel on either side looking up at the dove.

30v Mass of the Virgin. Ground is mainly pink. The Virgin and Child, rayed, rather more than half-length, surrounded by clouds.

43v Matins of the Virgin. Annunciation. The Virgin kneels inside a building. Gabriel (outside) enters on left. The Dove on a ray.

63v Lauds. The Visitation, on a grass plot in front of a brick house with stone dressings.

77v Prime. The Virgin (left) and Joseph adore the Child. Ox and ass behind. Rays descend vertically.

83v Tierce. Two shepherds in a field with a gate. Angel in pink, and star, in the sky.

—

245r Missus est. In text. Left, against a pink ground, the Virgin and Child. Right, against a blue ground, with gold leaves, a beardless man in dark habit, in prayer.

<decoDesc>
 <decoNote type="illustration">
  <p>The principal decoration consists of full-page illustrations and borders of fluid gold, incorporating birds, notably peacocks, together with naturalistic flowers and fruit. The borders, as in the first instance, are divided into panels by gold frames, with each panel set against a ground of blue or purple-red. The style is Flemish. The illustrations are full-page except where otherwise specified.</p>
  <list>
   <item>
    <locus from="13v" to="13v">13v</locus> Salue sanctafacies. Blue ground. Bust of Christ in purple robe holding a crystal orb with gold cross, and fleury. His head is rayed with flourished gold rays. The opposite page has full border, as in nearly all cases.</item>
   <item>
    <locus from="16v" to="16v">16v</locus> Hours of the Cross. The Crucifixion. The Virgin, John, and woman on left: two Jews on right. Peacocks in the border.</item>
   <item>
    <locus from="23v" to="23v">23v</locus> Hours of the Holy Ghost. Pentecost. The Virgin kneels at a desk in centre facing right. Apostles kneel on either side looking up at the dove. </item>
   <item>
    <locus from="30v" to="30v">30v</locus> Mass of the Virgin. Ground mainly pink. The Virgin and Child, rayed, rather more than half-length, surrounded by clouds.</item>
   <item>
    <locus from="43v" to="43v">43v</locus> Matins of the Virgin. Annunciation. The Virgin kneels inside a building. Gabriel (outside) enters on left. The Dove on a ray.</item>
   <item>
    <locus from="63v" to="63v">63v</locus> Lauds. The Visitation, on a grass plot in front of a brick house with stone dressings.</item>
   <item>
    <locus from="77v" to="77v">77v</locus> Prime. The Virgin (left) and Joseph adore the Child. Ox and ass behind. Rays descend vertically.</item>
   <item>
    <locus from="83v" to="83v">83v</locus> Tierce. Two shepherds in a field with a gate. Angel in pink, and star, in the sky.</item>
<!-- additional metadata -->
   <item>
    <locus from="245r" to="245r">245r</locus> Missus est. In text. Left, against a pink ground, the Virgin and Child. Right, against a blue ground, with gold leaves, a beardless man in dark habit, in prayer.</item>
  </list>
 </decoNote>
</decoDesc>

Horae (Mary, Queen of Scots): Latin MS 21

An additional level of encoding may be applied where iconographic detail is relevant. In such cases, the iconographic subjects of illustrations may be classified and encoded using <term type="iconography">. source and key may be used to reference a controlled vocabulary or thesaurus, such as Iconclass, which provides a universal classification system for the subject matter of works of art.

iconclass

Iconclass

Iconclass is a specialised classification system used to describe and organise the subject matter of images in art and visual culture. Developed by the Dutch art historian Henri van de Waal, it is one of the largest and most widely used systems for iconography, containing over 28,000 concepts arranged in a hierarchical structure. Each subject is represented by an alphanumeric code, allowing images to be indexed and retrieved by their content. The system is divided into ten main categories, such as Religion, Nature, History, and the Bible, and is widely used by museums, libraries, and researchers to standardise the description and analysis of visual materials

<classDecl>
 <taxonomy xml:id="iconclass">
  <bibl>
   <ref target="https://iconclass.org">Iconclass</ref>
  </bibl>
 </taxonomy>
</classDecl>

For example, folio 63v of Latin MS 21 contains an illustration of the Visitation, which may be encoded as follows.

• Decoration:

—

63v Lauds. The Visitation, on a grass plot in front of a brick house with stone dressings.

—

<decoDesc>
 <decoNote type="illustration">
<!-- additional metadata -->
  <list>
<!-- additional metadata -->
   <item>
    <locus from="63v" to="63v">63v</locus> Lauds. The <term type="iconography" source="#iconclass" key="73A6">Visitation</term>, on a grass plot in front of a brick house with stone dressings.</item>
<!-- additional metadata -->
  </list>
 </decoNote>
</decoDesc>

Horae (Mary, Queen of Scots): Latin MS 21

When the value #iconclass is used for source, it indicates that the identifier recorded in key is drawn from this authority. The prefix # signals a reference to an identifier defined elsewhere in the same TEI file. In this case, it points to <taxonomy> declared in the header with xml:id=iconclass. This value can be selected from a controlled dropdown list when entering the attribute and does not need to be typed manually.

General remarks: encoding principles and conventions

Miniature is a specialist art-historical term referring to a particular type of painted image and should not be used as a controlled classification value. Not all illustrations are miniatures, and the term is only applicable in specific descriptive contexts. Where a decorative feature is identified as a miniature, this should be described in the prose of <decoNote>, while type=illustration should continue to be used for classification.

subtype for initials and borders is restricted to four controlled values: historiated, decorated, inhabited, and plain. These provide a stable top‑level typology, ensuring that each initial is assigned to a single conceptual category. Further variation, such as puzzle, zoomorphic, anthropomorphic, pen-flourished, or Lombard forms, should be described in the prose of <decoNote> and should not be encoded as additional subtype values.

Motifs and detailed decorative features may also be encoded in <decoNote> using type, subtype, and style, and may additionally be encoded with <term type="motif"> where appropriate. More generally, <term> may be used to supply controlled terminology for decorative forms, motifs, materials, or techniques when greater specificity is required.

Terms referring to techniques, such as drawing, sketch, illumination, woodcut, and similar production methods, should not be encoded as attribute values. These describe how a decoration was executed, not what category it belongs to. Such information may be described in prose and encoded using <note type="technique"> or <term type="technique"> where appropriate.

Micrography is a scribal technique and should not be treated as a decorative category.

Values such as none, unknown, and similar placeholders must not be used in the controlled vocabulary. The values mixed and unspecified must not be used in <decoNote>. These do not represent valid decorative categories and introduce ambiguity.

rend and rendition must not be used within <decoDesc> or elsewhere in <teiHeader>. Decorative and visual features described in the header should instead be encoded through the appropriate descriptive elements and attributes. rend and rendition should be used only for transcription within <text>, <body>, and related elements.

<decoNote> within <decoDesc> is reserved for describing decorative features. Where decoration is specific to binding, seals, provenance, or other aspects of the item, it should be encoded using the appropriate TEI elements. For example, decoration associated with bindings should be encoded within <bindingDesc>, where <decoNote> may optionally be used within <binding>. Decoration associated with seals should be encoded within <sealDesc>, where <decoNote> may optionally be used within <seal>. Paratextual features such as the printer's device should be encoded within <origin> using <note type="printersDevice">.

For descriptive terminology, the following resources may be consulted:

Brown, Michelle P, et al. Understanding Illuminated Manuscripts : A Guide to Technical Terms, Revised Edition. Getty Trust Publications, 2018.

Beal, Peter. A Dictionary of English Manuscript Terminology, 1450 to 2000. Oxford University Press, 2008.

Hall, James. Dictionary of Subjects and Symbols in Art. Rev. ed., John Murray, 1997.

Hill Museum & Manuscript Library | HMML School Lexicon. Terms used in manuscript studies.

Getty Research Institute. Getty Art & Architecture Thesaurus® (AAT)

1.59 <additions> | additions

<additions> (additions) contains a description of any significant additions found within a manuscript or other object, such as marginalia or other annotations.

MDC display label — Additions

<additions> is used to record any additions introduced into or around the main text, whether during production or at later points in its history. These may be scribal, such as corrections by the original scribe or a corrector, or non-scribal, such as later annotations, informal markings, or censorship.

The following terms may be used to describe common forms of addition, correction, or textual intervention:

marginal insertion text added in the margin

interlinear insertion text added between existing lines

erasure text scraped, rubbed away, washed, or otherwise removed

cancellation or strikethrough text marked for deletion by being crossed out

expunctuation dots placed beneath letters or words to mark deletion

overwriting new writing added over existing writing

transformation letters or words altered into another form

superimposition new writing placed over earlier writing

Other additions and marks may include marginalia, annotations, scribblings, doodles, manicules, drawings, pen-trials, underlining, ink stamps, censorial marks, and other evidence of use. Censorial interventions may include text deliberately obscured with ink or otherwise made difficult to read. Guiding marks, such as caret signs or signes-de-renvoi, may also be recorded where they indicate the placement of supplementary text.

These categories may overlap. For example, marginal additions, erasures, underlining, or annotations may be contemporary with the main text or added later by a reader, owner, or annotator. Where possible, describe the nature of the intervention and its relationship to the main text, including date, hand, or function when this can be determined.

Identifiable ownership inscriptions, stamps, or other provenance marks should be recorded in <provenance> within <history>, with only unattributable marks remaining in <additions>. Non-contemporaneous additional material that accompanies the item but is not part of its physical structure should be described in <accMat>, not in <additions>.

Any additions made to the item itself should be recorded in <additions>, with each type described in a separate <p> and <locus> where relevant.

• Additions:

Omission of text by means of strikethrough and superscription on 75v, 81v.

Erasure of text and overwriting on 24r, 26r, 28r, 34r, 75r, and 84v.

Caret marks (˄) and interlineations or marginal additions on 44r, 47r, 61v, and 84r.

Erasures indicating post-Reformation political and religious censorship. The text has been scraped or rubbed off or crossed (blacked) out with ink on 4r-7v and 168v.

Marginal annotation on 85r, mainly extracting keywords, possibly in a fifteenth-century English hand.

The marginal numbering of the Psalms appears to correspond consistently with the numbering system found in the Hebrew Masoretic text (MT). It remains challenging to ascertain whether these numbers were added during the original production process of the manuscript or introduced by subsequent owners.

<additions>
 <p>Omission of text by means of strikethrough and superscription on <locus from="75v" to="75v">75v</locus> and <locus from="81v" to="81v">81v</locus>.</p>
 <p>Erasure of text and overwriting on <locus from="24r" to="24r">24r</locus>, <locus from="26r" to="26r">26r</locus>, <locus from="28r" to="28r">28r</locus>, <locus from="34r" to="34r">34r</locus>, <locus from="75r" to="75r">75r</locus>, and <locus from="84v" to="84v">84v</locus>.</p>
 <p>Caret marks (˄) and interlineations or marginal additions on <locus from="44r" to="44r">44r</locus>, <locus from="47r" to="47r">47r</locus>, <locus from="61v" to="61v">61v</locus>, and <locus from="84r" to="84r">84r</locus>.</p>
 <p>Erasures indicating post-Reformation political and religious censorship. The text has been scraped or rubbed off or crossed (blacked) out with ink on <locus from="4r" to="7v">4r-7v</locus> and <locus from="168v" to="168v">168v</locus>.</p>
 <p>Marginal annotation on <locus from="85r" to="85r">85r</locus>, mainly extracting keywords, possibly in a fifteenth-century English hand.</p>
 <p>The marginal numbering of the Psalms appears to correspond consistently with the numbering system found in the Hebrew Masoretic text (MT). It remains challenging to ascertain whether these numbers were added during the original production process of the manuscript or introduced by subsequent owners.</p>
</additions>

Psalter of Joan of Navarre: Latin MS 22

• Additions:

A notable instance of 'indulgence inflation' can be observed on 95r, where a small paper slip has been pasted over one word in the prayer (line 4) to increase the indulgence from seven to ten years: ...omnibus hic presentibus decem annos ....

<additions>
 <p>A notable instance of 'indulgence inflation' can be observed on <locus from="95r" to="95r">95r</locus>, where a small paper slip has been pasted over one word in the prayer (line 4) to increase the indulgence from seven to ten years: <hi rend="italic">...omnibus hic presentibus <hi rend="italic">decem</hi> annos ...</hi>
 </p>
</additions>

Colonna Missal II: Latin MS 32a

In printed materials, <additions> is used to record manuscript notes, inscriptions, markings, and other handwritten additions found on the item that cannot be confidently attributed to <provenance>.

• Additions:

Manuscript pencil note on Flyleaf 1v: Character of G. Huyon A | 1520?.

Various manuscript pencil prices[?] on Flyleaf 1v: £2.2.0 | 0 | 3/[?] | 1/4/2[?].

Manuscript pencil note on a1r: [?]De[?]dras[?].

<additions>
 <p>Manuscript pencil note on <locus from="Flyleaf_1v" to="Flyleaf_1v">Flyleaf 1v</locus>: <hi rend="italic">Character of G. Huyon A | 1520?</hi>.</p>
 <p>Various manuscript pencil prices[?] on <locus from="Flyleaf_1v" to="Flyleaf_1v">Flyleaf 1v</locus>: <hi rend="italic">£2.2.0 | 0 | 3/[?] | 1/4/2[?]</hi>.</p>
 <p>Manuscript inscription, mostly illegible, on the lower margin of <locus from="a1r" to="a1r">a1r</locus>: <hi rend="italic">[?]De[?]dras[?]</hi>.</p>
</additions>

Le terze rime di Dante: 18873

1.60 <bindingDesc> | binding description

<bindingDesc> (binding description) describes the present and former bindings of a manuscript or other object, either as a series of paragraphs or as a series of distinct <binding> elements, one for each binding of the manuscript.

MDC display label — Binding

<bindingDesc> contains information about the binding of a manuscript, printed book, or other codex-form item. This may include a description of a single binding or a sequence of bindings the item has received over time. It typically contains at least one <binding> describing a specific binding at a given point in time, and may record details such as the type of covering, the boards, structural features, decoration, condition, and physical dimensions.

1.61 <binding> | binding

<binding> (binding) contains a description of one binding, i.e. type of covering, boards, etc. applied to a manuscript or other object.

<binding> within <bindingDesc> should describe a single physical binding, normally using one or more <p>. TEI does not prescribe an internal sequence for this prose, so projects should adopt a consistent order to keep binding descriptions readable and comparable across files. The following order is recommended, moving from identification through materials, structure, and decoration, and concluding with condition (including any repairs or alterations) and the physical dimensions of the binding.

<binding> should include contemporary and at least one dating attribute, such as when, notBefore, notAfter, or from, to, to indicate the date the binding was created.

@contemporary

contemporary (contemporary) specifies whether or not the binding is contemporary with its contents.

The following values should be used:

true the binding is contemporary with its contents.

false the binding is not contemporary with its contents.

unknown it is not known whether the binding is contemporary with its contents.

When using notBefore and notAfter, ensure that the values reflect the correct start and end boundaries of the period being described, following the same dating conventions used for <origDate>. For example, a nineteenth-century binding that is not contemporary with its contents should be encoded as <binding @contemporary= "false" notBefore="1801" and notAfter="1900">, not as 1800–1899 or 1800–1900.

Where the binder is known, their name should be encoded using <name role="bnd">, containing either <persName> or <orgName> as appropriate. If the name is listed in VIAF, include both source and key on <name> to reference the appropriate VIAF authority record. Where both display and standard forms are required, include two <persName> or <orgName> within <name>, one with type=display and one with type=standard. Otherwise, a single <persName> or <orgName> within <name> may be used.

The physical dimensions of the binding should be encoded within <dimensions type="binding" unit="mm"> using <height>, <width> and optionally <depth>, each with quantity.

The following examples illustrate typical patterns of binding descriptions.

• Binding:

Nineteenth-century binding in full black morocco over boards.

Binding height: 270 mm, width: 170 mm.

<bindingDesc>
 <binding contemporary="false" notBefore="1801" notAfter="1900">
  <p>Nineteenth-century binding in full black morocco over boards.</p>
  <p>
   <dimensions type="binding" unit="mm">
    <height quantity="270">270</height>
    <width quantity="170">170</width>
   </dimensions>
  </p>
 </binding>
</bindingDesc>

Armorial: Latin MS 28

• Binding:

Mid-sixteenth-century binding in full red morocco over boards, crafted by Maestro Luigi de Gava (or de Gradi), a binder to several popes and other important patrons. Active from 1542 to at least 1565, he produced bindings for the Sistine Chapel and the Capella Segreta (Hobson 1975).

The front and back covers are blind-tooled in a rectilinear scheme, with a central rectangular panel formed by concentric rectangular frames. Double blind fillets frame the perimeter of the covers, with triple blind fillets dividing the surface into subsidiary panels. The principal panel consists of a repeated interlaced strapwork motif arranged in small rectangular compartments. The motif is formed by crossed C-shaped straps, each with a diaper-patterned infill of small square tooling.

The decoration expands outwards through successive frames of gilt fillets and foliate rolls. Just inside the outermost border is a slim band carrying a small gilt Colonna device on each of the left and right sides. The outermost frame is heavier, with a dense foliate roll and gilt cornerpieces containing double-headed eagles.

The spine is divided into five compartments by four raised bands, each outlined with a gilt fillet. The compartments are blind-tooled with a repeating foliate ground and centred with gold-tooled arabesque motifs.

All edges are gilt.

Binding height: 270 mm, width: 170 mm, depth: 49 mm.

<bindingDesc>
 <binding contemporary="true" notBefore="1542" notAfter="1565">
  <p>Mid-sixteenth-century binding in full red morocco over boards, crafted by <name role="bnd">
    <persName>Maestro Luigi de Gava (or de Gradi)</persName>
   </name>, a binder to several popes and other important patrons. Active from 1542 to at least 1565, he produced bindings for the Sistine Chapel and the Capella Segreta (Hobson 1975).</p>
  <p>The <locus from="Front_cover" to="Front_cover">front</locus> and <locus from="Back_cover" to="Back_cover">back</locus> covers are gold-tooled symmetrically with nested rectangular frames incorporating gilt arabesque cornerpieces and side panels. At the centre is the coat of arms of the House of Colonna: <heraldry> Gules, a column Argent, the base and capital Or, crowned antiquely of the last</heraldry>. The arms are set within a gilt oval frame and a lobed cartouche of scrolling foliage, with small flourishes above and below.</p>
  <p>The decoration expands outwards through successive frames of gilt fillets and foliate rolls. Just inside the outermost border is a slim band carrying a small gilt Colonna device on each of the left and right sides. The outermost frame is heavier, with a dense foliate roll and gilt cornerpieces containing double-headed eagles.</p>
  <p>The <locus from="Spine" to="Spine">spine</locus> is divided into five compartments by four raised bands, each outlined with a gilt fillet. The compartments are blind-tooled with a
     repeating foliate ground and centred with gold-tooled arabesque motifs.</p>
  <p>All edges are gilt.</p>
  <p>
   <dimensions type="binding" unit="mm">
    <height quantity="387">387</height>
    <width quantity="273">273</width>
    <depth quantity="49">49</depth>
   </dimensions>
  </p>
 </binding>
</bindingDesc>

Colonna Missal II: Latin MS 32a

• Binding:

Fifteenth-century binding in full brown leather over wooden boards.

The front and back covers are blind-tooled in a rectilinear scheme, with a central rectangular panel formed by concentric rectangular frames. Double blind fillets frame the perimeter of the covers, with triple blind fillets dividing the surface into subsidiary panels. The principal panel consists of a repeated interlaced strapwork motif arranged in small rectangular compartments. The motif is formed by crossed C-shaped straps, each with a diaper-patterned infill of small square tooling.

The spine is divided into ten compartments by nine raised bands. The third, fourth, and fifth compartments are gilt-lettered: Psalterium | Anglo-Norman | Saec. XIII.

Binding height: 273 mm, width: 190 mm.

<bindingDesc>
 <binding contemporary="false" notBefore="1860" notAfter="1975">
  <p>Fifteenth-century binding in full brown leather over wooden boards.</p>
  <p>The <locus from="Front_cover" to="Front_cover">front</locus> and <locus from="Back_cover" to="Back_cover">back</locus> covers are blind-tooled in a rectilinear scheme, with a central rectangular panel formed by concentric rectangular frames. Double blind fillets frame the perimeter of the covers, with triple blind fillets dividing the surface into subsidiary panels. The principal panel consists of a repeated interlaced strapwork motif arranged in small rectangular compartments. The motif is formed by crossed C-shaped straps, each with a diaper-patterned infill of small square tooling.</p>
  <p>The <locus from="Spine" to="Spine">spine</locus> is divided into ten compartments by nine raised bands. The third, fourth, and fifth compartments are gilt-lettered: <hi rend="italic">Psalterium | Anglo-Norman | Saec. XIII.</hi>
  </p>
  <p>
   <dimensions type="binding" unit="mm">
    <height quantity="273">273</height>
    <width quantity="190">190</width>
   </dimensions>
  </p>
 </binding>
</bindingDesc>

Psalter of Joan of Navarre: Latin MS 22

• Binding:

Late nineteenth- or twentieth-century binding in full dark green coated cloth over boards.

The spine is smooth, gold-blocked, with two horizontal foliate scroll motifs forming a single panel, and lettered: Erlebtes.| Band I. | 1817-1824.

Binding height: 370 mm, width: 240 mm.

<bindingDesc>
 <binding contemporary="false" notBefore="1860" notAfter="1975">
  <p>Late nineteenth- or twentieth-century binding in full dark green coated cloth over boards.</p>
  <p>The <locus from="Spine" to="Spine">spine</locus> is smooth, gold-blocked, with two horizontal foliate scroll motifs forming a single panel, and lettered: <hi rend="italic">Erlebtes.| Band I. | 1817-1824.</hi>
  </p>
  <p>
   <dimensions type="binding" unit="mm">
    <height quantity="370">370</height>
    <width quantity="240">240</width>
   </dimensions>
  </p>
 </binding>
</bindingDesc>

The Heinrich Simon Papers: The Pupil: SIM/1

Write centuries in words and lowercase, such as fifteenth century, rather than using numeric forms such as 15th century or 15th-century. Insert a hyphen when the term is used adjectivally, for example, fifteenth-century binding, and when expressing an uncertain or alternative range, connect the terms with a hyphen, for instance, fifteenth- or sixteenth-century binding.

<binding> is repeatable and may be used multiple times within a single <bindingDesc>. Additional <binding> may typically be used to record successive historical bindings, for example, when details of earlier bindings are known from documentary evidence, when a codex has been rebound, or when physical traces or surviving artefacts attest to the presence of an earlier binding.

Where heraldry associated with ownership appears as decoration on the binding, encode the description in detail within <binding> using <heraldry>. For guidance and examples, see the <heraldry> section in The John Rylands Library foundation collections.<!-- Insert hyperlink-->

Protective enclosures associated with a binding should be described within the same <binding>. Any enclosure dimensions should be recorded in an additional <dimensions> and distinguished by type, using values such as chemise, slipcase, satchel, case, or box.

<binding> may optionally include more granular encoding to distinguish decorative, material, and condition-related aspects. Within a binding description, <decoNote type="binding"> and <condition> are available, as alternatives to <p>, dealing exclusively with information about decorative features of a binding, or about its condition, respectively. Materials used in the binding may be encoded using <term type="material">. Other key features may also be encoded using <term> with a relevant type. For example, a bookmark or register, any movable or fixed device used to mark particular leaves or passages, may be encoded with <term type="bookmark">. When applicable, and in all cases where controlled vocabulary is used, <term> should include both source and key, with the appropriate AAT identifier.

General remarks: binding terminology and usage

Board(s) the rigid structural substrate of a binding, such as pasteboard, millboard, or another stiff support. Boards may survive even where no covering material remains.

Cover(s) the outer material applied over the boards, such as leather, parchment, cloth, or paper. Covers may be absent, partial, replaced, or later than the boards.

Do not use margin to describe areas of covers or boards. Margin applies to the area surrounding the text or image on a leaf or page. When describing areas of a cover or board, use cover-surface terms such as central area, upper outer area, or lower inner area.

Pastedown(s) an endleaf adhered to the inner cover. Free endleaf: an endleaf that is not adhered. Endleaves should be described in <binding> only where they are decorated or materially distinctive.

Turn-ins the portions of covering material folded over the edges of the boards and adhered to the inside surface.

Edge the physical outer boundaries of a bound volume. Use head, tail, fore edge, and spine for the physical edges of the bound volume or textblock. Fore edge should be treated as singular.

When describing decoration of the cut edges of leaves, use established edge-treatment terms, such as gilt, sprinkled, speckled, marbled, or painted. Use gauffered only where gilt edges are additionally impressed with decorative patterns. Avoid using tooled for leaf edges, to prevent confusion with tooling applied to binding surfaces.

Tooling is usually the appropriate general term unless there is clear evidence for a more specific technique, such as stamping or rolling. Where applicable, use the hyphenated forms blind-tooled and gold-tooled.

Any aspects of the binding that carry provenance significance should be mentioned briefly in <binding> to indicate their presence, but described in full in <provenance>, where their significance, context, and any associated evidence can be set out in detail.

1.62 <sealDesc> | seal description

<sealDesc> (seal description) describes the seals or similar items related to the object described, either as a series of paragraphs or as a series of <seal> elements.

MDC display label — Seal(s)

<sealDesc> contains information about seals or seal-like features present on an item. It commonly includes one or more <seal>, each instance describing a single seal. It may record details such as the number of seals present, whether they are original or later additions (where this can be determined), their location on the item, and their method of attachment or application. It may also describe physical characteristics such as material, shape, size, and colour, together with iconography or legend, any associated name or identifying information (including a person or organisation where this can be determined), associated inscriptions and their language, and condition, including whether a seal is intact or broken, or missing.

1.63 <seal> | seal

<seal> (seal) contains a description of one seal or similar applied to the object described.

<seal> within <sealDesc> should describe a single seal, normally using one or more <p>. Although the term seal is sometimes used to refer to the negative matrix of metal used to create an impression, it is also intended here to describe the resulting impression itself.

<seal> is repeatable and may be used multiple times within a single <sealDesc> to record each seal individually when more than one is present on a given item. Where appropriate, <summary> may be used within <sealDesc>, to provide context before listing each <seal>. Each <seal> must include n and one or more <p>. If the location of the seal is known, <locus> may be used within the description to indicate where it appears on the item. n should take values such as 1, 2, 3, numbered in the order in which the seals appear.

Each <seal> should include type, and subtype should also be used where further sub-classification is required.

type | subtype

type provides the primary classification of the seal.

subtype provides a further sub-classification of that seal.

When describing decoration, the following type and subtype values are recommended. All values should be encoded in the singular form, as they denote conceptual categories rather than multiple instances.

<seal> @type

pendent

Seal attached to the item and suspended from it by a joining mechanism such as cord, tag, tongue, ribbon, or similar.

applied

Seal impressed or marked directly onto the surface of the item and is not suspended from it.

<seal> @subtype

leadSeal

lead seal

<seal type="pendent" subtype="leadSeal">

Seal made of lead, of any shape, typically circular and double-sided. Most often pendent.

goldSeal

gold seal

<seal type="pendent" subtype="goldSeal">

Seal made of gold, of any shape, typically circular and double-sided. Most often pendent.

silverSeal

silver seal

<seal type="pendent" subtype="silverSeal">

Seal made of silver, of any shape, typically circular and double-sided. Most often pendent.

metalSeal

metal seal

<seal type="pendent" subtype="metalSeal">

Seal made of metal where the specific metal cannot be confidently identified, or where the seal is mixed metal or alloy. Use leadSeal, goldSeal, or silverSeal when identified.

claySeal

clay seal
