<seal type="pendent" subtype="claySeal"> <seal type="applied" subtype="claySeal">

Seal impression made in clay, of any shape. Can be pendent or applied.

waxSeal

wax seal

<seal type="pendent" subtype="waxSeal"> <seal type="applied" subtype="waxSeal">

Seal impression made by pressing a seal matrix into softened or melted wax. Can be pendent or applied.

waferSeal

wafer seal

<seal type="pendent" subtype="waferSeal"> <seal type="applied" subtype="waferSeal">

Seal impression made by pressing a matrix into an adhesive wafer. Can be pendent or applied.

embossedSeal

embossed seal

<seal type="pendent" subtype="embossedSeal"> <seal type="applied" subtype="embossedSeal">

A raised seal impression made by pressing a seal matrix or equivalent tool into the surface of an item using dry pressure. Most commonly applied directly to the item, but may also occur on a pendent paper or card tag.

paperedSeal

papered seal

<seal type="pendent" subtype="paperedSeal"> <seal type="applied" subtype="paperedSeal">

Seal impression created through paper laid over wax or other medium applied to the surface, producing a relief effect. Can be pendent or applied.

inkSeal

ink seal

<seal type="applied" subtype="inkSeal">

Seal impression made by applying ink to a seal matrix or equivalent tool and pressing it onto the surface of an item. Most often applied.

other

<seal type="pendent" subtype="other"> <seal type="applied" subtype="other">

Seal impression whose form does not match the categories above. Specify the form and material in the description.

uncertain

<seal type="pendent" subtype="uncertain"> <seal type="applied" subtype="uncertain">

Seal impression is present, and the attachment mode is known, but the seal manifestation cannot be confidently identified. Use only when necessary and provide a brief explanation in the description.

This typology is restricted to physical attachment and manifestation only. The shape of a seal should not be encoded as subtype. It should be described in prose using consistent terminology: circular, diamond, irregular, oval, pentagonal, polygonal, rectangular, square, and triangular. Do not use round or circle for circular, lozenge for diamond, oblong for oval, or rectangle for rectangular.

For pendent seals that display designs on both sides, the terms obverse and reverse may be used in descriptive prose. The obverse typically carries the principal or identifying design, such as a portrait, emblem, or insignia. The reverse often contains supplementary imagery or inscriptions. These terms are appropriate for double-sided seals, especially in formal or official contexts where both sides were intended to convey meaning.

A single-sided seal is created using a matrix engraved on one face only, producing an impression on a single surface of the sealing material. In contrast, a double-sided seal is made using a pair of matrices engraved on both sides.

<seal> may include contemporary to indicate whether a seal is original to the item or was added later.

@contemporary

contemporary (contemporary) specifies whether a seal is contemporary with the item to which it is affixed

The following values should be used:

true the seal is contemporary with the item.

false the seal is not contemporary with the item.

unknown it is unknown whether the seal is contemporary with the item.

This helps distinguish seals associated with the item's original production or authentication from those reflecting later ownership, custody, or administrative handling. It is particularly useful where multiple seals are present, as individual seals on the same item may belong to different periods.

Dating attributes such as when, notBefore, notAfter, from, and to are not recommended for <seal> as standard practice because the date associated with a seal is often ambiguous. A seal may bear a date in its legend, but this does not necessarily indicate when it was applied to the item, and seals can be reused over long periods. Applying dating attributes at the level of <seal> can therefore introduce false precision or be misinterpreted as the date of application. Where a seal bears a date, this should normally be recorded within the descriptive prose.

<date> can be used within <seal> to record dates associated with a seal, such as the date it was applied, issued, or created. The date may be given as free text, but it can also be normalised using when for exact dates, or notBefore and notAfter for approximate ranges.

Write centuries in words and lowercase, such as fifteenth century, rather than using numeric forms such as 15th century or 15th-century. Insert a hyphen when the term is used adjectivally, for example, fifteenth-century seal, and when expressing an uncertain or alternative range, connect the terms with a hyphen, for instance, fifteenth- or sixteenth-century seal.

Seals should be described fully within <seal>. If an individual or organisation associated with a seal is already encoded elsewhere in <msDesc>, for example as <author>, and the seal can be unambiguously attributed to that same entity, the name does not need to be encoded again within <seal>.

Where an additional name must be recorded within <seal>, it should be encoded using <name> containing either <persName> or <orgName> as appropriate. If the name is listed in VIAF, include both source and key on <name> to reference the relevant VIAF authority record. Where both display and standard forms are required, include two <persName> or <orgName> within <name>, one with type=display and one with type=standard. Otherwise, a single <persName> or <orgName> within <name> may be used.

Where a seal has provenance significance, it should also be recorded briefly in <provenance>, so that the ownership or custodial association indicated by the seal is made explicit. Within <provenance>, the associated individual or organisation should be encoded using <name role="fmo">, containing either <persName> or <orgName> with only the minimum contextual information necessary to support the provenance interpretation. The detailed physical description of the seal should remain in <seal>, while the provenance note should provide a concise statement of the association and the evidence on which it is based.

Where a seal incorporates heraldry associated with ownership, the heraldic description should be encoded in detail within <seal> using <heraldry>. For guidance and examples, see the <heraldry> section in The John Rylands Library foundation collections.<!-- Insert hyperlink-->

The physical size of the seal, including <height>, <width>, and <depth> each with quantity, should be encoded using <dimensions type="seal">.

• Seal(s):

Circular, double-sided pendent lead seal of Cosimo I de’ Medici, attached by cords and suspended from the lower edge of the parchment.

On the obverse, an eight-pointed cross of the Order of Saint Stephen, surrounded by the legend SANCTI STEPHANI SIGNVM RELIGIONIS.

On the reverse, the arms of the House of Medici: Or, six roundels, five Gules and in chief one Azure charged with three fleurs‑de‑lis Or, surrounded by the legend COSIMVS MAG. DUX ETR. MAGN. MAGIS.

Seal height 38-41 mm, width 38-41.

<sealDesc>
 <seal type="pendent" subtype="leadSeal" contemporary="true">
  <p>Circular, double-sided pendent lead seal of Cosimo I de’ Medici, attached by cords and suspended from the lower edge of the parchment.</p>
  <p>On the <locus from="4" to="4">obverse</locus>, an eight-pointed cross of the <name source="#viaf" key="156663616">
    <orgName type="display">Order of Saint Stephen</orgName>
    <orgName type="standard">Sacro militare ordine di Santo Stefano papa e martire</orgName>
   </name>, surrounded by the legend <hi rend="italic">SANCTI STEPHANI SIGNVM RELIGIONIS</hi>.</p>
  <p>On the <locus from="3" to="3">reverse</locus>, the arms of the House of Medici: <heraldry>Or, six roundels, five Gules and in chief one Azure charged with three fleurs‑de‑lis Or</heraldry>, surrounded by the legend <hi rend="italic">COSIMVS MAG. DUX ETR. MAGN. MAGIS.</hi>
  </p>
  <p>
   <dimensions type="seal" unit="mm">
    <height atLeast="38" atMost="41">38-41</height>
    <width atLeast="38" atMost="41">38-41</width>
   </dimensions>
  </p>
 </seal>
</sealDesc>

Patent of Cosimo I: MDC/27

• Seal(s):

Oval wafer seal of Ferdinando II de’ Medici, applied on the verso of the letter bearing the arms of the House of Medici: Or, six roundels, five Gules and in chief one Azure charged with three fleurs‑de‑lis Or, surrounded by the legend FERDINANDVS II MAG. DVX ETRVRIÆ III.

<sealDesc>
 <seal type="applied" subtype="waferSeal" contemporary="true">
  <p>Oval wafer seal of Ferdinando II de’ Medici applied on the <locus from="2" to="2">verso</locus> of the letter bearing the arms of the House of Medici: <heraldry>Or, six roundels, five Gules and in chief one Azure charged with three fleurs‑de‑lis Or</heraldry>, surrounded by the legend <hi rend="italic">FERDINANDVS II MAG. DVX ETRVRIÆ III</hi>.</p>
 </seal>
</sealDesc>

Letter from Ferdinando II: MDC/131

• Seal(s):

Irregular circular red wax seal of Heinrich Simon, applied on the verso of the folded letter: H Simon in calligraphic cursive with flourishes, framed by a cross-hatched (diamond-shaped) pattern.

<sealDesc>
 <seal type="applied" subtype="waxSeal" contemporary="true">
  <p>Irregular circular red wax seal of Heinrich Simon, applied on the <locus from="4" to="4">verso</locus> of the folded letter: <hi rend="italic">H Simon</hi> in calligraphic cursive with flourishes, framed by a cross-hatched (diamond-shaped) pattern.</p>
 </seal>
</sealDesc>

Letter from Heinrich Simon to his parents Herrmann and Minna Simon: SIM1/197

Use <hi rend="italic"> to present a literal transcription consistently and without quotation marks. Avoid using <quote> for seal legends, as a legend is an inscribed text on an object, not a quotation. If you prefer quotation marks, you may either record the legend as plain text in quotation marks or encode it using <q>, but do not use <quote>.

A seal that no longer survives may still be recorded using <seal> where physical or documentary evidence indicates that it was once present. This practice is optional and should be followed only where the project, catalogue, or descriptive pattern systematically records seals, including evidence for those now missing.

If traces of a former seal survive, such as remnants of wax, broken tags or cords, or stitch holes, <seal> may still be used to describe that evidence, even though the seal itself is missing. The surviving evidence should be described briefly and factually in <p>. type should be included where the method of attachment can be identified. subtype should be used only if the seal medium can be confidently inferred. contemporary may also be included where applicable.

Distinguish seals from stamps. <seal> should be used for seals or sealing devices with an authenticating, validating, authorising, or securing function. Inked impressions that do not function as seals should be described as stamps and may be encoded with <stamp>. Depending on their function, stamps may be recorded in <additions> or, where they provide evidence of ownership or custodial history, in <provenance>.

<seal> may optionally include more granular encoding to distinguish decorative, material, and condition-related aspects. Within a seal description, <decoNote type="seal"> and <condition> are available, as alternatives to <p>, dealing exclusively with information about decorative features of a seal, or about its condition, respectively. Materials used in the seal may be encoded using <term type="material">. Other key features may also be encoded using <term> with relevant type. When applicable, and in all cases where controlled vocabulary is used, <term> should include both source and key, with the appropriate AAT identifier.

1.64 <accMat> | accompanying material

<accMat> (accompanying material) contains details of any significant additional material which may be closely associated with the manuscript or object being described, such as non-contemporaneous documents or fragments bound in with it at some earlier historical period.

MDC display label — Accompanying material

<accMat> is the final component within <physDesc> and is used to record any material that is physically associated with the described item but was not originally part of its structure. It captures significant material that has been bound in, enclosed with, affixed to, or otherwise accompanies the item, but was not integrated into its production or textual formation.

<additions> records changes or interventions made directly to the item itself, such as annotations, corrections, or inscriptions, <accMat> describes additional material that accompanies the item in a physical or contextual sense.

Accompanying material may include loose sheets, inserts, or enclosures supplied with the item, as well as letters, notes, or slips inserted into it. It may also include separate leaflets, pamphlets, or booklets issued with the main item. Folded documents stored with the item, such as certificates or receipts, may also be included. Supplementary visual material, such as photographs, plates, or prints, should be treated as accompanying material where it is present as an addition rather than as integral content. External packaging, including labels, wrappers, or containers, may also be recorded where it materially accompanies the item and is significant enough to describe. This applies to packaging that is loose, curatorial, or not physically or historically part of the item.

If the wrapper or container is original, purpose-made, or historically associated with the item’s creation or use, for example, a textile case made for a manuscript, an attached chemise binding, or a historic slipcase with inscriptions or decoration, it should be described in <binding> as part of the item's original or historically intended enclosure.

Any accompanying material associated with the item should be recorded in <accMat>, with each entry described in a separate <p> and using <locus> where relevant to indicate where it is located. The description should record the quantity and nature of the material, how it is physically present, and any identifying features, such as handwriting, printed text, dates, names, or other identifying marks.

Within <accMat>, names are descriptive and do not normally require encoding with <name> or role. If a name needs to be indexed or displayed as an associated person or organisation, encode it using <name> with an appropriate <persName> or <orgName>. Where authority control is available, or where display and indexed forms need to be distinguished, <name> may contain both display and standard forms.

<date> can be used within <accMat> to record dates associated with accompanying material, such as the date a document was issued, added, or created. The date may be given as free text, but it can also be normalised using when for exact dates, or notBefore and notAfter for approximate ranges.

Where accompanying material is of provenance significance, it should also be recorded briefly in <provenance>, so that the ownership or custodial association indicated by the seal is made explicit. Within <provenance>, the associated individual or organisation should be encoded using <name role="fmo">, containing either <persName> or <orgName> with only the minimum contextual information necessary to support the provenance interpretation. The detailed physical description of the accompanying material should remain in <accMat>, while the provenance note should provide a concise statement of the association and the evidence on which it is based.

• Accompanying material:

Tipped-in letter from Henry Tattam to Johann Ludwig Krapf, concerning the manuscript, dated 17 January 1869.

<accMat>
 <p>
  <locus from="Endleaf_1r" to="Endleaf_1r">Tipped-in letter</locus> from Henry Tattam to Johann Ludwig Krapf, concerning the manuscript, dated <date when="1825-01-17">17 January 1869</date>.</p>
</accMat>

Gadla ḥawāryāt: Ethiopic MS 6

• Accompanying material:

Loose note inserted in the volume, probably an eighteenth-century letter cover: A sua Eccellenza ... Il Sigre Principe D. Matteo Barberini Colonna di Sciarra Roma.

<accMat>
 <p>
  <locus from="Tipped_in_note_r" to="Tipped_in_note_r">Loose note</locus> inserted in the volume, probably an eighteenth-century letter cover: <hi rend="italic">A sua Eccellenza ... Il Sig<hi rend="superscript">re</hi> Principe D. Matteo Barberini Colonna di Sciarra Roma</hi>. </p>
</accMat>

Colonna Missal I: Latin MS 32

A leaf that was missing from the codex at the time of acquisition but has since been recovered should be recorded in <accMat> if it is now preserved separately alongside the manuscript. Although it belongs to the manuscript, it accompanies it rather than forming part of its current physical structure. In such cases, the recovered leaf should also be described in full as a separate <msPart>.

• Accompanying material:

The leaf formerly missing between 132v and 133r has now been secured and is preserved separately as Latin MS 22 A.

<accMat>
 <p>The leaf formerly missing between 132v and 133r has now been secured and is preserved separately as <ref target="https://www.digitalcollections.manchester.ac.uk/view/MS-LATIN-00022/347">Latin MS 22 A</ref>.</p>
</accMat>

Psalter of Joan of Navarre: Latin MS 22

1.65 <history> | history

<history> (history) groups elements describing the full history of a manuscript, manuscript part, or other object.

<history> provides a structured container for recording what is known about an item’s history, from its creation to the present day. This may include information about its place and date of creation, later changes of ownership or custody, and the means by which it came into its current holding institution.

The following elements are used within <history> to record this information:

<teiHeader> <fileDesc>|<titleStmt>|<PublicationStmt>|<sourceDesc>|<msDesc>| <msContents>|<physDesc> <history> <origin> <!-- Metadata about the origin of an item, its place and date of its creation --> <!-- Contains <origDate> and <origPlace> --> </origin> <provenance> <!-- Metadata about the stages in the history of an item before acquisition--> </provenance> <acquisition> <!-- Metadata about the means by which an item was acquired --> </acquisition> </history> <additional/>|</msDesc>| </sourceDesc>|</fileDesc>|<encodingDesc>|<profileDesc>| <revisionDesc> </teiHeader>

1.66 <origin> | origin

<origin> (origin) contains any descriptive or other information concerning the origin of a manuscript, manuscript part, or other object.

MDC display label — Origin

<origin> may contain two elements, <origDate>, used to record the date of origin, and <origPlace>, used to record the place of origin. These elements may be used independently as standalone components or integrated into a prose description.

1.67 <origdate> | origin date

<origDate>  (origin date) contains any form of date, used to identify the date of origin for a manuscript, manuscript part, or other object.

MDC display label — Date of Creation

<origDate> within <origin> is used to record the date associated with the production or creation of the item. It should normally be included where date information is available, whether as a specific date, partial date, date span, or approximate range. If an exact date cannot be determined, the most precise date range supported by the evidence may be encoded.

<origDate> is a member of the att.datable class, which in turn inherits from the att.datable.w3c class. It therefore includes attributes such as when, from, to, notBefore and notAfter.

when | from, to | notBefore, notAfter

when supplies the value of a known single date in standard form.

from, to indicate the starting and ending points of the period in standard form.

notBefore, notAfter specify the earliest and latest possible dates for the item’s creation in standard form.

Values supplied in these attributes must conform to W3C ISO 8601 format. They are usually encoded as YYYY-MM-DD, but may be reduced to YYYY-MM or YYYY where full precision is not available.

In TEI-XML, standard dating attributes are interpreted as Gregorian or proleptic Gregorian by default. Consequently, calendar=gregorian is not required, since the calendar system is already assumed.

Where a date is expressed or normalised using a calendar system other than the Gregorian calendar, use the custom dating attributes together with datingMethod and calendar of the att.datable.custom class. For more information, see <!-- hyperlink non-Gregorian dates | other calendar system -->

BCE dates follow the same standard dating attributes, but require specific formatting in the encoded value. The standard attributes are based on the extended proleptic Gregorian calendar, meaning that the Gregorian calendar is applied retrospectively to dates that predate its historical introduction.

BCE (Before Common Era) | CE (Common Era)

Use BCE for dates before the Common Era. Use CE where a date range spans BCE and CE, for example, 75 BCE–15 CE. Use CE also where a year under 100 is presented in isolation, for example, 15 CE, as such small numbers can be misread without an era marker. In all other cases, dates without a BCE suffix are presumed to be in the Common or Current Era.

• Date of Creation: 75 BCE-15 CE

<origDate notBefore="-0075" notAfter="0015">75 BCE-15 CE</origDate>

BCE dates are encoded as negative four-digit numbers, using a minus sign, for example, 15 BCE is encoded as -0015. CE dates are encoded as four-digit numbers with leading zeroes where needed, for example 15 CE is encoded as 0015.

• Date of Creation: 15 BCE

<origDate when="-0015">15 BCE</origDate>

• Date of Creation: 15 CE

<origDate when="0015">15 CE</origDate>

Avoid using BC (Before Christ) or AD (Anno Domini). Instead, use the neutral and inclusive terms CE (Common or Current Era) and BCE (Before the Common or Current Era). Do not include full stops, use BCE and CE, not B.C.E. or C.E. Use CE only when the date would otherwise be ambiguous. For example, the Norman Conquest took place in 1066, not 1066 CE or AD 1066.

Known single date | @when

The date of creation is known as a single specific point in time. A single known date can be encoded as precisely as the available information allows.

@when may be used for a complete date, a month and year, or a year only. If the date is partial, any unspecified part of the value may be omitted.

full date is known | YYYY-MM-DD

• Date of Creation: 30 August 1821

<origDate when="1821-08-30">30 August 1821</origDate>

only month and year is known | YYYY-MM

• Date of Creation: August 1821

<origDate when="1821-08">August 1821</origDate>

only year is known | YYYY

• Date of Creation: 1821

<origDate when="1821">1821</origDate>

If the date appears in a contracted or non-standard form, expand shortened years, for example 1999 rather than 99. Use cardinal, not ordinal, forms for days, for example 21 March rather than 21st March. Use Arabic numerals rather than Roman numerals, for example 18 rather than XVIII. Do not include commas, add a leading zero to days 1 to 9, or abbreviate month names.

Do not include additional wording such as in the year and anno or days of the week within <origDate>. If If the wording of the date as it appears on the item is significant, transcribe it in an optional note using <p> within <origin>.

For example:

20 July 1698, where the item reads the 20th of July, anno Dom. 1698

25 December 1945, where the item reads Christmas Day, 1945

4 July 1760, where the item reads July 4 MDCCLX

If an item includes an explicit production date, such as in a colophon, transcribe the statement within <colophon>.

Do not use an MM-DD value for when. Without a year, the date cannot identify when the item was created. If the item gives a month and/or day but the year is missing, and a year can be supplied from other evidence, encode the conjectural date using notBefore and notAfter as appropriate (see <!--hyperlink-->).

Known date span | @from @to

The creation of the item took place over a defined period rather than at a single specific point in time.

from and to are used when both the beginning and end of the date span are known. This applies to items created over time, such as diaries, journal-letters, or sequences of correspondence written across multiple dates.

• Date of Creation: 16-22 November 1824

<origDate from="1824-11-16" to="1824-11-22">16-22 November 1824</origDate>

Letter from Heinrich Simon to his family (SIM/2/23 and 23a-e), written over several days

• Date of Creation: 1816-1824

<origDate from="1816" to="1824">1816-1814</origDate>

The Heinrich Simon papers: The Pupil: SIM/1

  • Inclusive dates are given as 1816-1824, not 1816-24

A date span encoded with from and to is less common than an imprecise date range. Where the date cannot be precisely determined but is known to fall between two possible points, use notBefore and notAfter instead.

uncertain date limits | @notBefore @notAfter

The exact date of creation is not known, but the item can be dated within known limits.

notBefore and notAfter are used for broad or approximate dating, where the evidence supports an earliest and/or latest possible date. This applies to periods such as centuries, parts of centuries, decades, or other unspecified spans.

These attributes may be used either individually or together. notBefore may be used where only the earliest limit (terminus a quo) can be established. notAfter may be used where only the latest limit (terminus ad quem) can be established. Both attributes should be used where the date can be bounded at both ends.

Centuries | parts of centuries

Historical and pre-modern materials often lack explicit evidence for a precise date of origin. Catalogues and records may also vary in the level of detail they provide, and in the conventions used to express approximate dating.

Where a precise date of creation cannot be established, an item may nevertheless be attributed to a particular century, half-century, part of a century, decade, or other approximate period.

The following conventions for centuries and parts of centuries are based on Ker’s dating system for medieval manuscripts. Ker-style and other legacy dating forms, such as s. XIII, should not be reproduced directly in <origDate>. Instead, the textual value should use ordinal numbers and Arabic numerals for centuries, for example 13th century, not thirteenth century or s. XIII. Further specifications should follow after a comma, for example, 13th century, first half. Superscript should not be used, and century should be recorded in lowercase. The corresponding limits should be encoded with notBefore and notAfter.

Century-level attributions should be encoded according to conventional calendrical boundaries. A century begins in the year ending in 01 and ends in the year ending in 00. For example, the thirteenth century is normalised as 1201-1300, not 1200-1299 or 1200-1300. Using 1200-1299 excludes the final year of the thirteenth century. Using 1200-1300 covers 101 years and misplaces 1200, which is the final year of the twelfth century. The values used for approximate subdivisions are inclusive and may overlap. For example, early, middle, and late parts of a century are not mutually exclusive. Use round numbers to define these limits, for example 1201 to 1250 for the first half of the thirteenth century.

Entire century | xx01–xx00

• Date of Creation: 13th century

<origDate notBefore="1201" notAfter="1300">13th century</origDate>

Beginning of a century | xx01–xx30

• Date of Creation: 13th century, early

<origDate notBefore="1201" notAfter="1230">13th century, early</origDate>

Middle of a century | xx30–xx70

• Date of Creation: 13th century, middle

<origDate notBefore="1230" notAfter="1270">13th century, middle</origDate>
  • Use middle, not mid or mid-, when recording the middle part of a century.

End of a century | (xx70–xx00)

• Date of Creation: 13th century, late

<origDate notBefore="1270" notAfter="1300">13th century, late</origDate>

1st half of a century | xx01–xx50

• Date of Creation: 13th century, first half

<origDate notBefore="1201" notAfter="1250">13th century, first half</origDate>

2nd half of a century | xx50–xx00

• Date of Creation: 13th century, second half

<origDate notBefore="1250" notAfter="1300">13th century, second half</origDate>

1st – 4th quarter of a century

First (xx01–xx25) | Second (xx25–xx50) | Third (xx50–xx75) | Fourth (xx75–xx00)

• Date of Creation: 13th century, first quarter

<origDate notBefore="1201" notAfter="1225">13th century, first quarter</origDate>

When encoding centuries and their subdivisions, use inclusive Gregorian ranges that reflect the inherent imprecision of historical dating. These intervals allow for intentional overlap and support the representation of transitions, ambiguity, and interpretative flexibility. Use rounded, inclusive boundaries to convey the approximate nature of period terms.

two centuries

• Date of Creation: 13th–14th centuries

<origDate notBefore="1201" notAfter="1400">13th-14th centuries</origDate>

Early to middle Xth century

• Date of Creation: 13th century, early–middle

<origDate notBefore="1201" notAfter="1270">13th century, early-middle</origDate>

Late Xth century to early Xth century

• Date of Creation: 13th century, late–14th century, early

<origDate notBefore="1270" notAfter="1330">13th century, late–14th century, early</origDate>

Conjectural dates | approximate | uncertain | unknown

Conjectural, approximate, uncertain, and supplied dates are dates based on incomplete, partial, indirect, or contextual evidence rather than direct confirmation from the item itself. Such dates may be based on historical references, physical evidence, textual evidence, related documentation, or scholarly judgement.

@notBefore @notAfter

Where the date of creation cannot be determined precisely, an estimated, probable, approximate, or supplied date may be recorded, provided that its uncertain status is encoded explicitly. Use notBefore and/or notAfter to record the earliest and latest possible date boundaries. The values of these attributes can range from a broad year to a more specific year-month or year-month-day, depending on the level of certainty available. This distinguishes an inferred or conjectural date from a known date recorded with when.

Where the date of creation cannot be determined precisely, an estimated, probable, approximate, or supplied date may be recorded, provided that its uncertain status is encoded explicitly using cert and, where appropriate, evidence.

@cert

cert (certainty) signifies the degree of certainty associated with the dating.

The following values should be used:

high the dating is generally considered to be correct.

Inferred | a certain date is known but not explicitly stated on the item. In other catalogues or finding aids, such dates may be recorded in square brackets, for example, [1821] or [182-] for something certainly published during the 1820s.

medium the dating is generally considered approximately correct.

Approximate | a date that may not be exact, but is approximated, or is a rough estimate. In other catalogues or finding aids, variations of circa may be used, with or without punctuation or spaces, for example, ca. 1821, c. 1821, or circa 1821.

low the dating is generally considered doubtful but possible.

Questionable | a date that is probable but might be questionable, uncertain, or incorrect. In other catalogues or finding aids, such dates may be recorded with a question mark, for example, 1821? or ?1821.

unknown no wish to assert an opinion, as the level of certainty is unknown.

Do not use cert with when, from and to, as these attributes record a known date or date interval. The absence of cert implies a high degree of certainty. Instead, use notBefore and notAfter to encode uncertain, approximate, or conjectural date boundaries.

@evidence

evidence indicates the nature of the evidence supporting the dating.

The following values should be used:

internal the dating is based on evidence internal to the item, for example, an inscription, a script.

external the dating is based on evidence external to the item, for example, a reference to the item in another context.

conjecture the dating is based on conjecture. No specific evidence is available to support the dating. The date has been inferred by the encoder, cataloguer, or scholar based on their expertise and analysis of the item.

• Date of Publication: 1475

• Origin:

Schreiber (1969-1976) states that the coat of arms of Pope Sixtus IV on leaf [*1] verso proves that the blockbook was made between 1471-1484, most probably in 1475 because of the Jubilee in that year.

<origin>
 <p>Schreiber (1969-1976) states that the coat of arms of Pope Sixtus IV on leaf [*1] verso proves that the blockbook was made between 1471-1484, most probably in <origDate notBefore="1475" notAfter="1475" cert="high" evidence="external">1475</origDate> because of the Jubilee in that year.</p>
</origin>

cert and evidence do not have to be used together.

If no date of creation can be found in the item itself or determined from another source, but a date can be estimated, record the most precise probable dating that the evidence supports. The basis for the supplied or conjectural date should be explained in a <p> within <origin>, including the source of the information and how the dating was determined. Maintain consistency in the language used to express uncertainty:

probably — highly likely to be the case than not (>50% chance) — cert=high

possibly — could be the case or be true but not certain (<50% chance) — cert=medium | low

approximately — around or about or close to an exact date, but not precise

Do not use square brackets ([ ]), question marks (?), or any other markup normally used in MARC | RDA, or other cataloguing standards to indicate uncertainty, supplied or conjectural dates, missing or incorrect information.

When assigning a date, particularly based on physical evidence, the distinction between an approximate or probable date and a known date relies on judgment.

Approximate | estimated dates

Where exact dates are unknown but can be inferred, terms such as approximately or circa are often used, both in historical records and modern documentation, to indicate an estimated date based on contextual evidence.

These terms are typically used when no direct evidence exists to support a precise date. Instead, the date is inferred by the encoder, cataloguer, or scholar through expertise and analytical judgement. Such inferences are educated estimates, grounded in indirect or incomplete evidence, including stylistic features, references to historical circumstances, physical evidence, production methods, or other contextual indicators.

Although circa is traditionally used in historical description, and approximately may be clearer in modern explanatory prose, neither form should be recorded as part of the textual value within <origDate>. In TEI, uncertainty should be encoded explicitly using the appropriate attributes and explained in prose where necessary.

When supplying or inferring a date, especially from physical or contextual evidence, use judgement to determine whether the evidence supports a single probable date or only a broader date range. If the evidence supports a single supplied or inferred date, use notBefore and notAfter with the same value to distinguish the supplied or inferred date from a known date encoded with when. If the date is based on educated judgement or indirect evidence, use cert to indicate the degree of certainty. Use evidence to indicate the nature of the evidence supporting the dating, such as internal, external, or conjecture. Explain the reasoning in a <p> within <origin>, including the evidence and other contextual details for the supplied date.

If the precise date of creation is not known, record the most precise probable date that the evidence supports. For example, if an item is likely to have been created during the mid-1850s, but the exact year is uncertain, the approximate year may be recorded as 1855.

• Date of Creation: 1855

• Origin:

The creation date is approximately 1855, inferred from the style and production methods of the item.

<origin>
 <p>The creation date is approximately <origDate notBefore="1855" notAfter="1855" cert="medium" evidence="internal">1855</origDate>, inferred from the style and production methods of the item..</p>
</origin>

Do not include approximate qualifiers such as approximately, circa, c., ca., around, or about in the textual value of <origDate>.

Where a broader range is more accurate than a supplied single date, record the range instead. For example, if one item in a collection is known or strongly suspected to date from around 1850 and another from around 1859, while the rest are likely to date from the same decade, record the terminal dates as a range rather than using the vaguer form 1850s.

• Date of Creation: 1850-1859

<origDate notBefore="1850" notAfter="1859">1850-1859</origDate>

Avoid ambiguous or redundant constructions such as 1850s or circa 1850s. Inclusive dates are given as 1850-1859, not 1850-9 or 1850-59.

1.67.1 Uncertain date within known range

between YYYY and YYYY

The creation date of an item is uncertain but falls within a specific date range.

• Date of Creation: 1846-1874

• Origin:

Broadside Ballads Online suggests that John Harkness, printer, of Preston, was active between 1840 and 1866, however advertisements in the 'Preston Chronicle' indicate that he was trading from 121 Church Street by August 1846 and continued there until at least 1874.

<origin>
 <origDate notBefore="1846" notAfter="1874" cert="high" evidence="external">1846-1874</origDate>
 <p>Broadside Ballads Online suggests that John Harkness, printer, of Preston, was active between 1840 and 1866. However, advertisements in the 'Preston Chronicle' indicate that he was trading from 121 Church Street by August 1846 and continued there until at least 1874.</p>
</origin>

Broadside Ballad: Peterloo: R226579.108

  • between 1846 and 1874 not between 1846-1874

1.67.2 Uncertain date | one date or the other

in YYYY or YYYY

The creation date of an item is uncertain and could be one of two possible dates or date ranges, such as one year or another, or one century or another.

• Date of Creation: 1476-1477

• Origin:

Believed to have been printed by Caxton in the earliest years of his innovative printing business in London, in either 1476 or 1477.

<origin>
 <origDate notBefore="1476" notAfter="1477" cert="high" evidence="external">1476-1477</origDate>
 <p>Believed to have been printed by Caxton in the earliest years of his innovative printing business in London, in either 1476 or 1477.</p>
</origin>

• Date of Creation: 17th-18th century

• Origin:

The style of the script suggests that the manuscript from which this originated was probably copied in the 17th or 18th century, although the sample is too small to confirm this with certainty.

<origin>
 <origDate notBefore="1601" notAfter="1800" cert="low" evidence="internal">17th-18th century</origDate>
 <p>The style of the script suggests that the manuscript from which this originated was probably copied in the seventeenth or eighteenth century, although the sample is too small to confirm this with certainty.</p>
</origin>

1.67.3 Date supplied from reference sources

The date is not directly available from the item but is supplied based on reference sources, such as historical records, external documentation, or scholarly analysis.

• Date of Creation: 1850

• Origin: Kornicki (1993) dates the map to 1850, using Beans (1951, page 26) as reference.

<origin>
 <p>Kornicki (1993) dates the map to <origDate notBefore="1850" notAfter="1850" cert="high" evidence="external">1850</origDate>, using Beans (1951, page 26) as reference.</p>
</origin>

Nikkō oyama no ezu: Japanese 109

• Date of Creation: 1475

• Origin: Schreiber (1969-1976) states that the coat of arms of Pope Sixtus IV on leaf [*1] verso proves that the blockbook was made between 1471-1484, most probably in 1475 because of the Jubilee in that year.

<origin>
 <p>Schreiber (1969-1976) states that the coat of arms of Pope Sixtus IV on leaf [*1] verso proves that the blockbook was made between 1471-1484, most probably in <origDate notBefore="1475" notAfter="1475" cert="high" evidence="external">1475</origDate> because of the Jubilee in that year.</p>
</origin>

1.67.4 Dates based on contextual evidence

Sometimes it might be impossible to give an exact date, but one can make an educated guess based on the content of an item. For example, it cannot have been written before a certain date if it refers to an event that happened on that date.

• Date of Creation:  2 March 1824

• Origin:

Based on contextual information, this undated document, mentioned in Heinrich Simon’s school diary (SIM/1/238), can be dated to 2 March 1824.

<origin>
 <p>Based on contextual information, this undated document, mentioned in Heinrich Simon’s school diary (SIM/1/238), can be dated to <origDate notBefore="1824-03-02" notAfter="1824-03-02" cert="high" evidence="internal">2 March 1824</origDate>.</p>
</origin>

For letters with a missing or partial date, provide an approximate date based on context clues such as events or situations discussed in the letter. In many instances, the exact date can nevertheless be inferred from the contents.

• Date of Creation:  25- 26 November 1825

• Origin:

Contextual information indicates that this letter was written just before the letter SIM/2/146, dated 1 December 1825, in which Heinrich Simon’s siblings refer to the news about Singer imparted here. As it was written on a Friday and Saturday, the dates of 25 and 26 November 1825 are most probable.

<origin>
 <origDate notBefore="1825-11-25" notAfter="1825-11-26" cert="high" evidence="internal">26-25 November 1825</origDate>
 <p>Contextual information indicates that this letter was written just before the letter <ref target="https://digitalcollections.manchester.ac.uk/view/AR-SIM-00002-00146/1">SIM/2/146</ref>, dated 1 December 1825, in which Heinrich Simon’s siblings refer to the news about Singer imparted here. As it was written on a Friday and Saturday, the dates of 26 and 25 November 1825 are most probable.</p>
</origin>

1.67.5 Non-Gregorian dates | other calendar systems

Origin dates may be expressed using calendars other than Gregorian, most commonly the Islamic Hijrī and Jewish calendars. Where a date is expressed in a non-Gregorian calendar, the original or source date may be retained in the textual content of <origDate> and identified using calendar. If the normalised value is also supplied in that non-Gregorian system, this must be encoded using the custom dating attributes, such as when-custom, from-custom, to-custom, notBefore-custom, notAfter-custom, together with datingMethod.

@x-custom | @datingMethod | @calendar

when-custom supplies the value of a date or time in some custom standard form.

notBefore-custom specifies the earliest possible date for the event in some custom standard form.

notAfter-custom specifies the latest possible date for the event in some custom standard form

from-custom indicates the starting point of the period in some custom standard form.

to-custom indicates the ending point of the period in some custom standard form.

datingMethod supplies a pointer to a calendar or other means of interpreting the values of the custom dating attributes.

calendar indicates the system or calendar to which the date represented by the content of this element belongs.

Although calendar and datingMethod may refer to the same calendrical system, they serve different purposes. calendar identifies the calendar system used in the displayed date string, while datingMethod identifies the system used for the values recorded in the custom dating attributes.

Custom dating attributes should be combined with standard dating attributes to encode both the non-Gregorian date and its Gregorian equivalent. Standard dating attributes are understood as Gregorian or proleptic Gregorian by default.

datingMethod and calendar values must be encoded in lowercase, without accents or spaces, with multi-word values in camelCase.

In the textual content of <origDate>, years should be in Western-style Arabic numerals. The non-Gregorian date should be followed by its Gregorian equivalent in brackets, with CE included.

Islamic calendar | Hijri Qamari (AH) | Hijri Shamsi (HS)

The Islamic tradition uses two distinct calendars: the Hijrī Qamarī, a lunar calendar used for religious observances, and the Hijrī Shamsī, a solar calendar used officially in countries like Iran and Afghanistan.

islamic

hijriQamari

Hijrī Qamarī

AH = Anno Hegirae | lunar calendar

The Hijri Qamari calendar is a lunar calendar based on the phases of the Moon. It consists of 12 lunar months, each beginning at the new moon. The calendar year is approximately 354 or 355 days long. It is used across the Islamic world to determine the dates of religious observances, including Ramadan, Eid al-Fitr, and Hajj. The calendar begins with the Hijra (migration of Prophet Muhammad from Mecca to Medina), traditionally dated to 16 July 622 CE. Dates in this calendar are traditionally abbreviated AH (Anno Hegirae, Latin for "in the year of the Hijra").

islamic

hijriShamsi

Hijrī Shamsī

SH = Solar Hijrī | solar calendar

The Hijri Shamsi calendar is a solar calendar used officially in Iran and Afghanistan. It is aligned with the Earth's revolution around the Sun and starts each year on the March equinox (typically 20 or 21 March in the Gregorian calendar). The year length is 365 or 366 days, and the calendar also traces its epoch to the Hijra (622 CE), though its solar calculation method differs from the lunar version. Dates are commonly abbreviated SH (Solar Hijri), to distinguish them from AH lunar dates.

  • Conversion of Islamic (Hijri) AH and Gregorian CE dates

Hijrī Qamarī months: 1- Muḥarram | 2- Ṣafar | 3 - Rabī I | 4 - Rabī II | 5- Jumādā I | 6- Jumādā II | 7- Rajab | 8- Shaʿbān | 9- Ramaḍān | 10- Shawwāl | 11- Ḏū al-Qaʿdah | 12- Ḏū al-Ḥijjah

Hijrī Shamsī months: 1- Farvardin | 2- Ordibehesht | 3- Khordad | 4- Tir | 5- Mordad | 6- Shahrivar | 7- Mehr | 8- Aban | 9- Azar | 10- Dey | 11- Bahman | 12- Esfand

Because year-only Hijrī Qamarī and Hijrī Shamsī dates can overlap two Gregorian years, notBefore and notAfter should be used to encode the earliest and latest possible Gregorian years associated with the Hijrī year.

• Date of Creation: 14 Jumadā I 1085 AH (16 August 1674 CE)

<origDate when="1674-08-16" when-custom="1085-05-14" datingMethod="islamic" calendar="hijriQamari">14 Jumādā I 1085 AH (16 August 1674 CE)</origDate>

• Date of Creation: 1085 AH (1674–1675 CE)

<origDate notBefore="1674" notAfter="1675" when-custom="1085" datingMethod="islamic" calendar="hijriQamari">1085 AH (1674–1675 CE)</origDate>

• Date of Creation:  1 Farvardin 1353 SH (21 March 1974 CE)

<origDate when="1974-03-21" when-custom="1353-01-01" datingMethod="islamic" calendar="hijriShamsi">1 Farvardin 1353 SH (21 March 1974 CE)</origDate>

• Date of Creation:  1353 SH (1974-1975 CE)

<origDate notBefore="1974" notAfter="1975" when-custom="1353" datingMethod="islamic" calendar="hijriShamsi">1353 SH (1974-1975 CE)</origDate>

Where the distinction is known, the more precise values hijriQamari or hijriShamsi must be used. Use calendar=hijri only when the source does not specify whether the date follows the lunar Hijrī Qamarī or the solar Hijrī Shamsī system, and this cannot be determined from contextual evidence. The generic value hijri should be treated as a fallback and not as a preferred encoding choice.

Samaritan calendar | Creation Era (C) | Entry Era (E)

The Samaritan tradition uses two principal chronological eras for dating: the Creation of the World and the Entry into Canaan, both derived from the biblical narrative.

samaritan

creationEra

C = Creation Era | lunisolar

The Creation Era counts years from the Creation of the World, calculated according to the genealogies and lifespans recorded in the Samaritan Pentateuch. The period from Creation to the Entry into Canaan is fixed at 2794 years. This era served as a principal chronological framework in Samaritan tradition and was used in manuscripts, inscriptions, and historical records. The total length of time from Creation differs from Jewish and Christian chronological traditions.

samaritan

entryEra

E = Entry Era | lunisolar

The Entry Era counts years from the entry of the Israelites into Canaan under Joshua. The first year of the Entry Era corresponds to the year 2800 of the Creation Era, establishing a defined internal relationship between the two systems. The date of the Entry is consistently transmitted in Samaritan chronicles, though minor variations occur in some manuscript traditions.

The Creation Era (C) and the Entry Era (E) are distinct chronological eras within the same Samaritan calendrical framework and not separate calendars in the structural sense. The lunar calendar mechanics remain constant. What changes is the epoch from which years are counted. The Samaritan calendar is lunisolar, so a Samaritan year does not correspond to a single Gregorian year and typically overlaps two consecutive Gregorian years.

For year-only Samaritan dates, notBefore and notAfter should be used to encode the earliest and latest possible Gregorian years associated with the Samaritan year.

• Date of Creation: 5594 C (1934-1935 CE)

<origDate notBefore="1934" notAfter="1935" when-custom="5594" datingMethod="samaritan" calendar="creationEra">5594 C (1934-1935 CE)</origDate>

• Date of Creation:  3574 E (1935 CE)

<origDate notBefore="1934" notAfter="1935" when-custom="3574" datingMethod="samaritan" calendar="entryEra">3574 E (1934-1935 CE)</origDate>

Where the distinction is known, the more precise values creationEra or entryEra must be used. Use calendar=samaritan only when the source does not specify whether the date follows the Samaritan Creation Era (C) or the Entry Era (E), and this cannot be determined from contextual evidence. The generic value samaritan should be treated as a fallback and not as a preferred encoding choice.

Jewish calendar | Anno Mundi (AM)

The Jewish calendar is a lunisolar system in which lunar months are aligned with the solar year through intercalation. Years are counted from the Creation of the World according to rabbinic chronology.

jewish

annoMundi

AM = Anno Mundi | lunisolar

The Jewish calendar is a lunisolar system in which lunar months are aligned with the solar year through a 19-year intercalation cycle. Years are counted according to the Anno Mundi era, from the Creation of the World, traditionally dated to 3761 BCE in the proleptic Gregorian calendar. The calendar regulates the observance of religious festivals and became the dominant chronological system in Jewish communities from the medieval period onward.

  • Conversion of Jewish (Hebrew) AM and Gregorian CE dates

Jewish calendar months: 1- Tishrei | 2- Cheshvan | 3- Kislev | 4 - Tevet | 5- Shevat | 6- Adar (Adar I in leap years) | 7- Nisan | 8- Iyar | 9- Sivan | 10- Tammuz | 11- Av | 12- Elul — Leap years: Adar I and Adar II.

The year may take two forms: with the thousands explicitly, referred to as the major era, such as 5428, or without the thousands, referred to as the minor era, such as 428, with the thousands understood from context. This distinction reflects a difference in notation only and does not indicate a different era or calendar system. When encoding, record the full numeric year in when-custom, even if the thousands are omitted in the source, and retain the form exactly as it appears within <origDate>.

• Date of Creation: 14 Nisan 5695 AM (17 April 1935 CE)

<origDate when="1935-04-17" when-custom="5695-07-14" datingMethod="jewish" calendar="annoMundi">14 Nisan 5695 AM (17 April 1935 CE)</origDate>

When a source records only a Jewish year in Anno Mundi (AM), the year must be treated as spanning two Gregorian years. Because the Jewish year begins in Tishrei (September or October), each AM year spans two consecutive Gregorian years and does not correspond to a single Gregorian year.

For year-only Jewish dates, notBefore and notAfter should be used to encode the earliest and latest possible Gregorian years associated with the Jewish year.

• Date of Creation:  5428 AM (1667–1668 CE)

<origDate notBefore="1667" notAfter="1668" when-custom="5428" datingMethod="jewish" calendar="annoMundi">5428 AM (1667–1668 CE)</origDate>

In this example, 5428 AM began at Rosh Hashanah in autumn 1667 and ended at Rosh Hashanah in autumn 1668. The use of notBefore and notAfter records the Gregorian year limits without introducing false precision. when-custom preserves the original Anno Mundi year value, while datingMethod=jewish and calendar=annoMundi identify the calendrical system and era.

Seleucid Era (SE) | Babylonian | Macedonian

The Seleucid Era (SE), also known as Anno Graecorum (AG), is a system of year numbering that begins with the re-conquest of Babylon by Seleucus I Nicator in 312/311 BCE, marking the foundation of the Seleucid Empire. It was applied within existing lunisolar calendrical frameworks derived from the Babylonian calendar. Two principal systems were employed: a Babylonian system and a Macedonian system. The era remained in use well beyond the Seleucid Empire itself, continuing in Jewish, Syriac, and Christian traditions into the medieval period and, in some communities, even later.

seleucid

seleucidBabylonian

Babylonian system

SE = Seleucid Era | lunisolar | spring

In the Babylonian system, year 1 began on 1 Nisanu (3 April 311 BCE). Each Seleucid year ran approximately from spring in one Gregorian year to spring in the next. This system was used in Babylon and Coele-Syria and was adopted by Jewish communities. Because the year began in spring, its correspondence with the Gregorian calendar differs by one year from the Macedonian system for part of each year.

seleucid

seleucidMacedonian

Macedonian system

SE = Seleucid Era | lunisolar | autumn

In the Macedonian system, year 1 began in autumn 312 BCE. The Macedonian court adopted the Babylonian lunisolar calendar structure but reckoned the New Year in autumn. Each Seleucid year therefore ran approximately from autumn in one Gregorian year to autumn in the next. Later West Syrian Christian usage standardised the year as running from 1 October to 30 September.

  • Conversion of Seleucid Era (SE) and Gregorian BCE | CE dates

If the Seleucid year (x) is less than 312, the corresponding BCE interval runs from autumn 313 − x to autumn 312 − x (Macedonian system) or from spring 312 − x to spring 311 − x (Babylonian system).

If the Seleucid year (x) is greater than 312, the corresponding CE interval runs from autumn x − 312 to autumn x − 311 (Macedonian system) or from spring x − 311 to spring x − 310 (Babylonian system).

Babylonian months: 1- Nisanu | 2- Ayyaru | 3- Simanu | 4 - Duʾuzu | 5- Abu | 6- Ululu | 7- Tashritu | 8- Arahsamnu | 9- Kislimu | 10- Tebetu | 11- Shabatu | 12- Addaru — Leap years add a second Ululu or Addaru.

Macedonian months: 1- Dios | 2- Apellaios | 3- Audnaios | 4 - Peritios | 5- Dystros | 6- Xandikos | 7- Artemisios | 8- Daisios | 9- Panemos | 10- Loios | 11- Gorpiaios | 12- Hyperberetaios

Because Seleucid years begin either in spring or in autumn, each SE year overlaps two Gregorian years and does not correspond to a single Gregorian year.

For year-only Seleucid dates, notBefore and notAfter should be used to encode the earliest and latest possible Gregorian years associated with the Seleucid year.

• Date of Creation: 670 SE (359–360 CE)

<origDate notBefore="0359" notAfter="0360" when-custom="670" datingMethod="seleucid" calendar="seleucidBabylonian">670 SE (359–360 CE)</origDate>

• Date of Creation: 15 Nisanu 670 SE (17 April 359 CE)

<origDate when="0359-04-17" when-custom="670-01-15" datingMethod="seleucid" calendar="seleucidBabylonian">15 Nisanu 670 SE (17 April 359 CE)</origDate>

• Date of Creation: 670 SE (358–359 CE)

<origDate notBefore="0358" notAfter="0359" when-custom="670" datingMethod="seleucid" calendar="seleucidMacedonian">670 SE (358–359 CE)</origDate>

• Date of Creation: 15 Dios 670 SE (15 October 358 CE)

<origDate when="0358-10-15" when-custom="670-01-15" datingMethod="seleucid" calendar="seleucidMacedonian">15 Dios 670 SE (15 October 358 CE) </origDate>

Where the distinction is known, the more precise values seleucidBabylonian or seleucidMacedonian must be used. Use calendar=seleucidEra only when the source does not specify whether the date follows the Babylonian or Macedonian system, and this cannot be determined from contextual evidence. The generic value seleucidEra should be treated as a fallback and not as a preferred encoding choice.

Gregorian calendar dates recorded in the standard dating attributes must follow ISO 8601 formatting. The custom dating attributes are defined for non-ISO calendar systems and store the date value in that system’s own numbering. ISO 8601 formatting rules do not apply to these attributes unless the original source itself presents the date in an ISO-compatible form. Where a source does not record a non-Gregorian date, or where only a normalised Gregorian equivalent is required, it is acceptable to supply the Gregorian date alone using the standard attributes without employing the custom dating attributes.

1.68 <origPlace> | origin Place

<origPlace>  (origin place) contains any form of place name, used to identify the place of origin for a manuscript, manuscript part, or other object.

MDC display label — Origin Place

<origPlace> within <origin> is used to record the place associated with the production or creation of the item.

Although TEI supports many different approaches to place encoding, the place of origin is typically recorded within <origPlace> using <country>, and, where appropriate, <settlement>, or <region>.

1.69 <country> | country | <settlement> | settlement | <region> | region

<country> (country) contains the name of a geo-political unit, such as a nation, country, colony, or commonwealth, larger than or administratively superior to a region and smaller than a bloc.

<settlement> (settlement) contains the name of a settlement such as a city, town, or village identified as a single geo-political or administrative unit.

<region> (region) contains the name of an administrative unit such as a state, province, or county, larger than a settlement, but smaller than a country.

When applicable, these elements must include source and key.

@source | @key

source specifies the authority or source from which the identifier is derived.

key a persistent, unique identifier for the named entity, provided by that source.

source and key should together provide a unique identifier drawn from a controlled vocabulary or classification system. The Getty Thesaurus of Geographic Names is the preferred source.

tgn

Getty Thesaurus of Geographic Names (TGN)

The Getty Thesaurus of Geographic Names (TGN) is a structured vocabulary, containing names, descriptions, and other metadata for extant and historical cities, empires, archaeological sites, and physical features.

<taxonomy xml:id="tgn">
 <bibl>
  <ref target="https://www.getty.edu/research/tools/vocabularies/tgn/index.html">Getty Thesaurus of Geographic Names (TGN)</ref>
 </bibl>
</taxonomy>

When the value #tgn is used for source, it indicates that the identifier recorded in key is drawn from this authority file. The prefix # indicates a reference to an identifier defined elsewhere in the same file. In this case, it refers to the <taxonomy> declared in the header with xml:id=tgn. This value can be selected from a controlled dropdown list when entering the attribute and does not need to be typed manually.

Place names are encoded using the preferred form from the Getty Thesaurus of Geographic Names (TGN). key must include the unique numeric ID for the place from TGN.

Italy

TGN ID: 1000080 | source="#tgn" key="1000080"

Permalink: http://vocab.getty.edu/page/tgn/1000080

<origPlace>
 <country source="#tgn" key="1000080">Italy</country>
</origPlace>

The preferred format for the textual value is to record the country first, followed by a comma and space, and then the name of the city, town, or region.

1.69.1 City, town, village

• Origin Place: Italy, Florence

<origPlace>
 <country source="#tgn" key="1000080">Italy</country>, <settlement source="#tgn" key="7000457">Florence</settlement>
</origPlace>

Region

• Origin Place: Italy, Tuscany

<origPlace>
 <country source="#tgn" key="1000080">Italy</country>, <region source="#tgn" key="7009760">Tuscany</region>
</origPlace>

Examples of administrative units that are larger than a settlement but smaller than a country:

Autonomous Community — Spain | Catalonia, Andalusia, Galicia

County — England | Greater Manchester, Cambridgeshire, Oxfordshire, Kent, Surrey

Prefecture — Japan | Tokyo, Osaka, Kyoto

Province — Canada | Ontario, Quebec, British Columbia

State — Germany | Bavaria, Saxony, Hesse

Region — France | Île-de-France, Provence-Alpes-Côte d'Azur, Brittany

Region — Italy | Tuscany, Lombardy, Sicily

State — Australia | New South Wales, Victoria, Queensland

State — United States | California, Texas, Florida

• Origin Place: Spain, Catalonia

<origPlace>
 <country source="#tgn" key="1000095">Spain</country>, <region source="#tgn" key="7030216">Catalonia</region>
</origPlace>

The Rylands Sephardi Haggadah: Hebrew MS 6

General region | cardinal points

Central | North | South | East | West | Northeast | Northwest | Southeast | Southwest

• Origin Place: Italy, Central

<origPlace>
 <country source="#tgn" key="1000080">Italy</country>
 <region source="#tgn" key="4005361">Central</region>
</origPlace>

Country

• Origin Place: Italy

<origPlace>
 <country source="#tgn" key="1000080">Italy</country>
</origPlace>

Continent

• Origin Place: Europe

<origPlace>
 <region source="#tgn" key="1000003">Europe</region>
</origPlace>

Sub-continent

• Origin Place: Europe, Northern

<origPlace>
 <region source="#tgn" key="4003757">Europe, Northern</region>
</origPlace>

The level of specificity applied when recording <origPlace> information depends on the details available. Where only broad geographical information is known, record the most specific level available, such as a continent or region. Where more detailed information is available, record the country and, where appropriate, the settlement or region.

If the country is unknown but a broader geographical area is known, record the most specific level available, such as a continent or broad region. For example, if the country is unknown but the continent or region is known, such as Africa, Europe, North Africa, or Central Europe, record that information.

For content concerning the entire United Kingdom rather than its individual countries, England, Scotland, Wales, and Northern Ireland, use the nation name: United Kingdom.

Do not record political or intergovernmental organisations, such as the European Union or United Nations, as places of origin. Instead, record the relevant member country or countries, based on the content and time of production.

Temporal | cultural places

Temporal and cultural place names refer to forms of place names that are specific to a particular historical period, cultural context, language, community, or source tradition. These forms may include ancient names, historic names, colonial names, Indigenous names, or modern established names.

When recording temporal or cultural place names, use the form of the place name appropriate to the item’s context. Where the place name or boundary has changed, include the modern established or equivalent form as well, and record both the historic and current geographical context where relevant.

Maintain a hierarchical structure within <origPlace> using <country> and, where appropriate, <settlement> or <region>. Where both a context-specific form and a modern or equivalent form are recorded, encode them as two separate <placeName> within the relevant place element, and use type to distinguish the form of name being recorded.

@type

type classifies the form of place name being recorded, according to its temporal or cultural context.

The following values should be used:

ancient used for place names that originate from ancient times, typically referring to places known from classical antiquity or prehistoric times. For instance, Babylon is the name for the ancient city located in present-day Bābil.

historic indicates that the place name is of historical significance. It may refer to a name used in historical contexts, historical records, or literature. For instance, Constantinople is the historic name for what is now Istanbul.

modern provided there is an equivalent modern place name, denotes that the place name is currently in use in the present day. It refers to the contemporary name of a place.

The preferred format for the textual value is to record the country first, followed by a comma and space, then the ancient, historic, colonial, Indigenous, or otherwise context-specific name, with the modern or equivalent name in brackets where relevant.

Additional equivalents that are neither modern forms nor names relevant to the item’s historical, cultural, or source context should not be included as part of the item’s geographic information.

• Origin Place: Egypt, Hermopolis (Al Ashmūnayn)

<origPlace>
 <country source="#tgn" key="7016833">Egypt</country>, <settlement>
  <placeName type="ancient" source="#tgn" key="7534548">Hermopolis</placeName> (<placeName type="modern" source="#tgn" key="7001218">Al Ashmūnayn</placeName>)</settlement>
</origPlace>
  • TGN preferred name | Al Ashmūnayn, without a hyphen.

Where TGN provides a single place identifier for either the modern settlement or the ancient or historic place, but not separate identifiers for both, place source and key on <settlement> and encode the ancient or historic and modern names as separate <placeName> within it.

• Origin Place: Egypt, Oxyrhynchus (Al Bahnasā)

<origPlace>
 <country source="#tgn" key="7001267">Egypt</country>, <settlement source="#tgn" key="7001267">
  <placeName type="ancient">Oxyrhynchus</placeName> (<placeName type="modern">Al Bahnasā</placeName>)</settlement>
</origPlace>
  • TGN preferred name | Al Bahnasā, without a hyphen.

When the place of creation differs from the place of discovery, use <origPlace> to record the place of creation and <provenance> to record the place of discovery. These should be kept separate, even where the locations are geographically close. (<!-- See <provenance> – Hyperlink).

The same approach may be used for historic country or regional names with modern equivalents, where both forms are relevant to the item’s context. In such cases, encode the historic and modern names within the relevant place element.

• Origin Place: Persia (Iran)

<origPlace>
 <country>
  <placeName type="historic" source="#tgn" key="7024079">Persia</placeName> (<placeName type="modern" source="#tgn" key="7000231">Iran</placeName>)</country>
</origPlace>

• Origin Place: Siam (Thailand)

<origPlace>
 <country source="#tgn" key="1000142">
  <placeName type="historic">Siam</placeName> (<placeName type="modern">Thailand</placeName>)</country>
</origPlace>

Case example | The Heinrich Simon Papers

The Heinrich Simon Papers consist principally of letters, most of them exchanged within the Simon family, diaries and other documents relating to the various stages of Heinrich Simon’s life. These materials were created in locations that are now part of modern-day Germany and Poland, but were within the historical Kingdom of Prussia.

The Kingdom of Prussia was a major German state that existed from 1701 to 1918. It played a crucial role in European history, particularly in the unification of Germany in the nineteenth century. Prussian territories included parts of modern-day Germany, Poland, Russia, Lithuania, Denmark, Belgium, and the Czech Republic.

The distinction between historical German place names and modern official Polish names is significant because many places in the collection have undergone changes in sovereignty, borders, and official naming. This approach preserves historical context while respecting historical naming practices and aligning with contemporary naming conventions, ensuring that the information remains accessible and meaningful to users today.

In the following example, Breslau is used as the historical German name found on the item, with Wrocław recorded in brackets as the modern Polish name. xml:lang is used on each <placeName> to specify the language of that form of the place name, distinguishing the German and Polish forms.

• Origin Place: Poland, Breslau (Wrocław)

<origin>
 <origPlace>
  <country source="#tgn" key="7006366">Poland</country>, <settlement source="#tgn" key="7007601">
   <placeName type="historic" xml:lang="ger">Breslau</placeName> (<placeName type="modern" xml:lang="pol">Wrocław</placeName>)</settlement>
 </origPlace>
</origin>

Letter from Herrmann Simon to his brother Heinrich Simon the Elder: HS/C/13

Case example | decolonising the Arctic: Manchester Geographical Society Lantern Slides Collection

The Manchester Geographical Society Lantern Slides Collection offers insight into how geography, exploration, and empire were visually represented and taught in late nineteenth- and early twentieth-century Britain. While the collection has educational and scientific value, it also reflects the colonial attitudes that shaped geographic knowledge at the time. Modern preservation and digitisation make the collection available as a resource for studying the history of geography, visual culture, and imperialism.

Decolonising the Arctic involves recognising Indigenous rights, knowledge, sovereignty, and connections to land. In descriptive metadata, this means moving beyond colonial naming conventions alone and recording Indigenous place names where they are relevant to the item and its historical context, so that the description more accurately reflects Indigenous relationships to place.

Recording both the English colonial name and the Indigenous Inuktitut name acknowledges the historical context of the item while supporting more inclusive and culturally sensitive description. This approach preserves historical context while respecting Indigenous naming practices and aligning with contemporary naming conventions, ensuring that the information remains accessible and meaningful to users today.

In the following example, Pond Inlet is used as the English historic or colonial name, with Mittimatalik recorded in brackets as the Indigenous Inuktitut name for the settlement in Nunavut, Canada. xml:lang is used on each <placeName> to specify the language and script of that form of the place name, distinguishing the English and romanised Inuktitut forms.

• Origin Place: Canada, Pond Inlet (Mittimatalik)

<origin>
 <origPlace>
  <country source="#tgn" key="7005685">Canada</country>, <settlement source="#tgn" key="1014385">
   <placeName type="historic" xml:lang="eng">Pond Inlet</placeName> (<placeName type="modern" xml:lang="iku-latn-x-lc">Mittimatalik</placeName>)</settlement>
 </origPlace>
</origin>

iku-latn-x-lc specifies that Mittimatalik is the romanised form of the Inuktitut name ᒥᑦᑎᒪᑕᓕᒃ, according to the ALA-LC Romanisation standard.

iku is the ISO 639-3 language code for Inuktitut.

latn is the ISO 15924 script code for the Latin alphabet. Normally, Inuktitut is written in the Inuktitut script, but here, it specifies that the content is in Inuktitut but written using the Latin alphabet (romanisation).

x-lc is a custom extension sub-tag indicating adherence to the ALA-LC Romanisation standard.

The ALA-LC Romanisation Tables are widely used in library cataloguing and bibliographic metadata for converting non-Latin scripts into Latin script.

1.69.2 Various | multiple places of origin

If an item was produced in more than one place, such as a travel diary, scrapbook, or composite volume, use judgement to determine the most appropriate level of place description. This may be the widest shared geographical area, or some or all of the individual places of production, where these are significant to the item’s creation or interpretation.

• Origin Place: Germany — Berlin, Hirschberg; Poland — Breslau (Wrocław), Brieg (Brzeg), Schmiedeberg im Riesengebirge (Kowary)

• Origin:

Individual items within the volume were created during the years 1816–1824, in various parts of present-day Germany and Poland, in the Kingdom of Prussia (1701-1918):

Germany — Berlin, Hirschberg Poland — Breslau (Wrocław), Brieg (Brzeg), Schmiedeberg im Riesengebirge (Kowary)

<origin>
 <p>Individual items within the volume were created during the years ..., in various parts of present-day Germany and Poland, in the Kingdom of Prussia (<date from="1701" to="1918">1701-1918 </date>):</p>
 <list>
  <item>
   <origPlace>
    <country source="#tgn" key="7000084">Germany</country> — <settlement source="#tgn" key="7003712">Berlin</settlement>, <settlement source="#tgn" key="7171504">Hirschberg</settlement>
   </origPlace>
  </item>
  <item>
   <origPlace>
    <country source="#tgn" key="7006366">Poland</country> — <settlement source="#tgn" key="7007601">
     <placeName type="historic" xml:lang="ger">Breslau</placeName> (<placeName type="modern" xml:lang="pol">Wrocław</placeName>)</settlement>, <settlement source="#tgn" key="7007623">
     <placeName type="historic" xml:lang="ger">Brieg</placeName> (<placeName type="modern" xml:lang="pol">Brzeg </placeName>)</settlement>, <settlement>Schmiedeberg im Riesengebirge (Kowary)</settlement>.
   </origPlace>
  </item>
 </list>
</origin>

Papers of Heinrich Simon: The Pupil: SIM/1

  • em dash (—) may be used to separate the country from the associated settlements where this improves clarity.

Conjectural | supplied places of origin

Conjectural places are hypothesised locations based on incomplete or indirect evidence, such as historical references, internal evidence in the item, external documentation, or geographical analysis, but lacking definitive proof. Where the place of origin or creation cannot be determined, an estimated, probable, or supplied place may be recorded, provided that its uncertain status is encoded explicitly using cert and, where appropriate, evidence.

@cert

cert (certainty) signifies the degree of certainty associated with the localisation

The following values should be used:

high the localisation is generally considered to be correct.

Inferred | a place name is known but not explicitly stated on the item, it is inferred. In other catalogues or finding aids, such names may be recorded in square brackets, for example, [Florence].

medium the localisation is generally considered approximately correct.

Approximate | a place name that is probable, most likely correct but might be doubtful. In other catalogues or finding aids, such names might be recorded with a question mark, for example, Florence? or ?Florence.

low the localisation is generally considered uncertain but possible.

Questionable | a place name that is possible but might be questionable, uncertain, or incorrect; tentative identification.

unknown no wish to assert an opinion, as the level of certainty is unknown.

@evidence

evidence indicates the nature of the evidence supporting the localisation

The following values should be used:

internal the localisation is based on evidence internal to the item, for example, an inscription, a script.

external the localisation is based on evidence external to the item, for example, a reference to the item in another context.

conjecture the localisation is based on conjecture. No specific evidence is available to support the localisation. The place name has been inferred by the encoder, cataloguer, or scholar based on contextual knowledge or expertise.

In the following example, Florence is recorded as a probable place of origin. cert=medium indicates that the localisation is probable but not definitive. The supporting evidence is described in prose within <origin>.

• Origin Place: Italy, Florence

• Origin:

Circumstantial evidence suggests that this manuscript may have been created in Tuscany, possibly in Florence. The use of the word 'huopo' instead of 'uo' suggests Florentine dialect, while the inclusion of the 'Visione contemplativa di San Bernardo', originally published in Tuscany in 1397, may hint at a geographical link. St Bernardo appears in Paradiso, acting as Dante's final guide (cantos 31 to 33).

<origin>
 <origPlace>
  <country source="#tgn" key="1000080">Italy</country>, <settlement source="#tgn" key="7000457" cert="medium" evidence="internal">Florence</settlement>
 </origPlace>
 <p>Circumstantial evidence suggests that this manuscript may have been created in Tuscany, possibly in Florence. The use of the word 'huopo' instead of 'uo' suggests Florentine dialect, while the inclusion of the 'Visione contemplativa di San Bernardo', originally published in Tuscany in 1397, may hint at a geographical link. St Bernardo appears in Paradiso, acting as Dante's final guide (cantos 31 to 33).</p>
</origin>

La Commedia: Italian MS 2

cert and evidence do not have to be used together.

The use of cert and evidence provide a clear spatial context for uncertain or probable places (supplied or conjectural), especially when the exact place of origin is unknown.

If no place of origin can be found in the item itself or determined from another source, but a place can be estimated, record the most precise probable location that the evidence supports. The basis for the supplied or conjectural place should be explained in a <p> within <origin>, including the source of the information and how the localisation was determined. Maintain consistency in the language used to express uncertainty:

probably — highly likely to be the case than not (>50% chance) — cert=high

possibly — could be the case or be true but not certain (<50% chance) — cert=medium | low

Do not use square brackets ([ ]) | question marks (?) or any other markup normally used in MARC | RDA, or other cataloguing standards to indicate uncertainty, supplied or conjectural places, missing or incorrect information. In TEI, uncertainty should be encoded explicitly using the appropriate attributes and explained in prose where necessary.

1.69.3 Uncertain place | one place or another

The place of origin is known to be one of two possible places.

The following encoding approach may be used where the available evidence does not allow one place to be confirmed, particularly when distinguishing between multiple settlements within a single country.

Where the possible places are settlements within the same country, the shared country may be recorded once, followed by each possible settlement. Where the possible places are countries, regions, or other places at the same geographical level, each possible place should be encoded separately within the explanatory prose. In all cases, the prose should make clear that the place of origin is uncertain.

• Origin Place: Italy, Padua; Venice

• Origin:

Stylistic evidence suggests that it was created in Italy, possibly in Padua or Venice.

<origin>
 <origPlace>
