  <country source="#tgn" key="1000080">Italy</country>, <settlement source="#tgn" key="7003085" cert="low" evidence="internal">Padua</settlement>; <settlement source="#tgn" key="7018159" cert="low" evidence="internal">Venice</settlement>
 </origPlace>
 <p>Stylistic evidence suggests that it was created in Italy, possibly in Padua or Venice.</p>
</origin>

Johannes Cassianus: Latin MS 49

• Origin Place: Greater Iran; Indian subcontinent

• Origin:

Possibly completed in either Greater Iran or the Indian subcontinent.

<origin> <p>Possibly completed in either <origPlace><region source="#tgn" key="7000235" cert="medium" >Greater Iran</region></origPlace> or <origPlace><region source="#tgn" key="7000208" cert="medium">Indian subcontinent</region></origPlace>.</p> <origin>

Nafāʾis al-Funūn fī ʻArāʾis al-ʻUyūn: Persian MS 147

• Origin Place: Italy; France

• Origin:

Possibly completed in either Italy or France.

<origin>
 <p>Possibly completed in either <origPlace>
   <country source="#tgn" key="1000080" cert="medium">Italy</country>
  </origPlace> or <origPlace>
   <country source="#tgn" key="1000070" cert="medium">France</country>
  </origPlace>.</p>
</origin>

Place information should not be recorded as plain text within <origPlace> where a structured place element can be used. Encode countries, regions, settlements, and place names using the appropriate TEI elements, such as <country>, <region>, <settlement>, and <placeName>. This is especially important where more than one possible place of origin is recorded, as each possible place should remain distinguishable in the encoding.

unknown origin <origDate> | <origPlace>

Where the place and date of origin cannot be established, and no estimate is intended, this may be recorded explicitly in <origin> where required or useful for a particular project. This approach is optional. Where used, it should be applied consistently across the project.

This approach may be useful where the item contains no internal or external evidence of origin or where supplying an estimated place or date would be misleading. Where Unknown is recorded explicitly, use cert=unknown on either <origDate> or <origPlace>, as appropriate, and provide Unknown as the content value. If the absence of information is the result of analytical judgement, include evidence=conjecture as well. Where useful, include <p> within <origin> to explain that the date, place, or both could not be determined.

date and place unknown

• Origin Place: Unknown

• Date of Creation: Unknown

<origin>
 <origDate cert="unknown" evidence="conjecture">Unknown</origDate>
 <origPlace cert="unknown" evidence="conjecture">Unknown</origPlace>
</origin>

If the project does not require Unknown origin information to be displayed or encoded, <origDate> and <origPlace> may be omitted.

1.69.4 Place known, date unknown | date known, place unknown

In cases where only one of the origin elements is unknown, place known | date unknown or date known | place unknown, apply the same principle: use cert=unknown on either <origPlace> or <origDate> as appropriate, and provide Unknown as the content value. If the absence is the result of analytical judgement, include evidence=conjecture as well.

date known, place unknown

• Origin Place: Unknown

• Date of Creation: 17th century, late

• Origin:

The date is estimated as the late seventeenth century, while the place of origin is unknown.

<origin>
 <origDate notBefore="1670" notAfter="1700" cert="medium">17th century, late</origDate>
 <origPlace cert="unknown" evidence="conjecture">Unknown</origPlace>
 <p>The date is estimated as the late seventeenth century, while the place of origin is unknown.</p>
</origin>

place known, date unknown

• Origin Place: Italy, Florence

• Date of Creation: Unknown

• Origin:

The place of origin is Italy, Florence, while the date is unknown.

<origin>
 <origDate cert="unknown" evidence="conjecture">Unknown</origDate>
 <origPlace>
  <country source="#tgn" key="1000080">Italy</country>, <settlement source="#tgn" key="7000457">Florence</settlement>
 </origPlace>
 <p>The place of origin is Italy, Florence, while the date is unknown.</p>
</origin>

Avoid using terms like no date, n.d., and date not identified within <origDate>, or terms such as s.d., S.l., S.n., and place not identified within <origPlace>.

1.70 <provenance> | provenance

<provenance> (provenance) contains any descriptive or other information concerning a single identifiable episode during the history of a manuscript, manuscript part, or other object after its creation but before its acquisition.

MDC display label — Provenance

<provenance> within <history> is used to record identifiable stages in the item’s post-creation history, tracing its chain of ownership, custody, discovery, and use as it passed between people or organisations before its acquisition by the present holding institution.

<provenance> may be used to record provenance information relating to the entire item or to an individual <msPart>.

<provenance> may take one of two forms:

Unstructured | a single <provenance> with one or more <p>, describing provenance stages narratively.

Structured | a sequence of <provenance>, each with one or more <p>, describing a distinct stage in the item’s provenance history.

Although unstructured encoding is permitted, it is not recommended as the default where multiple stages can be identified. Wherever possible, provenance information should be organised using one or more separate <provenance>, as this supports a clearer distinction between provenance stages.

Combining multiple stages within a single <provenance> is generally discouraged. It may occasionally be necessary in retroconversion workflows, particularly when existing catalogue information cannot be reliably separated into distinct stages.

Multiple <provenance> should be arranged in chronological order, from the earliest known stage to the most recent. The item’s acquisition by the present holding institution should be recorded separately in <acquisition>, where known.

<provenance> may include when, from, to, or notBefore, notAfter, to date the individual stages in the provenance chain.

when | from, to | notBefore, notAfter

when supplies the value of a single known provenance date in standard form.

from, to indicate known starting and ending points of a provenance stage in standard form.

notBefore, notAfter specify the earliest and latest possible dates of a provenance stage in standard form.

Dating attributes are recommended where the date or date range of a provenance stage can be established or reliably inferred. Where no reliable date can be assigned, dating attributes are not required, and <provenance> remains valid without them.

Where dates are mentioned within the prose of <provenance>, they may be recorded as text only, encoded using <date>, or encoded using <date> with dating attributes, as appropriate.

Any name associated with former ownership should be encoded using <name role="fmo">, containing either <persName> or <orgName> as appropriate. If the name is listed in VIAF, include both source and key on <name> to reference the relevant VIAF authority record. <persName> or <orgName> should be given in both a display and a standard form using type=display and type=standard respectively.

role specifies the relationship of a person or organisation to an item’s provenance. For the provenance heading, this value should normally be fmo, used for former owners, or, where appropriate, pat, used for patrons. Other MARC relator codes, such as bsl for booksellers or asn for associated names, may be used for names encoded within the supporting prose, where appropriate.

Each <provenance> should begin with a <p> containing the name of the associated former owner or patron. Do not include a full stop at the end of a provenance heading where <p> contains only the encoded name.

The following format is required for individuals and organisations:

For individuals, <persName type="display"> should give the display form of the name, followed by life dates in parentheses where known (birth and death years), and a brief descriptive qualifier indicating an occupation and or role.

Full personal name (YYYY-YYYY), descriptive qualifier

• Former Owner(s): Patarolo, Lorenzo, 1674-1757

• Provenance:

▪ Lorenzo Patarolo (1674-1757), scholar and antiquarian

<p>
 <name source="#viaf" key="44644175" role="fmo">
  <persName type="display">Lorenzo Patarolo (1674-1757), scholar and antiquarian</persName>
  <persName type="standard">Patarolo, Lorenzo, 1674-1757</persName>
 </name>
</p>

When a name functions as a provenance heading, a brief descriptive qualifier should be included in <persName type="display"> or <orgName type="display">. Where a name appears within narrative prose, descriptive qualifiers should, where necessary, be provided in the surrounding text rather than within <persName> or <orgName>.

For organisations, <orgName type="standard"> should give the authorised form of the name, following the preferred term from VIAF where available. VIAF forms may be qualified or unqualified depending on disambiguation needs. <orgName type="display"> should give the form intended for presentation in the MDC interface and may include an added geographical qualifier in parentheses where this improves clarity. Where a place qualifier is supplied in the display form, it should be given consistently as city, country, aligning with established VIAF presentation conventions.

Full organisation name (city, country)

• Former Owner(s): Accademia della Crusca

• Provenance:

▪ Accademia della Crusca (Florence, Italy)

<p>
 <name source="#viaf" key="143077072" role="fmo">
  <orgName type="display">Accademia della Crusca (Florence, Italy)</orgName>
  <orgName type="standard">Accademia della Crusca</orgName>
 </name>
</p>

Descriptive qualifiers, such as occupations or roles, should be derived primarily from the Oxford Dictionary of National Biography (DNB), and secondarily from VIAF®.

Where a personal name is accompanied by a descriptive qualifier, check whether the same name–qualifier combination is already in use within the system. Where an established form exists, it should be reused exactly as recorded. Where no such form is available, assess whether the proposed combination is current, authoritative, and appropriate, and ensure that it is consistent with established authority sources and local practice.

Supporting evidence should follow the provenance heading, in one or more subsequent <p> within the same <provenance>. These <p> should be ordered in a clear and logical sequence. Where applicable, give any relevant institutional or collection context before the supporting evidence of ownership, whether internal, external, or both.

Internal evidence may include, but is not limited to, bookplates, colophons, ownership inscriptions, autographs, signatures, manuscript numbers, sale prices, labels, stamps, shelfmarks, descriptions of ownership marks, including heraldic features where present, or other notes of ownership or use. Where the location of the evidence is known, <locus> should be used within the description to indicate where it appears on the item.

External evidence may derive from sources such as dealer or auction catalogues, booklists, inventories, wills, accounts, archival records, scholarly descriptions, reference works, or other published sources that mention the item. Sources should be cited clearly, either in full within the prose or by using a short-form reference with a corresponding full entry within <listBibl>.

• Former Owner(s): Patarolo, Lorenzo, 1674-1757

• Provenance:

▪ Lorenzo Patarolo (1674-1757), scholar and antiquarian

Early manuscript inscription on the lower margin of *1r: Laurentii Patarol.

<provenance>
 <p>
  <name source="#viaf" key="44644175" role="fmo">
   <persName type="display">Lorenzo Patarolo (1674-1757), scholar and antiquarian</persName>
   <persName type="standard">Patarolo, Lorenzo, 1674-1757</persName>
  </name>
 </p>
 <p>Early manuscript inscription on the lower margin of <locus from="*1r" to="*1r">*1r</locus>: <hi rend="italic">Laurentii Patarol</hi>.</p>
</provenance>

Vita nuoua di Dante Alighieri...: R17114

A patron is not normally a primary provenance figure in the same way as a former owner or custodian. Their role may, however, be relevant where commissioning, financial support, or other evidence of patronage forms part of the item’s history.

• Provenance:

▪ Lorenzo di Strozzi (-1400), nobleman of the influential Strozzi family

The colophon on 174r acknowledges Lorenzo Strozzi as the commissioner and Paolo as the scribe. It also includes a blessing for the wellbeing of both the patron and the scribe.

The lower borders on 3r and 105r bear the arms of the Strozzi family: Or, on a fess Gules, three crescent moons Argent.

Diagonal pencil inscription on the Flyleaf 1r: Written for Lorenzo di Strozzi | circa 1520.

▪ Theodor Oswald Weigel (1812-1881), bookseller, art dealer, publisher, and collector

Stamped in gilt on the lower margin of the front cover: the initials T.O.W.

<provenance>
 <p>
  <name role="pat">
   <persName type="display">Lorenzo di Strozzi (-1400), nobleman of the influential Strozzi family</persName>
   <persName type="standard">Strozzi, Lorenzo, -1400</persName>
  </name>
 </p>
 <p>The colophon on <locus from="174r" to="174r">174r</locus> acknowledges Lorenzo Strozzi as the commissioner and Paolo as the scribe. It also includes a blessing for the wellbeing of both the patron and the scribe.</p>
 <p>The lower borders on <locus from="3r" to="3r">3r</locus> and <locus from="105r" to="105r">105r</locus> bear the Strozzi family arms: <heraldry>Or, on a fess Gules, three crescent moons Argent</heraldry>.</p>
 <p>Diagonal pencil inscription on the <locus from="Flyleaf_1r" to="Flyleaf_1r">flyleaf 1r</locus>: <hi rend="italic">Written for Lorenzo di Strozzi | circa 1520</hi>.</p>
</provenance>
<provenance>
 <p>
  <name source="#viaf" key="40150737" role="fmo">
   <persName type="display">Theodor Oswald Weigel (1812-1881), bookseller, art dealer, publisher, and collector</persName>
   <persName type="standard">Weigel, T.O. (Theodor Oswald), 1812-1881 </persName>
  </name>
 </p>
 <p>Stamped in gilt on the lower margin of the <locus from="Front_cover" to="Front_cover">front cover</locus>: the initials <hi rend="italic">T.O.W</hi>.</p>
</provenance>

Il Canzoniere di Petrarca e Le Rime di Dante: Italian MS 1

<orgName> is used for any named collection of people regarded as a single unit, including a family, institution, or organisation.

When the place of creation differs from the place of discovery, the two should be encoded separately and unambiguously, even where the locations are geographically close. Use <origPlace> to encode the place where the item was created or written, representing its geographic origin at the point of production. Use <provenance> to encode the place where the item was found or discovered, describing a specific episode in the object’s history after its creation and prior to its acquisition.

• Origin Place: Egypt, Krokodilopolis (Al Fayyūm)

• Date of Creation: 14 June 250

• Origin: Written on 14 June 250, in Egypt, Krokodilopolis (Al Fayyūm).

• Provenance:

▪ Found at Theadelphia (Batn-Ihrît).

<origin>
 <p>Written on <origDate when="0250-06-14">14 June 250</origDate> in <origPlace>
   <country source="#tgn" key="7016833">Egypt</country>, <settlement>
    <placeName type="ancient" source="#tgn" key="7000991">Krokodilopolis</placeName> (<placeName type="modern" source="#tgn" key="7001197">Al Fayyūm</placeName>)</settlement>
  </origPlace>.</p>
</origin>
<provenance>
 <p>Found at <settlement>
   <placeName type="ancient" source="#tgn" key="5000721">Theadelphia</placeName> (<placeName type="modern">Batn-Ihrît</placeName>)</settlement>
 </p>
</provenance>

Certificate of Pagan Sacrifice: Greek P 12

When the value #viaf is used for source, it indicates that the identifier recorded in key is drawn from this authority file. The prefix # indicates a reference to an identifier defined elsewhere in the same file. In this case, it refers to the <taxonomy> declared in the header with xml:id=viaf. This value can be selected from a controlled dropdown list when entering the attribute and does not need to be typed manually.

role on <name> is constrained by a controlled vocabulary defined in our customised TEI schema, extended via an ODD (One Document Does it all). The permitted values for the MARC relator codes appear in the XML editor as a drop-down list. Additional terms may be included through the ODD. Only these values are valid. Any others will result in a schema validation error.

Do not use <list> for provenance evidence.

Evidence of former ownership or custody, including heraldry, former shelfmarks, notes on endleaves, or other inscriptions, should be recorded in <provenance> even where it cannot be securely attributed to a named person or institution. If no clear provenance connection can be established, use <additions> instead.

1.71 <acquisition> | acquisition

<acquisition> (acquisition) contains any descriptive or other information concerning the process by which a manuscript or manuscript part or other object entered the holding institution

MDC display label— Acquisition

<acquisition> within <history> is used to record how the item was acquired by the present holding institution as the final stage in its history.

Any information specific to the means by which the item was acquired by its present owners should be given as one or more <p> within <acquisition>.

Any name associated with the acquisition of an item should be encoded using <name>, containing either <persName> or <orgName> as appropriate, and must include a role indicating the nature of the relationship to the item in the context of acquisition. If the name is listed in VIAF, include both source and key on <name> to reference the relevant VIAF authority record. <persName> or <orgName> should be given in both a display and a standard form using type=display and type=standard respectively.

role specifies the relationship of a person or organisation to an item’s acquisition. In most cases, this value should normally be dnr, used for donors, and own for the current owner, that is, the holding institution that acquired the item. Other MARC relator codes, such as bsl for booksellers or asn for associated names, may be used for names encoded within the supporting prose, where appropriate, to reflect the acquisition pathway. For example, where the item was purchased from a named vendor, auction house, or intermediary.

The date of acquisition should be encoded within <acquisition> using <date> with when for single known dates, or notBefore and notAfter for approximate or inferred ranges, as appropriate. This date will be pulled through into the display field labelled Acquisition Date.

Acquisition information should follow a clear and consistent sentence structure, using the following pattern, omitting any components that are not applicable or not known:

Action — by agent [personal or organisation name, with life dates where applicable] — in/on [date] — to the Library Action — by the Library — in/on [date] — from agent [personal or organisation name, with life dates where applicable]

The statement should begin with the method of acquisition, for example, Purchased, Acquired, Presented, Donated, or Bequeathed. It should then record the relevant person or organisation involved in the acquisition, the date of acquisition, and the holding institution, where applicable. Additional transaction details, such as price, fund, or accession number, may be included where known.

In MDC, unless otherwise stated, The John Rylands Library is assumed to be the final custodian and current owner.

Any further evidence, whether internal or external, may be included in prose and, where necessary, recorded in one or more <p> within the same <acquisition>.

• Acquisition:

Presented by David Lindsay, 27th Earl of Crawford (1871-1940) in 1924 to The John Rylands Library.

• Date of Acquisition: 1924

<acquisition>
 <p>Presented by <name source="#viaf" key="46770980" role="dnr">
   <persName type="display">David Lindsay, 27th Earl of Crawford (1871-1940)</persName>
   <persName type="standard">Crawford, David Lindsay, Earl of, 1871-1940</persName>
  </name> in <date when="1924">1924</date> to <name source="#viaf" key="312795145" role="own">
   <orgName>The John Rylands Library</orgName>
  </name>.</p>
</acquisition>

• Acquisition:

Purchased by The John Rylands Library on 24 November 1908 from Bernard Quaritch Ltd for £225. Accession number: R16980.

• Date of Acquisition: 24 November 1908

<acquisition>
 <p>Purchased by <name source="#viaf" key="312795145" role="own">
   <orgName>The John Rylands Library</orgName>
  </name> on <date when="1908-11-24">24 November 1908</date> from <name source="#viaf" key="141331450" role="bsl">
   <orgName type="display">Bernard Quaritch Ltd </orgName>
   <orgName type="standard">Bernard Quaritch (Firm)</orgName>
  </name> for £225. Accession number: R16980.</p>
</acquisition>

• Acquisition:

Purchased by The John Rylands Library on 2 July 2024 from Sotheby’s for £16,000.

• Date of Acquisition: 2 July 2024

<acquisition>
 <p>Purchased by <name source="#viaf" key="312795145" role="own">
   <orgName>The John Rylands Library</orgName>
  </name> on <date when="2024-07-02">2 July 2024</date> from Sotheby's for £16,000.</p>
</acquisition>

Within <acquisition>, the institution that acquired or obtained the item should be recorded explicitly as the owner. This should be encoded using <name> with role=own and <orgName>. This records ownership in the context of acquisition and is distinct from <repository> and <idno> within <msIdentifier>, which identify the item’s current repository and shelfmark.

Where individuals or organisations are referenced multiple times within <history>, they should normally be encoded in a controlled form only once, at the first occurrence of the name. Subsequent occurrences may be given in plain text, unless additional encoding is required to express a different role, relationship, or authority context.

Transfer details may be included where known, such as acquisition from a previous holder or intermediary, or transfer to the current repository. Where the acquisition history includes multiple institutional stages, and the provenance cannot be separated into distinct transactions, for example, acquired by the University and later transferred to the Library, this should be recorded as a single narrative statement that reflects the sequence of custody as accurately as possible.

• Donor(s): Bullock, Hélène Louise, 1881-1973

• Acquisition:

Donated by Hélène Louise Bullock (1881-1973), widow of Walter Llewellyn Bullock, in 1944 to The University of Manchester, and later transferred to The John Rylands Library.

• Date of Acquisition: 1899

<acquisition>
 <p>Donated by <name role="dnr">
   <persName type="display">Hélène Louise Bullock (1881-1973)</persName>
   <persName type="standard">Bullock, Hélène Louise, 1881-1973</persName>
  </name>, widow of Walter Llewellyn Bullock, in <date when="1944">1944</date> to <name source="#viaf" key="157695433">
   <orgName>The University of Manchester</orgName>
  </name>, and later transferred to <name source="#viaf" key="312795145" role="own">
   <orgName>The John Rylands Library</orgName>
  </name>.</p>
</acquisition>

Acquisition information may sometimes include provenance-related information where this forms part of the available acquisition evidence, particularly where it provides the only available account of how the item came into the holding institution’s possession.

The John Rylands Library foundation collections

The John Rylands Library’s foundation collections were established through two major acquisitions, both personally overseen by Enriqueta Rylands. Together, these acquisitions underpinned the Library’s exceptional collections of rare books and manuscripts.

For provenance purposes, Enriqueta Rylands should be recorded as a former owner. Many early acquisitions were purchased by her directly, and her name therefore appears in early transaction records as purchaser or owner, even where the material was acquired with the explicit intention of forming the Library’s collections.

For acquisition purposes, Enriqueta Rylands should be recorded as the donor. The Spenceriana was transferred during her lifetime, while the Lindesiana manuscripts entered the Library by bequest. In both cases, the acquisition reflects her foundational role in establishing the Library’s holdings.

Bibliotheca Spenceriana | Spencer Collection

The Bibliotheca Spenceriana is one of the most celebrated private libraries of early printed books ever assembled. Formed by George John Spencer, 2nd Earl Spencer (1758–1834) at Althorp, the collection is particularly rich in incunabula, early Bibles, Aldines, Caxtons, and works of classical and Renaissance humanist scholarship. It is renowned not only for the rarity and quality of individual items but also for its scholarly coherence, careful organisation, and elegant bindings.

In 1892, the entire Althorp Library was purchased by Enriqueta Rylands from John Poyntz Spencer, 5th Earl Spencer (1835–1910) for £210,000. She donated the collection to The John Rylands Library in 1899, making it the foundational printed book collection of the institution. The Bibliotheca Spenceriana catalogue, compiled by Thomas Frognall Dibdin, remains a landmark of bibliographical literature.

The agreed form for the Spencer Collection is:

• Former Owner(s): Spencer, George John Spencer, Earl, 1758-1834; Spencer, John Poyntz Spencer, Earl, 1835-1910; Rylands, Enriqueta, 1843-1908

• Donor(s): Rylands, Enriqueta, 1843-1908

• Provenance:

▪ George John Spencer, 2nd Earl Spencer (1758-1834), politician and book collector

▪ John Poyntz Spencer, 5th Earl Spencer (1835-1910), politician and viceroy of Ireland

Formerly in the Spencer Library (Bibliotheca Spenceriana) at Althorp, Northamptonshire, largely assembled by George John Spencer and subsequently passing by descent to John Poyntz Spencer.

▪ Enriqueta Rylands (1843-1908), philanthropist and founder of The John Rylands Library

Purchased by Enriqueta Rylands in 1901 from John Poyntz Spencer, 5th Earl Spencer (1835-1910).

• Acquisition:

Donated by Enriqueta Rylands (1843-1908) in 1899 to The John Rylands Library.

• Date of Acquisition: 1899

</acquisition>

Bibliotheca Lindesiana | Crawford Collection

The Bibliotheca Lindesiana was one of the most ambitious and cosmopolitan manuscript libraries established in Britain during the nineteenth century. Developed by Alexander William Crawford Lindsay (1812–1880), 25th Earl of Crawford and 8th Earl of Balcarres, known for most of his life as Lord Lindsay, and his son, James Ludovic Lindsay (1847–1913), 26th Earl of Crawford and 9th Earl of Balcarres, the collection was housed at Haigh Hall in Haigh, near Wigan. Conceived as a repository of universal knowledge, it possessed exceptional strengths across both European and non-European traditions, encompassing medieval and early modern codices, scrolls, fragments, papyri, and printed books in a remarkable array of languages, scripts, materials, and formats.

In 1901, a significant portion of the manuscript collection was purchased by Enriqueta Rylands from James Ludovic Lindsay for £155,000, with the intention of enhancing the new Library’s holdings. However, the majority of the manuscripts remained in her personal possession at Longford Hall and were bequeathed to The John Rylands Library after her death in 1908. This bequest greatly expanded the Library’s global manuscript coverage and remains a cornerstone of its special collections.

The agreed form for the Crawford Collection is:

• Former Owner(s): Crawford, Alexander Crawford Lindsay, 1812-1880; Crawford, James Ludovic Lindsay, Earl of, 1847-1913; Rylands, Enriqueta, 1843-1908

• Donor(s): Rylands, Enriqueta, 1843-1908

• Provenance:

▪ Alexander Lindsay, 25th Earl of Crawford (1812-1880), book collector and writer on art

▪ James Ludovic Lindsay, 26th Earl of Crawford (1847-1913), astronomer and book collector

Formerly in the Crawford Library (Bibliotheca Lindesiana) at Haigh Hall, Lancashire, which was formed and developed by Alexander and James Ludovic Lindsay.

▪ Enriqueta Rylands (1843-1908), philanthropist and founder of The John Rylands Library

Purchased by Enriqueta Rylands in 1901 from James Ludovic Lindsay, 26th Earl of Crawford (1847-1913).

• Acquisition:

Bequeathed by Enriqueta Rylands (1843–1908) in 1908 to The John Rylands Library.

• Date of Acquisition: 1908

</provenance>

 <p>Bequeathed by <name role="dnr">
   <persName type="display">Enriqueta Rylands (1843-1908)</persName>
   <persName type="standard">Rylands, Enriqueta, 1843-1908</persName>
  </name> in <date when="1908">1908</date> to <name source="#viaf" key="312795145" role="own">
   <orgName>The John Rylands Library</orgName>

</acquisition>

There may be cases in which only one of the Crawfords and only one of the Spencers is associated with provenance, and the statement should reflect only the individual or entity directly involved.

It should be noted that the Bibliotheca Lindesiana manuscripts did not enter The John Rylands Library solely through Enriqueta Rylands’s 1901 purchase and 1908 bequest. Further material from the Crawford library continued to come to the Library during the early twentieth century through additional gifts and deposits by the Lindsay family, including the European Proclamations and Broadsides presented to The John Rylands Library by David Lindsay, 27th Earl of Crawford, in 1924.

• Former Owner(s): Crawford, Alexander Crawford Lindsay, 1812-1880; Crawford, James Ludovic Lindsay, Earl of, 1847-1913; Crawford, David Lindsay, Earl of, 1871-1940

• Donor(s): Crawford, David Lindsay, Earl of, 1871-1940

• Provenance:

▪ Alexander Lindsay, 25th Earl of Crawford (1812-1880), book collector and writer on art

▪ James Ludovic Lindsay, 26th Earl of Crawford (1847-1913), astronomer and book collector

Formerly in the Crawford Library (Bibliotheca Lindesiana) at Haigh Hall, Lancashire, which was formed and developed by Alexander and James Ludovic Lindsay.

During the last quarter of the nineteenth century, the 26th Earl of Crawford actively directed a series of large purchases at auction to expand the French Revolution collection, which his son, David Lindsay, 27th Earl of Crawford (1871-1940), politician and art connoisseur, then transferred to The John Rylands Library during the first half of the twentieth century in the form of a gift and a semi-permanent loan.

• Acquisition:

Presented by David Lindsay, 27th Earl of Crawford (1871-1940) in 1924 to The John Rylands Library.

• Date of Acquisition: 1924

  </name>, then transferred to The John Rylands Library during the first half of the twentieth century in the form of a gift and a semi-permanent loan.</p>
</provenance>

   <persName type="standard">Crawford, David Lindsay, Earl of, 1871-1940</persName>
  </name> in <date when="1924">1924</date> to <name source="#viaf" key="312795145" role="own">
   <orgName>The John Rylands Library</orgName>

Armorial bookplates associated with titled families such as the Earls of Crawford and Balcarres and the Earls of Spencer may appear in multiple heraldic variants, including forms with and without marital impalement. Descriptions should record the form present on the item. Where no individual is explicitly named, such bookplates should be attributed at the family or title level rather than to a specific person.

1.72 <heraldry> | heraldry

<heraldry> (heraldry) contains a heraldic formula or phrase, typically found as part of a blazon, coat of arms, etc.

<heraldry> is a phrase-level, context-dependent element used to encode heraldic description within prose.

In heraldry, an achievement is the complete armorial display associated with a person, family, or institution. This may comprise the coat of arms together with associated components such as crest, coronet, supporters, badges, motto, order, or mantling. A heraldic description may refer to the arms alone, or to part or all of an achievement.

<heraldry> may be used within <physDesc> where heraldry forms part of a binding, seal, or decorative programme and is not being recorded as provenance evidence. In such cases, the full heraldic description should be encoded in the relevant element, such as <binding>, <seal>, or <decoNote>.

Where heraldry has both a physical and a provenance function, the full heraldic description should normally be encoded in <provenance>. The relevant physical description element may refer to the presence of the heraldry but should not repeat the full heraldic description encoded using <heraldry>.

<heraldry> may also be used within <acquisition> where armorial evidence is mentioned as part of the acquisition history.

Where heraldry serves primarily as evidence of former ownership, patronage, commissioning, or custody, the full heraldic description should be encoded using <heraldry> within <provenance>.

For British armorial ownership marks on bindings, the British Armorial Bindings database may be consulted to support identification of owners, heraldic stamps, mottoes, and related armorial evidence.

Crawford and Spencer heraldry, and variant forms

Armorial evidence associated with titled families should be described according to the heraldic form present on the item, including its location using <locus> where appropriate. This is particularly important where arms associated with the same family or peerage title may appear in different variants, including forms with and without marital impalement. Where no individual is explicitly named, the evidence should be attributed to the family or peerage title rather than to a specific person, for example, bearing the arms of the Earls of Crawford and Balcarres or bearing the arms of the Earls of Spencer.

For Crawford material, the following pattern will usually be applicable:

..., the armorial bookplate (Ex Libris) bearing the arms of the Earls of Crawford and Balcarres: Quarterly, 1st and 4th, Gules a fess chequy Argent and Azure. 2nd and 3rd, Or a lion rampant Gules, debruised of a ribbon in bend Sable. Crest: a swan’s head, neck, and wings proper, issuing from an antique ducal coronet Or. Supporters: two lions, rampant guardant Gules. Motto: ENDURE FORT | Bibliotheca Lindesiana.

<p>..., the armorial bookplate (Ex Libris) bearing the arms of the Earls of Crawford and Balcarres: <heraldry>Quarterly, 1st and 4th, Gules a fess chequy Argent and Azure. 2nd and 3rd, Or a lion rampant Gules, debruised of a ribbon in bend Sable. Crest: a swan’s head, neck, and wings proper, issuing from an antique ducal coronet Or. Supporters: two lions, rampant guardant Gules. Motto: <hi rend="italic">ENDURE FORT</hi>
 </heraldry> | <hi rend="italic">Bibliotheca Lindesiana</hi>.</p>

For Spencer material, the same principle will usually apply, although the heraldry may appear in several variant forms. One frequently encountered variant is associated with George John Spencer, 2nd Earl Spencer:

..., the armorial bookplate (Ex Libris) bearing the arms of the Earls of Spencer, associated with the 2nd Earl Spencer: Quarterly, in the 2nd and 3rd quarters a fret Or, over all on a bend Sable three escallops Argent (Spencer), impaling Quarterly, 1st and 4th, a bend cotised between six crosses patty (Bingham), 2nd and 3rd, Ermine a lion rampant ducally crowned (Turberville). Supporters: a griffin and a wyvern erect on its tail, both collared and chained. Coronet of an Earl. Motto: DIEU DEFEND LE DROIT.

<p>..., the armorial bookplate (Ex Libris) bearing the arms of the Earls of Spencer, associated with the 2nd Earl Spencer: <heraldry>Quarterly, 2nd and 3rd a fret Or, over all on a bend Sable three escallops Argent (Spencer), impaling Quarterly, 1st and 4th, a bend cotised between six crosses patty (Bingham), 2nd and 3rd, Ermine a lion rampant ducally crowned (Turberville). Supporters: a griffin and a wyvern erect on its tail, both collared and chained. Coronet of an Earl. Motto: <hi rend="italic">DIEU DEFEND LE DROIT</hi>
 </heraldry>.</p>

A less frequently encountered variant is that associated with John Spencer, 1st Earl Spencer:

..., the armorial bookplate (Ex Libris) bearing the arms of the Earls of Spencer, associated with John Spencer, 1st Earl Spencer (1734-1783): Quarterly Argent and Gules, on the second and third quarters a fret Or, over all on a bend Sable three escallops Argent (Spencer), impaling Barry of eight Or and Gules (Poyntz). Crest: a ducal coronet. Supporters: a griffin and a wyvern erect on its tail, both collared and chained. Coronet: of an Earl. Motto: DIEU DEFEND LE DROIT.

<p>..., the armorial bookplate (Ex Libris) bearing the arms of the Earls of Spencer, associated with <name source="#viaf" key="43873805" role="fmo">
  <persName type="display">John Spencer, 1st Earl Spencer (1734-1783)</persName>
  <persName type="standard">Spencer, John Spencer, Earl, 1734-1783</persName>
 </name>: <heraldry>Quarterly Argent and Gules, on the 2nd and 3rd quarters a fret Or, over all on a bend Sable three escallops Argent (Spencer), impaling Barry of eight Or and Gules (Poyntz). Crest: a ducal coronet. Supporters: a griffin and a wyvern erect on its tail, both collared and chained. Coronet: of an Earl. Motto: <hi rend="italic">DIEU DEFEND LE DROIT</hi>
 </heraldry>.</p>

The following examples provide model patterns for recording recurring forms of armorial provenance evidence associated with Crawford and Spencer material. They show how to describe the physical evidence present on the item, attribute arms at family or title level where no individual is explicitly named, record identifiable heraldic variants where appropriate, and encode the heraldic description using <heraldry>. The wording should be adapted to the specific evidence found on the item.

• Provenance:

▪ Lorenzo di Strozzi (-1400), nobleman of the influential Strozzi family

The colophon on 174r acknowledges Lorenzo Strozzi as the commissioner and Paolo as the scribe. It also includes a blessing for the wellbeing of both the patron and the scribe.

The lower borders on 3r and 105r bear the arms of the Strozzi family: Or, on a fess Gules, three crescent moons Argent.

Diagonal pencil inscription on the Flyleaf 1r: Written for Lorenzo di Strozzi | circa 1520.

▪ Alexander Lindsay, 25th Earl of Crawford (1812-1880), book collector and writer on art

▪ James Ludovic Lindsay, 26th Earl of Crawford (1847-1913), astronomer and book collector

Formerly in the Crawford Library (Bibliotheca Lindesiana) at Haigh Hall, Lancashire, which was formed and developed by Alexander and James Ludovic Lindsay.

On the front pastedown, the armorial bookplate (Ex Libris) bearing the arms of the Earls of Crawford and Balcarres: Quarterly, 1st and 4th, Gules a fess chequy Argent and Azure. 2nd and 3rd, Or a lion rampant Gules, debruised of a ribbon in bend Sable. Crest: a swan’s head, neck, and wings proper, issuing from an antique ducal coronet Or. Supporters: two lions, rampant guardant Gules. Motto: ENDURE FORT | Bibliotheca Lindesiana | pencilled L/4.

The manuscript was purchased by Bernard Quaritch (1819-1899), bookseller and publisher, for Lord Lindsay for £250 at the Guglielmo Libri sale on 2 April 1859, the sixth day of the auction, lot 784 (Sotheby & Wilkinson, 1859, page 174).

▪ Enriqueta Rylands (1843-1908), philanthropist and founder of The John Rylands Library

Purchased by Enriqueta Rylands in 1901 from James Ludovic Lindsay, 26th Earl of Crawford (1847-1913).

• Acquisition:

Bequeathed by Enriqueta Rylands (1843–1908) in 1908 to The John Rylands Library.

• Date of Acquisition: 1908

<provenance> <p><name role="pat"><persName type="display">Lorenzo di Strozzi (-1400), nobleman of the influential Strozzi family</persName><persName type="standard">Strozzi, Lorenzo, -1400</persName></name></p> <p>The colophon on <locus from="174r" to="174r">174r</locus> acknowledges Lorenzo Strozzi as the commissioner and Paolo as the scribe. It also includes a blessing for the wellbeing of both the patron and the scribe.</p> <p>The lower borders on <locus from="3r" to="3r">3r</locus> and <locus from="105r" to="105r">105r</locus> bear the Strozzi family arms: <heraldry>Or, on a fess Gules, three crescent moons Argent</heraldry>.</p> <p>Diagonal pencil inscription on the <locus from="Flyleaf_1r" to="Flyleaf_1r">flyleaf 1r</locus>: <hi rend="italic">Written for Lorenzo di Strozzi | circa 1520</hi>.</p> </provenance> <provenance> <p><name source="#viaf" key="40150737" role="fmo"><persName type="display">Theodor Oswald Weigel (1812-1881), bookseller, art dealer, publisher, and collector</persName><persName type="standard">Weigel, T.O. (Theodor Oswald), 1812-1881 </persName></name></p> <p>Stamped in gilt on the lower margin of the <locus from="Front_cover" to="Front_cover">front cover</locus>: the initials <hi rend="italic">T.O.W</hi>.</p> </provenance> <p><name source="#viaf" key="97751058" role="fmo"><persName type="display">Alexander Lindsay, 25th Earl of Crawford (1812-1880), book collector and writer on art</persName><persName type="standard">Crawford, Alexander Crawford Lindsay, 1812-1880</persName></name></p> <p><name source="#viaf" key="9985420" role="fmo"><persName type="display">James Ludovic Lindsay, 26th Earl of Crawford (1847-1913), astronomer and book collector</persName><persName type="standard">Crawford, James Ludovic Lindsay, Earl of, 1847-1913</persName></name></p> <p>Formerly in the Crawford Library (Bibliotheca Lindesiana) at Haigh Hall, Lancashire, which was formed and developed by Alexander and James Ludovic Lindsay.</p> <p>On the <locus from="Inner_front_cover" to="Inner_front_cover">front pastedown</locus>, the armorial bookplate (Ex Libris), bearing the arms of the Earls of Crawford and Balcarres: <heraldry>Quarterly, 1st and 4th, Gules a fess chequy Argent and Azure. 2nd and 3rd, Or a lion rampant Gules, debruised of a ribbon in bend Sable. Crest: a swan’s head, neck, and wings proper, issuing from an antique ducal coronet Or. Supporters: two lions, rampant guardant Gules. Motto: <hi rend="italic">ENDURE FORT</hi></heraldry> |<hi rend="italic">Bibliotheca Lindesiana</hi> | pencilled <hi rend="italic">L/4</hi>.</p> <p>The manuscript was purchased by <name source="#viaf" key="5679225" role="bsl"><persName type="display">Bernard Quaritch (1819-1899)</persName><persName type="standard">Quaritch, Bernard, 1819–1899</persName></name>, bookseller and publisher, for Lord Lindsay for £250 at the Guglielmo Libri sale on <date when="1859-04-02">2 April 1859</date>, the sixth day of the auction, lot 784 (Sotheby &amp;amp; Wilkinson, 1859, page 174).</p> </provenance> <provenance> <p><name role="fmo"><persName type="display">Enriqueta Rylands (1843-1908), philanthropist and founder of The John Rylands Library</persName><persName type="standard">Rylands, Enriqueta, 1843-1908</persName></name></p> <p>Purchased by Enriqueta Rylands in <date when="1901">1901</date> from James Ludovic Lindsay, 26th Earl of Crawford (1847-1913).</p> </provenance> <acquisition> <p>Bequeathed by <name role="dnr"><persName type="display">Enriqueta Rylands (1843-1908)</persName><persName type="standard">Rylands, Enriqueta, 1843-1908</persName></name> in <date when="1908">1908</date> to <name source="#viaf" key="312795145" role="own"><orgName>The John Rylands Library</orgName></name>.</p> </acquisition>

It is quite rare for Spencer material to feature heraldic bookplates. More often, the arms of the Earls of Spencer appear stamped on the front cover of the binding. Evidence of Spencer provenance, usually indicating former ownership, is more commonly found in the form of a leather label pasted to the inner front cover or front pastedown.

• Former Owner(s): Nicolet, Jean Baptiste Thomas; Fréret, Nicolas, 1688-1749; Spencer, George John Spencer, Earl, 1758-1834; Spencer, John Poyntz Spencer, Earl, 1835-1910; Rylands, Enriqueta, 1843-1908

• Donor(s): Rylands, Enriqueta, 1843-1908

• Provenance:

▪ Jean Baptiste Thomas Nicolet

Bookplate on the back pastedown: EX LIBRIS Joannis Baptistæ Thomæ NICOLET.

▪ Nicolas Fréret (1688-1749), historian and linguist

Manuscript inscription in the upper right corner of a1r: N. Freret | # | 1710.

▪ George John Spencer, 2nd Earl Spencer (1758-1834), politician and book collector

▪ John Poyntz Spencer, 5th Earl Spencer (1835-1910), politician and viceroy of Ireland

Formerly in the Spencer Library (Bibliotheca Spenceriana) at Althorp, Northamptonshire, largely assembled by George John Spencer and subsequently passing by descent to John Poyntz Spencer.

Armorial binding with the arms of the Earls of Spencer, associated with the 2nd Earl Spencer on both the front back covers: Quarterly, 2nd and 3rd a fret Or, over all on a bend Sable three escallops Argent (Spencer), impaling Quarterly, 1st and 4th, a bend cotised between six crosses patty (Bingham), 2nd and 3rd, Ermine a lion rampant ducally crowned (Turberville). Supporters: a griffin and a wyvern erect on its tail, both collared and chained. Coronet of an Earl. Motto: DIEU DEFEND LE DROIT.

Oval leather label on the front pastedown: E BIBLIOTHECA SPENCERIANA.

▪ Enriqueta Rylands (1843-1908), philanthropist and founder of The John Rylands Library

Purchased by Enriqueta Rylands in 1901 from John Poyntz Spencer, 5th Earl Spencer (1835-1910).

• Acquisition:

Donated by Enriqueta Rylands (1843-1908) in 1899 to The John Rylands Library.

On the front pastedown: the armorial bookplate of the John Rylands Library, with the Latin motto: NIHIL SINE LABORE.

On the a1r: a circular red stamp of The John Rylands Library, with the interlaced monogram: JR.

• Date of Acquisition: 1899

<provenance> <p><name role="fmo"><persName type="display">Jean Baptiste Thomas Nicolet</persName><persName type="standard">Nicolet, Jean Baptiste Thomas</persName></name></p> <p>Bookplate on the <locus from="Inner_back_cover" to="Inner_back_cover">back pastedown</locus>: <hi rend="italic">EX LIBRIS Joannis Baptistæ Thomæ NICOLET</hi>.</p> </provenance> <p><name source="#viaf" key="34456779" role="fmo"><persName type="display">Nicolas Fréret (1688-1749), historian and linguist</persName><persName type="standard">Fréret, Nicolas, 1688-1749 </persName></name></p> <p>Manuscript inscription in the upper right corner of <locus from="a1r" to="a1r">a1r</locus>: <hi rend="italic">N. Freret | # | 1710</hi>.</p> </provenance> <p><name source="#viaf" key="17584030" role="fmo"><persName type="display">George John Spencer, 2nd Earl Spencer (1758-1834), politician and book collector</persName><persName type="standard">Spencer, George John Spencer, Earl, 1758-1834</persName></name></p> <p><name source="#viaf" key="54160187" role="fmo"><persName type="display">John Poyntz Spencer, 5th Earl Spencer (1835-1910), politician and viceroy of Ireland</persName><persName type="standard">Spencer, John Poyntz Spencer, Earl, 1835-1910</persName></name></p> <p>Formerly in the Spencer Library (Bibliotheca Spenceriana) at Althorp, Northamptonshire, largely assembled by George John Spencer and subsequently passing by descent to John Poyntz Spencer.</p> <p>Armorial binding with the arms of the Earls of Spencer, associated with the 2nd Earl Spencer on both the <locus from="Front_cover" to="Front_cover">front</locus> and <locus from="Back_cover" to="Back_cover">back</locus> covers: <heraldry>Quarterly, 2nd and 3rd a fret Or, over all on a bend Sable three escallops Argent (Spencer), impaling Quarterly, 1st and 4th, a bend cotised between six crosses patty (Bingham), 2nd and 3rd, Ermine a lion rampant ducally crowned (Turberville). Supporters: a griffin and a wyvern erect on its tail, both collared and chained. Coronet of an Earl. Motto: <hi rend="italic">DIEU DEFEND LE DROIT</hi></heraldry>.</p> <p>Oval leather label on the <locus from="Inner_front_cover" to="Inner_front_cover">front pastedown</locus>: <hi rend="italic">E BIBLIOTHECA SPENCERIANA</hi>.</p> </provenance> <provenance> <p><name role="fmo"><persName type="display">Enriqueta Rylands (1843-1908), philanthropist and founder of The John Rylands Library</persName><persName type="standard">Rylands, Enriqueta, 1843-1908</persName></name></p> <p>Purchased by Enriqueta Rylands in <date when="1892">1892</date> from John Poyntz Spencer, 5th Earl Spencer (1835-1910).</p> </provenance> <acquisition> <p>Donated by <name role="dnr"><persName type="display">Enriqueta Rylands (1843-1908)</persName><persName type="standard">Rylands, Enriqueta, 1843-1908</persName></name> in <date when="1899">1899</date> to <name source="#viaf" key="312795145" role="own"><orgName>The John Rylands Library</orgName></name>.</p> <p>On the <locus from="Inner_front_cover" to="Inner_front_cover">front pastedown</locus>: the armorial bookplate of the John Rylands Library, with the Latin motto: <hi rend="italic">NIHIL SINE LABORE</hi>.</p> <p>On <locus from="a1r" to="a1r">a1r</locus>: a circular red stamp of The John Rylands Library, with the interlaced monogram: <hi rend="italic">JR</hi>.</p> </acquisition>

1.73 <additional> | additional

<additional> (additional) groups additional information, combining bibliographic information about a manuscript or other object, or surrogate copies of it, with curatorial or administrative information.

<additional> is used to record supplementary descriptive information that supports but falls outside the core intellectual, physical, and historical description of the item. This may include the source of the descriptive metadata, access and availability conditions, custodial history, references to surrogate copies or facsimiles, and relevant bibliographic citations.

<additional> appears immediately after <history> and forms the final component of <msDesc>, unless <msPart> is present, and may include the following elements where relevant:

<teiHeader> <fileDesc>|<titleStmt>|<publicationStmt>|<sourceDesc>|<msDesc>| <msContents>|<physDesc> |<history> <additional> <adminInfo> <!-- Metadata about relating to the curation and management of an item --> <!-- Contains <recordHist> | <availability> | <custodialHist> --> </adminInfo> <surrogates> <!-- Metadata linking to digital facsimiles --> <!-- N/A to MDC - only used in online catalogues: FIHRIST + Bodleian Medieval --> </surrogates> <listBibl> <!-- Metadata bibliographic references listing referring to the item --> <!-- Contains <bibl> and <ref> --> </listBibl> </additional> </msDesc>|</sourceDesc>|</fileDesc>|<encodingDesc>|<profileDesc>|<revisionDesc> </teiHeader>

1.74 <adminInfo> | administrative information

<adminInfo> (administrative information) contains information about the present custody and availability of the manuscript or other object, and also about the record description itself.

<adminInfo> is used to record information relating to the curation and management of the item described. It commonly includes source attribution and revision history, access and availability conditions, usage guidance, and details concerning the custody or public display of the physical object, as defined by the holding institution.

In MDC, <adminInfo> is not displayed in the public interface. As administrative metadata, it is suppressed in the public-facing view but remains essential and should be recorded consistently, following the standards and guidance defined below. The aim is to keep the interface focused on descriptive and interpretive information, while administrative content remains accessible only through the Download Metadata option, which provides full access to the complete TEI output.

This information is typically structured using the elements <recordHist> for the source, attribution, and revision of the description, <availability> for access conditions and usage notes, and <custodialHist> for institutional custody or exhibition history.

1.75 <recordHist> | recorded history | <source> | source

<recordHist> (recorded history) provides information about the source and revision status of the parent manuscript or object description itself.

<source> (source) describes the original source for the information contained with a manuscript or object description.

<source> within <recordHist> is used to record the origin of the descriptive metadata. It refers to earlier catalogues, handlists, or other sources on which the current description is based. This is particularly relevant where the description draws on or updates previous cataloguing. It provides transparency about how the description was constructed and supports attribution of earlier descriptive work.

In MDC, <source> is treated as internal source metadata and is not intended for public display. It serves as a form of metadata provenance and is used primarily for internal or scholarly reference.

Typically, <source> refers to a source on which the description is based, whether published or unpublished, and is cited using a brief author–date reference. If the description has been editorially revised, this should be noted. Include your name only if you have substantively revised or expanded the description. Full bibliographic details should not be included in this element. Instead, the cited source must also be referenced in <listBibl>, using <bibl> that contains complete publication details.

• Data Source(s):

Description based on Tyson (1930), revised and expanded by Ourania Karapasia.

<recordHist>
 <source>
  <p>Description based on Tyson (1930), revised and expanded by Ourania Karapasia.</p>
 </source>
</recordHist>

Il Canzoniere di Petrarca e Le Rime di Dante: Italian MS 1

1.76 <availability> | availability

<availability> (availability) supplies information about the availability of a text, for example any restrictions on its use or distribution, its copyright status, any licence applying to it, etc.

<availability> is used to provide information about the availability and access conditions of the physical item, including any restrictions, consultation arrangements, and relevant contact details.

In MDC, <availability> usually contains a standard statement for material held by The John Rylands Library. This statement is treated as boilerplate and should be used consistently across TEI files unless institutional guidance changes or the item belongs to an external institution with different access conditions. Here, status=restricted indicates that access restrictions apply, as is standard for Special Collections material.

<adminInfo>
 <availability status="restricted">
  <p>The item is available for consultation by any accredited reader.</p>
  <p>For information on eligibility and admissions procedures, see <ref target="https://www.library.manchester.ac.uk/rylands/special-collections/using-the-reading-rooms/john-rylands-library/">Using Special Collections at The John Rylands Library</ref>.</p>
  <p>For further information on the availability of the item, please contact the <ref target="uml.special-collections@manchester.ac.uk">Special Collections Reader Services</ref>.</p>
 </availability>
</adminInfo>

Il Canzoniere di Petrarca e Le Rime di Dante: Italian MS 1

1.77 <custodialHist> | custodial history

<custodialHist> (custodial history) contains a description of a manuscript or other object's custodial history, either as running prose or as a series of dated custodial events.

<custodialHist> is used to describe the custodial history of an item, recording any significant events noted during the period that it has been located within its holding institution. It may contain either a series of <p> or a sequence of <custEvent>, each describing a distinct incident or event.

1.78 <custEvent> | custodial event

<custEvent> (custodial event) describes a single event during the custodial history of a manuscript or other object.

<custEvent> within <custodialHist> is used to record a single, dated custodial action or incident involving the item, primarily during its time in the care of the current institution. It is typically used to record conservation treatment or exhibition history, whether the item was featured as part of an internal or external exhibition.

<custEvent> is repeatable and must contain at least one <p>. Each <custEvent> may include a descriptive statement, a responsible agency, and a date. The nature of the event can be recorded using type, with values such as conservation and exhibition. Other values may be used where appropriate, but usage should remain consistent across files. It should also have at least one dating attribute, such as when, notBefore, notAfter, or from, to, to indicate the date or date range of the event.

<custodialHist>
 <custEvent type="exhibition" from="2012-06-19" to="2012-11-04">
  <p>Exhibited in <title>
    <ref target="https://www.uffizi.it/en/events/gleam-gold-gothic-uffizi-2012">Bagliori dorati. Il Gotico Internazionale a Firenze 1375–1440</ref>
   </title> (The Gleam of Gold. The International Gothic Style in Florence 1375–1440), Uffizi Gallery, Florence, 19 June to 4 November 2012.</p>
 </custEvent>
</custodialHist>

Il Canzoniere di Petrarca e Le Rime di Dante: Italian MS 1

<custodialHist>
 <custEvent type="conservation" notBefore="2023-11-01" to="2024-10-14">
  <p>Conserved between 1 November 2023 and 14 October 2024 by Heather Garner and Zoë Lanceley at the conservation studio of The John Rylands Library.</p>
 </custEvent>
</custodialHist>

Esther scroll: Hebrew MS 54

<custodialHist>
 <custEvent type="digitisation" when="2026-03-26">
  <p>Digitised on 26 March 2026 by Jo Castle at the Rylands Heritage Imaging Lab of The John Rylands Library as part of the X Project.</p>
 </custEvent>
</custodialHist>

1.79 <surrogates> | surrogates

<surrogates> (surrogates) contains information about any representations of the manuscript or other object being described which may exist in the holding institution or elsewhere.

<surrogates> is not relevant in MDC, where each digital resource consists of a TEI XML file describing a physical item, linked directly to a set of digital facsimile images using <facsimile>.

The use of <surrogates> applies only in other TEI frameworks, such as FIHRIST and the Bodleian Medieval, where it is used to reference or link to digital facsimiles hosted independently of the catalogue. Such information is typically recorded using <bibl> with type=digital-facsimile, followed by subtype with values full, where a manuscript has been digitised in its entirety, or partial, where only selected leaves are digitised.

Digital Images

Manchester Digital Collections (full digital resource)

<surrogates>
 <bibl type="digital-facsimile" subtype="full">
  <ref target="https://www.digitalcollections.manchester.ac.uk/view/MS-PERSIAN-00051">Manchester Digital Collections</ref>.
 <note>(full digital resource).</note>
 </bibl>
</surrogates>

An album of paintings and calligraphic specimens: Persian MS 51

1.80 <listBibl> | citation list

<listBibl> (citation list) contains a list of bibliographic citations of any kind.

<listBibl> within <additional> provides a container for bibliographic references to external sources in which the item has been identified, listed, or described. It may include a reference to the publicly available institutional catalogue record, serving as an access point for discovery and indicating where an authoritative bibliographic record is available.

Depending on the collection and material type, this may take the form of an entry in Library Search (Primo), WorldCat (worldwide union catalogue), FIHRIST (union catalogue of manuscripts from the Islamicate world), Medieval Manuscripts in Oxford Libraries, or other relevant union catalogues, directories, or finding aids such as Archives Hub (UK gateway to archive descriptions).

In addition, <listBibl> records references to external catalogues, bibliographies, and scholarly resources, including printed short-title catalogues, collection-specific descriptive catalogues, exhibition catalogues, and major reference databases such as EDIT16 (Edizioni Italiane del XVI Secolo), ISTC (Incunabula Short Title Catalogue), Bod-Inc Online (Bodleian Libraries’ Incunable Catalogue), Trismegistos (interdisciplinary portal of papyrological and epigraphical resources), Pinakes (IRHT database of Greek Manuscripts online), CDLI (Cuneiform Digital Library Initiative).

Each reference within <listBibl> is encoded using a <bibl>, which represents a single bibliographic citation.

Bibliographical information relating to the item as a whole should be recorded within <additional>, typically using <listBibl>. Where bibliographical references apply only to an individual text or component described in the record, <bibl>, or <listBibl> if multiple citations are required, may instead be recorded within the relevant <msItem> or <msPart>.

1.81 <bibl> | bibliographic citation

<bibl> (bibliographic citation) contains a loosely-structured bibliographic citation of which the sub-components may or may not be explicitly tagged.

<bibl> within <listBibl> is used to record a single catalogue entry, bibliography, database record, exhibition catalogue, or scholarly publication. The citation may be encoded as plain text or with more granular tagging, depending on the level of structure required by the project framework.

<bibl> entries should, wherever possible, be listed in chronological order, from the earliest dated source to the most recent, reflecting the development of the item’s bibliographic record over time. In MDC, references may ideally be grouped by type, with the institutional catalogue record listed first, such as Library Search or FIHRIST, followed by major reference databases including ISTC, EDIT16, and then printed catalogues and scholarship arranged in chronological order from oldest to newest.

This ordering should be followed when creating new TEI files. However, as is often the case with MARC-derived metadata, the order of entries may initially reflect the structure or sequence of the source Alma record. In such cases, the original arrangement may be retained at the point of conversion, but entries should be reordered and standardised wherever feasible, in line with the MDC framework.

Where available, a <bibl> entry should include a link to the cited resource using <ref> with a target supplying the URL. This enables the bibliography to serve both as a citation and as a direct access point to an external catalogue record or electronic resource, such as WorldCat or another authoritative database.

The bibliographic references should follow the citation style of the Modern Language Association (MLA) 8th or 9th edition. For example, an Alma record may appear in Primo as: Adams, H. M. Catalogue of Books Printed on the Continent of Europe, 1501–1600, in Cambridge Libraries, D84. In MDC, this would be encoded as:

• Bibliography:

• Adams, Herbert Mayow. Catalogue of books printed on the Continent of Europe, 1501-1600, in Cambridge Libraries. University Press, 1967, D84

<listBibl>
 <bibl>
  <ref target="https://search.worldcat.org/en/title/892641">Adams, Herbert Mayow. Catalogue of books printed on the Continent of Europe, 1501-1600, in Cambridge Libraries. University Press, 1967, D84</ref>
 </bibl>
</listBibl>

The following format should be used in MDC to record a link to the University of Manchester Library Search record in Primo:

• Bibliography:

• Recorded in University of Manchester Library Search

<listBibl>
 <bibl>Recorded in University of Manchester <ref target="https://www.librarysearch.manchester.ac.uk/permalink/44MAN_INST/elks2a/alma9913563574401631">Library Search</ref>
 </bibl>
</listBibl>

The following format should be used in MDC when linking to an external catalogue record such as FIHRIST:

• Bibliography:

• Catalogue record in FIHRIST

<listBibl>
 <bibl>Catalogue record in <ref target="https://www.fihrist.org.uk/catalog/manuscript_6964">FIHRIST</ref>
 </bibl>
</listBibl>

The following examples demonstrate the standard MDC format for recording bibliographic references within <listBibl>, with database entries followed by printed scholarship arranged chronologically.

• Bibliography:

• Trismegistos: TM 6947

• Mertens-Pack 3 Online: MP³ 1106

• Hunt, Arthur Surridge. Catalogue of the Greek Papyri in the John Rylands Library, Manchester, Volume I, Literary Texts. Manchester University Press, 1911, page 91

• West, Martin Litchfield. Homerus, Odyssea. Bibliotheca Scriptorum Graecorum et Romanorum Teubneriana. De Gruyter, 2017

<listBibl>
 <bibl>Trismegistos: <ref target="http://www.trismegistos.org/text/60947">TM 60947</ref>
 </bibl>
 <bibl>Mertens-Pack 3 Online: <ref target="http://www.cedopalmp3.uliege.be/cdp_MP3_display.aspx?numNot=01106.000">MP³ 1106</ref>
 </bibl>
 <bibl>
  <ref target="https://archive.org/details/catalogueofgreek01johnuoft/page/90">Hunt, Arthur Surridge. Catalogue of the Greek Papyri in the John Rylands Library, Manchester, Volume I,
     Literary Texts. Manchester University Press, 1911, page 91</ref>
 </bibl>
 <bibl>
  <ref target="https://search.worldcat.org/title/1008765676">West, Martin Litchfield. Homerus, Odyssea. Bibliotheca Scriptorum Graecorum et Romanorum Teubneriana. De Gruyter, 2017</ref>
 </bibl>
</listBibl>

Odyssey: Greek P 53

• Bibliography:

• Recorded in University of Manchester Library Search

• Bodleian Libraries. Bod-Inc Online: D–008

• British Library. Incunabula Short Title Catalogue (ISTC): id00025000

• Staatsbibliothek zu Berlin. Gesamtkatalog der Wiegendrucke (GW): 7961

Bibliotheca Spenceriana, or, A descriptive catalogue of the books printed in the fifteenth century, lately forming part of the library of the Duke di Cassano Serra, and now the property of George John, earl Spencer. With a general index of authors and editions contained in the present volume, and in the Bibliotheca Spenceriana and Aedes Althorpianae. W. Nicol, Shakespeare Press, 1823, volume 7, no. 68, page 44
