Incominciano le cantiche de la Comedia di Dante Alleghieri firentino: 18321

Do not end <bibl> with a full stop. Spell out page and pages in full rather than using abbreviated forms such as p. or pp.

Optional workflow: importing bibliographic references from Zotero

Where bibliographical references are already maintained in Zotero, they may be exported in TEI format and incorporated into <listBibl>. This is an optional workflow and should only be used where the encoder is confident in reviewing and normalising the exported XML.

Bibliographic entries can be exported from Zotero in TEI format as follows:

— Open Zotero and navigate to the relevant shelfmark folder.

— Select all entries within the folder, then right-click and select Export Items.

— Select the export format TEI. Ensure that the following options are enabled (ticked): Export Notes, Generate XML IDs, and Full TEI Document.

— Save the XML file in an appropriate location and open it in Oxygen.

— Apply formatting and indentation. Click the Format and Indent button (or press CTRL+SHIFT+P).

After export, the XML will require editorial refinement:

— Remove page references encoded within <note> that do not relate to the specific item being described. These can be verified by checking the shelfmark references within Zotero.

— Use CTRL+F to replace all <note> that represent citation ranges with <citedRange>. Ensure both opening and closing tags are updated.

— Rearrange entries into chronological order. Bibliographic entries are typically encoded as <biblStruct> within <listBibl>.

— Once normalised, copy <listBibl> and its contents into the appropriate location within <additional> in the TEI header.

In practice, <biblStruct> is preferable where full bibliographic structure is required, with discrete elements for authors, titles, publication details, and identifiers. Within MDC TEI, the choice between <bibl> and <biblStruct> should depend on whether a simple citation is sufficient or whether a fully structured, machine-processable representation is needed.

For further detail on the encoding of bibliographic citations and references, including alternative modelling approaches and more granular structures, consult the TEI Guidelines for Electronic Text Encoding and Interchange, section 3.12 Bibliographic Citations and References.

1.82 <msPart> | manuscript part

<msPart> (manuscript part) contains information about an originally distinct manuscript or part of a manuscript, which is now part of a composite manuscript.

<msPart> is used for composite manuscripts, that is, manuscripts comprising two or more originally distinct parts now kept together as a single unit. Definitions and understandings of composite manuscripts vary across codicological traditions, but the element is generally used when a single item contains multiple distinct parts that require separate description.

Although the element name refers to manuscripts, <msPart> may also be used to describe multi-part, composite, and similar structures across a range of Special Collections materials, such as photograph albums, scrapbooks, and compilations containing works by different authors or dealing with different subjects.

Structurally, each <msPart> follows the same general model as <msDesc> and may include elements such as <msIdentifier>, <msContents>, <physDesc>, <history>, and <additional>.

<msDesc>
 <msIdentifier/>
 <head/>
 <msContents/>
 <physDesc/>
 <history/>
 <additional/>
 <msPart n="1">
<!-- first distinct part -->
  <msIdentifier/>
  <msContents/>
  <physDesc/>
  <history/>
  <additional/>
 </msPart>
 <msPart n="2">
<!-- second distinct part -->
  <msIdentifier/>
  <msContents/>
  <physDesc/>
  <history/>
  <additional/>
 </msPart>
</msDesc>

When <msPart> is used, <msDesc> must have <head> to provide the overarching title for the entire item. Without it, the MDC interface will not prioritise or even display a title at all. This does not replace the structural requirements of <msContents>, which must still contain at least one <msItem> with a <title>.

<msPart> should have its own <msIdentifier> with <idno> to record the identifier of that part, in addition to <msIdentifier> provided for the item as a whole within <msDesc>.

Each <msPart> should be numbered using n and may include xml:id. While optional in TEI, the use of xml:id. is recommended for composite items in order to provide a stable identifier for each structural part and to support citation, linking, indexing, and internal referencing.

<msPart> @xml:id

xml:id derives from the unique identifier of the physical item and matches the XML filename.

Where xml:id is used on <msPart>, the identifier should extend the XML filename using a suffix corresponding to the part identifier, for example, -A, -B, -C, or -1, -2, -3. This convention provides a persistent identifier for each component of a composite item.

<TEI xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="MS-LATIN-000XX"> <teiHeader> <fileDesc> <!-- additional metadata --> <sourceDesc> <msDesc> <msIdentifier> <!-- additional metadata --> <idno>Latin MS XX</idno> </msIdentifier> <head/> <msContents> <summary/> <msItem> <!-- top-level <msItem> | additional metadata --> </msItem> </msContents> <!-- additional metadata --> <additional/> <msPart n="1" xml:id="MS-LATIN-000XX-A"> <!-- additional metadata --> </msPart> <msPart n="2" xml:id="MS-LATIN-000XX-B"> <!-- additional metadata --> </msPart> <msPart n="3" xml:id="MS-LATIN-000XX-C"> <!-- additional metadata --> </msPart> </msDesc> </sourceDesc> </fileDesc> <!-- additional metadata --> </teiHeader>

<msPart> is used to describe distinct physical or structural parts of the item, whereas <msItem> is used to describe the intellectual or content units contained within those parts.

<msContents> within <msPart> must contain at least one <msItem>. This part-level <msItem> will normally include <locus> to indicate the folio or page range where the part occurs. One or more nested <msItem> should then be numbered using n and should include <locus> to indicate where each textual unit begins and ends within that range.

<textLang> within the top-level <msItem> within <msDesc> must have mainLang and, where applicable, otherLangs, providing the language statement for the item as a whole. Within <msPart>, the part-level <msItem> must also include <textLang> to record the language of that part. Each nested <msItem> should repeat <textLang> where necessary to maintain accurate language identification and display.

<msPart n="1" xml:id="MS-LATIN-000XX-A">
 <msIdentifier>
  <idno>Latin MS XX A</idno>
 </msIdentifier>
 <msContents>
  <msItem>
   <locus from="1r" to="30v">1r-30v</locus>
<!-- part-level <msItem> -->
   <title>title of this part</title>
   <textLang mainLang="lat" otherLangs="ara">Latin and Arabic</textLang>
<!-- additional metadata -->
   <msItem n="1">
<!-- first nested <msItem> -->
    <locus from="1r" to="20v">1r-20v</locus>
    <title>title of this unit</title>
    <textLang mainLang="lat">Latin</textLang>
   </msItem>
   <msItem n="2">
<!-- second nested <msItem> -->
    <locus from="21r" to="30v">21r-30v</locus>
    <title>title of this unit</title>
    <textLang mainLang="ara">Arabic</textLang>
<!-- additional metadata -->
   </msItem>
  </msItem>
 </msContents>
<!-- additional metadata -->
</msPart>

Each <msItem> may include xml:id, depending on project requirements. See also <msItem> @xml:id <!--insert hyperlink--> .

<msPart n="1" xml:id="MS-LATIN-000XX-A"> <!-- first distinct part --> <!-- additional metadata --> <msContents> <msItem> <locus from="1r" to="30v">1r-30v</locus> <!-- part-level <msItem> --> <!-- additional metadata --> <msItem n="1" xml:id="MS-LATIN-000XX-A-item1"> <!-- first nested <msItem> --> <locus from="1r" to="20v">1r-20v</locus> <!-- additional metadata --> </msItem> <msItem n="2" xml:id="MS-LATIN-000XX-A-item2"> <!-- second nested <msItem> --> <locus from="21r" to="30v">21r-30v</locus> <!-- additional metadata --> </msItem> </msItem </msContents> <!-- additional metadata --> </msPart>

The remainder of <msPart> follows the same encoding principles as the general <msDesc> model and may include the same core components, such as <physDesc>, <history>, and <additional>, together with their subordinate elements and attributes wherever applicable.

Information that applies to the entire item should be recorded at the <msDesc> level rather than repeated within individual <msPart>. For example, features common to all parts, such as <binding> of a codex, are encoded in <physDesc> within <msDesc>. Similarly, <extent> at the <msDesc> level may record the total number of leaves for the complete item, while each <msPart> may include its own <extent> to specify the number of leaves within that part.

Where the original item survives only as separate fragments rather than as a single physical object, <msFrag> may be used.

1.83 <msFrag> | manuscript fragment

<msFrag> (manuscript fragment) contains information about a fragment described in relation to a prior context, typically as a description of a virtual reconstruction of a manuscript or other object whose fragments were catalogued separately

<msFrag> within <msDesc> may be used where an item no longer survives as a single physical unit, but only as fragments that once formed part of the same item. For example, this may apply where several detached fragments are understood to have come from the same manuscript of Homer’s Odyssey.

Structurally, <msFrag> follows the same general model as <msPart>, and may include elements such as <msIdentifier>, <msContents>, <physDesc>, <history>, and <additional>.

The main difference between <msFrag> and <msPart> is one of meaning: <msPart> describes parts that are now physically combined, while <msFrag> describes fragments that are now physically separate.

1.84 <encodingDesc> | encoding description

<encodingDesc> (encoding description) documents the relationship between an electronic text and the source or sources from which it was derived.

<encodingDesc> is the second major subdivision of <teiHeader>. In MDC, it serves two primary functions. Where relevant, it may provide contextual information about the project or purpose for which the digital resource has been created. More importantly, it records the source of persistent identifiers drawn from recognised controlled vocabularies and authority files whenever these are used within <teiHeader>.

<encodingDesc> may contain the following elements, as applicable:

<teiHeader> <fileDesc>|<titleStmt>|<publicationStmt>|<sourceDesc>|<msDesc>| <msContents>|<physDesc>|<history>|<additional>|</msDesc>|</sourceDesc>|</fileDesc> <encodingDesc> <projectDesc> <!-- Metadata describing the background and scope of the project --> <!-- Contains <p> --> </projectDesc> <classDecl> <!-- Metadata declaring controlled vocabularies and authority sources --> <!-- Contains <taxonomy>, <bibl> and <ref> --> </classDecl> </encodingDesc> <profileDesc>|<revisionDesc> </teiHeader>

1.85 <projectDesc> | project description

<projectDesc> (project description) describes in detail the aim or purpose for which an electronic file was encoded, together with any other relevant information concerning the process by which it was assembled or collected.

MDC display label — Project Information

<projectDesc> may be used to provide contextual information about the project framework of the digital resource, outlining its aims, scope, funding context, and institutional setting.

The content of <projectDesc> is generally informed by the project lead and is typically expressed in prose using one or more <p>.

• Project Information:

Created as part of the AHRC-funded research project: “Envisioning Dante, c. 1472-c. 1630: Seeing and Reading the Early Printed Page” (AH/W005220/1), on the Dante Alighieri early printed editions held at The John Rylands Research Institute and Library, led by Professor Guyda Armstrong (Principal Investigator, School of Arts, Languages and Cultures, The University of Manchester); Professor Simon Gilson (Co-Investigator, Faculty of Medieval and Modern Languages, University of Oxford); Dr Giles Bergel, Research Fellow (Visual Geometry Group, Department of Engineering Science, University of Oxford); Dr Rebecca Bowen, Postdoctoral Research Associate (Faculty of Medieval and Modern Languages, University of Oxford), and Dr Gloria Moorman (School of Arts, Languages and Cultures, The University of Manchester).

<projectDesc>
 <p>Created as part of the AHRC-funded research project: "Envisioning Dante, c. 1472-c. 1630: Seeing and Reading the Early Printed Page" (AH/W005220/1), on the Dante Alighieri early printed editions held at The John Rylands Research Institute and Library, led by Professor Guyda Armstrong (Principal Investigator, School of Arts, Languages and Cultures, The University of Manchester); Professor Simon Gilson (Co-Investigator, Faculty of Medieval and Modern Languages, University of Oxford); Dr Giles Bergel, Research Fellow (Visual Geometry Group, Department of Engineering Science, University of Oxford); Dr Rebecca Bowen, Postdoctoral Research Associate (Faculty of Medieval and Modern Languages, University of Oxford), and Dr Gloria Moorman (School of Arts, Languages and Cultures, The University of Manchester).</p>
</projectDesc>

Le terze rime di Dante: 18873

1.86 <classDecl> | classification declarations

<classDecl> (classification declarations) contains one or more taxonomies defining any classificatory codes used elsewhere in the text.

<classDecl> within <encodingDesc> serves as a container in which classification systems, controlled vocabularies, taxonomies, and authority files are declared. It does not contain the individual authority identifiers. Instead, it declares the authority sources from which those identifiers are taken.

When source and key are used together on relevant elements, source specifies the controlled vocabulary, authority source, or taxonomy being used, while key provides the identifier taken from that source. The value of source must correspond to a <taxonomy> declared within <classDecl>.

<classDecl> contains one or more <taxonomy>.

1.87 <taxonomy> | taxonomy

<taxonomy> (taxonomy) defines a typology either implicitly, by means of a bibliographic citation, or explicitly by a structured taxonomy.

<taxonomy> within <classDecl> is repeatable, allowing the declaration of multiple authority sources within a single TEI file. In MDC, taxonomies most commonly take the implicit form: they refer to an external vocabulary or authority source, rather than listing all terms within <taxonomy>.

Each <taxonomy> includes xml:id, which acts as the internal identifier for that vocabulary or authority source, and <bibl>, which typically includes <ref target="…"> that provides a resolvable URI for the authority source.

Instead of embedding a full URI in ref for each occurrence, the vocabulary or authority source is declared once in <classDecl> using <taxonomy>. When a vocabulary declared in <taxonomy> is referenced elsewhere in <teiHeader> provide the relevant identifier in key and refer to the declared source in source.

The value of source or scheme must match the xml:id of the corresponding <taxonomy>, preceded by #. For example, scheme=#lcsh or source=#viaf refers to the <taxonomy> declared with xml:id=lcsh or xml:id=viaf within <classDecl>.

The following <classDecl> forms part of the standard MDC TEI-XML template. These six vocabularies constitute the standard authority declarations.

<classDecl>
 <taxonomy xml:id="lcsh">
  <bibl>
   <ref target="http://id.loc.gov/authorities/subjects.html">Library of Congress Subject Headings (LCSH)</ref>
  </bibl>
 </taxonomy>
 <taxonomy xml:id="iso639-2">
  <bibl>
   <ref target="https://www.loc.gov/standards/iso639-2/php/code_list.php">ISO 639-2 Codes for the Representation of Names of Languages (LC)</ref>
  </bibl>
 </taxonomy>
 <taxonomy xml:id="marcrelator">
  <bibl>
   <ref target="https://www.loc.gov/marc/relators/relaterm.html">MARC Code List for Relators (LC)</ref>
  </bibl>
 </taxonomy>
 <taxonomy xml:id="viaf">
  <bibl>
   <ref target="https://viaf.org">Virtual International Authority File (VIAF)</ref>
  </bibl>
 </taxonomy>
 <taxonomy xml:id="tgn">
  <bibl>
   <ref target="https://www.getty.edu/research/tools/vocabularies/tgn/index.html">Getty Thesaurus of Geographic Names® (TGN)</ref>
  </bibl>
 </taxonomy>
 <taxonomy xml:id="aat">
  <bibl>
   <ref target="https://www.getty.edu/research/tools/vocabularies/aat">Getty Art &amp; Architecture Thesaurus® (AAT)</ref>
  </bibl>
 </taxonomy>
</classDecl>

These taxonomies should not be deleted or amended unless a vocabulary is demonstrably not used in <teiHeader> or has been formally replaced by an explicitly required alternative. Where key values are identified by source as belonging to one of these vocabularies, the corresponding <taxonomy> declaration must remain in place.

Where manuscripts catalogued in FIHRIST are included in MDC and identifiers from FIHRIST authority databases are recorded using source=#fihrist, a corresponding <taxonomy xml:id="fihrist"> must be declared within <classDecl>.

<classDecl>
 <taxonomy xml:id="lcsh"/>
 <taxonomy xml:id="iso639-2"/>
 <taxonomy xml:id="marcrelator"/>
 <taxonomy xml:id="viaf"/>
 <taxonomy xml:id="tgn"/>
 <taxonomy xml:id="aat"/>
 <taxonomy xml:id="fihrist">
  <bibl>
   <ref target="https://www.fihrist.org.uk">FIHRIST: Union Catalogue of Manuscripts from the Islamicate World</ref>
  </bibl>
 </taxonomy>
</classDecl>

1.88 <profileDesc> | text-profile description

<profileDesc> (text-profile description) provides a detailed description of non-bibliographic aspects of a text, specifically the languages and sublanguages used, the situation in which it was produced, the participants and their setting.

<profileDesc> is the third major subdivision of <teiHeader> and is used to record subject headings and, in the case of letters, structured correspondence metadata.

<profileDesc> may contain the following elements:

<teiHeader> <fileDesc>|<titleStmt/>|<publicationStmt/>|<sourceDesc>|<msDesc>| <msContents/>|<physDesc/>|<history/>|<additional/>|</msDesc>|</sourceDesc>|</fileDesc>|</encodingDesc> <profileDesc> <textClass> <!-- Metadata for subject classification --> <!-- Contains <keywords> --> </textClass> <correspDesc> <!-- Metadata for an act of correspondence --> <!-- Contains <correspAction> --> </correspDesc> </profileDesc> <revisionDesc/> </teiHeader>

1.89 <textClass> | text classification

<textClass> (text classification) groups information which describes the nature or topic of a text in terms of a standard classification scheme, thesaurus, etc.

<textClass> within <profileDesc> is used to record subject classification metadata for the item and contains <keywords> listing the subject headings.

1.90 <keywords> | keywords

<keywords> (keywords) contains a list of keywords or phrases identifying the topic or nature of a text.

MDC display label — Subject(s)

<keywords> is used to record the subject headings associated with the item. These headings describe the subject, topic, or thematic content of the item and are used to classify it for discovery and retrieval.

At least three subject headings should be included where possible. These may be broad or more specific depending on the nature of the material.

The subject headings are encoded using <list> containing three or more <item>. Each <item> must contain <term> with key giving the authority identifier for that heading.

<keywords> includes scheme that identifies the controlled vocabulary governing the list of subject terms.

@scheme

scheme identifies the controlled vocabulary within which the set of keywords concerned is defined.

scheme with value #lcsh indicates that the subject headings are drawn from the Library of Congress Subject Headings (LCSH).

lcsh

Library of Congress Subject Headings (LCSH)

The Library of Congress Subject Headings (LCSH) is perhaps the most widely adopted subject indexing language in the world, has been translated into many languages, and is used around the world by libraries large and small. LCSH has been actively maintained since 1898 to catalogue materials held at the Library of Congress.

<taxonomy xml:id="lcsh">
 <bibl>
  <ref target="http://id.loc.gov/authorities/subjects.html">Library of Congress Subject Headings (LCSH)</ref>
 </bibl>
</taxonomy>

When the value #lcsh is used for scheme, it indicates that the subject headings recorded in <keywords> are drawn from this authority. The prefix # indicates a reference to an identifier defined elsewhere in the same file. In this case, it refers to the <taxonomy> declared in the header with xml:id=lcsh. This value can be selected from a controlled dropdown list when entering the attribute and does not need to be typed manually.

Because the vocabulary is declared at the <keywords> level, it applies to all subject terms listed within that element and does not need to be repeated for each individual <term>.

• Subjects: Manuscripts, Medieval--Italy--14th century; Illumination of books and manuscripts; Literature, Medieval; Italian literature-- To 1400; Italian poetry--To 1400

<textClass>
 <keywords scheme="#lcsh">
  <list>
   <item>
    <term key="sh85080744">Manuscripts, Medieval--Italy--14th century</term>
   </item>
   <item>
    <term key="sh85064332">Illumination of books and manuscripts</term>
   </item>
   <item>
    <term key="sh85077548">Literature, Medieval</term>
   </item>
   <item>
    <term key="sh85068817">Italian literature--To 1400</term>
   </item>
   <item>
    <term key="sh85068842">Italian poetry--To 1400</term>
   </item>
  </list>
 </keywords>
</textClass>

Il Canzoniere di Petrarca e Le Rime di Dante: Italian MS 1

<keywords> is repeatable within <textClass>, allowing multiple sets of subject terms to be recorded where different vocabularies or classification schemes are used. Each <keywords> normally corresponds to a single controlled vocabulary, identified through scheme. Additional <keywords> should be used only where subject terms from other controlled vocabularies or classification systems need to be recorded.

Where subject terms are drawn from a controlled vocabulary other than those already declared in <classDecl>, the corresponding vocabulary must be declared as a <taxonomy> in the header before it is referenced in <keywords>.

Where terms do not correspond to an external authority or controlled vocabulary, scheme=local may be used.

In cases where it is necessary to distinguish between subject and genre terms, type on <term> may be used with values such as subject or genre respectively.

Where <correspDesc> is included, <keywords> should be included after <correspDesc>, not before.

1.91 <correspDesc> | correspondence description

<correspDesc> (correspondence description) contains a description of the actions related to one act of correspondence.

<correspDesc> within <profileDesc> provides structured correspondence-specific metadata concerning the communicative aspects of an act of correspondence, such as sending and receiving, and identifies the participants involved. This information complements the detailed description of the physical item, such as a letter, postcard, or other form of correspondence, typically provided in <sourceDesc>.

The individual stages of the correspondence are encoded using <correspAction>.

1.92 <correspAction> | correspondence action

<correspAction> (correspondence action) contains a structured description of the place, the name of a person/organization and the date related to the sending/receiving of a message or any other action related to the correspondence.

<correspAction> within <correspDesc> is used to record each action associated with a correspondence event, most commonly the sending or receiving of a communication. It may record the person, organisation, place, or date associated with that action.

type on <correspAction> should be used to distinguish correspondence actions.

@type

type indicates the nature of the correspondence action.

The following values should be used:

sent indicates the action of sending a communication.

received indicates the action of receiving a communication.

The table below shows how MDC display labels match the relevant TEI elements used to encode correspondence.

MDC display label

TEI <>|@

Author(s)

<correspAction type="sent"> + <name>

Date of Creation

<origin> — <origDate>

Origin Place

<origin> — <origPlace>

Date of Sending

<correspAction type="sent"> + <date>

Recipient(s)

<correspAction type="received"> + <name>

Destination

<correspAction type="received"> + <placeName>

Date of Receipt

<correspAction type="received"> + <date>

Correspondence titles recorded in <title> within <msItem> should follow a clear and consistent title pattern, using one of the following forms:

Letter from [sender] to [recipient] Letter to [recipient] from [sender]

Once adopted, the same pattern should be used consistently throughout a project.

For correspondence projects centred on a principal correspondent, the title pattern may prioritise that person consistently, whether as sender or recipient. For example, titles in The Heinrich Simon Papers prioritise Heinrich Simon, using forms such as Letter from Heinrich Simon to … or Letter to Heinrich Simon from ….

Names in <title> should appear in full. Avoid abbreviations, initials, or shortened forms unless these are the only forms attested in the source.

The sender (author) and recipient should be recorded within the appropriate <correspAction> using <name>. Within <name>, include <persName> for a personal name or <orgName> for an organisational name. If the name is listed in VIAF, include both source and key on <name> to reference the appropriate VIAF authority record.

<date> is used within <correspAction> to record dates associated with actions, such as when a communication was sent or received. Where the exact date is known, it should be recorded using <date> with when. In many cases, however, the exact date of sending cannot be established even though the letter preserves the date on which it was written. In such cases, the date the letter was written should be recorded within <origin> using <origDate>, rather than in <correspAction type="sent">, which is used to record the date of sending when this is known.

<placeName> is used within <correspAction> to record the place associated with actions, such as the place from which a communication was sent or at which it was received. Within <placeName>, record the normalised form of the place name. If the place is listed in TGN, include both source and key on <placeName> to reference the appropriate TGN authority record. In many cases, the place from which the letter was sent cannot be established even though the letter preserves the place where it was written. In such cases, the place the letter was written should be recorded within <origin> using <origPlace>, rather than in <correspAction type="sent">, which is used to record the place of sending when this is known.

The following example shows a letter for which the date and place of writing are known, but the exact date of sending is not recorded. In this case, the title of the item is given in <msItem>, while the writing date and place are recorded within <origin> using <origDate> and <origPlace>. The participants in the correspondence are recorded within <correspDesc> using <correspAction>. The place of receipt is recorded using <placeName>. This pattern reflects an important distinction in correspondence encoding: the date on which a letter was written is not necessarily the same as the date on which it was sent. Likewise, the date of receipt may not always be known.

Letter from Heinrich Simon to his father Herrmann Simon (SIM/1/198)

Heinrich Simon asks his father for permission to attend physics lessons with a travelling private teacher.

—

• Title: Letter from Heinrich Simon to his father Herrmann Simon

• Language(s): German

—

• Author(s): Simon, Heinrich, 1805-1860

• Origin Place: Poland, Brieg (Brzeg)

• Date of Creation: 29 April 1823

• Recipient(s): Simon, Friedrich Herrmann (Heymann), 1781-1851

• Destination: Poland, Breslau (Wrocław)

—

<teiHeader>
<!-- additional metadata -->
 <msContents>
  <summary>
   <p>Heinrich Simon asks his father for permission to attend physics lessons with a travelling private teacher.</p>
  </summary>
  <msItem>
   <title>Letter from Heinrich Simon to his father Herrmann Simon</title>
   <textLang mainLang="ger">German</textLang>
  </msItem>
 </msContents>
<!-- additional metadata -->
 <history>
  <origin>
   <origDate when="1823-04-29">29 April 1823</origDate>
   <origPlace>
    <country source="#tgn" key="7006366">Poland</country>, <settlement source="#tgn" key="7007623">
     <placeName type="historic" xml:lang="ger">Brieg</placeName> (<placeName type="modern" xml:lang="pol">Brzeg</placeName>)</settlement>
   </origPlace>
  </origin>
 </history>
<!-- additional metadata -->
 <correspDesc>
  <correspAction type="sent">
   <name source="#viaf" key="50001971">
    <persName>Simon, Heinrich, 1805-1860 </persName>
   </name>
  </correspAction>
  <correspAction type="received">
   <name>
    <persName>Simon, Friedrich Herrmann (Heymann), 1781-1851</persName>
   </name>
   <placeName source="#tgn" key="7007601">Poland, Breslau (Wrocław)</placeName>
  </correspAction>
 </correspDesc>
<!-- additional metadata -->
</teiHeader>

Letter from Heinrich Simon to his father Herrmann Simon: SIM/1/198

If the full correspondence information were available, the same letter could be encoded more fully by recording the dates and places associated with the sending and receiving actions directly within the relevant <correspAction>. In such a case, <origin> would still record the date and place at which the letter was written, while <correspDesc> would record the subsequent communicative actions. This is especially important because a letter may be written on one date and sent on another.

Heinrich Simon asks his father for permission to attend physics lessons with a travelling private teacher.

—

• Title: Letter from Heinrich Simon to his father Herrmann Simon

• Language(s): German

—

• Author(s): Simon, Heinrich, 1805-1860

• Origin Place: Poland, Brieg (Brzeg)

• Date of Creation: 29 April 1823

• Date of Sending: 1 May 1823

• Recipient(s): Simon, Friedrich Herrmann (Heymann), 1781-1851

• Destination: Poland, Breslau (Wrocław)

• Date of Receipt: 3 May 1823

—

<teiHeader>
<!-- additional metadata -->
 <msContents>
  <summary>
   <p>Heinrich Simon asks his father for permission to attend physics lessons with a travelling private teacher.</p>
  </summary>
  <msItem>
   <title>Letter from Heinrich Simon to his father Herrmann Simon</title>
   <textLang mainLang="ger">German</textLang>
  </msItem>
 </msContents>
<!-- additional metadata -->
 <history>
  <origin>
   <origDate when="1823-04-29">29 April 1823</origDate>
   <origPlace>
    <country source="#tgn" key="7006366">Poland</country>, <settlement source="#tgn" key="7007623">
     <placeName type="historic" xml:lang="ger">Brieg</placeName> (<placeName type="modern" xml:lang="pol">Brzeg</placeName>)</settlement>
   </origPlace>
<!-- additional metadata -->
  </origin>
 </history>
 <correspDesc>
  <correspAction type="sent">
   <name source="#viaf" key="50001971">
    <persName>Simon, Heinrich, 1805-1860 </persName>
   </name>
   <date when="1823-05-01">1 May 1823</date>
   <placeName source="#tgn" key="7007623">Poland, Brieg (Brzeg)</placeName>
  </correspAction>
  <correspAction type="received">
   <name>
    <persName>Simon, Friedrich Herrmann (Heymann), 1781-1851</persName>
   </name>
   <date when="1823-05-03">3 May 1823</date>
   <placeName source="#tgn" key="7007601">Poland, Breslau (Wrocław)</placeName>
  </correspAction>
 </correspDesc>
<!-- additional metadata -->
</teiHeader>

Any authority file referenced using source must be declared in a corresponding <taxonomy> within <teiHeader>. The prefix # indicates that the value refers to the identifier defined there.

<origDate> and <origPlace> indicate when and where the letter was written, whereas <date> and <placeName> in <correspAction type="sent"> indicate when and from where it was sent. These may coincide, but they should not be assumed to do so.

If the date or place of sending cannot be established, the corresponding <date> or <placeName> may be omitted.

Full dates should be given in the order day, month, year. Use cardinal, not ordinal, forms for days, for example, 30 August 1821 rather than 30th August, 1821. Do not use commas, leading zeroes for days 1 to 9, or abbreviated month names.

<origDate> and its attributes follow the same encoding principles outlined in Section X <!--insert hyperlink--> of these guidelines.

<date> usually refers to a modern or historically standardised calendar date, such as the day a letter was sent or received. In these cases, TEI assumes the Gregorian calendar by default, so calendar=gregorian is normally unnecessary.

1.93 <revisionDesc> | revision description

<revisionDesc> (revision description) summarizes the revision history for a file.

MDC display label — Author(s) of the Record

<revisionDesc> is the final component of <teiHeader> and is used to record major changes to the file, including who made each change and when. It must contain at least one <change>.

<change> (change) documents a change or set of changes made during the production of a source document, or during the revision of an electronic file.

<change> is repeatable, allowing multiple revision entries to be recorded within a single <revisionDesc>. These entries should be recorded in reverse chronological order, with the most recent change appearing first.

Each <change>, should include when to record the date. Within <change>, the name of the contributor or revisor should be recorded first, typically using <persName>, followed, where necessary, by a brief description of the change itself.

Only significant revisions should be recorded, such as the re-dating or re-location of an item, the addition of provenance information, or the identification of previously unrecognised contents.

At a minimum, the date on which the TEI file was created and the name of the contributor responsible for its creation should be recorded.

• Author(s) of the Record: Vanellope von Schweetz

<revisionDesc>
 <change when="2026-03-15">
  <persName>Vanellope von Schweetz</persName>
 </change>
</revisionDesc>

Optionally, if a contributor has been defined in <respStmt> with xml:id, <change> may instead include who in addition to when to reference the contributor responsible for the recorded revision. This approach requires a matching identifier defined elsewhere, for example:

<respStmt xml:id="VvS">
<!-- additional metadata -->
 <persName>Vanellope von Schweetz</persName>
</respStmt>
<!-- additional metadata -->
<revisionDesc>
 <change when="2026-03-15" who="#VvS"/>
</revisionDesc>

Unless the system is configured to resolve who references and retrieve the contributor’s name from the referenced element, <persName> must be included within <change> if the name is intended to appear in the interface.

Decisions about whether the information recorded in <revisionDesc> should be visible on the public interface should ultimately be determined at the project level. In practice, however, such information is most often retained for internal documentation purposes and therefore kept hidden from public view.

1.93.1 Customised <note> types and display fields

<note> in TEI provides a flexible mechanism for recording supplementary descriptive information. While the element itself forms part of the TEI standard, the values used for type are not fixed and may be defined locally to support project-specific descriptive requirements.

In some cases, the TEI schema does not provide dedicated elements for recording certain kinds of information needed to describe particular collection formats, especially early printed books, maps, and visual collections. In such cases, locally defined type on <note> are used to encode this information.

Each type value is mapped by the system to a predefined display label in the MDC interface, ensuring consistent presentation across TEI files.

The following sections describe each customised <note> type, outlining its purpose, the collection formats to which it applies, and guidance on how the information should be encoded.

1.94 <note type="publisher"> | publication

The publication statement appearing in a printed book, usually on the title page or colophon.

MDC display label — Publication

<note type="publisher"> within <origin> is used to record verbatim publication or imprint statements for printed books. This may include the place of publication, the name of the publisher or printer, and the date of publication. In MARC-derived metadata and retroconversion workflows, this information usually derives from the publication or imprint information recorded in MARC fields 260 or 264.

In catalogue metadata exported from Alma and displayed through Primo, publication information may appear in MARC field 264 and may also be reflected in the transcription of the colophon.

Publisher Venetia : Appresso Giouambattista, Marchiò Sessa, & Fratelli

264

_1 |a Venetia : |b Appresso Giouambattista, Marchiò Sessa, & Fratelli, |c 1578.

Description

—

Colophon reads: In Venetia, Appresso gli Heredi di Francesco Rampazetto. Ad instantia di Giouambattista, Marchio Sessa, & Fratelli. MDLXXVIII.

To ensure consistency across TEI files within the same project collection, publication statements are standardised following the format adopted by ICCU’s Edizioni Italiane del XVI Secolo (EDIT16).

For example, the EDIT16 record CNCE 1177 provides the following standardised publication statement:

Imprint

In Venetia : appresso Giouambattista Marchiò Sessa et fratelli, 1578 (In Venetia : appresso gli heredi di Francesco Rampazetto : ad instantia di Giouambattista Marchio Sessa et fratelli, 1578)

This form is adopted in MDC and encoded as:

• Publication: In Venetia: appresso Giouambattista, Marchiò Sessa, & Fratelli, 1578.

<origin>
<!-- additional metadata -->
 <note type="publisher">In Venetia: appresso Giouambattista, Marchiò Sessa, &amp; Fratelli, 1578. </note>
</origin>

The space before the colon used in MARC or ISBD punctuation should not be retained in MDC. Venetia : is encoded as Venetia:.

1.95 <note type="printersDevice"> | printer’s device

The printer’s device appearing in a printed book, usually on the title page or colophon.

MDC display label — Printer’s Device

<note type="printersDevice"> within <origin> is used to describe the printer’s device appearing in early printed books. A printer’s device (or printer's mark) is a distinctive design or visual trademark placed by the printer or publisher on the title page or elsewhere in the text, to distinguish their productions.

Descriptions typically summarise the device as it appears in the book, for example by noting the main iconographic elements or motto associated with the device.

• Printer's Device: Aldine Press device on [pi1r] and [H7v]: a dolphin entwined around an anchor, Aldo.

Variant of this device on a1r: Aldvs.

<origin>
<!-- additional metadata -->
 <note type="printersDevice">Aldine Press device on <locus from="[pi1r]" to="[pi1r]">[pi1r]</locus> and <locus from="[H7v]" to="[H7v]">[H7v]</locus>: a dolphin entwined around an anchor, Aldo.
 <p>Variant of this device on <locus from="A1r" to="A1r">A1r</locus> a1r: Aldvs.</p>
 </note>
</origin>

1.96 <note type="scale"> | scale

The scale of a map or other cartographic material.

MDC display label — Scale

<note type="scale"> within <msItem> is used to record the scale of a map.

Map scale refers to the relationship, or ratio, between distance on a map and the corresponding distance on the ground. The scale may be transcribed verbatim from the item or inferred where it is not explicitly stated but can be determined. It may be expressed as a representative fraction (RF) or other numerical scale, or as a textual scale statement.

• Scale: 1:100,000

<msItem>
<!-- additional metadata -->
 <note type="scale">1:100,000</note>
<!-- additional metadata -->
</msItem>

1.97 <note type="technique"> | technique

The method or process used to produce an item in art, visual, or material culture.

MDC display label — Technique

<note type="technique"> within <msItem> is used primarily for visual collections and similar objects to record information about the artistic, photographic, or production technique used to create the item. This note may describe processes such as engraving, etching, lithography, woodcut, photographic processes, drawing techniques, or other methods of manufacture or artistic execution.

It is strongly recommended that values for <note type="technique"> are drawn from a controlled vocabulary. The preferred source is the Getty Art & Architecture Thesaurus (AAT).

• Technique: Gelatin dry plate process

<msItem>
<!-- additional metadata -->
 <note type="technique">Gelatin dry plate process</note>
<!-- additional metadata -->
</msItem>

• Technique: Albumen process

<msItem>
<!-- additional metadata -->
 <note type="technique">Albumen process</note>
<!-- additional metadata -->
</msItem>

• Technique: Painting

<msItem>
<!-- additional metadata -->
 <note type="technique">Painting</note>
<!-- additional metadata -->
</msItem>

1.98 <note type="medium"> | medium

The material of the tool used to produce an item in art, visual, or material culture.

MDC display label — Medium

<note type="medium"> within <msItem> is used primarily for visual collections and similar objects, to record the medium used to create the item. This note captures the specific substance or material applied by the maker in the creation process. It may include drawing media such as graphite, charcoal, and coloured pencil. It may include painting media such as oil paint, acrylic, tempera, and watercolour. It may include photographic media such as emulsion for glass plate negatives.

It is strongly recommended that values for <note type="medium"> are drawn from a controlled vocabulary. The preferred source is the Getty Art & Architecture Thesaurus (AAT).

• Medium: Emulsion

• Technique: Gelatin dry plate process

<msItem>
<!-- additional metadata -->
 <note type="technique">Gelatin dry plate process</note>
 <note type="medium">Emulsion</note>
<!-- additional metadata -->
</msItem>

• Medium: Oil paint

• Technique: Painting

<msItem>
<!-- additional metadata -->
 <note type="technique">Painting</note>
 <note type="medium">Oil paint</note>
<!-- additional metadata -->
</msItem>

<note type="technique"> and <note type="medium"> are used only when it is desirable for this information to appear as labelled fields in MDC.

Otherwise, alternative encoding may be used. For example, medium information may be recorded using <term type="medium"> within <decoNote>. Similarly, technique information may be recorded using <term type="technique">.

1.99 <note type="shape"> | shape

The physical shape or outline of an object in material culture.

MDC display label — Shape

<note type="shape"> within <msItem> is used to record the shape of the item. This note identifies the overall form, outline, or geometric configuration of the object. The shape should be recorded using consistent terminology. Acceptable terms include circular, diamond, irregular, oval, pentagonal, polygonal, rectangular, square, and triangular. Do not use round or circle for circular, lozenge for diamond, oblong for oval, or rectangle for rectangular.

• Shape: Circular

<msItem>
<!-- additional metadata -->
 <note type="shape">Circular</note>
<!-- additional metadata -->
</msItem>

1.100 <note type="mode"> | mode

The mode of use of an object in material culture.

MDC display label — Mode

<note type="mode"> within <msItem> is used to record the mode in which an item is worn, carried, used, or positioned. This note may describe how an object is worn on the body, carried by a person, associated with a particular individual, or placed within a space or setting.

• Mode: Worn or carried on the person.

<msItem>
<!-- additional metadata -->
 <note type="mode">Worn or carried on the person.</note>
<!-- additional metadata -->
</msItem>

1.101 <note type="function"> | function

The intended use or purpose of an object in material culture.

MDC display label — Function

<note type="function"> within <msItem> is used to record the functional purpose or intended use of an object, particularly when this provides additional contextual information about how the object operated within cultural, social, or ritual practice. This note may describe purposes such as protection, healing, commemoration, exchange, personal adornment, or symbolic or ritual intent.

• Function: Protection and prosperity.

<msItem>
<!-- additional metadata -->
 <note type="function">Protection and prosperity.</note>
<!-- additional metadata -->
</msItem>

<note type="shape">, <note type="mode">, and <note type="function"> were introduced as part of the Gaster Amulets project and are currently used only within this context.

While these <note> types may also be applicable to other visual or material collections, their broader use within MDC has not yet been tested and should therefore be considered provisional.

The forthcoming introduction of <object> may provide more appropriate TEI structures for recording this type of information. When implemented, the use of these <note> types may need to be reviewed and potentially replaced by more suitable elements.
